explanation.correct: Correct. Per ACLS guidelines, if pulseless VT/VF persists after the initial amiodarone 300 mg dose and subsequent defibrillation, a second dose of amiodarone 150 mg IV push may be given. This is the recommended second dose in refractory VT/VF.
explanation.a: Incorrect. High-dose epinephrine is not recommended in ACLS. The standard dose remains 1 mg IV every 3–5 minutes. Studies have not shown improved survival with high-dose epinephrine and it may worsen post-arrest outcomes.
explanation.b: Incorrect. While lidocaine is an alternative antiarrhythmic in VT/VF arrest, it is typically used when amiodarone is unavailable. Since amiodarone has already been started, the next step per ACLS is the second dose of amiodarone (150 mg), not switching to an alternative agent.
explanation.c: Incorrect. IV magnesium is indicated for torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT with prolonged QT), not for monomorphic VT or standard VF arrest. There is no evidence of torsades in this scenario.
explanation.d: Correct. Per ACLS guidelines, if pulseless VT/VF persists after the initial amiodarone 300 mg dose and subsequent defibrillation, a second dose of amiodarone 150 mg IV push may be given. This is the recommended second dose in refractory VT/VF.

cardiovascular.question: A 55-year-old woman presents with a blood pressure of 240/130 mmHg, severe headache, and blurred vision. Fundoscopic exam reveals papilledema and flame-shaped hemorrhages. Serum creatinine is 3.1 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL). Which IV antihypertensive is the most appropriate first-line agent?

answers.text: Clevidipine
IV hydralazine 20 mg bolus
Oral clonidine loading
Nicardipine

explanation.correct: Correct. IV nicardipine is an excellent first-line agent for hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage (hypertensive encephalopathy, acute kidney injury, retinopathy). It provides smooth, titratable BP reduction without reflex tachycardia. Goal is to reduce MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Clevidipine (IV dihydropyridine CCB) is primarily used for perioperative hypertension. While effective, it is not the first-line agent for hypertensive emergencies with end-organ damage involving the brain and kidneys.
explanation.b: Incorrect. IV hydralazine has an unpredictable dose response, long duration of action (up to 12 hours), and causes reflex tachycardia. It cannot be precisely titrated and may cause precipitous, uncontrolled BP drops.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Oral medications are inappropriate for hypertensive emergencies requiring rapid, titratable blood pressure control. Oral clonidine has unpredictable absorption and cannot be titrated to effect. IV agents are mandatory.
explanation.d: Correct. IV nicardipine is an excellent first-line agent for hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage (hypertensive encephalopathy, acute kidney injury, retinopathy). It provides smooth, titratable BP reduction without reflex tachycardia. Goal is to reduce MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour.

cardiovascular.question: A 48-year-old man presents with dyspnea and hypotension 10 days after cardiac surgery. Exam reveals muffled heart sounds, JVD, and pulsus paradoxus of 18 mmHg. Echocardiogram shows a loculated pericardial effusion compressing the right atrium. ECG does not show electrical alternans. What is the most appropriate intervention?

answers.text: Emergent pericardiocentesis under fluoroscopic guidance
High-dose ibuprofen and colchicine
IV fluid resuscitation and initiate dobutamine
Emergent surgical pericardial drainage (pericardial window)

explanation.correct: Correct. Post-surgical pericardial effusions causing tamponade are frequently loculated and posterior, making them inaccessible to percutaneous pericardiocentesis. Surgical drainage via a subxiphoid pericardial window or thoracotomy is the definitive approach for loculated post-operative effusions.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Pericardiocentesis is the standard approach for most pericardial tamponade. However, loculated effusions — common after cardiac surgery — are not amenable to blind or standard needle drainage due to the loculated, often posterior location of the fluid collection.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Anti-inflammatory therapy is appropriate for pericarditis without hemodynamic compromise. This patient has tamponade physiology (hypotension, JVD, pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg) requiring emergent drainage, not medical management.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While IV fluids can temporarily augment preload in tamponade, this is a bridge measure only. Dobutamine does not address the mechanical obstruction. Definitive drainage is required for hemodynamically significant tamponade — temporizing measures alone will not resolve this.
explanation.d: Correct. Post-surgical pericardial effusions causing tamponade are frequently loculated and posterior, making them inaccessible to percutaneous pericardiocentesis. Surgical drainage via a subxiphoid pericardial window or thoracotomy is the definitive approach for loculated post-operative effusions.

cardiovascular.question: A 58-year-old man with newly diagnosed HFrEF (EF 35%) has been started on lisinopril and carvedilol. He is euvolemic with a heart rate of 72 bpm and blood pressure of 118/72 mmHg. Per current guideline-directed medical therapy, which additional medication should be initiated?

answers.text: Spironolactone
Amlodipine
Hydralazine/isosorbide dinitrate
Ivabradine

explanation.correct: Correct. Current ACC/AHA guidelines recommend the four pillars of GDMT for HFrEF: ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone), and SGLT2 inhibitor. This patient is on an ACEi and beta-blocker; adding an MRA is the next appropriate step (along with an SGLT2i).
explanation.a: Correct. Current ACC/AHA guidelines recommend the four pillars of GDMT for HFrEF: ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone), and SGLT2 inhibitor. This patient is on an ACEi and beta-blocker; adding an MRA is the next appropriate step (along with an SGLT2i).
explanation.b: Incorrect. Amlodipine is a dihydropyridine CCB that provides no mortality benefit in HFrEF. While it is not contraindicated (unlike non-dihydropyridines), it is not part of the four pillars of GDMT and should not be added as routine HF therapy.
explanation.c: Incorrect. The combination of hydralazine and isosorbide dinitrate is an alternative for patients who cannot tolerate ACEi/ARB (due to angioedema, hyperkalemia, or renal dysfunction) or as add-on therapy in African American patients already on optimal GDMT. This patient tolerates lisinopril.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Ivabradine is indicated for HFrEF patients with a resting heart rate ≥70 bpm who are already on maximum-tolerated beta-blocker therapy. This patient's heart rate is 72 bpm, but he has not yet been optimized on carvedilol — titrate the beta-blocker first and add the remaining GDMT pillars.

cardiovascular.question: A 52-year-old woman with anterior STEMI undergoes emergent PCI but develops cardiogenic shock post-procedure. Despite norepinephrine and dobutamine, her MAP remains 55 mmHg. PA catheter shows PCWP 26 mmHg, CI 1.6 L/min/m², SVR 2,400 dynes. What is the most appropriate next intervention?

answers.text: Intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP)
Increase norepinephrine to maximum dose
Initiate Impella CP or VA-ECMO for mechanical circulatory support
Switch dobutamine to milrinone

explanation.correct: Correct. In AMI-related cardiogenic shock refractory to pharmacologic support (CI <1.8, PCWP >18, MAP <60 despite vasopressors/inotropes), escalation to percutaneous mechanical circulatory support (Impella or VA-ECMO) is indicated. These devices can augment cardiac output and allow myocardial recovery.
explanation.a: Incorrect. The SHOCK trial demonstrated that IABP does not improve mortality in cardiogenic shock post-MI. While still used as a bridge in some centers, it is no longer considered the preferred mechanical support device for refractory cardiogenic shock.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Escalating vasopressors in refractory cardiogenic shock increases afterload and myocardial oxygen demand, worsening ischemia and ventricular function. The already elevated SVR (2,400 dynes) indicates excessive vasoconstriction. Mechanical circulatory support is needed.
explanation.c: Correct. In AMI-related cardiogenic shock refractory to pharmacologic support (CI <1.8, PCWP >18, MAP <60 despite vasopressors/inotropes), escalation to percutaneous mechanical circulatory support (Impella or VA-ECMO) is indicated. These devices can augment cardiac output and allow myocardial recovery.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Simply substituting one inotrope for another in refractory cardiogenic shock is unlikely to change outcomes. Both agents work to increase cardiac output, but neither provides the degree of hemodynamic support this patient requires. Mechanical support is the next appropriate escalation.

cardiovascular.question: A 65-year-old man presents with 4 hours of chest pain. Initial troponin is elevated at 2.4 ng/mL (normal <0.04). ECG shows ST depressions in V3–V6 and T-wave inversions in leads I and aVL. He is hemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate risk stratification and management strategy?

answers.text: NSTEMI — administer aspirin, heparin, P2Y12 inhibitor, and pursue early invasive strategy within 24 hours
STEMI — activate the cath lab for emergent PCI within 90 minutes
Unstable angina — serial troponins and stress test before discharge
NSTEMI — manage conservatively with medical therapy and outpatient catheterization in 1–2 weeks

explanation.correct: Correct. This patient has NSTEMI (elevated troponin with ST depressions/T-wave inversions without ST elevation). Guidelines recommend an early invasive strategy (cardiac catheterization within 24 hours) for NSTEMI with high-risk features (elevated troponin, dynamic ECG changes). Dual antiplatelet therapy plus anticoagulation should be initiated immediately.
explanation.a: Correct. This patient has NSTEMI (elevated troponin with ST depressions/T-wave inversions without ST elevation). Guidelines recommend an early invasive strategy (cardiac catheterization within 24 hours) for NSTEMI with high-risk features (elevated troponin, dynamic ECG changes). Dual antiplatelet therapy plus anticoagulation should be initiated immediately.
explanation.b: Incorrect. STEMI requires ST-segment elevation in 2+ contiguous leads or new LBBB. This patient has ST depressions and T-wave inversions, which characterize NSTEMI. Emergent PCI (door-to-balloon <90 min) is not indicated for NSTEMI.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Unstable angina has ischemic symptoms and ECG changes but NEGATIVE troponin. This patient's troponin is markedly elevated (2.4 ng/mL), confirming myocardial necrosis and a diagnosis of NSTEMI, not unstable angina.
explanation.d: Incorrect. A purely conservative (ischemia-guided) strategy may be considered for very low-risk NSTEMI. This patient has high-risk features (significantly elevated troponin, multi-lead ECG changes) warranting early invasive strategy within 24 hours per ACC/AHA guidelines.

cardiovascular.question: A 78-year-old woman presents after a syncopal episode. ECG reveals a ventricular rate of 34 bpm with complete AV dissociation — P waves march through at a rate of 82 bpm and QRS complexes are wide at 140 ms. Which of the following is the most appropriate definitive management?

answers.text: Permanent pacemaker implantation
IV atropine 1 mg and observe
Oral theophylline for chronotropic support
IV isoproterenol infusion

explanation.correct: Correct. This ECG demonstrates third-degree (complete) heart block with a wide-complex ventricular escape rhythm, indicating an infranodal block. Third-degree AV block with symptomatic bradycardia (syncope) is a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation regardless of the underlying cause.
explanation.a: Correct. This ECG demonstrates third-degree (complete) heart block with a wide-complex ventricular escape rhythm, indicating an infranodal block. Third-degree AV block with symptomatic bradycardia (syncope) is a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation regardless of the underlying cause.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Atropine acts on the AV node to improve conduction and increase rate. In complete heart block with a wide-complex escape rhythm (infranodal block), atropine is typically ineffective because the block is below the AV node. Temporary pacing and definitive permanent pacemaker are needed.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Theophylline may be considered in select cases of mild sinus node dysfunction, but it is not appropriate definitive therapy for complete heart block with syncope. A permanent pacemaker is the standard of care.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While isoproterenol can temporarily increase ventricular escape rate as a bridge to pacing, it is not definitive therapy. It also increases myocardial oxygen demand and may trigger ventricular arrhythmias, making it a temporizing measure only.

cardiovascular.question: A 64-year-old man is post-operative day 1 from CABG surgery. He becomes acutely hypotensive with MAP 48 mmHg, HR 120 bpm, and chest tube output of 400 mL over the past hour (previously 50 mL/hr). CVP drops from 12 to 4 mmHg. What is the most likely cause and immediate management?

answers.text: Cardiac tamponade — return to the OR for emergent re-exploration
Surgical hemorrhage — transfuse blood products and prepare for emergent re-exploration
Hypovolemia from third-spacing — administer 2 L crystalloid bolus
Vasodilatory shock from protamine reaction — start vasopressin

explanation.correct: Correct. Acute increase in chest tube output (>400 mL/hr or >200 mL/hr for 2+ hours) after cardiac surgery indicates surgical hemorrhage requiring emergent re-exploration. Immediate management includes volume resuscitation with packed RBCs, FFP, and platelets while preparing for return to the OR.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While post-surgical tamponade is always a concern, the dramatically increased chest tube output argues against tamponade (in tamponade, fluid is trapped in the pericardium and chest tube output typically decreases). The high drainage suggests surgical bleeding.
explanation.b: Correct. Acute increase in chest tube output (>400 mL/hr or >200 mL/hr for 2+ hours) after cardiac surgery indicates surgical hemorrhage requiring emergent re-exploration. Immediate management includes volume resuscitation with packed RBCs, FFP, and platelets while preparing for return to the OR.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While the patient is hypovolemic, the cause is active hemorrhage (not third-spacing), as evidenced by the sudden increase in chest tube output. Crystalloid alone is insufficient — this patient needs blood product transfusion and likely surgical intervention to control the bleeding source.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Protamine reactions typically occur during or immediately after surgery when protamine is administered to reverse heparin. On POD 1 with high chest tube output and dropping CVP, active hemorrhage is far more likely than a delayed protamine reaction.

cardiovascular.question: A 70-year-old man with chronic HFrEF presents for a routine visit. His echocardiogram shows an EF of 30%. He reports NYHA Class II symptoms — comfortable at rest but symptomatic with ordinary physical activity. Which BNP result would be most consistent with his clinical presentation?

answers.text: BNP 15 pg/mL
BNP 450 pg/mL
BNP 2,500 pg/mL
BNP 85 pg/mL

explanation.correct: Correct. A BNP of 450 pg/mL is consistent with compensated but symptomatic HFrEF (NYHA Class II). BNP values in the 100–500 pg/mL range are typical for stable, chronic HF patients who are symptomatic with exertion but not acutely decompensated.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A BNP of 15 pg/mL is within the normal range and would be inconsistent with a known EF of 30% and symptomatic heart failure. Even in compensated HFrEF, BNP is typically elevated above the diagnostic threshold of 100 pg/mL.
explanation.b: Correct. A BNP of 450 pg/mL is consistent with compensated but symptomatic HFrEF (NYHA Class II). BNP values in the 100–500 pg/mL range are typical for stable, chronic HF patients who are symptomatic with exertion but not acutely decompensated.
explanation.c: Incorrect. A BNP >1,500 pg/mL typically indicates acute decompensated heart failure with significant volume overload. This patient is presenting for a routine visit with NYHA Class II symptoms — a markedly elevated BNP would suggest worsening or acutely decompensated HF.
explanation.d: Incorrect. A BNP <100 pg/mL has a strong negative predictive value for heart failure. In a patient with known HFrEF and an EF of 30%, a near-normal BNP would be unexpected and inconsistent with the clinical picture.

cardiovascular.question: A 72-year-old man with atrial fibrillation and PAD presents with sudden-onset severe left leg pain, pallor, and absent popliteal and pedal pulses. The leg is cool and mottled below the knee. Motor function of the foot is intact but sensation is diminished. How long does the ACNP have to restore perfusion before irreversible muscle necrosis occurs?

answers.text: 12–24 hours — there is ample time for diagnostic workup
4–6 hours — emergent revascularization (thromboembolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis) is required
48 hours — chronic PAD patients have developed collateral circulation
The limb is already nonviable — proceed to amputation

explanation.correct: Correct. Acute limb ischemia is classified using the Rutherford system. This patient has a threatened limb (class IIa — salvageable with prompt treatment). Skeletal muscle tolerates approximately 4–6 hours of warm ischemia. Treatment: IV heparin bolus immediately (prevent clot propagation), then emergent surgical thromboembolectomy (balloon catheter) or catheter-directed thrombolysis depending on duration and severity. Post-revascularization: monitor for reperfusion syndrome (hyperkalemia, myoglobinuria, metabolic acidosis, compartment syndrome).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Acute limb ischemia (ALI) from arterial embolism is a time-sensitive emergency. Skeletal muscle tolerates only 4–6 hours of warm ischemia before irreversible necrosis begins. A 12–24 hour window would result in limb loss in most cases.
explanation.b: Correct. Acute limb ischemia is classified using the Rutherford system. This patient has a threatened limb (class IIa — salvageable with prompt treatment). Skeletal muscle tolerates approximately 4–6 hours of warm ischemia. Treatment: IV heparin bolus immediately (prevent clot propagation), then emergent surgical thromboembolectomy (balloon catheter) or catheter-directed thrombolysis depending on duration and severity. Post-revascularization: monitor for reperfusion syndrome (hyperkalemia, myoglobinuria, metabolic acidosis, compartment syndrome).
explanation.c: Incorrect. While chronic PAD patients may have collateral vessels, ACUTE embolic occlusion does not allow time for collateral recruitment. The sudden onset (versus gradual atherosclerotic stenosis) distinguishes ALI from chronic limb ischemia. Acute occlusion requires emergent revascularization within hours.
explanation.d: Incorrect. This limb has intact motor function and only diminished (not absent) sensation — Rutherford class IIa (threatened but salvageable). Irreversible ischemia (class III) presents with profound sensory loss, paralysis, muscle rigidity, and fixed mottling. This limb can be saved with prompt revascularization.

cardiovascular.question: A 60-year-old man presents with palpitations and lightheadedness. ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm with sawtooth flutter waves best seen in leads II, III, and aVF. He is hemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate initial rate control strategy?

answers.text: IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push
IV diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg bolus
Synchronized cardioversion at 50 J
IV amiodarone 150 mg bolus for chemical cardioversion

explanation.correct: Correct. For hemodynamically stable atrial flutter with rapid ventricular response, IV calcium channel blockers (diltiazem) or beta-blockers (metoprolol) are first-line for rate control. Atrial flutter has a typical atrial rate of 300 bpm with 2:1 AV conduction producing a ventricular rate of ~150 bpm. Diltiazem slows AV nodal conduction, reducing the ventricular rate. For definitive management, catheter ablation of the cavotricuspid isthmus has >95% success rate and is the treatment of choice for recurrent typical flutter.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Adenosine transiently blocks AV nodal conduction and may unmask flutter waves diagnostically, but it does not provide sustained rate control for atrial flutter. Its ultra-short half-life (<10 seconds) means any effect is momentary. Adenosine is primarily used for diagnosis or treatment of SVT involving the AV node (AVNRT, AVRT).
explanation.b: Correct. For hemodynamically stable atrial flutter with rapid ventricular response, IV calcium channel blockers (diltiazem) or beta-blockers (metoprolol) are first-line for rate control. Atrial flutter has a typical atrial rate of 300 bpm with 2:1 AV conduction producing a ventricular rate of ~150 bpm. Diltiazem slows AV nodal conduction, reducing the ventricular rate. For definitive management, catheter ablation of the cavotricuspid isthmus has >95% success rate and is the treatment of choice for recurrent typical flutter.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While cardioversion is effective for atrial flutter (and uses lower energy than AF — typically 50–100 J), it is reserved for hemodynamically UNSTABLE patients or as elective rhythm control. This stable patient should first receive rate control. Cardioversion may be appropriate later if rate control is inadequate.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Amiodarone has limited efficacy for acute conversion of atrial flutter and may paradoxically slow the atrial rate enough to allow 1:1 AV conduction, potentially INCREASING the ventricular rate. Rate control with AV nodal blocking agents should be established before attempting pharmacologic rhythm control.

cardiovascular.question: A 78-year-old man presents after a syncopal episode while standing in line at the grocery store. He had no prodrome and no post-ictal confusion. ECG shows a normal sinus rhythm with a bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior fascicular block). Echocardiogram shows EF 55%. Orthostatic vitals are negative. Inpatient telemetry monitoring for 48 hours shows no arrhythmias, and electrophysiology study is non-diagnostic. What is the most appropriate next step for long-term monitoring?

answers.text: Tilt table testing to evaluate vasovagal syncope
Implantable loop recorder (ILR) or continuous cardiac monitoring
Carotid sinus massage in the ED
CT head to evaluate for stroke

explanation.correct: Correct. After an unrevealing inpatient workup (telemetry, EPS), this patient has unexplained syncope with bifascicular block — a conduction system abnormality that places him at risk for intermittent high-grade AV block. An implantable loop recorder (ILR) provides continuous ECG monitoring for up to 3 years and is the recommended next step when initial inpatient evaluation is non-diagnostic in patients with high-risk features for arrhythmic syncope. The ILR can capture infrequent arrhythmic events that short-duration monitoring misses.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Tilt table testing is useful for recurrent syncope suspected to be vasovagal (typically younger patients with prodrome of nausea, diaphoresis, warmth). This elderly patient had no prodrome and has a conduction abnormality — cardiac syncope from intermittent complete heart block must be excluded first.
explanation.b: Correct. After an unrevealing inpatient workup (telemetry, EPS), this patient has unexplained syncope with bifascicular block — a conduction system abnormality that places him at risk for intermittent high-grade AV block. An implantable loop recorder (ILR) provides continuous ECG monitoring for up to 3 years and is the recommended next step when initial inpatient evaluation is non-diagnostic in patients with high-risk features for arrhythmic syncope. The ILR can capture infrequent arrhythmic events that short-duration monitoring misses.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While carotid sinus hypersensitivity can cause syncope in elderly patients, carotid sinus massage carries risks (especially in patients with carotid disease) and is a low-yield diagnostic test. This patient has already undergone comprehensive inpatient evaluation including EPS — the appropriate next step is long-term ambulatory monitoring with an ILR to capture intermittent arrhythmic events that were not detected during the initial workup.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Stroke does not typically present with transient loss of consciousness with full recovery. Syncope is caused by transient global cerebral hypoperfusion, not focal ischemia. CT head is indicated if there is focal neurological deficit, head trauma from the fall, or concern for subarachnoid hemorrhage — none of which are described here. This patient needs long-term arrhythmia monitoring after a nondiagnostic inpatient workup.

cardiovascular.question: A 50-year-old woman with a prosthetic aortic valve presents with fever for 3 weeks, night sweats, and a new diastolic murmur. Three sets of blood cultures grow Enterococcus faecalis. Transesophageal echocardiogram shows a 1.4 cm vegetation on the prosthetic valve with a perivalvular abscess. How many major Duke criteria does this patient meet?

answers.text: One major criterion (positive blood cultures only)
Two major criteria (typical organism on blood cultures + echocardiographic evidence of endocarditis)
Three major criteria (positive cultures + vegetation + abscess count separately)
Zero major criteria — Duke criteria do not apply to prosthetic valve endocarditis

explanation.correct: Correct. The modified Duke criteria include 2 major, 5 minor criteria. Major criteria: (1) Blood cultures positive for a typical organism (Enterococcus faecalis from multiple cultures = major criterion); (2) Echocardiographic evidence of endocardial involvement (vegetation, abscess, or new prosthetic dehiscence on TEE = major criterion). Two major criteria = definite endocarditis. This patient also requires surgery given the prosthetic valve involvement, perivalvular abscess, and large vegetation (>10 mm).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Positive blood cultures with a typical endocarditis organism (Enterococcus) from ≥2 separate cultures is ONE major Duke criterion. However, the TEE showing a vegetation on a prosthetic valve is a SECOND major Duke criterion. This patient meets two major criteria.
explanation.b: Correct. The modified Duke criteria include 2 major, 5 minor criteria. Major criteria: (1) Blood cultures positive for a typical organism (Enterococcus faecalis from multiple cultures = major criterion); (2) Echocardiographic evidence of endocardial involvement (vegetation, abscess, or new prosthetic dehiscence on TEE = major criterion). Two major criteria = definite endocarditis. This patient also requires surgery given the prosthetic valve involvement, perivalvular abscess, and large vegetation (>10 mm).
explanation.c: Incorrect. The vegetation and perivalvular abscess are both echocardiographic findings that together constitute ONE major Duke criterion (evidence of endocardial involvement), not two separate major criteria. The Duke criteria group all echo findings under a single major criterion.
explanation.d: Incorrect. The modified Duke criteria apply to BOTH native and prosthetic valve endocarditis. In fact, prosthetic valve endocarditis is specifically included as a predisposing condition (minor criterion). The criteria are the standard diagnostic framework regardless of valve type.

cardiovascular.question: A 45-year-old woman with a mechanical mitral valve replacement is on warfarin with a target INR of 2.5–3.5. She presents for a routine visit and her INR is 2.0. She reports no missed doses or dietary changes, and there are no new medications. What is the most appropriate action?

answers.text: No change — an INR of 2.0 is within therapeutic range for all valve types
Increase warfarin dose to achieve target INR 2.5–3.5
Switch to apixaban for more predictable anticoagulation
Add aspirin 81 mg daily and keep warfarin at current dose

explanation.correct: Correct. Mechanical mitral valves have a higher thrombotic risk than mechanical aortic valves due to lower flow velocity and larger surface area. ACC/AHA guidelines recommend INR 2.5–3.5 for mechanical mitral valves (vs. 2.0–3.0 for mechanical aortic valves in low-risk patients). An INR of 2.0 is subtherapeutic and places the patient at increased risk for valve thrombosis and systemic embolization. The warfarin dose should be increased with close INR follow-up.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While INR 2.0–3.0 is therapeutic for most indications (DVT, AF, bioprosthetic valves), mechanical mitral valve replacements require a HIGHER target INR of 2.5–3.5 due to the greater thrombotic risk of the mitral position. An INR of 2.0 is subtherapeutic for this patient.
explanation.b: Correct. Mechanical mitral valves have a higher thrombotic risk than mechanical aortic valves due to lower flow velocity and larger surface area. ACC/AHA guidelines recommend INR 2.5–3.5 for mechanical mitral valves (vs. 2.0–3.0 for mechanical aortic valves in low-risk patients). An INR of 2.0 is subtherapeutic and places the patient at increased risk for valve thrombosis and systemic embolization. The warfarin dose should be increased with close INR follow-up.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are CONTRAINDICATED in patients with mechanical heart valves. The RE-ALIGN trial (dabigatran in mechanical valves) was terminated early due to excess thromboembolic and bleeding events. Warfarin remains the ONLY approved anticoagulant for mechanical valves.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While low-dose aspirin may be added to warfarin for certain high-risk mechanical valve patients, it does not substitute for achieving the target INR. The primary intervention is to increase the warfarin dose to achieve the recommended INR of 2.5–3.5. Aspirin alone does not provide adequate thromboprophylaxis for mechanical valves.

cardiovascular.question: A 60-year-old woman presents to the ED with chest pain. Her initial high-sensitivity troponin I (hs-cTnI) is 18 ng/L (99th percentile URL = 16 ng/L). She has a history of CKD stage 3 and stable HFrEF. A repeat troponin drawn 3 hours later is 19 ng/L. What is the most accurate interpretation?

answers.text: Acute myocardial infarction — the troponin is above the 99th percentile
Demand ischemia from tachycardia — start rate control
False positive from hemolysis or lab error — repeat immediately
Chronic myocardial injury from CKD and HF — no acute delta, pursue non-ACS workup

explanation.correct: Correct. High-sensitivity troponin assays detect chronic low-grade myocardial injury common in CKD, HF, pulmonary hypertension, and other conditions. The key to diagnosing acute MI is the DELTA (rise and/or fall) in serial troponins, not a single elevated value. A change of <20% in the low-range (or <50% at very low values) over 3 hours is considered non-significant. This patient's stable troponin (18 → 19) indicates chronic injury, not acute ischemia. The Fourth Universal Definition of MI requires both elevated troponin AND a significant rise/fall pattern.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While the troponin exceeds the 99th percentile URL, the diagnosis of acute MI requires a RISE AND/OR FALL pattern (delta) in serial troponins, not just a single elevated value. This patient's troponin is essentially unchanged (18 → 19 ng/L), suggesting chronic myocardial injury — not acute MI.
explanation.b: Incorrect. While type 2 MI (demand ischemia) can elevate troponin, it requires evidence of supply-demand mismatch (tachycardia, hypotension, anemia) AND a significant rise/fall pattern. The flat troponin trend (18 → 19) and absence of described hemodynamic stressor do not support this diagnosis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. High-sensitivity troponin assays are highly specific for cardiac myocyte injury. A value of 18 ng/L in a patient with CKD and HFrEF is entirely expected from chronic myocardial stress — it is not a false positive. The clinical interpretation depends on the delta between serial values, not the absolute number.
explanation.d: Correct. High-sensitivity troponin assays detect chronic low-grade myocardial injury common in CKD, HF, pulmonary hypertension, and other conditions. The key to diagnosing acute MI is the DELTA (rise and/or fall) in serial troponins, not a single elevated value. A change of <20% in the low-range (or <50% at very low values) over 3 hours is considered non-significant. This patient's stable troponin (18 → 19) indicates chronic injury, not acute ischemia. The Fourth Universal Definition of MI requires both elevated troponin AND a significant rise/fall pattern.

pulmonary.question: A 44-year-old woman with septic shock from pneumonia is intubated and placed on VC-AC ventilation. Current settings: Vt 520 mL (8 mL/kg IBW), RR 20, FiO₂ 0.80, PEEP 8. ABG: pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 48, PaO₂ 68. Plateau pressure is 32 cmH₂O. CXR shows bilateral infiltrates. P/F ratio is 85. Which ventilator adjustment is the highest priority?

answers.text: Reduce tidal volume to 6 mL/kg IBW and accept permissive hypercapnia
Increase RR to 28 to correct the respiratory acidosis
Increase PEEP to 14 and FiO₂ to 1.0 to improve oxygenation
Switch to pressure control mode to reduce peak airway pressures

explanation.correct: Correct. Lung-protective ventilation with 6 mL/kg IBW is the highest-priority intervention in ARDS. The ARMA trial mortality benefit is one of the strongest findings in critical care. Plateau pressure of 32 also approaches the ≤30 cmH₂O target — reducing Vt addresses both.
explanation.a: Correct. Lung-protective ventilation with 6 mL/kg IBW is the highest-priority intervention in ARDS. The ARMA trial mortality benefit is one of the strongest findings in critical care. Plateau pressure of 32 also approaches the ≤30 cmH₂O target — reducing Vt addresses both.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Increasing RR addresses the pH but does not correct the underlying problem — injurious tidal volumes causing VILI. Higher RR also increases auto-PEEP risk and does not reduce lung stretch.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While oxygenation needs improvement, increasing FiO₂ to 1.0 risks oxygen toxicity and does not address the mechanically injurious Vt. PEEP optimization should follow Vt correction and be done systematically.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Switching modes does not inherently reduce Vt or VILI — the volume delivered depends on the pressure set. Mode change without addressing the Vt misses the core lung protection principle.

pulmonary.question: A mechanically ventilated patient on VC-AC has the following data: set Vt 500 mL, RR 16, PEEP 10, FiO₂ 0.55. Peak inspiratory pressure 42 cmH₂O, plateau pressure 38 cmH₂O, auto-PEEP 6 cmH₂O. The patient's IBW is 70 kg. Which is the most accurate interpretation and appropriate next step?

answers.text: High resistance from bronchospasm; administer bronchodilator and reassess
Elevated plateau and auto-PEEP indicate dynamic hyperinflation; reduce RR and increase expiratory time
Elevated peak pressure with normal plateau indicates secretion burden; suction and reassess
Plateau pressure is acceptable; no ventilator changes needed at this time

explanation.correct: Correct. Elevated plateau (38) + auto-PEEP (6) + narrow peak-plateau gradient = dynamic hyperinflation from inadequate expiratory time. Reducing RR is the primary fix; consider also reducing Vt given Plt >30.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Bronchospasm causes increased airway resistance, which manifests as a high peak-to-plateau gradient (>10 cmH₂O). Here the gradient is only 4 cmH₂O — resistance is not the problem.
explanation.b: Correct. Elevated plateau (38) + auto-PEEP (6) + narrow peak-plateau gradient = dynamic hyperinflation from inadequate expiratory time. Reducing RR is the primary fix; consider also reducing Vt given Plt >30.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Secretions cause increased resistance, producing a high peak-to-plateau gradient. This patient's gradient is narrow (4 cmH₂O), ruling out airway resistance as the primary issue.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Plateau pressure of 38 cmH₂O exceeds the lung-protective target of ≤30 cmH₂O, and auto-PEEP indicates air trapping that can cause hemodynamic compromise and barotrauma. Action is required.

pulmonary.question: A 51-year-old man with severe ARDS (P/F ratio 74) has been on lung-protective ventilation for 36 hours: Vt 6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP 14, FiO₂ 0.80, RR 24. He remains supine. Plateau pressure is 28 cmH₂O. Driving pressure is 20 cmH₂O. SpO₂ is 84% despite these settings. Which intervention has the strongest evidence for reducing mortality at this point?

answers.text: Increase PEEP to 20 cmH₂O using the ARDSNet PEEP-FiO₂ table
Initiate prone positioning for at least 16 hours per day
Begin inhaled nitric oxide to improve V/Q matching
Initiate high-frequency oscillatory ventilation (HFOV)

explanation.correct: Correct. PROSEVA trial: prone positioning ≥16 hours/day in severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 28-day mortality by 50%. This is the strongest level of evidence for any rescue therapy in severe ARDS beyond lung-protective ventilation.
explanation.a: Incorrect. PEEP of 20 may cause hemodynamic compromise and barotrauma. The ALVEOLI and LOV trials did not show mortality benefit from high PEEP strategies over standard PEEP in unselected ARDS patients.
explanation.b: Correct. PROSEVA trial: prone positioning ≥16 hours/day in severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 28-day mortality by 50%. This is the strongest level of evidence for any rescue therapy in severe ARDS beyond lung-protective ventilation.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Inhaled nitric oxide improves oxygenation transiently but has not demonstrated mortality benefit in ARDS in multiple RCTs. It is considered a bridge to definitive therapy, not a mortality-reducing intervention.
explanation.d: Incorrect. HFOV was studied in the OSCILLATE and OSCAR trials, which showed no mortality benefit and potential harm (OSCILLATE showed increased mortality with HFOV). It is not recommended in current ARDS management.

pulmonary.question: A 58-year-old woman 5 days post-hip replacement develops sudden pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. HR 118, BP 102/68, SpO₂ 90% on 4L NC, RR 26. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with S1Q3T3 pattern. Bedside echo shows RV dilation with McConnell's sign and septal bowing. CT-PA is ordered but the patient is deteriorating. Troponin returns elevated. Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

answers.text: Start IV heparin infusion and await CT-PA results before further intervention
Administer systemic thrombolytics (tPA 100 mg IV over 2 hours)
Place an IVC filter and start therapeutic enoxaparin
Start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for septic PE

explanation.correct: Correct. Massive PE (hemodynamic compromise + echo RV strain + troponin elevation) in a deteriorating patient warrants systemic thrombolysis. CT-PA confirmation is not required when clinical + echo diagnosis is clear and the patient cannot safely be transported.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Heparin anticoagulation alone is insufficient for massive PE with hemodynamic compromise. Heparin prevents propagation but does not lyse existing clot rapidly enough in a deteriorating patient.
explanation.b: Correct. Massive PE (hemodynamic compromise + echo RV strain + troponin elevation) in a deteriorating patient warrants systemic thrombolysis. CT-PA confirmation is not required when clinical + echo diagnosis is clear and the patient cannot safely be transported.
explanation.c: Incorrect. IVC filters prevent new PE but do not treat existing massive PE. Enoxaparin is not appropriate in massive PE requiring immediate clot lysis. Subcutaneous dosing also has unpredictable absorption in hemodynamically compromised patients.
explanation.d: Incorrect. There is no indication for septic PE here — the presentation is classic mechanical thromboembolism post-orthopedic surgery. Antibiotics would delay appropriate life-saving treatment.

pulmonary.question: A 67-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 28% predicted) is intubated for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure after failing NIV. ABG post-intubation: pH 7.18, PaCO₂ 88, PaO₂ 74 on FiO₂ 0.40. Settings: VC-AC, Vt 500 mL, RR 20, PEEP 5, I:E 1:2. He is 80 kg IBW. Which ventilator adjustment is most appropriate to prevent dynamic hyperinflation?

answers.text: Increase RR to 28 to rapidly correct hypercapnia
Reduce RR to 12, reduce Vt to 6 mL/kg IBW, and extend expiratory time to I:E 1:4 or 1:5
Add PEEP 10 to stent open airways and reduce air trapping
Switch to SIMV mode at rate 10 and allow spontaneous breathing

explanation.correct: Correct. Low RR + low Vt + prolonged expiration = the foundation of ventilator management in obstructive lung disease. This minimizes air trapping, reduces auto-PEEP, and allows gradual CO₂ correction rather than acute normalization.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Increasing RR dramatically worsens air trapping in obstructive disease. Each breath stacks on the last, causing dynamic hyperinflation, rising auto-PEEP, and potential cardiovascular collapse.
explanation.b: Correct. Low RR + low Vt + prolonged expiration = the foundation of ventilator management in obstructive lung disease. This minimizes air trapping, reduces auto-PEEP, and allows gradual CO₂ correction rather than acute normalization.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Adding extrinsic PEEP above auto-PEEP in COPD worsens hyperinflation. Extrinsic PEEP is only used cautiously (≤80% of auto-PEEP) to reduce inspiratory work in spontaneously breathing patients, not to stent airways.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Allowing spontaneous breathing in an acutely decompensated, severely acidotic COPD patient is inappropriate. The patient needs full ventilatory support with a controlled, lung-protective strategy until the acute exacerbation is treated.

pulmonary.question: A 55-year-old woman has been mechanically ventilated for 6 days for ARDS that has resolved. Current settings: CPAP 5, PS 8, FiO₂ 0.35. ABG: pH 7.42, PaCO₂ 40, PaO₂ 88. RR 14, Vt 480 mL, HR 82, BP 124/76. GCS 15, cough is strong, she can lift her head off the pillow. Secretions are minimal. RSBI is 48. Which is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Continue current settings and reassess readiness in 24 hours
Perform a spontaneous breathing trial on T-piece or minimal support for 30-120 minutes
Reduce pressure support to 4 cmH₂O and reassess in 4 hours
Extubate immediately without SBT given favorable parameters

explanation.correct: Correct. All weaning readiness criteria are met. An SBT of 30-120 minutes on T-piece or low-level PS (5/0) is the evidence-based next step. Successful SBT completion should be followed by extubation.
explanation.a: Incorrect. This patient meets all readiness criteria. Delaying SBT unnecessarily prolongs mechanical ventilation, increasing VAP risk, ICU LOS, and complications.
explanation.b: Correct. All weaning readiness criteria are met. An SBT of 30-120 minutes on T-piece or low-level PS (5/0) is the evidence-based next step. Successful SBT completion should be followed by extubation.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Gradually weaning PS is an older, slower strategy not supported by current evidence. Once readiness criteria are met, proceeding directly to SBT is faster and equally safe.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Extubating without an SBT skips the most important predictive test. The SBT is the gold standard for assessing extubation readiness — favorable static parameters alone do not predict success as reliably.

pulmonary.question: A 62-year-old man with known metastatic lung cancer presents with progressive dyspnea. CXR shows a large right pleural effusion. Thoracentesis yields 1200 mL of straw-colored fluid. Pleural fluid analysis: LDH 380 U/L (serum LDH 210), protein 4.8 g/dL (serum protein 6.2 g/dL), glucose 48 mg/dL, pH 7.18, WBC 2400 with 85% lymphocytes. Which interpretation is correct?

answers.text: Transudate — consistent with malignant effusion from hypoalbuminemia
Exudate — pleural fluid LDH/serum LDH ratio >0.6 meets Light's criteria
Empyema — low glucose and low pH require immediate chest tube drainage
Chylothorax — lymphocyte predominance confirms thoracic duct injury

explanation.correct: Correct. Pleural/serum LDH ratio 1.81 (>0.6) meets Light's exudate criteria. In a patient with metastatic lung cancer, this is a malignant exudative effusion. Cytology is the next diagnostic step.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Transudates result from hydrostatic/oncotic pressure imbalances (CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome). This fluid meets exudate criteria by Light's. Malignant effusions are virtually always exudates.
explanation.b: Correct. Pleural/serum LDH ratio 1.81 (>0.6) meets Light's exudate criteria. In a patient with metastatic lung cancer, this is a malignant exudative effusion. Cytology is the next diagnostic step.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Empyema requires frank pus or positive Gram stain/culture — neither is present here. In a malignant effusion, low glucose and low pH reflect high metabolic activity from tumor cells and carry prognostic significance (pH <7.3 predicts poor pleurodesis response), but they do not indicate infection or mandate chest tube drainage. This is a malignant exudate, not an empyema.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Chylothorax is diagnosed by triglyceride content >110 mg/dL and milky appearance. Lymphocyte predominance in pleural fluid is common in malignant and tuberculous effusions — it does not indicate chylothorax.

pulmonary.question: A 70-year-old man with COPD (home O₂ 2L NC) presents with 3 days of worsening dyspnea and increased sputum. RR 28, HR 104, BP 138/84. ABG on 4L NC: pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 72, PaO₂ 62, HCO₃ 32. CXR shows hyperinflation without infiltrate. GCS 14. Which is the most appropriate initial respiratory intervention?

answers.text: Increase supplemental O₂ to 10L non-rebreather mask
Initiate non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV/BiPAP)
Proceed to immediate endotracheal intubation
Administer IV sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidosis

explanation.correct: Correct. NIPPV is first-line for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure in COPD with pH 7.25-7.35. Multiple RCTs (Brochard, Plant) demonstrate reduced intubation rates and mortality. GCS 14 is adequate for safe NIV use.
explanation.a: Incorrect. High-flow O₂ in hypercapnic COPD suppresses hypoxic drive and worsens CO₂ retention. Target SpO₂ 88-92% in COPD. A 10L NRB would likely worsen hypercapnia and precipitate respiratory arrest.
explanation.b: Correct. NIPPV is first-line for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure in COPD with pH 7.25-7.35. Multiple RCTs (Brochard, Plant) demonstrate reduced intubation rates and mortality. GCS 14 is adequate for safe NIV use.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Intubation is reserved for NIV failure, severe encephalopathy (GCS <8), hemodynamic instability, or inability to protect the airway. This patient warrants an NIV trial first — intubating a COPD patient commits them to a difficult and prolonged wean.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IV bicarbonate does not treat respiratory acidosis — it requires CO₂ elimination. Bicarbonate administration generates CO₂ (HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ → H₂O + CO₂), potentially worsening hypercapnia in a patient who cannot increase ventilation.

pulmonary.question: A 68-year-old man with diabetes is admitted directly to the ICU with community-acquired pneumonia. He requires vasopressors and is intubated. He has no recent healthcare exposure or antibiotic use. Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters. Which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate while awaiting culture results?

answers.text: Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily plus azithromycin 500 mg IV daily
Vancomycin IV plus piperacillin-tazobactam IV
Vancomycin IV plus ceftriaxone IV plus azithromycin IV
Levofloxacin 750 mg IV daily as monotherapy

explanation.correct: Correct. Vancomycin (MRSA coverage given gram stain) + ceftriaxone (pneumococcal/gram-negative coverage) + azithromycin (atypical coverage) addresses all major severe CAP pathogens including MRSA. This follows IDSA/ATS severe CAP guidelines with MRSA risk factor.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Standard CAP therapy without MRSA coverage is insufficient when gram stain suggests S. aureus. In ICU-level CAP, empiric MRSA coverage is warranted pending cultures — especially with gram-positive cocci in clusters.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Piperacillin-tazobactam covers gram-negatives and anaerobes but lacks atypical coverage. For severe CAP, atypical coverage (macrolide or fluoroquinolone) is required per IDSA guidelines. Pip-tazo alone does not fulfill CAP guidelines.
explanation.c: Correct. Vancomycin (MRSA coverage given gram stain) + ceftriaxone (pneumococcal/gram-negative coverage) + azithromycin (atypical coverage) addresses all major severe CAP pathogens including MRSA. This follows IDSA/ATS severe CAP guidelines with MRSA risk factor.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Fluoroquinolone monotherapy does not provide MRSA coverage. Levofloxacin as monotherapy may be appropriate for non-severe CAP but is inadequate for ICU-level CAP with gram stain suggesting S. aureus.

pulmonary.question: A 72-year-old man with atrial fibrillation is diagnosed with a submassive PE (RV dilation, troponin elevated, hemodynamically stable, BP 118/74). He is currently on no anticoagulation. He has no contraindications to anticoagulation and normal renal function. Which is the most appropriate anticoagulation strategy?

answers.text: Systemic thrombolytics given the elevated troponin and RV dilation
Apixaban 10 mg BID for 7 days then 5 mg BID as long-term therapy
IV heparin infusion with transition to warfarin targeting INR 2-3
Apixaban 5 mg BID starting immediately — same dose used for AF

explanation.correct: Correct. Apixaban is guideline-recommended first-line therapy for PE. The AMPLIFY trial established the PE dosing regimen: 10 mg BID for 7 days (loading), then 5 mg BID. The loading dose is critical — starting at 5 mg BID (the AF dose) without the loading phase provides subtherapeutic anticoagulation during the highest-risk period.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Systemic thrombolytics are indicated for massive (hemodynamically unstable) PE only. The PEITHO trial demonstrated that thrombolytics in submassive PE increased major bleeding and intracranial hemorrhage without a mortality benefit.
explanation.b: Correct. Apixaban is guideline-recommended first-line therapy for PE. The AMPLIFY trial established the PE dosing regimen: 10 mg BID for 7 days (loading), then 5 mg BID. The loading dose is critical — starting at 5 mg BID (the AF dose) without the loading phase provides subtherapeutic anticoagulation during the highest-risk period.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While IV heparin with warfarin bridge is an acceptable PE treatment strategy, DOACs are preferred per ACC/AHA guidelines given equivalent efficacy with lower bleeding risk, no monitoring requirement, and greater convenience. Warfarin requires bridging during the INR subtherapeutic period.
explanation.d: Incorrect. The AF dose of apixaban (5 mg BID) is not equivalent to the PE treatment dose. PE requires 10 mg BID for 7 days as a loading dose to achieve rapid therapeutic anticoagulation. Starting at the AF maintenance dose without loading provides inadequate coverage during the acute phase.

pulmonary.question: A 48-year-old obese woman (BMI 42) with angioedema from an ACE inhibitor is brought to the ICU with progressive stridor and inability to swallow secretions. Her voice is muffled. SpO₂ 91% on 15L NRB. Attempts at bag-mask ventilation show poor chest rise. Laryngoscopy reveals a massively swollen tongue and epiglottis. A second intubation attempt fails. SpO₂ is now 78%. Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

answers.text: Administer succinylcholine and attempt a third laryngoscopy with a different blade
Attempt intubation with a video laryngoscope
Perform emergency surgical cricothyrotomy
Insert a supraglottic airway (LMA) and ventilate

explanation.correct: Correct. CICO = immediate surgical airway. Cricothyrotomy (scalpel-finger-bougie or commercial device) is the definitive intervention in cannot-intubate, cannot-oxygenate. Every second of delay worsens hypoxic injury. This is the DAS difficult airway algorithm endpoint.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A third laryngoscopy attempt in a CICO scenario wastes precious seconds and is unlikely to succeed when the anatomy is completely obscured by angioedema. Succinylcholine may have already been used — further paralysis in a failed airway is dangerous.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Video laryngoscopy is an appropriate early rescue attempt but is unlikely to succeed when direct laryngoscopy has already failed twice due to supraglottic swelling obscuring the view. In a CICO emergency, continuing laryngoscopy attempts delays definitive airway access.
explanation.c: Correct. CICO = immediate surgical airway. Cricothyrotomy (scalpel-finger-bougie or commercial device) is the definitive intervention in cannot-intubate, cannot-oxygenate. Every second of delay worsens hypoxic injury. This is the DAS difficult airway algorithm endpoint.
explanation.d: Incorrect. An LMA is a supraglottic device — it sits above the obstruction and will not bypass the swollen tongue and epiglottis. It may provide partial ventilation in some cases but is unlikely to be effective in severe supraglottic angioedema and does not address the underlying obstruction.

pulmonary.question: A 41-year-old woman with known Group 1 PAH on ambrisentan and tadalafil is admitted to the ICU with acute decompensation. BP 84/52, HR 122, SpO₂ 86% on 6L NC. Echo shows severely dilated RV with flattened IVS and estimated RVSP 88 mmHg. She is in acute RV failure. Which intervention is most appropriate to reduce RV afterload?

answers.text: IV norepinephrine 0.1 mcg/kg/min to improve systemic perfusion pressure
Inhaled nitric oxide or IV epoprostenol to selectively reduce pulmonary vascular resistance
IV furosemide 80 mg to reduce RV preload and improve RV function
IV metoprolol for rate control to improve RV filling time

explanation.correct: Correct. Inhaled NO and IV epoprostenol are both selective pulmonary vasodilators that reduce PVR — the primary driver of acute RV failure in PAH. They are guideline-recommended first-line treatments for decompensated PAH with acute RV failure.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While systemic hypotension requires support, norepinephrine alone increases RV afterload by raising PVR in addition to SVR. If vasopressors are needed, vasopressin is preferred as it preferentially increases SVR without worsening PVR as dramatically as norepinephrine.
explanation.b: Correct. Inhaled NO and IV epoprostenol are both selective pulmonary vasodilators that reduce PVR — the primary driver of acute RV failure in PAH. They are guideline-recommended first-line treatments for decompensated PAH with acute RV failure.
explanation.c: Incorrect. In acute RV failure, the RV is already struggling against high afterload — it needs adequate preload to generate forward flow. Aggressive diuresis drops preload and can precipitate cardiovascular collapse. Modest diuresis may be appropriate if there is clear volume overload, but aggressive IV furosemide is dangerous.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Beta-blockers are contraindicated in acute RV failure. The tachycardia is compensatory — the elevated HR maintains cardiac output in the face of severely impaired RV stroke volume. Rate control will cause hemodynamic collapse.

pulmonary.question: A 38-year-old woman with moderate ARDS (P/F 148) on lung-protective ventilation develops severe ventilator dyssynchrony. She is on fentanyl 100 mcg/hr and propofol 40 mcg/kg/min. Despite adequate sedation, she is fighting the ventilator with double-triggering and breath stacking. Her plateau pressure is 34 cmH₂O and driving pressure is 22 cmH₂O. Which intervention is most appropriate?

answers.text: Increase propofol to 80 mcg/kg/min
Add a short course of neuromuscular blockade with cisatracurium
Switch to pressure control mode to reduce peak pressures
Increase Vt to 8 mL/kg IBW to reduce respiratory drive

explanation.correct: Correct. Cisatracurium infusion for 48 hours is guideline-supported for moderate-severe ARDS with refractory dyssynchrony. It eliminates ventilator-patient asynchrony, reduces VILI from breath stacking, and lowers inflammatory mediators. Use a CICP or TOF monitor.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Propofol at 40 mcg/kg/min with fentanyl already represents significant sedation. Escalating propofol to 80 mcg/kg/min risks propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS) — metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac failure. This is not the appropriate next step.
explanation.b: Correct. Cisatracurium infusion for 48 hours is guideline-supported for moderate-severe ARDS with refractory dyssynchrony. It eliminates ventilator-patient asynchrony, reduces VILI from breath stacking, and lowers inflammatory mediators. Use a CICP or TOF monitor.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Switching modes does not eliminate patient effort or dyssynchrony. The patient will still trigger breaths in any mode. The fundamental problem is patient-ventilator dyssynchrony from high respiratory drive, not the mode itself.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Increasing Vt to 8 mL/kg would worsen VILI and violate lung-protective ventilation principles. Higher Vt may temporarily reduce respiratory drive by increasing CO₂ elimination but causes injurious overdistension — this is never the right trade-off in ARDS.

pulmonary.question: A 55-year-old man on mechanical ventilation develops acute hemodynamic instability. BP drops from 128/82 to 74/46 within 20 minutes. HR increases to 134. The ventilator alarms show rising peak pressures. Breath sounds are absent on the left. Tracheal deviation to the right is noted. Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

answers.text: Obtain emergent CXR to confirm pneumothorax before intervention
Perform needle decompression at the left 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line
Increase PEEP to 14 to re-expand the collapsed lung
Perform bronchoscopy to rule out mucus plug causing left lung collapse

explanation.correct: Correct. Immediate needle decompression is life-saving in tension pneumothorax. The 2nd ICS MCL is the traditional landmark (4th/5th ICS anterior axillary line is an alternative with higher success rates). This is followed by definitive chest tube placement.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Tension pneumothorax is a clinical emergency — waiting for CXR in a hemodynamically unstable patient with classic signs wastes critical time. Death can occur within minutes. Treat first, image after.
explanation.b: Correct. Immediate needle decompression is life-saving in tension pneumothorax. The 2nd ICS MCL is the traditional landmark (4th/5th ICS anterior axillary line is an alternative with higher success rates). This is followed by definitive chest tube placement.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Increasing PEEP in a tension pneumothorax would worsen the tension by pushing more air through the defect into the pleural space, further collapsing the lung and worsening mediastinal shift. This would be fatal.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Mucus plugging causes atelectasis and absent breath sounds but does not cause tracheal deviation, hemodynamic collapse, or rising ventilator pressures in this pattern. Bronchoscopy during hemodynamic instability in this presentation would delay life-saving treatment.

pulmonary.question: A 29-year-old woman with severe persistent asthma presents in status asthmaticus. She has received three doses of albuterol nebulization and IV methylprednisolone 125 mg. RR 34, HR 128, SpO₂ 90% on 10L NRB, accessory muscle use, unable to speak full sentences. PEFR is 25% of predicted. She is becoming fatigued. Which is the most appropriate next intervention before considering intubation?

answers.text: IV magnesium sulfate 2g over 20 minutes
Add ipratropium nebulization every 20 minutes for 3 doses
Initiate heliox 70:30 via non-rebreather mask
Immediate endotracheal intubation with succinylcholine RSI

explanation.correct: Correct. IV magnesium sulfate 2g over 20 minutes is guideline-recommended for severe asthma not responding to initial therapy. It acts as a bronchodilator via smooth muscle calcium channel blockade and is well-tolerated with minimal side effects. GINA and NAEPP guidelines support its use in this scenario.
explanation.a: Correct. IV magnesium sulfate 2g over 20 minutes is guideline-recommended for severe asthma not responding to initial therapy. It acts as a bronchodilator via smooth muscle calcium channel blockade and is well-tolerated with minimal side effects. GINA and NAEPP guidelines support its use in this scenario.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Ipratropium has already been shown to be beneficial when added to albuterol early in status asthmaticus, but this patient has already received multiple rounds of bronchodilators. Ipratropium at this point is a reasonable adjunct but not the most impactful next intervention — magnesium takes priority.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Heliox reduces turbulent airflow in large airways and may reduce work of breathing, but evidence for mortality or intubation benefit is weak. It is an adjunct, not a primary rescue therapy. Magnesium has much stronger evidence.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Intubation in status asthmaticus carries significant risk — dynamic hyperinflation, auto-PEEP, and hemodynamic collapse post-intubation are common. It should be reserved for imminent respiratory arrest or failure of all pharmacologic options. This patient is fatigued but has not exhausted all options.

pulmonary.question: A 54-year-old man is admitted with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure. ABG on 4L NC: pH 7.44, PaCO₂ 32, PaO₂ 54, A-a gradient 62 mmHg. CXR shows bilateral perihilar infiltrates. He has no fever, normal WBC, troponin mildly elevated, BNP 1840 pg/mL, and bilateral lower extremity edema. Which diagnosis best explains his presentation and what is the most appropriate initial intervention?

answers.text: ARDS from occult infection; initiate broad-spectrum antibiotics and lung-protective ventilation
Cardiogenic pulmonary edema; initiate IV diuresis and consider NIV
Bilateral pneumonia; initiate HCAP antibiotic coverage and supplemental oxygen
Pulmonary fibrosis exacerbation; initiate high-dose corticosteroids

explanation.correct: Correct. Classic cardiogenic pulmonary edema: BNP elevation, bilateral perihilar infiltrates, edema, hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis. IV furosemide and NIV (CPAP reduces preload and improves oxygenation in acute pulmonary edema per 3CPO trial) are first-line.
explanation.a: Incorrect. ARDS requires bilateral infiltrates + P/F <300 + not fully explained by cardiac failure. This patient has a clear cardiac cause (BNP 1840, edema, perihilar pattern). ARDS and cardiogenic pulmonary edema can coexist but treating as ARDS first would miss the primary diagnosis.
explanation.b: Correct. Classic cardiogenic pulmonary edema: BNP elevation, bilateral perihilar infiltrates, edema, hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis. IV furosemide and NIV (CPAP reduces preload and improves oxygenation in acute pulmonary edema per 3CPO trial) are first-line.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Bilateral pneumonia causing HCAP-pattern disease would present with fever, leukocytosis, and productive cough. This patient has no fever, normal WBC, and the presentation is far more consistent with cardiac failure given the BNP and edema.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Acute exacerbation of pulmonary fibrosis (AE-IPF) typically presents in a patient with known ILD, has a characteristic CT pattern, and BNP would not be markedly elevated. High-dose steroids may be used in AE-IPF but are not indicated here.

pulmonary.question: A 60-year-old man is mechanically ventilated on VC-AC: Vt 480 mL (6 mL/kg IBW), RR 22, PEEP 10, FiO₂ 0.65. ABG: pH 7.48, PaCO₂ 28, PaO₂ 96. He is afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and RASS -1. The team wants to improve oxygenation efficiency. Plateau pressure is 24 cmH₂O. Which change best optimizes oxygenation while maintaining lung protection?

answers.text: Increase Vt to 8 mL/kg IBW to recruit atelectatic alveoli
Increase PEEP to 14 cmH₂O and reduce FiO₂ to 0.50 if tolerated
Increase RR to 30 to improve minute ventilation and oxygenation
Switch to APRV mode to improve mean airway pressure and oxygenation

explanation.correct: Correct. Increasing PEEP recruits alveoli, improves oxygenation, and allows FiO₂ reduction. Plateau is 24 with room to increase PEEP. The ARDSNet PEEP-FiO₂ table guides PEEP titration. Simultaneously reducing FiO₂ minimizes oxygen toxicity risk.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Increasing Vt to 8 mL/kg violates lung-protective ventilation. The patient already has respiratory alkalosis — increasing Vt worsens it and adds VILI risk. Vt should be maintained at 6 mL/kg.
explanation.b: Correct. Increasing PEEP recruits alveoli, improves oxygenation, and allows FiO₂ reduction. Plateau is 24 with room to increase PEEP. The ARDSNet PEEP-FiO₂ table guides PEEP titration. Simultaneously reducing FiO₂ minimizes oxygen toxicity risk.
explanation.c: Incorrect. RR is already causing hypocapnia (PaCO₂ 28). Increasing RR worsens the alkalosis. RR should be decreased, not increased. Oxygenation is driven by FiO₂ and PEEP — not RR.
explanation.d: Incorrect. APRV is a complex mode with limited RCT evidence for improved outcomes over standard lung-protective ventilation. It carries risk of asynchrony and is not recommended as first-line escalation in a patient who simply needs PEEP optimization.

pulmonary.question: A 45-year-old woman presents with right leg swelling and pleuritic chest pain 2 weeks after a long international flight. Wells score is 6 (high probability). She has no renal impairment, no active bleeding, and is hemodynamically stable. D-dimer returns at 4.2 mcg/mL. Which is the most appropriate diagnostic and treatment approach?

answers.text: D-dimer is elevated — no further testing needed; start therapeutic anticoagulation
Proceed directly to CT pulmonary angiography; start anticoagulation empirically while awaiting results
D-dimer elevation is non-specific; repeat D-dimer in 24 hours before imaging
Obtain bilateral compression ultrasound; if negative, no further workup needed

explanation.correct: Correct. High pre-test probability (Wells 6) → CT-PA is the diagnostic gold standard. Start anticoagulation empirically before imaging in high-probability patients per ACEP and ESC guidelines — imaging delay should not delay treatment.
explanation.a: Incorrect. D-dimer alone is never sufficient to diagnose PE — it is a screening tool with poor specificity. An elevated D-dimer in a high-probability patient confirms the need for imaging but does not establish diagnosis.
explanation.b: Correct. High pre-test probability (Wells 6) → CT-PA is the diagnostic gold standard. Start anticoagulation empirically before imaging in high-probability patients per ACEP and ESC guidelines — imaging delay should not delay treatment.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Repeating D-dimer is not indicated. In a high-probability patient, D-dimer is irrelevant — the test has already been established as non-diagnostic in this context. Imaging is mandatory.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Lower extremity ultrasound is useful to identify DVT as a source but a negative US does not rule out PE. Most PE patients do not have sonographically detectable DVT at the time of presentation. CT-PA is required for diagnosis.

pulmonary.question: A 71-year-old man with a history of CHF and COPD passes a 2-hour spontaneous breathing trial. He is extubated. Forty-five minutes after extubation, he develops stridor, increased work of breathing, and SpO₂ drops from 96% to 92%. RR 24, HR 118. Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

answers.text: Immediate reintubation for post-extubation respiratory failure
Apply high-flow nasal cannula at 60L/min, FiO₂ 1.0
Apply NIV and administer racemic epinephrine nebulization for stridor
Administer IV dexamethasone 10 mg and observe for 30 minutes

explanation.correct: Correct. Racemic epinephrine nebulization is the first-line treatment for post-extubation laryngeal edema — it reduces stridor within 10-20 minutes. NIV simultaneously supports oxygenation and ventilation. This dual approach maximizes chance of avoiding reintubation. Should this fail, be prepared for reintubation.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Immediate reintubation is premature — post-extubation stridor from laryngeal edema is often reversible with racemic epinephrine and NIV. Reintubation carries significant risk and should follow failure of medical management.
explanation.b: Incorrect. High-flow nasal cannula provides oxygenation support but does not deliver positive pressure to reduce work of breathing, does not treat laryngeal edema, and does not adequately support ventilation in a patient with CHF and COPD. NIV is superior for this combined pathophysiology.
explanation.c: Correct. Racemic epinephrine nebulization is the first-line treatment for post-extubation laryngeal edema — it reduces stridor within 10-20 minutes. NIV simultaneously supports oxygenation and ventilation. This dual approach maximizes chance of avoiding reintubation. Should this fail, be prepared for reintubation.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IV dexamethasone has a delayed onset of action (hours). While steroids are used prophylactically for high-risk patients before extubation, they do not provide rapid relief of acute post-extubation laryngeal edema. Observation alone during active stridor with SpO₂ 87% is unsafe.

pulmonary.question: A 66-year-old woman is on VC-AC ventilation for pneumonia. Vt 420 mL (6 mL/kg IBW), RR 18, PEEP 8, FiO₂ 0.55. You perform an end-inspiratory hold: plateau pressure 26 cmH₂O. You then perform an end-expiratory hold: total PEEP reads 14 cmH₂O. Set PEEP is 8 cmH₂O. What is the clinical significance and what is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Auto-PEEP of 6 cmH₂O is present; reduce RR and assess for causes of air trapping
The total PEEP of 14 cmH₂O is therapeutic; no changes needed
Increase set PEEP to 14 to match total PEEP and improve oxygenation
The plateau pressure of 26 cmH₂O is above target; reduce Vt to 4 mL/kg IBW

explanation.correct: Correct. Auto-PEEP = total PEEP - set PEEP = 14 - 8 = 6 cmH₂O. This indicates dynamic hyperinflation. The primary intervention is reducing RR to extend expiratory time. Investigate underlying cause (bronchospasm, high secretion load, inappropriate I:E ratio).
explanation.a: Correct. Auto-PEEP = total PEEP - set PEEP = 14 - 8 = 6 cmH₂O. This indicates dynamic hyperinflation. The primary intervention is reducing RR to extend expiratory time. Investigate underlying cause (bronchospasm, high secretion load, inappropriate I:E ratio).
explanation.b: Incorrect. Auto-PEEP is not therapeutic — it is unintended and represents incomplete exhalation causing air trapping, increased intrathoracic pressure, and risk of hemodynamic compromise and barotrauma. It requires correction.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Increasing set PEEP to match auto-PEEP does not eliminate air trapping — it adds additional pressure on top of already elevated intrinsic PEEP. This approach is only used cautiously in COPD to reduce inspiratory threshold load, not to 'normalize' the PEEP reading.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Plateau pressure of 26 cmH₂O is within the lung-protective target of ≤30 cmH₂O. Reducing Vt to 4 mL/kg is below the evidence-based range of 4-8 mL/kg and may increase dead space ventilation without additional benefit.

pulmonary.question: A 74-year-old man with diabetes and CKD stage 3 is admitted from a long-term care facility with fever, productive cough, and hypoxia. He was hospitalized and received IV antibiotics 6 weeks ago. CXR shows a right lower lobe consolidation. He is not intubated and his BP is stable. Which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?

answers.text: Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin
Piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin
Levofloxacin monotherapy
Amoxicillin-clavulanate orally plus doxycycline

explanation.correct: Correct. HCAP risk factors (LTCF + recent hospitalization + recent IV antibiotics) mandate empiric coverage for MDR organisms including Pseudomonas and MRSA. Pip-tazo + vancomycin is the standard empiric HCAP regimen.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin is standard CAP therapy. This patient has HCAP risk factors requiring broader gram-negative and MRSA coverage — standard CAP regimens are insufficient.
explanation.b: Correct. HCAP risk factors (LTCF + recent hospitalization + recent IV antibiotics) mandate empiric coverage for MDR organisms including Pseudomonas and MRSA. Pip-tazo + vancomycin is the standard empiric HCAP regimen.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Levofloxacin monotherapy covers typical CAP organisms and some gram-negatives but does not adequately cover Pseudomonas or MRSA. Monotherapy is insufficient for HCAP.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Oral therapy is inappropriate for a hospitalized patient with hypoxia and a consolidation. Amoxicillin-clavulanate does not cover Pseudomonas or MRSA.

pulmonary.question: A 62-year-old man with a 40 pack-year smoking history presents for evaluation of progressive dyspnea on exertion. He has no wheezing at rest. Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC 0.58 (post-bronchodilator), FEV1 54% predicted. He currently uses albuterol PRN only. Which is the most appropriate next step in management per GOLD guidelines?

answers.text: Add inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) as monotherapy
Add a long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) as maintenance therapy
Start oral prednisone 40 mg daily for 5 days
Referral for lung volume reduction surgery

explanation.correct: Correct. GOLD Group B (moderate symptoms, low exacerbation risk) with FEV1 54% predicted — first-line is long-acting bronchodilator monotherapy. LAMA is preferred given evidence for exacerbation reduction and symptom improvement. This is a step up from SABA PRN only.
explanation.a: Incorrect. ICS monotherapy is not recommended in COPD. ICS is added to LABA/LAMA in patients with frequent exacerbations or eosinophilic phenotype, not as initial monotherapy. ICS alone does not improve lung function in COPD.
explanation.b: Correct. GOLD Group B (moderate symptoms, low exacerbation risk) with FEV1 54% predicted — first-line is long-acting bronchodilator monotherapy. LAMA is preferred given evidence for exacerbation reduction and symptom improvement. This is a step up from SABA PRN only.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Oral corticosteroids are used for acute COPD exacerbations, not stable COPD management. Chronic oral steroids cause significant harm (myopathy, osteoporosis, infections) with no mortality benefit in stable COPD.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Lung volume reduction surgery is considered only in severe emphysema (FEV1 <45%, upper lobe predominant) after maximal medical therapy. This patient has moderate COPD that has not received appropriate medical management.

pulmonary.question: A 50-year-old man with diabetes and alcoholism is admitted with pneumonia. He was started on ceftriaxone 3 days ago but remains febrile. CXR shows a right-sided effusion that has increased since admission. Thoracentesis yields turbid fluid: pH 7.12, glucose 38 mg/dL, LDH 2800 U/L, WBC 28,000 with 95% neutrophils. Gram stain is negative. Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

answers.text: Continue current antibiotics and repeat thoracentesis in 48 hours
Place a chest tube for drainage and broaden antibiotic coverage
Add metronidazole to current regimen and monitor clinically
Proceed to VATS decortication given failure to improve on antibiotics

explanation.correct: Correct. Chest tube drainage is mandatory for complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema per BTS and ACCP guidelines. The pleural fluid criteria (pH 7.12, glucose 38, LDH 2800, turbid, neutrophilic) all meet drainage criteria. Antibiotic broadening addresses failure of ceftriaxone.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Complicated parapneumonic effusions (pH <7.20, glucose <40, LDH >1000, turbid fluid) require drainage — not watchful waiting. Delay increases risk of loculation, fibrothorax, and septic complications.
explanation.b: Correct. Chest tube drainage is mandatory for complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema per BTS and ACCP guidelines. The pleural fluid criteria (pH 7.12, glucose 38, LDH 2800, turbid, neutrophilic) all meet drainage criteria. Antibiotic broadening addresses failure of ceftriaxone.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Adding metronidazole alone without drainage is insufficient for a complicated effusion. Antibiotics do not penetrate a thick, loculated pleural space adequately to sterilize it. Drainage must accompany antibiotic therapy.
explanation.d: Incorrect. VATS decortication is reserved for organized empyema not amenable to chest tube drainage — typically after failed tube drainage with documented loculation or organized peel. Chest tube is the appropriate first step before surgical escalation.

pulmonary.question: A 38-year-old woman presents with progressive exertional dyspnea and two syncopal episodes over 6 months. Echo shows RVSP 72 mmHg, RV dilation, and no left heart abnormality. Right heart catheterization: mPAP 52 mmHg, PCWP 8 mmHg, PVR 9 Wood units, CO 3.8 L/min. Vasoreactivity testing with inhaled nitric oxide shows a reduction in mPAP to 38 mmHg and PVR to 4 Wood units. Which management is most appropriate?

answers.text: Initiate dual oral therapy with ambrisentan and tadalafil
Initiate high-dose calcium channel blocker therapy
List for lung transplantation given severity of disease
Start IV epoprostenol infusion given high PVR

explanation.correct: Correct. A positive vasoreactivity test (mPAP drop >10 mmHg to <40 mmHg with maintained or improved CO) identifies patients who may respond dramatically to high-dose CCB therapy. This is a rare but important distinction — CCB responders can achieve near-normal hemodynamics and survival.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Dual oral PAH-specific therapy (ambrisentan + tadalafil — AMBITION trial) is first-line for non-vasoreactive PAH. This patient has a strongly positive vasoreactivity test, making her a candidate for CCB therapy, which is simpler and potentially curative in responders.
explanation.b: Correct. A positive vasoreactivity test (mPAP drop >10 mmHg to <40 mmHg with maintained or improved CO) identifies patients who may respond dramatically to high-dose CCB therapy. This is a rare but important distinction — CCB responders can achieve near-normal hemodynamics and survival.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Transplant listing is premature — the patient has not received appropriate medical therapy. Furthermore, vasoreactive patients often have excellent long-term outcomes on CCB therapy alone. Transplant is reserved for WHO functional class IV patients failing maximal medical therapy.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IV epoprostenol is reserved for high-risk, WHO Class IV PAH patients or those failing oral therapy. A vasoreactive patient who has not yet tried CCB therapy should not be started on IV prostacyclin as first-line management.
