pulmonary.question: A 35-year-old man with no significant medical history is admitted with rapidly progressive dyspnea, diffuse bilateral infiltrates on CXR, and P/F ratio of 210. He reports starting a new medication 3 weeks ago for rheumatoid arthritis. No fever, WBC normal, cultures negative. CT chest shows bilateral ground-glass opacities with peribronchovascular distribution. Which diagnosis is most consistent and what is the most appropriate management?

answers.text: COVID-19 pneumonia; initiate remdesivir and dexamethasone
Drug-induced pneumonitis; discontinue the offending agent and initiate corticosteroids
Cardiogenic pulmonary edema; initiate IV diuresis and echo
PCP pneumonia; initiate trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and prednisone

explanation.correct: Correct. New bilateral infiltrates + P/F 210 + negative workup + new RA medication 3 weeks ago = drug-induced pneumonitis until proven otherwise. Discontinuing the causative agent is the most critical step; corticosteroids are added for significant hypoxemia.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Without COVID-19 testing confirmation and with a clear temporal relationship to a new RA medication, COVID-19 pneumonia should not be assumed. Drug-induced pneumonitis is a more parsimonious diagnosis.
explanation.b: Correct. New bilateral infiltrates + P/F 210 + negative workup + new RA medication 3 weeks ago = drug-induced pneumonitis until proven otherwise. Discontinuing the causative agent is the most critical step; corticosteroids are added for significant hypoxemia.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Cardiogenic pulmonary edema would present with elevated BNP, cardiomegaly, pleural effusions, and a perihilar bat-wing pattern in a patient with cardiac risk factors. This young, previously healthy patient with peribronchovascular pattern and new medication does not fit this profile.
explanation.d: Incorrect. PCP pneumonia typically occurs in immunocompromised patients (HIV with low CD4, chronic immunosuppression). While RA patients on biologics have some immunosuppression risk, normal WBC and the clear temporal relationship to a new medication makes drug-induced pneumonitis far more likely. PCP would also typically show more diffuse ground-glass without peribronchovascular predominance.

pulmonary.question: A 58-year-old man is in the ICU on mechanical ventilation for ARDS from pneumonia. He has been intubated for 8 days. He develops a new fever, rising WBC, and increasing FiO₂ requirements. BAL is performed. Which BAL result is most consistent with a diagnosis of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP)?

answers.text: BAL cell count 2×10⁵ cells/mL with 40% neutrophils, culture with mixed flora at 10³ CFU/mL
BAL culture with Pseudomonas aeruginosa at 10⁵ CFU/mL with 70% neutrophils on cell differential
BAL culture negative, cell count 1×10⁵ with 30% neutrophils
BAL culture with Candida albicans at 10⁶ CFU/mL

explanation.correct: Correct. Pseudomonas aeruginosa at 10⁵ CFU/mL exceeds the BAL diagnostic threshold of ≥10⁴ CFU/mL. Combined with clinical signs (fever, rising WBC, worsening oxygenation, prolonged intubation), this meets IDSA/ATS criteria for VAP. Pseudomonas is a common VAP pathogen after >5 days of ventilation.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Mixed flora at 10³ CFU/mL is below the diagnostic threshold and most likely represents upper airway contamination during sampling. Mixed flora rarely represents true VAP — it suggests colonization or sampling artifact.
explanation.b: Correct. Pseudomonas aeruginosa at 10⁵ CFU/mL exceeds the BAL diagnostic threshold of ≥10⁴ CFU/mL. Combined with clinical signs (fever, rising WBC, worsening oxygenation, prolonged intubation), this meets IDSA/ATS criteria for VAP. Pseudomonas is a common VAP pathogen after >5 days of ventilation.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Negative culture with low neutrophil count does not support VAP. This pattern is more consistent with non-infectious cause of deterioration (worsening underlying ARDS, atelectasis, fluid overload).
explanation.d: Incorrect. Candida in BAL virtually always represents colonization, not true infection. Candida pneumonia is exceedingly rare and not a recognized cause of VAP in immunocompetent patients. Candida in BAL should not trigger antifungal treatment per IDSA guidelines.

pulmonary.question: A 55-year-old woman with well-controlled asthma on fluticasone/salmeterol presents for an annual visit. She reports using her albuterol rescue inhaler 4 times per week and waking with symptoms twice monthly. She has had no exacerbations requiring oral steroids in the past year. Spirometry is normal. Which best describes her asthma control and appropriate next step?

answers.text: Well-controlled; continue current regimen
Partly controlled; step up therapy by adding a LAMA or increasing ICS dose
Uncontrolled; initiate oral prednisone burst and biologic referral
Well-controlled; step down by trialing ICS monotherapy

explanation.correct: Correct. Partly controlled asthma on ICS/LABA warrants a step up. Options include increasing ICS dose, adding LAMA (tiotropium shown to reduce exacerbations as add-on in GINA Step 4-5), or considering biologic if eosinophilic phenotype. Symptom-driven step-up is appropriate.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Albuterol use 4 times per week exceeds the GINA threshold of >2 days/week for partly controlled asthma. Nocturnal symptoms also confirm inadequate control. Continuing the current regimen is inappropriate.
explanation.b: Correct. Partly controlled asthma on ICS/LABA warrants a step up. Options include increasing ICS dose, adding LAMA (tiotropium shown to reduce exacerbations as add-on in GINA Step 4-5), or considering biologic if eosinophilic phenotype. Symptom-driven step-up is appropriate.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Uncontrolled asthma per GINA requires 3 or more of the partly-controlled criteria AND/OR frequent exacerbations. This patient meets only 2 criteria with no recent exacerbations. An oral steroid burst is not indicated for stable partly controlled asthma.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Stepping down therapy when the patient is symptomatic and using rescue medication frequently would worsen control. Step-down is only considered when asthma has been well controlled for at least 3 months.

pulmonary.question: A 66-year-old man with a 10-year history of worsening dyspnea on exertion is admitted for evaluation. He worked in shipbuilding for 25 years. PFTs show: FVC 58% predicted, FEV1 62% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.82, DLCO 44% predicted, TLC 62% predicted. CT chest shows bilateral basal-predominant subpleural reticular opacities with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis. Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?

answers.text: COPD with air trapping and emphysema
Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF)
Asbestosis
Hypersensitivity pneumonitis

explanation.correct: Correct. Restrictive PFTs + reduced DLCO + UIP pattern on CT + significant asbestos exposure (shipbuilding) = asbestosis. Latency is typically 20-40 years after exposure, consistent with this timeline.
explanation.a: Incorrect. COPD shows an obstructive pattern (reduced FEV1/FVC <0.70) with air trapping (increased RV). This patient has a normal FEV1/FVC of 0.82 and reduced TLC — a restrictive, not obstructive pattern.
explanation.b: Incorrect. IPF and asbestosis are radiographically and histologically identical (both show UIP pattern). The only distinguishing factor is occupational history. Without asbestos exposure, this would be IPF. With 25 years of shipbuilding exposure, asbestosis is the correct diagnosis. The distinction matters for medicolegal and compensation purposes.
explanation.c: Correct. Restrictive PFTs + reduced DLCO + UIP pattern on CT + significant asbestos exposure (shipbuilding) = asbestosis. Latency is typically 20-40 years after exposure, consistent with this timeline.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) shows upper or mid-lung predominant ground-glass opacities and mosaic attenuation on CT, with centrilobular nodules. Chronic HP can cause fibrosis but typically has a different CT distribution and is associated with organic antigen exposure (birds, hot tubs, farming), not asbestos.

pulmonary.question: A 52-year-old obese man (BMI 38) with hypertension is referred by his primary care provider after his wife noticed he stops breathing during sleep. He reports excessive daytime sleepiness, morning headaches, and nocturia. Epworth Sleepiness Scale score is 16. ABG awake: pH 7.38, PaCO₂ 51, PaO₂ 68, HCO₃ 29. Which is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

answers.text: Overnight pulse oximetry at home
Full in-laboratory polysomnography (PSG)
Home sleep apnea test (HSAT)
Full in-laboratory polysomnography with CO₂ monitoring given daytime hypercapnia

explanation.correct: Correct. Daytime hypercapnia + obesity + OSA symptoms = obesity hypoventilation syndrome until proven otherwise. In-lab PSG with CO₂ monitoring (transcutaneous or end-tidal) characterizes the hypoventilation pattern, determines if CPAP is sufficient or if BiPAP/AVAPS is needed, and distinguishes OHS from central hypoventilation syndromes.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Overnight pulse oximetry alone provides no data on apnea-hypopnea index, arousal index, sleep staging, or CO₂ levels. It is insufficient for diagnosis of OSA and completely inadequate for evaluating OHS.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Standard PSG without CO₂ monitoring would evaluate for OSA but miss the hypoventilation pattern critical in OHS. The daytime hypercapnia mandates CO₂ monitoring during the study to characterize nocturnal hypoventilation.
explanation.c: Incorrect. HSAT is appropriate for high-pretest-probability uncomplicated OSA without comorbid hypoventilation. This patient's daytime hypercapnia makes OHS the primary concern — HSAT cannot measure CO₂ or adequately evaluate hypoventilation.
explanation.d: Correct. Daytime hypercapnia + obesity + OSA symptoms = obesity hypoventilation syndrome until proven otherwise. In-lab PSG with CO₂ monitoring (transcutaneous or end-tidal) characterizes the hypoventilation pattern, determines if CPAP is sufficient or if BiPAP/AVAPS is needed, and distinguishes OHS from central hypoventilation syndromes.

pulmonary.question: A 47-year-old previously healthy man presents with 10 days of fever, nonproductive cough, headache, and myalgias. He works at a university and several colleagues have similar illness. CXR shows patchy, bilateral interstitial infiltrates disproportionate to his mild-appearing symptoms. WBC is 9.2, procalcitonin 0.18 ng/mL. SpO₂ is 94% on room air. Which organism is most likely responsible and what is the treatment of choice?

answers.text: Streptococcus pneumoniae; amoxicillin 500 mg TID for 5 days
Mycoplasma pneumoniae; azithromycin 500 mg on day 1 then 250 mg daily for 4 days
Legionella pneumophila; levofloxacin 750 mg daily for 5 days
Influenza A; oseltamivir 75 mg BID for 5 days

explanation.correct: Correct. Mycoplasma pneumoniae is the classic cause of atypical/walking pneumonia: subacute onset, extrapulmonary features, bilateral interstitial infiltrates worse than symptoms suggest, low procalcitonin, community cluster. Azithromycin (macrolide) is first-line treatment.
explanation.a: Incorrect. S. pneumoniae causes typical bacterial pneumonia with acute onset, productive cough, lobar consolidation, elevated WBC, and elevated procalcitonin. The clinical-radiographic dissociation and cluster presentation strongly argue against typical bacterial pneumonia. Beta-lactams are ineffective against Mycoplasma.
explanation.b: Correct. Mycoplasma pneumoniae is the classic cause of atypical/walking pneumonia: subacute onset, extrapulmonary features, bilateral interstitial infiltrates worse than symptoms suggest, low procalcitonin, community cluster. Azithromycin (macrolide) is first-line treatment.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Legionella also causes atypical pneumonia but typically presents with higher fever, GI symptoms (diarrhea), neurological changes, hyponatremia, elevated LFTs, and is more severe. Community clustering in a university setting among young adults is more characteristic of Mycoplasma.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Influenza presents with acute onset, prominent systemic symptoms early, and typically resolves within 7-10 days. This patient has had symptoms for 10 days with ongoing CXR changes — influenza pneumonia would be unusual to present this way. Influenza antigen testing would be needed to confirm.

pulmonary.question: A 52-year-old woman with bacterial pneumonia meets Berlin criteria for severe ARDS (P/F ratio 68 on PEEP 10). She is ventilated with Vt 6 mL/kg IBW. Plateau pressure is 34 cmH₂O, driving pressure is 22 cmH₂O, FiO₂ 0.85. She remains severely hypoxic (SpO₂ 84%). Which intervention has the strongest level-1 evidence for mortality benefit in severe ARDS?

answers.text: Inhaled nitric oxide 20 ppm to improve ventilation-perfusion matching
Prone positioning for at least 16 hours per day
High-frequency oscillatory ventilation (HFOV) to reduce ventilator-induced lung injury
IV methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day to reduce pulmonary inflammation

explanation.correct: Correct. PROSEVA (Guérin 2013): prone positioning ≥16 hrs/day in early severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced mortality by 50%. This is the strongest evidence-based intervention available. Current SCCM/ATS guidelines strongly recommend prone positioning for severe ARDS.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Inhaled nitric oxide improves oxygenation transiently but has NOT been shown to reduce mortality in ARDS (multiple trials including NINOS, Michael). It is not a first-line intervention and is reserved for refractory hypoxia as a bridge.
explanation.b: Correct. PROSEVA (Guérin 2013): prone positioning ≥16 hrs/day in early severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced mortality by 50%. This is the strongest evidence-based intervention available. Current SCCM/ATS guidelines strongly recommend prone positioning for severe ARDS.
explanation.c: Incorrect. HFOV was studied in OSCAR and OSCILLATE trials — OSCILLATE was stopped early due to INCREASED mortality in the HFOV group. HFOV is no longer recommended for ARDS.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Corticosteroid use in ARDS remains controversial. The ACURASYS trial showed benefit in early ARDS but the ROSE trial did not. Early routine steroids are not standard of care. Steroids are considered for unresolving ARDS after 7-10 days.

pulmonary.question: A ventilated ARDS patient has the following settings: PC-AC mode, rate 22, PEEP 14, FiO₂ 0.70. Peak airway pressure is 42 cmH₂O. You add a 0.5-second inspiratory pause and measure a plateau pressure of 28 cmH₂O. What do these numbers tell you and what should you adjust?

answers.text: Elevated plateau pressure indicating poor lung compliance; reduce Vt
Normal plateau with elevated peak-plateau gradient indicating elevated airway resistance; consider bronchodilators or check for tube obstruction
Both pressures are acceptable; no changes needed
Auto-PEEP is present; increase respiratory rate to 28 to improve CO₂ clearance

explanation.correct: Correct. Peak-plateau gradient > 10 cmH₂O indicates elevated airway resistance. Plateau 28 cmH₂O is within acceptable range for lung protection. The problem is in the airways (bronchospasm, secretions, tube obstruction), not alveolar compliance. Bronchodilators and suction are appropriate first steps.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Elevated plateau pressure (>28-30 cmH₂O) would indicate reduced lung compliance. Here the PLATEAU is acceptable at 28 — it is the PEAK that is elevated, indicating resistance, not compliance, as the problem.
explanation.b: Correct. Peak-plateau gradient > 10 cmH₂O indicates elevated airway resistance. Plateau 28 cmH₂O is within acceptable range for lung protection. The problem is in the airways (bronchospasm, secretions, tube obstruction), not alveolar compliance. Bronchodilators and suction are appropriate first steps.
explanation.c: Incorrect. A peak pressure of 42 cmH₂O should not be dismissed. While the plateau is acceptable, elevated peak pressures from airway resistance should be investigated and treated to avoid barotrauma and work of breathing issues.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Auto-PEEP is detected by an expiratory hold (not inspiratory hold) — it causes incomplete exhalation and stacking. Auto-PEEP is managed by reducing rate, extending expiratory time, or reducing tidal volume — never increasing rate.

pulmonary.question: A patient has been ventilated for 8 days for ARDS. Today his P/F ratio is 290, FiO₂ 0.40, PEEP 8. You perform an SBT using a T-piece for 30 minutes. After 25 minutes, RR increases from 18 to 32, SpO₂ decreases from 97% to 91%, and the patient becomes diaphoretic and agitated. Which is the most appropriate action?

answers.text: Extend the SBT to 60 minutes — 25 minutes is insufficient to assess readiness
Terminate the SBT immediately and return to full ventilatory support
Add 5 cmH₂O of PSV to support the SBT and reassess in 15 minutes
Extubate now — the patient tolerated 25 of 30 minutes successfully

explanation.correct: Correct. Failure criteria met: rising RR (32), desaturation (SpO₂ 91%), diaphoresis, agitation. Terminate immediately and return to full support. Investigate reason for failure (secretions, retained secretions, diaphragm fatigue, residual sedation). Retry SBT in 24 hours after addressing cause.
explanation.a: Incorrect. SBT duration is typically 30-120 minutes, but the purpose is not to complete a set time — it is to assess failure. Once failure criteria are met, continuing the SBT risks respiratory failure and fatigue.
explanation.b: Correct. Failure criteria met: rising RR (32), desaturation (SpO₂ 91%), diaphoresis, agitation. Terminate immediately and return to full support. Investigate reason for failure (secretions, retained secretions, diaphragm fatigue, residual sedation). Retry SBT in 24 hours after addressing cause.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Adding PSV during a T-piece SBT converts it to a different SBT modality and extends the duration with ventilator support. This does not address the current failure and may mask the extent of respiratory compromise.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Extubating a patient who is failing an SBT at 25 minutes is dangerous. The failure criteria (RR trending up, desaturating, diaphoretic, agitated) predict high likelihood of post-extubation respiratory failure.

pulmonary.question: A 58-year-old man presents with massive pulmonary embolism: BP 72/44, HR 132, SpO₂ 82% on 15L NRB. Echo shows severely dilated RV with McConnell's sign, D-shaped LV, and estimated RVSP 68 mmHg. He has no contraindications to thrombolysis. Which is the most appropriate treatment?

answers.text: Systemic anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin and hemodynamic support
IV alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours
Catheter-directed thrombolysis via pulmonary arterial catheter
Surgical embolectomy with cardiopulmonary bypass

explanation.correct: Correct. Systemic alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours is first-line for massive PE with hemodynamic collapse. Rapidly reduces pulmonary vascular resistance and RV afterload. Start UFH concurrently. Contraindications: recent surgery/stroke, active bleeding, known intracranial malignancy.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Anticoagulation alone is adequate for submassive PE (RV dysfunction without hemodynamic compromise) and is the standard for non-massive PE. In MASSIVE PE with shock, anticoagulation alone is insufficient — thrombolysis is required.
explanation.b: Correct. Systemic alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours is first-line for massive PE with hemodynamic collapse. Rapidly reduces pulmonary vascular resistance and RV afterload. Start UFH concurrently. Contraindications: recent surgery/stroke, active bleeding, known intracranial malignancy.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Catheter-directed thrombolysis delivers lower doses of thrombolytic directly to the clot. It is used for SUBMASSIVE PE or when systemic thrombolysis is relatively contraindicated. For massive PE requiring urgent treatment, full-dose systemic thrombolysis is faster and more effective.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Surgical embolectomy is reserved for massive PE when thrombolysis fails or is absolutely contraindicated. It requires OR availability and is not first-line when thrombolysis can be administered immediately.

pulmonary.question: A 67-year-old COPD patient is intubated for acute exacerbation. ABG: pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 78, PaO₂ 78, HCO₃ 35. Vent settings: AC-VC, RR 18, Vt 450 (6 mL/kg IBW), FiO₂ 0.40, PEEP 5. After 30 minutes, his peak pressures are 52 cmH₂O, plateau is 32 cmH₂O. He is hemodynamically unstable. Auto-PEEP measured at 14 cmH₂O. Which ventilator adjustment is most appropriate?

answers.text: Increase RR to 24 to correct hypercapnia
Reduce RR to 12 and increase expiratory time to allow complete exhalation
Increase PEEP to 10 to overcome auto-PEEP and improve triggering
Switch to CPAP mode to allow spontaneous breathing

explanation.correct: Correct. Reducing RR from 18 to 12 increases expiratory time per cycle, allowing more complete exhalation and reducing auto-PEEP. Combined with extending I:E ratio (e.g., 1:3-1:4), this is the primary intervention for auto-PEEP in COPD. Permissive hypercapnia (PaCO₂ up to 80-90) is acceptable with pH >7.20.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Increasing RR with obstructive physiology severely worsens auto-PEEP by reducing expiratory time further. Each breath stacks on the previous incompletely exhaled breath, worsening dynamic hyperinflation and potentially causing tamponade physiology.
explanation.b: Correct. Reducing RR from 18 to 12 increases expiratory time per cycle, allowing more complete exhalation and reducing auto-PEEP. Combined with extending I:E ratio (e.g., 1:3-1:4), this is the primary intervention for auto-PEEP in COPD. Permissive hypercapnia (PaCO₂ up to 80-90) is acceptable with pH >7.20.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Setting extrinsic PEEP at 80% of measured auto-PEEP can improve triggering by reducing the work to initiate a breath. However, it is NOT the primary treatment — it does not reduce auto-PEEP and can worsen hyperinflation if set incorrectly.
explanation.d: Incorrect. A newly intubated hemodynamically unstable patient with auto-PEEP 14 and significant acidosis is not a candidate for immediate transition to spontaneous breathing. Full ventilatory support with optimized settings is required.

pulmonary.question: A 44-year-old man with hereditary angioedema presents with rapidly progressive oropharyngeal swelling, drooling, and stridor. His voice is muffled. SpO₂ is 94% on 4L NC. Which is the most appropriate immediate airway management plan?

answers.text: IV diphenhydramine 50 mg and methylprednisolone 125 mg; reassess in 30 minutes
Awake video laryngoscopy or fiberoptic intubation now; prepare surgical airway as backup
IV epinephrine 0.3 mg IM then attempt RSI with rocuronium
BiPAP to support ventilation and reduce work of breathing while swelling improves

explanation.correct: Correct. Progressive HAE with stridor and SpO₂ decline requires immediate definitive airway management. Awake intubation (fiberoptic or video laryngoscopy with topicalization) maintains spontaneous respiration if the attempt fails. Cricothyrotomy kit must be immediately available. RSI is dangerous in distorted angioedema anatomy.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Antihistamines and steroids are not effective for HAE (bradykinin-mediated, not histamine-mediated). Reassessing in 30 minutes risks complete airway obstruction. HAE-specific treatment (C1-esterase inhibitor, icatibant, ecallantide) can be given but does NOT replace securing the airway immediately.
explanation.b: Correct. Progressive HAE with stridor and SpO₂ decline requires immediate definitive airway management. Awake intubation (fiberoptic or video laryngoscopy with topicalization) maintains spontaneous respiration if the attempt fails. Cricothyrotomy kit must be immediately available. RSI is dangerous in distorted angioedema anatomy.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Epinephrine has limited efficacy in HAE and provides only transient benefit. RSI with rocuronium in a patient with potentially cannot-intubate-cannot-oxygenate (CICO) scenario eliminates spontaneous respiration, risking a fatal outcome if intubation fails.
explanation.d: Incorrect. BiPAP provides positive pressure support but does not secure the airway. As swelling progresses, even BiPAP mask ventilation will become impossible. Deferring intubation until the patient cannot breathe or tolerate BiPAP risks complete obstruction in an increasingly difficult airway.

pulmonary.question: A 38-year-old woman with known Group 1 pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) on treprostinil infusion is admitted to the ICU in RV failure. Her nurse notifies you that the continuous treprostinil infusion has been interrupted for 2 hours due to pump malfunction. The patient is now hypotensive and in acute decompensation. What is the most urgent concern and action?

answers.text: Start a new treprostinil infusion at a lower rate to prevent rebound vasoconstriction
Abrupt prostacyclin discontinuation is life-threatening; restore infusion immediately at the previous rate while addressing RV failure
Switch to oral sildenafil as bridge therapy while pump is repaired
Start inhaled nitric oxide and systemic norepinephrine for hemodynamic support

explanation.correct: Correct. Prostacyclin analog interruption is a true ICU emergency in PAH patients. Restore treprostinil at the previous rate immediately using a backup pump/syringe. Concurrently manage RV failure with volume optimization, vasopressors (vasopressin preferred to avoid tachycardia), and oxygen. Notify the PAH specialist.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Restarting at a LOWER rate risks inadequate vasorelaxation and ongoing RV failure. The infusion must be restored at the PREVIOUS RATE. Dose reduction is only considered when titrating under stable conditions, not in emergent restoration.
explanation.b: Correct. Prostacyclin analog interruption is a true ICU emergency in PAH patients. Restore treprostinil at the previous rate immediately using a backup pump/syringe. Concurrently manage RV failure with volume optimization, vasopressors (vasopressin preferred to avoid tachycardia), and oxygen. Notify the PAH specialist.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Oral sildenafil does not provide adequate and immediate vasorelaxation to replace an interrupted prostacyclin infusion acutely. It has a different mechanism (PDE-5 inhibition) and slower onset. It may be used as long-term adjunct therapy but not as emergent bridge.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Inhaled nitric oxide and norepinephrine are appropriate adjuncts for RV failure but do not address the primary problem — absence of prostacyclin vasodilation. Restoring the infusion is the most urgent priority.

pulmonary.question: A mechanically ventilated patient develops a new fever, increased secretions, WBC 18,200, and new right lower lobe infiltrate on CXR after 6 days of mechanical ventilation. BAL is sent for culture. Which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate for suspected VAP in a non-immunocompromised patient with risk factors for MRSA and Pseudomonas?

answers.text: Azithromycin plus ceftriaxone — community-acquired pneumonia coverage
Vancomycin plus an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime)
Meropenem alone for broad-spectrum coverage
Clindamycin and aztreonam for anaerobic and gram-negative coverage

explanation.correct: Correct. IDSA/ATS VAP guidelines: vancomycin (MRSA coverage) plus antipseudomonal beta-lactam (Pseudomonas and gram-negative coverage). This two-drug regimen targets the highest-risk organisms in VAP. Culture-directed de-escalation within 48-72 hours is essential for stewardship.
explanation.a: Incorrect. CAP empiric therapy (azithromycin + ceftriaxone) does not cover MRSA or Pseudomonas — the key organisms in VAP, especially after 4+ days of ventilation and prior antibiotics. VAP organisms are vastly different from CAP.
explanation.b: Correct. IDSA/ATS VAP guidelines: vancomycin (MRSA coverage) plus antipseudomonal beta-lactam (Pseudomonas and gram-negative coverage). This two-drug regimen targets the highest-risk organisms in VAP. Culture-directed de-escalation within 48-72 hours is essential for stewardship.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Meropenem alone does not cover MRSA. While carbapenems are antipseudomonal, MRSA requires vancomycin or linezolid. Monotherapy with carbapenem also risks selecting carbapenem-resistant organisms unnecessarily.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Clindamycin + aztreonam misses MRSA (clindamycin has no MRSA activity in most strains) and doesn't provide optimal Pseudomonas coverage. This combination is inadequate for VAP.

pulmonary.question: A 29-year-old woman with severe asthma is intubated for status asthmaticus. Despite bronchodilators, she remains ventilated with Vt 6 mL/kg, RR 16, PEEP 0, FiO₂ 0.60. Her PaCO₂ is 72, pH 7.18. Peak pressure is 62 cmH₂O, plateau is 44 cmH₂O. She remains in status despite all bronchodilators. What is the appropriate next pharmacologic intervention?

answers.text: IV magnesium sulfate 2g over 20 minutes
IV ketamine 1-2 mg/kg loading dose then infusion
IV theophylline aminophylline infusion
IV epinephrine 0.5 mg IM

explanation.correct: Correct. IV ketamine is the rescue agent of choice in refractory mechanically ventilated status asthmaticus. Its bronchodilatory mechanism is distinct from beta-agonists and anticholinergics, providing additive benefit. It also provides excellent sedation-analgesia for the ventilated asthmatic.
explanation.a: Incorrect. IV magnesium 2g is an appropriate adjunct in non-intubated severe asthma (MAGICS trial, reduces hospital admissions). In an already intubated, maximally treated patient, magnesium would already be given as standard care. It is not the 'next' rescue agent in intubated refractory status.
explanation.b: Correct. IV ketamine is the rescue agent of choice in refractory mechanically ventilated status asthmaticus. Its bronchodilatory mechanism is distinct from beta-agonists and anticholinergics, providing additive benefit. It also provides excellent sedation-analgesia for the ventilated asthmatic.
explanation.c: Incorrect. IV aminophylline has fallen out of favor due to narrow therapeutic window, significant adverse effects (tachycardia, arrhythmias, seizures), and modest bronchodilatory benefit. Multiple trials have not shown benefit when added to beta-agonists and anticholinergics in severe asthma.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IM epinephrine is used for anaphylaxis and severe bronchospasm in non-intubated patients as a temporizing measure. In an intubated patient receiving maximal bronchodilator therapy, IM epinephrine is not the appropriate next step and has inferior delivery compared to nebulized or IV routes.

pulmonary.question: A 55-year-old man presents with a new right pleural effusion. Thoracentesis fluid shows: protein 4.8 g/dL (serum protein 7.1 g/dL), LDH 380 U/L (serum LDH 240 U/L). Fluid protein/serum protein ratio = 0.68. Fluid LDH/serum LDH ratio = 1.58. Pleural fluid pH is 7.08, glucose is 38 mg/dL (serum glucose 112 mg/dL). Which is the most likely diagnosis and management?

answers.text: Transudative effusion from heart failure; IV furosemide
Complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema; chest tube drainage required
Malignant effusion; pleurodesis and comfort measures
Exudative effusion — no intervention, observe for resolution

explanation.correct: Correct. Light's criteria confirm exudate. Pleural fluid pH 7.08 and glucose 38 meet criteria for complicated parapneumonic effusion requiring chest tube drainage. pH <7.20 in parapneumonic effusion indicates septation and bacterial activity that will not resolve with antibiotics alone. Drain immediately with chest tube.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Transudates have protein ratio <0.5 and LDH ratio <0.6 — this is clearly exudative. Heart failure causes transudates (bilateral effusions from elevated hydrostatic pressure). This would be inappropriate treatment.
explanation.b: Correct. Light's criteria confirm exudate. Pleural fluid pH 7.08 and glucose 38 meet criteria for complicated parapneumonic effusion requiring chest tube drainage. pH <7.20 in parapneumonic effusion indicates septation and bacterial activity that will not resolve with antibiotics alone. Drain immediately with chest tube.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Malignant effusions have variable pH/glucose but typically have positive cytology and clinical context suggesting malignancy. While the pH could suggest malignancy, the clinical scenario (parapneumonic) and very low glucose (<40) more strongly suggest complicated parapneumonic/empyema requiring drainage rather than palliation.
explanation.d: Incorrect. A complicated parapneumonic effusion with pH 7.08 and glucose 38 will NOT resolve without drainage. Observation without drainage risks progression to organized empyema requiring surgical decortication.

pulmonary.question: A 72-year-old with COPD (FEV₁/FVC 0.48, FEV₁ 38% predicted) is admitted with acute exacerbation. ABG on 2L NC: pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 68, PaO₂ 54, SaO₂ 87%. She is alert but using accessory muscles. ARDS is not present. Which is the most appropriate initial ventilatory management?

answers.text: Intubation and mechanical ventilation — PaCO₂ 68 indicates respiratory failure requiring full ventilatory support
Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) — BiPAP — targeting pH normalization and dyspnea relief
High-flow nasal cannula at 40 L/min, FiO₂ 0.40
Controlled low-flow oxygen at 1-2 LPM to prevent hypoxic respiratory drive suppression

explanation.correct: Correct. COPD exacerbation with hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 68) and clinical signs of distress (accessory muscles, tachypnea) is the primary indication for NIV. Alert, cooperative patient can tolerate a mask interface. NIV is the standard of care with level-1 evidence for reduced intubation and mortality.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Intubation is not yet indicated — the patient is alert, protecting airway, and NIV has not been attempted. Intubation carries significant risks in COPD (VAP, difficult weaning, post-extubation failure). NIV is the bridge that may avoid intubation entirely.
explanation.b: Correct. COPD exacerbation with hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 68) and clinical signs of distress (accessory muscles, tachypnea) is the primary indication for NIV. Alert, cooperative patient can tolerate a mask interface. NIV is the standard of care with level-1 evidence for reduced intubation and mortality.
explanation.c: Incorrect. High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) primarily addresses hypoxemia and provides modest positive pressure support. It is less effective than BiPAP for hypercapnia reduction and has weaker evidence in acute COPD exacerbation. For hypercapnic respiratory failure, BiPAP is preferred.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Withholding oxygen from hypoxic COPD patients to 'preserve hypoxic drive' is outdated and dangerous. Modern understanding: titrate O₂ to SpO₂ 88-92% (avoid supranormal saturations due to Haldane effect), but do not withhold oxygen from a patient with SpO₂ 87%.

pulmonary.question: An ARDS patient (moderate, P/F 140) has been ventilated at 6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP 12, plateau pressure 26 cmH₂O, driving pressure 14 cmH₂O. Despite optimization, SpO₂ remains 87% on FiO₂ 0.80. You add inhaled nitric oxide 20 ppm. SpO₂ improves to 93%. What is the correct characterization of this intervention?

answers.text: First-line rescue therapy that improves mortality in moderate-severe ARDS
Rescue oxygenation strategy that improves gas exchange but has not been shown to reduce mortality
Contraindicated in ARDS due to increased risk of pulmonary hypertension rebound
Should be used before prone positioning in the ARDS management algorithm

explanation.correct: Correct. iNO improves P/F ratio and oxygenation through selective pulmonary vasodilation in ventilated alveoli. However, this improvement in gas exchange has not translated into mortality benefit in clinical trials. iNO is used as rescue oxygenation when other methods fail, particularly as a bridge to prone positioning or ECMO evaluation.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Inhaled NO has NOT been shown to reduce mortality in any ARDS trial despite improving oxygenation. It is not first-line therapy. Prone positioning (PROSEVA) is the only intervention with demonstrated mortality benefit in severe ARDS.
explanation.b: Correct. iNO improves P/F ratio and oxygenation through selective pulmonary vasodilation in ventilated alveoli. However, this improvement in gas exchange has not translated into mortality benefit in clinical trials. iNO is used as rescue oxygenation when other methods fail, particularly as a bridge to prone positioning or ECMO evaluation.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Rebound pulmonary hypertension occurs when iNO is ABRUPTLY DISCONTINUED — requiring gradual weaning. iNO is not contraindicated in ARDS; pulmonary hypertension may actually be a specific indication for iNO in ARDS.
explanation.d: Incorrect. The ARDS management hierarchy per evidence: lung-protective ventilation first, then prone positioning (PROSEVA level-1 evidence), then recruitment maneuvers, then rescue therapies like iNO and ECMO. iNO should come AFTER prone positioning has been attempted.

pulmonary.question: A 61-year-old man is intubated in AC-VC mode: Vt 500 mL (7.5 mL/kg IBW 67 kg), RR 16, PEEP 8, FiO₂ 0.55. He is triggering the ventilator but you notice he is generating additional spontaneous breaths between ventilator breaths, with the ventilator delivering breaths that appear unsynchronized. His ABG shows pH 7.48, PaCO₂ 32, PaO₂ 88. His respiratory muscle work appears significant. Which ventilator adjustment best addresses the problem?

answers.text: Increase sedation to eliminate spontaneous breathing and prevent dyssynchrony
Reduce tidal volume to 6 mL/kg IBW and reduce trigger sensitivity to improve synchrony
Reduce set RR to 10-12 — the patient is over-breathing the ventilator due to alkalosis-driven hyperventilation; address the primary alkalosis
Reduce set rate and consider switching to SIMV+PSV or PS mode to allow patient-controlled breathing with support

explanation.correct: Correct. The patient is actively breathing faster than the set rate, causing double-triggering, alkalosis, and respiratory muscle overwork. Reducing set rate allows the patient's drive to determine frequency. SIMV+PSV or PSV mode allows supported spontaneous breathing at the patient's chosen rate, reducing dyssynchrony and work of breathing.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Increasing sedation to suppress spontaneous breathing is not the preferred approach — PADIS guidelines recommend minimizing sedation. Spontaneous breathing is beneficial in ARDS and other conditions. Matching the mode to the patient's needs is preferable to over-sedating.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Reducing Vt to 6 mL/kg is appropriate for ARDS lung protection. However, improving trigger sensitivity does not address the fundamental problem — the patient is over-breathing the set rate. Adjusting trigger sensitivity affects how easily the patient triggers but doesn't fix the rate mismatch.
explanation.c: Incorrect. The alkalosis is a CONSEQUENCE of over-breathing in AC mode, not the primary driver. Reducing the set rate is part of the solution but addressing the mode and allowing patient-controlled breathing with appropriate support is the comprehensive answer.
explanation.d: Correct. The patient is actively breathing faster than the set rate, causing double-triggering, alkalosis, and respiratory muscle overwork. Reducing set rate allows the patient's drive to determine frequency. SIMV+PSV or PSV mode allows supported spontaneous breathing at the patient's chosen rate, reducing dyssynchrony and work of breathing.

pulmonary.question: A 64-year-old woman with COPD is admitted for exacerbation. She is on NIV with improving pH (7.36). She requires frequent suctioning for thick secretions. Chest physiotherapy is ordered. Her GOLD classification would be GOLD 3 based on FEV₁ 38% predicted. Which maintenance inhaler class has the strongest evidence for reducing COPD exacerbation frequency?

answers.text: Short-acting beta-agonists (SABAs) used regularly four times daily
Long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMAs) such as tiotropium
Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) monotherapy
Methylxanthines (theophylline) as bronchodilator maintenance

explanation.correct: Correct. LAMA monotherapy (tiotropium, UPLIFT trial) reduces COPD exacerbation rate and hospitalization. GOLD 2024 guidelines recommend LAMA or LABA as initial maintenance therapy, with LAMA preferred for exacerbation prevention. For GOLD 3 (severe) with exacerbations, LAMA+LABA+ICS triple therapy should be considered.
explanation.a: Incorrect. SABAs (albuterol) are rescue inhalers for acute bronchospasm. Regular scheduled use provides no additional benefit over PRN use and does not reduce exacerbation frequency. GOLD guidelines recommend against regular SABA as maintenance monotherapy.
explanation.b: Correct. LAMA monotherapy (tiotropium, UPLIFT trial) reduces COPD exacerbation rate and hospitalization. GOLD 2024 guidelines recommend LAMA or LABA as initial maintenance therapy, with LAMA preferred for exacerbation prevention. For GOLD 3 (severe) with exacerbations, LAMA+LABA+ICS triple therapy should be considered.
explanation.c: Incorrect. ICS monotherapy is NOT recommended in COPD — it does not improve survival, and its use as monotherapy is discouraged due to side effects (pneumonia) without adequate benefit. ICS is used in combination with LABA (ICS/LABA) or triple therapy in high-risk patients.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Theophylline has bronchodilatory effects but a narrow therapeutic window, significant drug interactions, and adverse effects (arrhythmias, seizures at toxic levels). GOLD guidelines recommend theophylline only as add-on therapy when LAMAs and LABAs are inadequate — not as preferred maintenance.

pulmonary.question: A 35-year-old previously healthy woman develops rapidly progressive respiratory failure from influenza pneumonitis. P/F ratio is 62 despite lung-protective ventilation at Vt 6 mL/kg, PEEP 16, FiO₂ 1.0, and 16 hours of prone positioning. SpO₂ 80%. Plateau pressure 36 cmH₂O. Which intervention should be pursued next?

answers.text: Increase PEEP to 20 cmH₂O and FiO₂ to 1.0 and reassess in 4 hours
Veno-venous ECMO evaluation and cannulation
Neuromuscular blockade with cisatracurium for 48 hours
IV methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg for influenza-associated lung inflammation

explanation.correct: Correct. VV-ECMO criteria met: P/F <80 on maximal support including proning and FiO₂ 1.0. Young patient with potentially reversible cause (influenza) has excellent ECMO candidacy. ECMO provides oxygenation and CO₂ removal while allowing ultra-protective ventilation. Transfer to ECMO center if not available.
explanation.a: Incorrect. PEEP is already at 16 and FiO₂ is already at 1.0. Increasing PEEP to 20 risks barotrauma (plateau already 36). The patient has already been proned for 16 hours. Maximal conventional therapy has been reached.
explanation.b: Correct. VV-ECMO criteria met: P/F <80 on maximal support including proning and FiO₂ 1.0. Young patient with potentially reversible cause (influenza) has excellent ECMO candidacy. ECMO provides oxygenation and CO₂ removal while allowing ultra-protective ventilation. Transfer to ECMO center if not available.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Neuromuscular blockade (ACURASYS trial) showed benefit in early moderate-severe ARDS, but the ROSE trial (2019) did not confirm this benefit. More importantly, cisatracurium for 48 hours would be an early intervention — this patient has already failed multiple rescue strategies and needs ECMO, not NMB.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Corticosteroids in influenza-associated ARDS are potentially HARMFUL — observational studies suggest increased viral replication and secondary bacterial infections. Steroids are not recommended for influenza-associated respiratory failure without another indication.

pulmonary.question: A 68-year-old woman has been mechanically ventilated for 10 days after ARDS from bacterial pneumonia. She is now improving — P/F 280, FiO₂ 0.35, PEEP 6, RR 14 (set), pH 7.42. She is alert, following commands, hemodynamically stable on vasopressors weaned to off. Cough is present but weak. Secretion burden is moderate. RSBI is 58. She passes her SBT. Should she be extubated?

answers.text: Yes — RSBI <105 and passing SBT indicate readiness; extubate now
Consider extubation but evaluate cough strength and secretion burden carefully; high-risk for post-extubation failure
No — she needs 72 more hours of observation to ensure respiratory stability before extubation
No — perform tracheostomy given 10 days of ventilation and high reintubation risk

explanation.correct: Correct. She meets readiness criteria but has multiple risk factors for post-extubation failure. Cough strength (peak cough flow), secretion burden, cognitive status, and swallowing function should all be assessed. Post-extubation prophylactic NIV should be strongly considered (reduces extubation failure in high-risk patients).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Simply meeting RSBI <105 and passing SBT does not fully predict extubation success, especially in high-risk patients. Age, secretion burden, cough strength, and duration of ventilation all contribute to post-extubation failure risk. A more nuanced assessment is required.
explanation.b: Correct. She meets readiness criteria but has multiple risk factors for post-extubation failure. Cough strength (peak cough flow), secretion burden, cognitive status, and swallowing function should all be assessed. Post-extubation prophylactic NIV should be strongly considered (reduces extubation failure in high-risk patients).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Delaying extubation in a patient who passes readiness criteria unnecessarily prolongs ventilator days, increases VAP risk, and delays recovery. The evidence supports extubating when ready, not waiting for an arbitrary time period.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Tracheostomy is not indicated solely based on 10 days of ventilation or moderate secretion burden if the patient passes extubation readiness criteria. Tracheostomy is considered if long-term ventilation (>14-21 days) is anticipated, or if multiple extubation failures occur.

pulmonary.question: A 48-year-old man with newly diagnosed DVT and PE is started on rivaroxaban. He has no known renal impairment. Pharmacy calls to confirm the prescribed dose. Which rivaroxaban dosing regimen is correct for VTE treatment?

answers.text: 15 mg twice daily for 21 days, then 20 mg once daily
20 mg once daily from day 1 throughout treatment
10 mg twice daily for 7 days, then 20 mg once daily
Bridging with low molecular weight heparin for 5 days, then rivaroxaban 20 mg daily

explanation.correct: Correct. FDA-approved rivaroxaban VTE dosing: 15 mg BID × 21 days → 20 mg daily. This regimen avoids the need for parenteral bridging (advantage over warfarin), has predictable pharmacokinetics, and was validated in EINSTEIN trials for both DVT and PE.
explanation.a: Correct. FDA-approved rivaroxaban VTE dosing: 15 mg BID × 21 days → 20 mg daily. This regimen avoids the need for parenteral bridging (advantage over warfarin), has predictable pharmacokinetics, and was validated in EINSTEIN trials for both DVT and PE.
explanation.b: Incorrect. 20 mg once daily from day 1 is the atrial fibrillation dose, not the VTE treatment dose. The VTE regimen requires the 15 mg BID loading phase for the first 21 days to achieve adequate anticoagulation during the acute thrombotic phase.
explanation.c: Incorrect. 10 mg BID is the extended prophylaxis dose (EINSTEIN-EXTENSION trial: 10 mg daily after initial VTE treatment). It is not the acute treatment dose. Using an inadequate dose during the acute phase risks thrombotic recurrence.
explanation.d: Incorrect. One of rivaroxaban's key advantages is eliminating the need for initial parenteral bridging. Unlike warfarin (which requires 5-7 days of heparin bridging to therapeutic INR), rivaroxaban achieves therapeutic anticoagulation immediately. Bridging is unnecessary and adds cost and complexity.

pulmonary.question: A 54-year-old man with ARDS is on lung-protective ventilation: Vt 380 mL (6 mL/kg IBW 63 kg), RR 24, PEEP 14, FiO₂ 0.65. Plateau pressure 28 cmH₂O, driving pressure 14 cmH₂O. pH 7.27, PaCO₂ 58, PaO₂ 72. SpO₂ 93%. The team wants to reduce the CO₂. Which is the most appropriate intervention?

answers.text: Increase tidal volume to 8 mL/kg to increase minute ventilation and CO₂ clearance
Increase respiratory rate to 28-30 breaths per minute, monitoring for auto-PEEP
Decrease PEEP to 8 to reduce hyperinflation and allow more CO₂ clearance per breath
Add supplemental CO₂ to inspired gas to stimulate respiratory drive

explanation.correct: Correct. Increasing RR increases minute ventilation without increasing Vt. Safe upper limit is approximately 28-35 bpm — monitor for auto-PEEP with expiratory holds. At RR 28, the I:E ratio must be maintained to allow full exhalation. Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable if pH remains >7.20.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Increasing Vt above 6 mL/kg in ARDS directly increases ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI). The ARMA trial demonstrated that Vt >10 mL/kg significantly increases mortality. Vt should not be increased to manage CO₂ in ARDS — permissive hypercapnia is preferable.
explanation.b: Correct. Increasing RR increases minute ventilation without increasing Vt. Safe upper limit is approximately 28-35 bpm — monitor for auto-PEEP with expiratory holds. At RR 28, the I:E ratio must be maintained to allow full exhalation. Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable if pH remains >7.20.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Reducing PEEP in ARDS risks alveolar derecruitment, worsening oxygenation, and atelectrauma. PEEP should not be reduced to improve CO₂ clearance — the oxygenation cost outweighs the minimal benefit on CO₂. PEEP is optimized for oxygenation and lung recruitment.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Adding inhaled CO₂ (carbogen) has no role in ARDS management and would worsen hypercapnia and acidosis. This approach has been studied in specific situations (head injury, COPD) to stimulate breathing but is not applicable here.

pulmonary.question: A 58-year-old woman with GBS (ascending paralysis, now affecting respiratory muscles) has NIF -18 cmH₂O, FVC 0.9 L (28% predicted), and MEP 28 cmH₂O. She is alert, SpO₂ 97% on RA, RR 22. She says she feels short of breath lying flat (orthopnea). Which is the most appropriate management?

answers.text: Administer IVIG and reassess respiratory function in 48 hours before considering intubation
Elective intubation now — NIF -18 cmH₂O, FVC 28%, and MEP 28 cmH₂O all meet 20-30-40 rule thresholds for intubation
Initiate BiPAP for respiratory support and avoid intubation in GBS
Continue observation with q4h NIF/FVC measurements in the ICU

explanation.correct: Correct. Meeting all three 20-30-40 thresholds (FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF >-30, MEP <40) plus orthopnea indicating diaphragm weakness = elective intubation indicated now. Elective intubation by an experienced team is far safer than emergent intubation after respiratory arrest.
explanation.a: Incorrect. IVIG should be started promptly but takes days to weeks to show respiratory improvement. Waiting 48 hours to assess response while ALL three 20-30-40 thresholds are met risks catastrophic respiratory failure. IVIG and intubation should proceed simultaneously.
explanation.b: Correct. Meeting all three 20-30-40 thresholds (FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF >-30, MEP <40) plus orthopnea indicating diaphragm weakness = elective intubation indicated now. Elective intubation by an experienced team is far safer than emergent intubation after respiratory arrest.
explanation.c: Incorrect. BiPAP is not effective in GBS — the issue is neuromuscular (the patient cannot generate adequate inspiratory effort), not obstructive or parenchymal. BiPAP requires the patient to generate adequate negative pressure to trigger, which these patients cannot do reliably. GBS patients who fail conservative management require intubation.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While ICU monitoring with serial measurements is appropriate for patients approaching thresholds, this patient has already CROSSED all three thresholds. Continued monitoring delays necessary intubation and risks emergent intubation in a patient with a potentially difficult airway from progressing paralysis.

pulmonary.question: Right heart catheterization in a 52-year-old woman with dyspnea and leg edema shows: mPAP 38 mmHg, PCWP 22 mmHg, PVR 1.8 Wood units, CO 5 L/min. Transpulmonary gradient (TPG) = mPAP - PCWP = 20 mmHg. Which pulmonary hypertension Group classification does this represent?

answers.text: Group 1 PAH — idiopathic or associated with connective tissue disease
Group 2 PH — left heart disease with passive pulmonary hypertension
Group 3 PH — lung disease and/or hypoxia
Group 2 PH with superimposed pre-capillary component (combined post- and pre-capillary PH)

explanation.correct: Correct. PCWP 22 mmHg (>15) with elevated mPAP = pulmonary hypertension due to left heart disease (Group 2). The primary driver is elevated LA/PCWP from LV dysfunction, diastolic dysfunction, or mitral/aortic valve disease. Treatment targets the underlying left heart condition, NOT pulmonary vasodilators.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Group 1 PAH requires PCWP ≤15 mmHg (normal left heart filling pressure) with elevated mPAP and PVR. This patient's PCWP of 22 mmHg clearly indicates left-heart disease as the cause — ruling out Group 1.
explanation.b: Correct. PCWP 22 mmHg (>15) with elevated mPAP = pulmonary hypertension due to left heart disease (Group 2). The primary driver is elevated LA/PCWP from LV dysfunction, diastolic dysfunction, or mitral/aortic valve disease. Treatment targets the underlying left heart condition, NOT pulmonary vasodilators.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Group 3 PH is associated with lung disease, chronic hypoxia, COPD, ILD. These cause pulmonary vasoconstriction from hypoxia. Group 3 typically has PCWP <15 (pre-capillary PH). This patient's elevated PCWP identifies a left heart cause.
explanation.d: Incorrect. CpcPH requires PCWP >15 AND PVR ≥3 Wood units AND DPG ≥7 mmHg. While this patient is borderline (PVR 3.2), the primary classification remains Group 2. The distinction matters clinically: Group 1 vasodilators (sildenafil, ambrisentan) are potentially harmful in Group 2 PH and are not approved for this indication.

pulmonary.question: A 56-year-old woman with pneumonia develops acute respiratory failure. She is intubated and placed on mechanical ventilation. ABG shows PaO2 68 mmHg on FiO2 80% and PEEP 10 cmH2O. CXR shows bilateral opacities not explained by effusions or atelectasis. Echocardiogram shows normal LV function. Her PaO2/FiO2 ratio is 85. What severity of ARDS does this represent?

answers.text: Mild ARDS
Moderate ARDS
Severe ARDS
Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema

explanation.correct: Correct. Severe ARDS is defined by Berlin criteria as PaO2/FiO2 ≤100 with PEEP ≥5 cmH2O, bilateral opacities on imaging, and respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload. This patient meets all criteria with a P/F ratio of 85.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Mild ARDS is defined by a PaO2/FiO2 ratio of 200–300 with PEEP ≥5 cmH2O. This patient's ratio of 85 is far below the mild threshold.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Moderate ARDS is defined as PaO2/FiO2 100–200 with PEEP ≥5 cmH2O. This patient's ratio of 85 falls below the moderate range.
explanation.c: Correct. Severe ARDS is defined by Berlin criteria as PaO2/FiO2 ≤100 with PEEP ≥5 cmH2O, bilateral opacities on imaging, and respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload. This patient meets all criteria with a P/F ratio of 85.
explanation.d: Incorrect. The echocardiogram shows normal LV function, ruling out cardiogenic pulmonary edema. ARDS is defined by bilateral opacities NOT explained by cardiac failure, which is the case here.

pulmonary.question: A patient with severe ARDS (P/F ratio 72) remains hypoxemic despite FiO2 100%, PEEP 16 cmH2O, and low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW). Plateau pressure is 28 cmH2O. What is the most appropriate next intervention to improve oxygenation?

answers.text: Increase tidal volume to 8 mL/kg IBW
Prone positioning for ≥16 hours per day
Switch to high-frequency oscillatory ventilation (HFOV)
Administer inhaled nitric oxide (iNO) at 20 ppm

explanation.correct: Correct. The PROSEVA trial demonstrated that prone positioning for ≥16 hours/day significantly reduces mortality in severe ARDS (P/F <150). Prone positioning improves V/Q matching, recruits dependent lung regions, and reduces overdistension of non-dependent areas. It should be initiated early in severe ARDS.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Increasing tidal volumes above 6 mL/kg IBW causes ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) and increases mortality in ARDS. The ARDSNet trial demonstrated that low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW) with plateau pressures ≤30 cmH2O reduces mortality.
explanation.b: Correct. The PROSEVA trial demonstrated that prone positioning for ≥16 hours/day significantly reduces mortality in severe ARDS (P/F <150). Prone positioning improves V/Q matching, recruits dependent lung regions, and reduces overdistension of non-dependent areas. It should be initiated early in severe ARDS.
explanation.c: Incorrect. The OSCILLATE and OSCAR trials showed no benefit (and potential harm) with HFOV in adults with ARDS. It is not recommended as a rescue strategy for refractory hypoxemia in adult ARDS.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While iNO can transiently improve oxygenation through selective pulmonary vasodilation, multiple trials have failed to demonstrate a mortality benefit in ARDS. It is not recommended as standard therapy and should not delay proven interventions like prone positioning.

pulmonary.question: A 62-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 28% predicted) presents with acute exacerbation — increased dyspnea, purulent sputum, and accessory muscle use. ABG shows pH 7.28, PaCO2 68 mmHg, PaO2 54 mmHg on 2 L NC. What is the most appropriate respiratory intervention?

answers.text: Immediate endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation
Bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP)
High-flow nasal cannula at 50 L/min
Increase supplemental oxygen to 6 L NC to target SpO2 >96%

explanation.correct: Correct. NIV with BiPAP is first-line for acute COPD exacerbations with hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.35, PaCO2 >45). The IPAP augments ventilation to reduce PaCO2 while EPAP provides PEEP to stent open airways. Evidence shows reduced intubation rates and mortality compared to standard therapy alone.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Intubation is a last resort in COPD exacerbation. NIV is the first-line intervention for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure in COPD (pH 7.25–7.35) and reduces the need for intubation, ICU length of stay, and mortality.
explanation.b: Correct. NIV with BiPAP is first-line for acute COPD exacerbations with hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.35, PaCO2 >45). The IPAP augments ventilation to reduce PaCO2 while EPAP provides PEEP to stent open airways. Evidence shows reduced intubation rates and mortality compared to standard therapy alone.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While HFNC provides some PEEP and washout of dead space, it does not provide the same degree of ventilatory support as BiPAP for hypercapnic respiratory failure. BiPAP is the evidence-based first-line intervention for acute COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Excessive oxygen supplementation in COPD patients can worsen hypercapnia via the Haldane effect and suppression of hypoxic ventilatory drive. Target SpO2 is 88–92% in COPD exacerbation. This intervention could precipitate worsening respiratory acidosis.

pulmonary.question: A 58-year-old woman develops sudden-onset dyspnea and hypotension (SBP 78 mmHg) on post-operative day 3 after hip replacement. HR is 128 bpm. CT pulmonary angiography reveals a saddle pulmonary embolism with RV dilation. Troponin is elevated. What is the most appropriate treatment?

answers.text: Systemic tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) 100 mg IV over 2 hours
Unfractionated heparin bolus and infusion alone
Catheter-directed thrombolysis as first-line therapy
IVC filter placement and initiate rivaroxaban

explanation.correct: Correct. This patient has a massive PE — defined by hemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg) with confirmed PE and RV dysfunction. Systemic thrombolysis with tPA is indicated for massive PE as it rapidly reduces clot burden and RV afterload. The mortality benefit outweighs the bleeding risk in hemodynamically unstable patients.
explanation.a: Correct. This patient has a massive PE — defined by hemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg) with confirmed PE and RV dysfunction. Systemic thrombolysis with tPA is indicated for massive PE as it rapidly reduces clot burden and RV afterload. The mortality benefit outweighs the bleeding risk in hemodynamically unstable patients.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Anticoagulation alone is appropriate for submassive or low-risk PE. In massive PE with hemodynamic instability, anticoagulation alone is insufficient — the clot burden must be reduced rapidly with thrombolytics or catheter-directed therapy to prevent cardiovascular collapse.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While catheter-directed therapy is an option for massive PE, it requires interventional radiology availability and procedural time. For a hemodynamically unstable patient requiring immediate treatment, systemic tPA is the first-line intervention given its rapid administration without needing procedural setup.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IVC filters are indicated when anticoagulation is contraindicated, not as primary treatment for massive PE. Rivaroxaban (oral anticoagulant) is inappropriate for acute massive PE requiring immediate hemodynamic rescue — systemic thrombolysis is indicated.

pulmonary.question: A 71-year-old man is admitted from a nursing home with fever, productive cough, and a new left lower lobe infiltrate. He was hospitalized 3 weeks ago for a UTI and received IV ceftriaxone. Sputum culture is pending. Which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?

answers.text: Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin
Vancomycin plus cefepime plus levofloxacin
Amoxicillin-clavulanate oral
Meropenem monotherapy

explanation.correct: Correct. Patients with validated risk factors for multidrug-resistant organisms (hospitalization within 90 days, nursing home residence, recent IV antibiotics) require broad-spectrum empiric coverage per ATS/IDSA CAP guidelines. Vancomycin covers MRSA, cefepime covers Pseudomonas and gram-negatives, and levofloxacin provides atypical coverage and additional gram-negative activity. The decision to broaden coverage should be based on these individual risk factors, not the retired HCAP classification.
explanation.a: Incorrect. This is a standard community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) regimen. However, this patient has validated risk factors for MRSA and Pseudomonas infection — recent hospitalization within 90 days, residence in a nursing home, and recent IV antibiotic exposure. ATS/IDSA guidelines recommend broader empiric coverage when these specific risk factors are present, rather than standard CAP therapy alone.
explanation.b: Correct. Patients with validated risk factors for multidrug-resistant organisms (hospitalization within 90 days, nursing home residence, recent IV antibiotics) require broad-spectrum empiric coverage per ATS/IDSA CAP guidelines. Vancomycin covers MRSA, cefepime covers Pseudomonas and gram-negatives, and levofloxacin provides atypical coverage and additional gram-negative activity. The decision to broaden coverage should be based on these individual risk factors, not the retired HCAP classification.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Oral antibiotics are insufficient for a patient sick enough to be admitted with risk factors for resistant organisms. This regimen does not cover MRSA or Pseudomonas, which are key concerns in healthcare-associated pneumonia.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While meropenem covers Pseudomonas and most gram-negatives, it does not provide MRSA coverage. In patients with MRSA risk factors (recent hospitalization, nursing home), vancomycin or linezolid must be included in the regimen.

pulmonary.question: A mechanically ventilated patient has the following settings: AC mode, Vt 450 mL, RR 16, FiO2 40%, PEEP 8 cmH2O. ABG shows pH 7.52, PaCO2 28 mmHg, PaO2 112 mmHg. What is the most appropriate ventilator adjustment?

answers.text: Decrease respiratory rate to 12 breaths/min
Increase PEEP to 12 cmH2O
Increase tidal volume to 550 mL
Switch to pressure support ventilation

explanation.correct: Correct. The ABG shows respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.52, PaCO2 28) and hyperoxia (PaO2 112). Reducing the set respiratory rate will decrease minute ventilation, allowing PaCO2 to rise toward normal. The FiO2 can also be weaned given the high PaO2, but the primary issue to address is the alkalosis.
explanation.a: Correct. The ABG shows respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.52, PaCO2 28) and hyperoxia (PaO2 112). Reducing the set respiratory rate will decrease minute ventilation, allowing PaCO2 to rise toward normal. The FiO2 can also be weaned given the high PaO2, but the primary issue to address is the alkalosis.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Increasing PEEP would improve oxygenation (which is already excessive) and does not address the respiratory alkalosis. PEEP changes do not significantly affect CO2 elimination. The problem is excessive minute ventilation causing hypocapnia.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Increasing tidal volume would increase minute ventilation further, worsening the respiratory alkalosis. Additionally, higher tidal volumes increase the risk of ventilator-induced lung injury. The tidal volume should remain at 6–8 mL/kg IBW.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While PSV allows patient-triggered breathing, switching modes does not directly address the acute respiratory alkalosis. The immediate intervention should be reducing the set respiratory rate in the current mode. Mode changes can be considered once acid-base status is corrected.

pulmonary.question: A 55-year-old man with cirrhosis develops a right-sided pleural effusion. Thoracentesis reveals: protein 1.2 g/dL (serum 5.8 g/dL), LDH 62 IU/L (serum 180 IU/L), cell count 120/μL with 80% lymphocytes. Which of the following is the most likely etiology?

answers.text: Hepatic hydrothorax
Empyema
Malignant pleural effusion
Tuberculous pleuritis

explanation.correct: Correct. This is a transudative effusion by Light's criteria: pleural/serum protein ratio <0.5 (1.2/5.8 = 0.21), pleural/serum LDH ratio <0.6 (62/180 = 0.34). In a patient with cirrhosis, transudative right-sided effusion is hepatic hydrothorax — caused by passage of ascitic fluid through diaphragmatic defects. It occurs in approximately 5–10% of cirrhosis patients.
explanation.a: Correct. This is a transudative effusion by Light's criteria: pleural/serum protein ratio <0.5 (1.2/5.8 = 0.21), pleural/serum LDH ratio <0.6 (62/180 = 0.34). In a patient with cirrhosis, transudative right-sided effusion is hepatic hydrothorax — caused by passage of ascitic fluid through diaphragmatic defects. It occurs in approximately 5–10% of cirrhosis patients.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Empyema presents as an exudative effusion with high protein, elevated LDH, low pH (<7.20), low glucose, and a neutrophil-predominant cell count. This fluid is clearly transudative with a low cell count and lymphocyte predominance — inconsistent with empyema.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Malignant effusions are exudative with elevated protein and LDH that meet Light's criteria. While lymphocyte predominance can be seen in malignancy, the transudative chemistry (low protein and LDH ratios) points to hepatic hydrothorax rather than malignancy.
explanation.d: Incorrect. TB pleuritis produces a lymphocyte-predominant exudative effusion with high protein, elevated LDH, and low glucose. Although this effusion has lymphocyte predominance, the chemistry is transudative, ruling out TB pleuritis.

pulmonary.question: A 22-year-old tall, thin man presents with sudden-onset right-sided chest pain and dyspnea. He has no significant medical history. Chest X-ray reveals a 25% right pneumothorax. Vital signs are stable with SpO2 97% on room air. What is the most appropriate initial management?

answers.text: Emergent needle decompression followed by chest tube
Observation with supplemental oxygen and repeat CXR in 6 hours
Immediate large-bore (28 Fr) chest tube insertion
Surgical thoracoscopy with pleurodesis

explanation.correct: Correct. For a first-episode primary spontaneous pneumothorax in a hemodynamically stable patient with a small-to-moderate pneumothorax (<3 cm apex-to-cupola), observation with supplemental oxygen and serial imaging is appropriate. Supplemental O2 increases the rate of pleural air reabsorption approximately 4-fold.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Needle decompression is indicated for tension pneumothorax (hemodynamic instability, tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds). This patient is hemodynamically stable with a simple primary spontaneous pneumothorax — needle decompression is not indicated.
explanation.b: Correct. For a first-episode primary spontaneous pneumothorax in a hemodynamically stable patient with a small-to-moderate pneumothorax (<3 cm apex-to-cupola), observation with supplemental oxygen and serial imaging is appropriate. Supplemental O2 increases the rate of pleural air reabsorption approximately 4-fold.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Large-bore chest tubes are reserved for traumatic pneumothoraces, hemothoraces, or large/tension pneumothoraces. For a stable primary spontaneous pneumothorax, observation or small-bore catheter aspiration is preferred. Large-bore tubes carry higher complication rates.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Surgical intervention (VATS with pleurodesis) is reserved for recurrent pneumothorax, bilateral pneumothorax, persistent air leak >5–7 days, or certain high-risk occupations (pilots, divers). First-episode PSP in a stable patient does not warrant surgical intervention.

pulmonary.question: A 48-year-old woman with moderate persistent asthma presents for follow-up. She uses her albuterol rescue inhaler 4 times weekly and reports nighttime awakening twice per month. She is currently on a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) only. What is the appropriate step-up in therapy?

answers.text: Add a short-acting muscarinic antagonist (SAMA) scheduled QID
Increase to high-dose ICS monotherapy
Add a long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) to current low-dose ICS
Start oral montelukast and discontinue ICS

explanation.correct: Correct. Per NAEPP/GINA guidelines, Step 3 therapy for uncontrolled moderate persistent asthma is low-dose ICS plus LABA (e.g., fluticasone/salmeterol or budesonide/formoterol). Adding a LABA to ICS improves symptom control and lung function more effectively than increasing the ICS dose alone.
explanation.a: Incorrect. SAMAs such as ipratropium are used in COPD management and acute asthma exacerbations, not as step-up controller therapy in persistent asthma. The appropriate step-up adds a long-acting beta-agonist to the existing ICS.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Jumping to high-dose ICS monotherapy is not the recommended next step. Guidelines recommend adding a LABA to low-dose ICS (Step 3) before escalating to higher ICS doses, as combination therapy is more effective than simply increasing the steroid dose.
explanation.c: Correct. Per NAEPP/GINA guidelines, Step 3 therapy for uncontrolled moderate persistent asthma is low-dose ICS plus LABA (e.g., fluticasone/salmeterol or budesonide/formoterol). Adding a LABA to ICS improves symptom control and lung function more effectively than increasing the ICS dose alone.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast) are less effective than ICS as controller therapy and should not replace ICS. Montelukast may be considered as add-on therapy but is inferior to adding a LABA as a step-up strategy.

pulmonary.question: A 67-year-old man with COPD is being discharged after an acute exacerbation. Room air ABG at rest shows PaO2 52 mmHg and SpO2 86%. He has no evidence of pulmonary hypertension or cor pulmonale. According to CMS criteria, which of the following qualifies him for home oxygen therapy?

answers.text: PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88% at rest on room air
PaO2 ≤65 mmHg on room air regardless of comorbidities
SpO2 ≤92% during a 6-minute walk test only
Requires documentation of active smoking cessation before qualifying

explanation.correct: Correct. CMS criteria for home oxygen qualification include: PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88% at rest on room air. This patient meets criteria with PaO2 52 and SpO2 86%. The qualifying ABG must be obtained during a period of clinical stability (not during an acute exacerbation).
explanation.a: Correct. CMS criteria for home oxygen qualification include: PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88% at rest on room air. This patient meets criteria with PaO2 52 and SpO2 86%. The qualifying ABG must be obtained during a period of clinical stability (not during an acute exacerbation).
explanation.b: Incorrect. A PaO2 of 56–59 mmHg qualifies for home oxygen ONLY with evidence of cor pulmonale, pulmonary hypertension, or erythrocytosis (Hct >55%). A threshold of ≤65 without qualifying conditions is not a CMS criterion.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While exercise desaturation can qualify a patient for ambulatory oxygen, the standard resting criteria (PaO2 ≤55 or SpO2 ≤88%) already qualify this patient. Exercise testing is used when resting values do not meet the threshold.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While smoking cessation is strongly recommended for safety reasons (oxygen is an oxidizer), it is not a CMS requirement for home oxygen qualification. The qualifying criterion is based on objective hypoxemia demonstrated by ABG or pulse oximetry.

pulmonary.question: A patient on mechanical ventilation (AC mode, Vt 420 mL, RR 18, PEEP 12, FiO2 60%) has a plateau pressure of 34 cmH2O and peak inspiratory pressure of 48 cmH2O. The difference between PIP and Pplat is 14 cmH2O. What does this finding most likely indicate?

answers.text: Decreased lung compliance (e.g., worsening ARDS or pulmonary fibrosis)
Ventilator dyssynchrony due to inadequate sedation
Auto-PEEP from air trapping
Increased airway resistance (e.g., bronchospasm, mucus plugging, or kinked ETT)

explanation.correct: Correct. The PIP-Pplat gradient reflects airway resistance. A gradient >5–10 cmH2O indicates elevated airway resistance, which can be caused by bronchospasm, mucus plugging, biting on the ETT, or a kinked/narrowed tube. Assessment should include suctioning, bronchodilator administration, and inspection of the ETT circuit.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Decreased lung compliance increases BOTH peak and plateau pressures proportionally. The elevated Pplat (34) does suggest reduced compliance, but the large PIP-Pplat gradient (14 cmH2O) specifically indicates an additional resistive component — high airway resistance.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Patient-ventilator dyssynchrony can cause irregular pressure waveforms and patient discomfort, but it does not consistently produce an elevated PIP-Pplat gradient. The gradient specifically reflects resistive forces during inspiratory flow, pointing to an airway resistance problem.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While auto-PEEP can elevate PIP, it is identified by performing an end-expiratory hold maneuver (total PEEP minus set PEEP = auto-PEEP). Auto-PEEP raises the baseline pressure, not specifically the PIP-Pplat gradient. The large gradient here points directly to airway resistance.
explanation.d: Correct. The PIP-Pplat gradient reflects airway resistance. A gradient >5–10 cmH2O indicates elevated airway resistance, which can be caused by bronchospasm, mucus plugging, biting on the ETT, or a kinked/narrowed tube. Assessment should include suctioning, bronchodilator administration, and inspection of the ETT circuit.

pulmonary.question: A 45-year-old woman with a history of unprovoked DVT 2 years ago (completed 6 months of anticoagulation) now presents with a new unprovoked DVT in the contralateral leg. Hypercoagulability workup reveals she is heterozygous for Factor V Leiden. What is the recommended duration of anticoagulation?

answers.text: 3 months of anticoagulation, then reassess
6 months of anticoagulation, then discontinue
Indefinite anticoagulation with periodic reassessment
12 months of anticoagulation followed by aspirin indefinitely

explanation.correct: Correct. Recurrent unprovoked VTE is a strong indication for indefinite (lifelong) anticoagulation per ACCP and ASH guidelines. The risk of recurrence after stopping anticoagulation in this population is approximately 10% per year. Factor V Leiden heterozygosity adds additional thrombotic risk. Annual reassessment of the risk-benefit ratio is recommended.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Three months is the minimum duration for a first unprovoked VTE. This is a recurrent unprovoked event, which carries a significantly higher risk of further recurrence (approximately 10% per year off anticoagulation). Indefinite therapy is recommended.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Six months may be appropriate for a first unprovoked VTE in patients with moderate risk. Recurrent unprovoked VTE — especially with an identified thrombophilia — warrants indefinite anticoagulation due to the high recurrence risk.
explanation.c: Correct. Recurrent unprovoked VTE is a strong indication for indefinite (lifelong) anticoagulation per ACCP and ASH guidelines. The risk of recurrence after stopping anticoagulation in this population is approximately 10% per year. Factor V Leiden heterozygosity adds additional thrombotic risk. Annual reassessment of the risk-benefit ratio is recommended.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While the ASPIRE trial showed low-dose aspirin provides modest reduction in VTE recurrence after stopping anticoagulation, it is significantly less effective than continued anticoagulation. In recurrent unprovoked VTE, indefinite anticoagulation is standard.

pulmonary.question: A 38-year-old previously healthy man presents with 5 days of dry cough, low-grade fever, headache, and bilateral patchy interstitial infiltrates on chest X-ray. Physical exam reveals bullous myringitis of the right ear. Which organism is most likely responsible?

answers.text: Streptococcus pneumoniae
Legionella pneumophila
Mycoplasma pneumoniae
Pneumocystis jirovecii

explanation.correct: Correct. The presentation is classic for Mycoplasma pneumoniae — a young, otherwise healthy patient with subacute onset of dry cough, low-grade fever, headache, bilateral interstitial infiltrates, and bullous myringitis (a classic but uncommon association). Treatment is with a macrolide, doxycycline, or respiratory fluoroquinolone.
explanation.a: Incorrect. S. pneumoniae is the most common cause of typical community-acquired pneumonia, presenting with abrupt onset of high fever, productive cough, and lobar consolidation — not the subacute, atypical presentation with interstitial infiltrates seen here.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Legionella causes atypical pneumonia but is associated with GI symptoms (diarrhea), hyponatremia, and elevated transaminases. Bullous myringitis is not a feature of Legionella infection.
