explanation.c: Correct. The presentation is classic for Mycoplasma pneumoniae — a young, otherwise healthy patient with subacute onset of dry cough, low-grade fever, headache, bilateral interstitial infiltrates, and bullous myringitis (a classic but uncommon association). Treatment is with a macrolide, doxycycline, or respiratory fluoroquinolone.
explanation.d: Incorrect. PCP (Pneumocystis pneumonia) occurs in immunocompromised patients, particularly those with HIV and CD4 <200 cells/μL. This patient is previously healthy with no risk factors for immunodeficiency. The presentation including bullous myringitis is characteristic of Mycoplasma.

pulmonary.question: A 44-year-old woman with progressive dyspnea on exertion is found to have a mean pulmonary artery pressure of 38 mmHg on right heart catheterization, PCWP of 10 mmHg, and PVR of 6 Wood units. Echocardiogram shows RV dilation. V/Q scan, sleep study, and PFTs are normal. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

answers.text: CT pulmonary angiography to rule out chronic thromboembolic disease
Pulmonary vasoreactivity testing with inhaled nitric oxide
Right heart catheterization with exercise challenge
Lung biopsy to confirm pulmonary veno-occlusive disease

explanation.correct: Correct. This patient has confirmed precapillary pulmonary hypertension (mPAP >20 mmHg, PCWP ≤15, PVR >2 WU) with normal workup for secondary causes, consistent with WHO Group 1 PAH. Pulmonary vasoreactivity testing is required to identify the subset (~10%) of idiopathic PAH patients who respond to calcium channel blockers, which is a first-line therapy with excellent long-term outcomes in responders.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A normal V/Q scan effectively rules out chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH, WHO Group 4). V/Q scan has a sensitivity >96% for CTEPH. CT angiography adds limited diagnostic value when V/Q is negative.
explanation.b: Correct. This patient has confirmed precapillary pulmonary hypertension (mPAP >20 mmHg, PCWP ≤15, PVR >2 WU) with normal workup for secondary causes, consistent with WHO Group 1 PAH. Pulmonary vasoreactivity testing is required to identify the subset (~10%) of idiopathic PAH patients who respond to calcium channel blockers, which is a first-line therapy with excellent long-term outcomes in responders.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Exercise right heart catheterization is used to evaluate exercise-induced pulmonary hypertension in patients with borderline resting hemodynamics. This patient already has confirmed PH at rest (mPAP 38 mmHg); exercise testing adds no additional diagnostic value.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Lung biopsy carries significant hemorrhagic risk in PH patients and is rarely performed. PVOD is suggested by specific CT findings (septal lines, ground-glass opacities, mediastinal lymphadenopathy) and desaturation with vasodilator therapy — not by biopsy.

pulmonary.question: A 58-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 22% predicted) is intubated for an acute exacerbation. After 30 minutes on mechanical ventilation (AC mode, Vt 450 mL, RR 14, FiO2 40%, PEEP 5), he becomes acutely hypotensive with MAP 52 mmHg. Breath sounds are bilateral but diminished. Peak airway pressures have risen to 45 cmH2O. What is the most likely cause?

answers.text: Tension pneumothorax
Mucus plug in the right mainstem bronchus
Dynamic hyperinflation (auto-PEEP) with decreased venous return
Acute right ventricular failure from PE

explanation.correct: Correct. Severe COPD patients on mechanical ventilation are at high risk for dynamic hyperinflation (breath stacking). Trapped air creates intrinsic PEEP (auto-PEEP), increasing intrathoracic pressure and reducing venous return, causing hypotension. Diagnosis is confirmed by end-expiratory hold. Treatment: disconnect the ventilator briefly to allow exhalation, then reduce RR, increase expiratory time, and minimize PEEP.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While tension pneumothorax can occur in mechanically ventilated COPD patients, it typically presents with unilateral absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation. This patient has bilateral diminished breath sounds, which is more consistent with dynamic hyperinflation.
explanation.b: Incorrect. A mucus plug would cause unilateral loss of breath sounds and potentially a post-obstructive collapse. Bilateral diminished breath sounds with elevated pressures and hypotension point to a bilateral process like auto-PEEP and dynamic hyperinflation.
explanation.c: Correct. Severe COPD patients on mechanical ventilation are at high risk for dynamic hyperinflation (breath stacking). Trapped air creates intrinsic PEEP (auto-PEEP), increasing intrathoracic pressure and reducing venous return, causing hypotension. Diagnosis is confirmed by end-expiratory hold. Treatment: disconnect the ventilator briefly to allow exhalation, then reduce RR, increase expiratory time, and minimize PEEP.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While PE can cause acute RV failure and hypotension in mechanically ventilated patients, the acute rise in peak airway pressures immediately after initiation of mechanical ventilation in a severe COPD patient is most consistent with auto-PEEP from dynamic hyperinflation.

pulmonary.question: A 65-year-old man with pneumonia has been mechanically ventilated for 5 days. His pneumonia is improving. The team wants to assess readiness for extubation. Which set of parameters suggests the patient is ready for a spontaneous breathing trial (SBT)?

answers.text: FiO2 100%, PEEP 15 cmH2O, heavy sedation, and vasopressor support
FiO2 40%, PEEP 5 cmH2O, alert and following commands, minimal vasopressor support, adequate cough and secretion management
Rapid shallow breathing index (RSBI) >120 breaths/min/L
The patient must be on zero PEEP and zero pressure support for 24 hours before extubation

explanation.correct: Correct. SBT readiness criteria include: (1) Resolution/improvement of underlying condition; (2) FiO2 ≤40% and PEEP ≤5–8 cmH2O; (3) Adequate oxygenation (PaO2/FiO2 >150–200); (4) Hemodynamically stable (no or low-dose vasopressors); (5) Neurologically appropriate (alert, following commands); (6) Adequate cough and manageable secretions. The SBT itself is conducted on minimal support (T-piece, CPAP 5, or PSV 5–8) for 30–120 minutes, monitoring for signs of failure (tachypnea, tachycardia, desaturation, diaphoresis, paradoxical breathing).
explanation.a: Incorrect. SBT readiness requires clinical improvement: FiO2 ≤40%, PEEP ≤5–8 cmH2O, patient alert and following commands, hemodynamically stable without significant vasopressor support. This patient's settings indicate continued critical illness and is not ready for an SBT.
explanation.b: Correct. SBT readiness criteria include: (1) Resolution/improvement of underlying condition; (2) FiO2 ≤40% and PEEP ≤5–8 cmH2O; (3) Adequate oxygenation (PaO2/FiO2 >150–200); (4) Hemodynamically stable (no or low-dose vasopressors); (5) Neurologically appropriate (alert, following commands); (6) Adequate cough and manageable secretions. The SBT itself is conducted on minimal support (T-piece, CPAP 5, or PSV 5–8) for 30–120 minutes, monitoring for signs of failure (tachypnea, tachycardia, desaturation, diaphoresis, paradoxical breathing).
explanation.c: Incorrect. An RSBI >105 (frequency/tidal volume ratio) PREDICTS SBT failure, not readiness. An RSBI <105 suggests the patient can sustain spontaneous ventilation. An RSBI >120 strongly predicts weaning failure and indicates the patient is not ready for extubation.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Zero PEEP is not required — SBTs are commonly performed at CPAP 5 cmH2O or low-level PSV (5–8 cmH2O), which approximates the work of breathing through the endotracheal tube. Requiring 24 hours of zero support is unnecessarily prolonged and delays extubation. A successful 30–120 minute SBT is sufficient.

pulmonary.question: A 58-year-old man with septic shock (on vasopressors, BP 82/50 mmHg) and acute respiratory failure requires emergent intubation. He has a predicted difficult airway (short neck, Mallampati IV, limited mouth opening). Pre-intubation SpO2 is 88% after preoxygenation. Serum potassium is 6.2 mEq/L. The ACNP plans to perform rapid sequence intubation (RSI). Which induction agent and paralytic combination is most appropriate?

answers.text: Propofol 2 mg/kg plus succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg
Ketamine 1.5 mg/kg plus rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg
Etomidate 0.3 mg/kg plus succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg
Midazolam 0.2 mg/kg plus vecuronium 0.1 mg/kg

explanation.correct: Correct. Ketamine is the preferred induction agent in septic shock because it maintains sympathetic tone (supports BP and HR), unlike propofol or etomidate which can worsen hypotension. The potassium of 6.2 mEq/L is a CONTRAINDICATION to succinylcholine — succinylcholine causes a transient rise in serum potassium (~0.5–1.0 mEq/L) through depolarization of skeletal muscle, which could trigger fatal cardiac arrhythmia in this hyperkalemic patient. Rocuronium at 1.2 mg/kg (RSI dose) provides excellent intubating conditions within 60 seconds without affecting potassium, and is fully reversible with sugammadex if intubation fails.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Propofol causes significant dose-dependent hypotension through vasodilation and myocardial depression. In a hypoxic patient who may already be hemodynamically compromised, propofol can precipitate cardiovascular collapse. While succinylcholine provides rapid paralysis, propofol is a poor induction choice in this unstable scenario.
explanation.b: Correct. Ketamine is the preferred induction agent in septic shock because it maintains sympathetic tone (supports BP and HR), unlike propofol or etomidate which can worsen hypotension. The potassium of 6.2 mEq/L is a CONTRAINDICATION to succinylcholine — succinylcholine causes a transient rise in serum potassium (~0.5–1.0 mEq/L) through depolarization of skeletal muscle, which could trigger fatal cardiac arrhythmia in this hyperkalemic patient. Rocuronium at 1.2 mg/kg (RSI dose) provides excellent intubating conditions within 60 seconds without affecting potassium, and is fully reversible with sugammadex if intubation fails.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Etomidate provides hemodynamic stability but causes adrenal suppression (inhibits 11-beta-hydroxylase) even from a single dose, which may worsen outcomes in septic shock — a setting where adrenal reserve is already stressed. Furthermore, succinylcholine is CONTRAINDICATED with K+ 6.2 mEq/L because it causes additional potassium release from depolarizing muscle cells, risking fatal hyperkalemic cardiac arrest.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Midazolam has a slow onset (2–3 minutes), unpredictable dose response, and causes hypotension — all undesirable for RSI. Vecuronium at standard dose (0.1 mg/kg) has a slow onset (3–5 minutes), which is too slow for RSI where rapid paralysis is essential. Neither agent is appropriate for emergent intubation.

pulmonary.question: A 52-year-old obese man (BMI 38) presents with excessive daytime sleepiness, loud snoring, and witnessed apneas. His Epworth Sleepiness Scale score is 16 (normal <10). He has refractory hypertension requiring 3 medications. What is the gold standard diagnostic test and first-line treatment?

answers.text: Overnight pulse oximetry and oral modafinil for daytime sleepiness
In-lab polysomnography (PSG) and continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP)
Neck circumference measurement and surgical uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP)
Home sleep apnea testing (HSAT) alone is sufficient — no in-lab PSG needed

explanation.correct: Correct. In-lab polysomnography is the gold standard for diagnosing OSA — it measures AHI (apnea-hypopnea index), oxygen desaturations, sleep stages, and arousal index. AHI ≥5 with symptoms or AHI ≥15 regardless of symptoms = OSA diagnosis. CPAP is first-line treatment — it splints the airway open, eliminates obstructive events, improves daytime sleepiness, and reduces cardiovascular risk. OSA is an underrecognized cause of resistant hypertension; treating OSA can reduce BP by 2–10 mmHg.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Overnight oximetry alone has poor sensitivity for OSA — it detects desaturations but cannot quantify apneas, hypopneas, or arousals. Modafinil is an adjunct for residual daytime sleepiness AFTER CPAP therapy is optimized, not a primary OSA treatment. It does not address the airway obstruction or cardiovascular consequences.
explanation.b: Correct. In-lab polysomnography is the gold standard for diagnosing OSA — it measures AHI (apnea-hypopnea index), oxygen desaturations, sleep stages, and arousal index. AHI ≥5 with symptoms or AHI ≥15 regardless of symptoms = OSA diagnosis. CPAP is first-line treatment — it splints the airway open, eliminates obstructive events, improves daytime sleepiness, and reduces cardiovascular risk. OSA is an underrecognized cause of resistant hypertension; treating OSA can reduce BP by 2–10 mmHg.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While neck circumference >17 inches (men) or >16 inches (women) is a risk factor, it is not a diagnostic test. UPPP is a surgical option only for patients who fail or cannot tolerate CPAP, and its long-term success rate is only ~40–50%. CPAP is always first-line.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While HSAT is an acceptable alternative for patients with high pretest probability of moderate-to-severe OSA without significant comorbidities, in-lab PSG remains the gold standard and is preferred when comorbidities are present (heart failure, COPD, neuromuscular disease) or when HSAT is negative but clinical suspicion remains high. However, in clinical practice, HSAT is increasingly used as an initial test — this is an area of evolving guidelines.

pulmonary.question: A 75-year-old man with a recent stroke and dysphagia is found unresponsive with vomitus in his oropharynx. After intubation and suctioning, CXR shows a right lower lobe infiltrate. He develops fever and hypoxemia over the next 24 hours. What is the most important distinction between aspiration pneumonitis and aspiration pneumonia for determining antibiotic therapy?

answers.text: Aspiration pneumonitis is a chemical injury that does NOT initially require antibiotics; aspiration pneumonia is a bacterial infection that does
Both aspiration pneumonitis and aspiration pneumonia require immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics
Aspiration pneumonitis only occurs with witnessed aspiration; aspiration pneumonia occurs silently
Anaerobic coverage is never needed in aspiration pneumonia because anaerobes are rarely involved

explanation.correct: Correct. Aspiration pneumonitis is a sterile chemical inflammatory response to gastric acid (Mendelson syndrome) — it occurs within hours, causes fever, hypoxemia, and infiltrates, but is NOT infectious. Antibiotics are NOT indicated unless symptoms persist >48 hours or worsen, suggesting secondary bacterial superinfection. Aspiration pneumonia is a bacterial infection from aspiration of oropharyngeal secretions colonized with pathogens — it requires antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin for anaerobic coverage). The distinction is critical to avoid unnecessary antibiotic use.
explanation.a: Correct. Aspiration pneumonitis is a sterile chemical inflammatory response to gastric acid (Mendelson syndrome) — it occurs within hours, causes fever, hypoxemia, and infiltrates, but is NOT infectious. Antibiotics are NOT indicated unless symptoms persist >48 hours or worsen, suggesting secondary bacterial superinfection. Aspiration pneumonia is a bacterial infection from aspiration of oropharyngeal secretions colonized with pathogens — it requires antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin for anaerobic coverage). The distinction is critical to avoid unnecessary antibiotic use.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Reflexively starting antibiotics for all aspiration events leads to unnecessary antibiotic exposure, promotes resistance, and increases C. difficile risk. Aspiration pneumonitis is a chemical injury — antibiotics do not treat inflammation from gastric acid. Antibiotics are reserved for confirmed or suspected bacterial aspiration pneumonia.
explanation.c: Incorrect. This distinction is not accurate. Both can occur with or without witnessed aspiration events. Many aspiration events are silent (especially in stroke patients with impaired cough reflex). The distinction is based on pathophysiology: chemical injury (pneumonitis) vs. bacterial infection (pneumonia), not whether the event was witnessed.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Anaerobic bacteria (Peptostreptococcus, Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella) are important pathogens in aspiration pneumonia, particularly in patients with poor dentition, periodontal disease, or delayed presentation. Empiric coverage should include anaerobic activity (ampicillin-sulbactam, or clindamycin, or meropenem).

pulmonary.question: A 55-year-old man has been mechanically ventilated for 14 days following a severe TBI. He has failed multiple spontaneous breathing trials. GCS is 8T. The team is considering tracheostomy placement. What is the primary advantage of tracheostomy over prolonged translaryngeal intubation?

answers.text: Tracheostomy eliminates the risk of ventilator-associated pneumonia
Tracheostomy reduces dead space, facilitates secretion management, improves patient comfort, and enables earlier mobilization
Tracheostomy guarantees successful ventilator weaning within 7 days
Tracheostomy is primarily performed to prevent tracheal stenosis from the endotracheal tube

explanation.correct: Correct. Tracheostomy offers multiple advantages over prolonged translaryngeal intubation: (1) Reduced anatomic dead space (shorter airway circuit) — facilitates weaning; (2) Improved secretion suctioning and pulmonary toilet; (3) Decreased sedation requirements and improved patient comfort; (4) Earlier mobilization and physical therapy; (5) Potential for oral nutrition and speech (with speaking valves); (6) Reduced laryngeal injury from prolonged endotracheal tube. Guidelines generally recommend tracheostomy when ventilation is anticipated to be needed >14–21 days.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While some studies suggest tracheostomy may reduce VAP rates by improving pulmonary toilet and reducing oropharyngeal secretion pooling, it does NOT eliminate VAP risk. Tracheostomy patients can still develop VAP. The evidence for VAP reduction is mixed across trials.
explanation.b: Correct. Tracheostomy offers multiple advantages over prolonged translaryngeal intubation: (1) Reduced anatomic dead space (shorter airway circuit) — facilitates weaning; (2) Improved secretion suctioning and pulmonary toilet; (3) Decreased sedation requirements and improved patient comfort; (4) Earlier mobilization and physical therapy; (5) Potential for oral nutrition and speech (with speaking valves); (6) Reduced laryngeal injury from prolonged endotracheal tube. Guidelines generally recommend tracheostomy when ventilation is anticipated to be needed >14–21 days.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Tracheostomy facilitates weaning by reducing dead space and improving comfort, but it does not guarantee successful weaning. Many tracheostomized patients remain ventilator-dependent, particularly those with severe neurological injury or neuromuscular disease. Weaning success depends on the underlying condition and respiratory reserve.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While tracheal stenosis from prolonged intubation is a concern (cuff-related mucosal ischemia), it is not the primary indication for tracheostomy. Modern high-volume, low-pressure ETT cuffs have reduced this complication. The primary advantages are improved patient comfort, reduced sedation, better secretion management, and facilitation of weaning.

pulmonary.question: A mechanically ventilated patient with ARDS has the following settings: AC mode, Vt 6 mL/kg IBW, RR 28, PEEP 14, FiO2 70%. ABG shows pH 7.22, PaCO2 62 mmHg, PaO2 72 mmHg. Plateau pressure is 28 cmH2O. The resident wants to increase the tidal volume to correct the hypercapnia. Why should the ACNP decline this request?

answers.text: The PaCO2 of 62 mmHg is immediately life-threatening and requires emergent intervention other than ventilator changes
Increasing tidal volume above 6 mL/kg IBW increases mortality in ARDS — permissive hypercapnia is acceptable to a pH of ~7.20
The PEEP of 14 cmH2O is too high and should be reduced instead of changing tidal volume
The respiratory rate of 28 should be reduced first to allow more expiratory time

explanation.correct: Correct. The ARDSNet ARMA trial demonstrated that low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW) reduces mortality by 22% compared to 12 mL/kg. Permissive hypercapnia (tolerating elevated PaCO2) is a deliberate strategy to maintain lung protection. Acceptable pH range is generally ≥7.20 (some experts accept ≥7.15). If pH drops below 7.20, options include: increasing respiratory RATE (not volume), ensuring full expiratory time, and in refractory cases, bicarbonate infusion. Tidal volume should NOT exceed 6 mL/kg IBW — the plateau pressure of 28 (below 30) confirms safe distending pressures.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A PaCO2 of 62 mmHg with pH 7.22 represents moderate respiratory acidosis that is expected in lung-protective ventilation for ARDS. While the acidosis is significant, it is not immediately life-threatening and does not require emergent non-ventilator interventions. This is managed within the ventilator strategy.
explanation.b: Correct. The ARDSNet ARMA trial demonstrated that low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW) reduces mortality by 22% compared to 12 mL/kg. Permissive hypercapnia (tolerating elevated PaCO2) is a deliberate strategy to maintain lung protection. Acceptable pH range is generally ≥7.20 (some experts accept ≥7.15). If pH drops below 7.20, options include: increasing respiratory RATE (not volume), ensuring full expiratory time, and in refractory cases, bicarbonate infusion. Tidal volume should NOT exceed 6 mL/kg IBW — the plateau pressure of 28 (below 30) confirms safe distending pressures.
explanation.c: Incorrect. PEEP 14 with plateau pressure 28 cmH2O yields a driving pressure of 14 cmH2O (Pplat − PEEP), which is acceptable (<15 is the target). Reducing PEEP would likely worsen oxygenation by allowing alveolar derecruitment. The PEEP level is appropriate for moderate-severe ARDS and should not be reduced to address hypercapnia.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Reducing RR would worsen hypercapnia by decreasing minute ventilation (minute vent = Vt × RR). With a fixed low tidal volume, respiratory rate is the primary tool to maintain minute ventilation and CO2 clearance. If anything, increasing RR (while monitoring for auto-PEEP) would improve CO2 clearance without sacrificing lung protection.

neurology.question: A 71-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation on warfarin presents with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and facial droop. Last seen normal 90 minutes ago. NIHSS is 14. CT head shows no hemorrhage and no early ischemic changes. INR returns at 2.6. BP is 172/94. Which is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Administer IV tPA 0.9 mg/kg and activate stroke team for possible thrombectomy
Proceed directly to mechanical thrombectomy without IV tPA
Hold tPA due to therapeutic anticoagulation; proceed to CT angiography for thrombectomy evaluation
Administer 4-factor PCC to reverse warfarin and then give tPA

explanation.correct: Correct. tPA is contraindicated (INR 2.6 >1.7). Mechanical thrombectomy is not contraindicated by anticoagulation and should be pursued if LVO is identified on CTA. This is the correct pathway for anticoagulated stroke patients within the thrombectomy window.
explanation.a: Incorrect. INR 2.6 is an absolute contraindication to IV tPA. Therapeutic anticoagulation with warfarin (INR >1.7) prohibits systemic thrombolysis regardless of time window or stroke severity.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Proceeding directly to thrombectomy without CT angiography first is premature — LVO must be confirmed on CTA before catheterization. Thrombectomy without confirmed LVO exposes the patient to procedural risk unnecessarily.
explanation.c: Correct. tPA is contraindicated (INR 2.6 >1.7). Mechanical thrombectomy is not contraindicated by anticoagulation and should be pursued if LVO is identified on CTA. This is the correct pathway for anticoagulated stroke patients within the thrombectomy window.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Reversing anticoagulation with PCC to then administer tPA is not a guideline-supported strategy and would introduce additional delay. The correct approach is to skip tPA and evaluate directly for thrombectomy.

neurology.question: A 68-year-old man was last seen normal 10 hours ago and woke up with dense right hemiplegia and aphasia. NIHSS is 18. CT head shows no hemorrhage. CT perfusion shows a 12 mL ischemic core and 94 mL penumbra. CTA shows a left M1 occlusion. BP is 168/92. Which is the most appropriate intervention?

answers.text: IV tPA 0.9 mg/kg — patient is within 24 hours of symptom onset
Mechanical thrombectomy based on favorable perfusion imaging
Supportive care only — outside the standard 6-hour thrombectomy window
Hemicraniectomy for malignant MCA syndrome given stroke severity

explanation.correct: Correct. DAWN and DEFUSE-3 established extended window thrombectomy (up to 24 hours) for patients with LVO and favorable perfusion mismatch. Small core (12 mL) with large penumbra (94 mL) = ideal candidate. M1 occlusion with NIHSS 18 confirms the clinical-radiographic profile.
explanation.a: Incorrect. IV tPA is only approved within 4.5 hours of last known well. At 10 hours, tPA is absolutely contraindicated regardless of imaging findings. Wake-up strokes with unknown onset time are not exceptions to the tPA time window.
explanation.b: Correct. DAWN and DEFUSE-3 established extended window thrombectomy (up to 24 hours) for patients with LVO and favorable perfusion mismatch. Small core (12 mL) with large penumbra (94 mL) = ideal candidate. M1 occlusion with NIHSS 18 confirms the clinical-radiographic profile.
explanation.c: Incorrect. The standard 6-hour window has been extended to 24 hours for carefully selected patients based on perfusion imaging. This patient meets DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria. Supportive care alone abandons a treatable intervention with strong level 1 evidence.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Hemicraniectomy is performed for malignant MCA syndrome with cerebral edema and herniation — typically 24-48 hours after a completed infarct. This patient has a large penumbra suggesting salvageable tissue; thrombectomy should be attempted before considering decompressive surgery.

neurology.question: A 64-year-old woman presents with sudden severe headache, vomiting, and altered mental status. CT head shows a large right basal ganglia hemorrhage with intraventricular extension. GCS is 11. BP is 214/118. She is not on anticoagulation. Which BP management strategy is most appropriate in the first hour?

answers.text: Lower BP to <180/105 — permissive hypertension preserves cerebral perfusion pressure
Lower systolic BP to <140 mmHg using IV nicardipine or labetalol
Administer IV nitroprusside to rapidly normalize BP to <120/80
Hold antihypertensives — elevated BP is required to maintain cerebral perfusion in ICH

explanation.correct: Correct. AHA/ASA 2022 ICH guidelines recommend SBP <140 mmHg as a reasonable target for patients presenting with SBP 150-220 mmHg. IV nicardipine or labetalol provides smooth, titratable BP control. The INTERACT2 trial provided the primary evidence for this target.
explanation.a: Incorrect. The <180 mmHg permissive target is no longer the standard of care. Current AHA/ASA guidelines support a <140 mmHg SBP target as safe and reasonable. Withholding treatment at 214/118 risks ongoing hematoma expansion — the primary driver of early neurological deterioration in ICH.
explanation.b: Correct. AHA/ASA 2022 ICH guidelines recommend SBP <140 mmHg as a reasonable target for patients presenting with SBP 150-220 mmHg. IV nicardipine or labetalol provides smooth, titratable BP control. The INTERACT2 trial provided the primary evidence for this target.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Nitroprusside causes cerebral vasodilation, which increases ICP and can worsen neurological outcomes in ICH. Additionally, rapid normalization below 120/80 risks cerebral hypoperfusion. It is contraindicated in ICH.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Permissive hypertension in ICH leads to continued hematoma expansion — the primary cause of early neurological deterioration and death in ICH. The evidence from INTERACT2 and ATACH-2 clearly supports active BP reduction to <140 mmHg as safe.

neurology.question: A 58-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents with sudden right arm and leg weakness that completely resolved within 45 minutes. MRI-DWI shows no acute infarct. He is in normal sinus rhythm. BP 158/92. He takes aspirin 81 mg daily. ABCD2 score is 6. Which management is most appropriate in the first 24-48 hours?

answers.text: Discharge home with cardiology follow-up in 2 weeks for outpatient cardiac monitoring
Admit for workup including MRI, echocardiogram, and cardiac monitoring; add clopidogrel 75 mg for dual antiplatelet therapy
Admit for urgent workup; load with clopidogrel 600 mg and continue aspirin 81 mg for 21 days then reassess
Start warfarin for presumed cardioembolic TIA and admit for INR monitoring

explanation.correct: Correct. High-risk TIA (ABCD2 ≥4) warrants urgent admission, MRI, echocardiogram, and cardiac monitoring. POINT/CHANCE trial evidence supports clopidogrel 600 mg load + aspirin 81 mg for 21 days to reduce early stroke risk by approximately 25%.
explanation.a: Incorrect. ABCD2 score of 6 identifies a high-risk TIA with approximately 8% 2-day stroke risk. Discharging to outpatient follow-up in 2 weeks is dangerous — the highest risk of stroke after TIA is in the first 48-72 hours.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Admission and workup are correct, but the dual antiplatelet loading dose matters — clopidogrel 75 mg without a loading dose takes 5-7 days to reach full platelet inhibition. A 600 mg load achieves therapeutic inhibition within hours, which is critical in the immediate post-TIA period.
explanation.c: Correct. High-risk TIA (ABCD2 ≥4) warrants urgent admission, MRI, echocardiogram, and cardiac monitoring. POINT/CHANCE trial evidence supports clopidogrel 600 mg load + aspirin 81 mg for 21 days to reduce early stroke risk by approximately 25%.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Warfarin is indicated for cardioembolic TIA with confirmed atrial fibrillation or other high-risk cardiac sources. This patient is in normal sinus rhythm and no cardiac embolism has been established. Starting warfarin empirically without a clear indication is inappropriate and introduces bleeding risk.

neurology.question: A 32-year-old man is admitted to the neuro-ICU after a severe TBI (GCS 6). An ICP monitor shows ICP 28 mmHg. MAP is 82 mmHg. The patient is already on head-of-bed elevation to 30°, sedation, and normocapnia. Which intervention should be prioritized next?

answers.text: Administer IV mannitol 1 g/kg to reduce ICP
Increase MAP to achieve CPP ≥60 mmHg with vasopressors
Hyperventilate to PaCO₂ 25-30 mmHg to reduce cerebral blood volume
Administer 3% hypertonic saline 250 mL to reduce ICP

explanation.correct: Correct. CPP = 82 - 28 = 54 mmHg — below the BTF target of 60-70 mmHg. The most immediate intervention to restore cerebral perfusion is vasopressor support to raise MAP. This is faster and more direct than waiting for osmotherapy to reduce ICP.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Mannitol reduces ICP but takes 15-30 minutes to act and may transiently reduce MAP before ICP falls — potentially worsening CPP. With CPP already at 54 mmHg, raising MAP immediately is the priority. Osmotherapy follows, not leads.
explanation.b: Correct. CPP = 82 - 28 = 54 mmHg — below the BTF target of 60-70 mmHg. The most immediate intervention to restore cerebral perfusion is vasopressor support to raise MAP. This is faster and more direct than waiting for osmotherapy to reduce ICP.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Prophylactic hyperventilation to PaCO₂ 25-30 mmHg is contraindicated in TBI — it causes cerebral vasoconstriction that reduces CBF and worsens ischemia. Brief hyperventilation (PaCO₂ 35 mmHg) may be used as a temporizing measure for acute herniation only, not as a routine ICP management strategy.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Hypertonic saline reduces ICP via osmotic effect but takes time to act. Like mannitol, it does not address the immediate CPP deficit of 54 mmHg as rapidly as vasopressor support. MAP augmentation takes priority when CPP is critically low.

neurology.question: A 25-year-old man is brought to the ED after a motor vehicle collision. GCS is 7 (E2V2M3). CT head shows a right epidural hematoma with 8 mm midline shift and lens-shaped hyperdense collection. Right pupil is 6 mm and sluggishly reactive. Left pupil is 3 mm and brisk. BP 168/94, HR 52. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

answers.text: Administer IV mannitol 1 g/kg and repeat CT in 2 hours
Intubate, hyperventilate to PaCO₂ 30-35 mmHg, and emergent neurosurgical consultation for operative evacuation
Bilateral ICP monitor placement and medical ICP management
Administer 3% hypertonic saline 250 mL and transfer to neuro-ICU

explanation.correct: Correct. Epidural hematoma with herniation is a neurosurgical emergency with excellent outcomes if evacuated promptly. Intubation protects the airway and allows temporary hyperventilation to reduce ICP while definitive surgical treatment is arranged. Target PaCO₂ 35 mmHg — lower values risk ischemia.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Mannitol is a bridging measure while awaiting surgery, not a substitute for it. Repeating CT in 2 hours in a patient with active herniation (blown pupil, Cushing's triad) wastes time — this patient needs the OR within minutes.
explanation.b: Correct. Epidural hematoma with herniation is a neurosurgical emergency with excellent outcomes if evacuated promptly. Intubation protects the airway and allows temporary hyperventilation to reduce ICP while definitive surgical treatment is arranged. Target PaCO₂ 35 mmHg — lower values risk ischemia.
explanation.c: Incorrect. ICP monitoring without surgical evacuation is inappropriate when there is a surgically accessible hematoma causing herniation. Medical management alone cannot reverse structural compression from an EDH. This delay risks permanent herniation and death.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Hypertonic saline is a temporizing measure, not definitive treatment. Transfer to neuro-ICU without operative planning wastes critical minutes. The neurosurgeon must be activated immediately and the patient taken to the OR.

neurology.question: A 55-year-old woman with a large subarachnoid hemorrhage (Hunt-Hess Grade III) is day 7 post-bleed. She develops new confusion and right arm weakness not present on admission. CT head shows no new hemorrhage and the aneurysm was coiled on day 1. TCD shows MCA velocities of 210 cm/s bilaterally. Which is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate management?

answers.text: Hydrocephalus; place external ventricular drain
Cerebral vasospasm; initiate induced hypertension and confirm with CTA or DSA
Re-bleeding from the coiled aneurysm; emergent CTA and neurosurgical consultation
Hyponatremia from SIADH; restrict free water and recheck sodium

explanation.correct: Correct. Day 7 post-SAH + new focal deficit + TCD velocities >200 cm/s = severe cerebral vasospasm with DCI. Induced hypertension is the immediate intervention. CTA confirms the diagnosis and DSA allows endovascular treatment if hypertension fails. This is the classic vasospasm window.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Hydrocephalus from SAH typically presents earlier (days 1-3) with progressive obtundation, upward gaze palsy, and CT showing enlarged ventricles. TCD velocities of 210 cm/s are diagnostic of vasospasm, not hydrocephalus.
explanation.b: Correct. Day 7 post-SAH + new focal deficit + TCD velocities >200 cm/s = severe cerebral vasospasm with DCI. Induced hypertension is the immediate intervention. CTA confirms the diagnosis and DSA allows endovascular treatment if hypertension fails. This is the classic vasospasm window.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Re-bleeding after coil embolization is rare with a well-packed coil. CT shows no new hemorrhage, ruling out re-bleed. TCD velocities are diagnostic of vasospasm, not re-bleeding. Re-bleeding would present with sudden severe headache and CT evidence of new hemorrhage.
explanation.d: Incorrect. SAH commonly causes cerebral salt wasting (CSW) with hyponatremia — but the treatment is isotonic volume replacement, not free water restriction (which would worsen cerebral salt wasting). Hyponatremia should be checked but does not explain TCD velocities of 210 cm/s. Vasospasm is the priority diagnosis here.

neurology.question: A 44-year-old woman with no seizure history presents with generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity that has been ongoing for 8 minutes. EMS administered lorazepam 4 mg IV en route without effect. She arrives still seizing. Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

answers.text: Repeat lorazepam 4 mg IV — the initial dose may not have been adequate
Administer levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV (max 4500 mg) or fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV
Proceed to rapid sequence intubation and propofol infusion
Administer IV dextrose 50% and thiamine 100 mg empirically before further anticonvulsants

explanation.correct: Correct. Benzodiazepine-refractory status epilepticus requires second-line antiepileptic therapy. Levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, and valproate are all guideline-recommended options with comparable efficacy per the ESETT trial. Agent selection is based on clinical context.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Lorazepam 4 mg IV is an adequate first-line benzodiazepine dose. Repeating the same agent after failure primarily risks respiratory depression and sedation without additional anticonvulsant benefit. The algorithm advances to second-line agents after benzodiazepine failure.
explanation.b: Correct. Benzodiazepine-refractory status epilepticus requires second-line antiepileptic therapy. Levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, and valproate are all guideline-recommended options with comparable efficacy per the ESETT trial. Agent selection is based on clinical context.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Intubation with propofol or midazolam infusion is third-line therapy for refractory status epilepticus — after failure of both first-line (benzodiazepine) and second-line (levetiracetam/fosphenytoin/valproate) agents. Proceeding directly to intubation skips the evidence-based second-line step.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Empiric dextrose and thiamine are appropriate for undifferentiated seizure with possible hypoglycemia or Wernicke's, but a fingerstick glucose should be checked first. In ongoing convulsive status epilepticus, anticonvulsant escalation takes priority — dextrose/thiamine can be given simultaneously but should not delay second-line AED therapy.

neurology.question: A 67-year-old man with a history of ischemic stroke is found unresponsive by family. GCS is 9. He had a witnessed generalized seizure 30 minutes prior that lasted 2 minutes and self-terminated. He is now motionless but his eyes are open and he is blinking inconsistently. CT head shows no acute changes. Which is the most important next diagnostic step?

answers.text: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging to evaluate for new stroke
Continuous EEG monitoring to evaluate for non-convulsive status epilepticus
LP to rule out meningitis given altered mental status and seizure
Obtain serum AED levels and observe — this is likely postictal state

explanation.correct: Correct. The clinical presentation — altered mental status after a seizure with subtle motor phenomena (inconsistent blinking) — is classic for NCSE. Continuous EEG is the gold standard diagnostic test. If NCSE is confirmed, treatment follows the same algorithm as convulsive status epilepticus.
explanation.a: Incorrect. MRI is important to evaluate for new ischemia in a stroke patient with new seizure, but it does not diagnose NCSE. If NCSE is present and untreated while awaiting MRI, ongoing seizure activity causes progressive neuronal injury. EEG takes priority.
explanation.b: Correct. The clinical presentation — altered mental status after a seizure with subtle motor phenomena (inconsistent blinking) — is classic for NCSE. Continuous EEG is the gold standard diagnostic test. If NCSE is confirmed, treatment follows the same algorithm as convulsive status epilepticus.
explanation.c: Incorrect. LP may be appropriate later to evaluate for infectious or inflammatory cause of seizure, but it does not diagnose or treat NCSE. It should not be prioritized before EEG in a patient with a high clinical suspicion for NCSE. Additionally, a seizure itself can transiently raise CSF WBC.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Postictal state is a diagnosis of exclusion. A 30-minute postictal state with eye opening and inconsistent blinking is atypical — NCSE must be ruled out first. Observing without EEG risks missing treatable status epilepticus. AED levels are useful but do not diagnose NCSE.

neurology.question: A 31-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation presents with new-onset seizure. BP is 178/112 and she has 3+ proteinuria. She has a persistent headache and visual changes. She seizes again in the ED. Labs show platelets 88,000, AST 220, ALT 198. Which is the most appropriate anticonvulsant and definitive management?

answers.text: Lorazepam 4 mg IV for acute seizure control, then levetiracetam for prophylaxis; plan induction after 36 weeks
IV magnesium sulfate 4-6 g loading dose for seizure control and prophylaxis; urgent delivery
Fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV; control BP with IV hydralazine and observe
IV diazepam for acute seizure; start oral labetalol and admit for monitoring until 37 weeks

explanation.correct: Correct. Magnesium sulfate 4-6 g IV loading dose is the standard treatment for eclamptic seizures. It is also used for prophylaxis in severe preeclampsia. HELLP syndrome with eclampsia at 34 weeks requires urgent delivery — maternal stabilization first, then delivery.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Levetiracetam is not established for eclampsia prophylaxis. Delaying delivery until 36 weeks in active eclampsia with HELLP syndrome is dangerous — the only definitive treatment is delivery. Continuing the pregnancy worsens maternal and fetal outcomes.
explanation.b: Correct. Magnesium sulfate 4-6 g IV loading dose is the standard treatment for eclamptic seizures. It is also used for prophylaxis in severe preeclampsia. HELLP syndrome with eclampsia at 34 weeks requires urgent delivery — maternal stabilization first, then delivery.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Fosphenytoin is ineffective for eclampsia — the Magpie trial definitively showed magnesium is superior to phenytoin for preventing recurrent eclamptic seizures. Observation without delivery in HELLP syndrome is inappropriate.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Diazepam terminates the acute seizure but does not prevent recurrence as effectively as magnesium in eclampsia. Oral labetalol and observation until 37 weeks is dangerous — HELLP syndrome with eclampsia is an obstetric emergency requiring delivery.

neurology.question: A 38-year-old woman presents with 10 days of progressive ascending leg weakness that has reached her hips. She reports tingling in her feet. Deep tendon reflexes are absent bilaterally. CSF shows protein 180 mg/dL with WBC 4 cells/mm³. She can still ambulate with support. NIF is -22 cmH₂O and FVC is 1.6 L (52% predicted). Which is the most appropriate next step regarding respiratory management?

answers.text: Discharge with close outpatient neurology follow-up given preserved ambulation
Admit to monitored unit; intubate now given NIF meets threshold criteria
Admit to ICU for serial respiratory monitoring using the 20-30-40 rule
Start IVIG immediately and recheck NIF after first dose before deciding on intubation

explanation.correct: Correct. NIF -22 cmH₂O meets the -30 cmH₂O monitoring threshold (less negative = weaker). ICU admission for serial NIF, FVC, and MEP monitoring every 4-6 hours is the standard approach. GBS patients can deteriorate rapidly — early elective intubation before crisis is the goal. IVIG should be initiated simultaneously.
explanation.a: Incorrect. GBS with NIF -22 cmH₂O (meeting the <-30 cmH₂O monitoring threshold), areflexia, and ascending weakness to the hips requires inpatient ICU-level monitoring. Outpatient follow-up risks unwitnessed respiratory failure.
explanation.b: Incorrect. NIF -22 cmH₂O meets the 20-30-40 monitoring threshold and warrants ICU admission and close monitoring, but elective intubation timing is guided by trajectory, FVC trend, bulbar symptoms, and clinical status — not the NIF threshold alone. This patient can still ambulate and FVC is 52% predicted. Serial monitoring with a low threshold for elective intubation is appropriate.
explanation.c: Correct. NIF -22 cmH₂O meets the -30 cmH₂O monitoring threshold (less negative = weaker). ICU admission for serial NIF, FVC, and MEP monitoring every 4-6 hours is the standard approach. GBS patients can deteriorate rapidly — early elective intubation before crisis is the goal. IVIG should be initiated simultaneously.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IVIG should be started promptly as disease-modifying therapy for GBS, but respiratory monitoring decisions cannot wait for IVIG response — it takes days to weeks to show effect. Respiratory management and IVIG initiation proceed simultaneously, not sequentially.

neurology.question: A 52-year-old woman with myasthenia gravis presents with 2 days of worsening dyspnea and dysphagia. She has been on pyridostigmine 60 mg TID and prednisone 20 mg daily. NIF is -24 cmH₂O, FVC 1.1 L (38% predicted). She is diaphoretic with excessive secretions and miosis. HR is 54, BP 108/72. Which is the most important distinction to make before initiating treatment?

answers.text: Whether she needs IVIG or plasmapheresis for myasthenic crisis
Whether this is myasthenic crisis or cholinergic crisis, as treatment differs fundamentally
Whether to intubate now or attempt BiPAP first given her FVC
Whether to increase pyridostigmine dose given inadequate disease control

explanation.correct: Correct. Cholinergic signs (SLUDGE: excessive secretions, diaphoresis, miosis, bradycardia) suggest cholinergic crisis from pyridostigmine excess. Myasthenic crisis has weakness without cholinergic features. The distinction is critical — treatment is opposite: withhold anticholinesterases in cholinergic crisis vs. escalate immunotherapy in myasthenic crisis.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While IVIG vs. plasmapheresis is an important decision in confirmed myasthenic crisis, it is premature before distinguishing myasthenic from cholinergic crisis. Giving IVIG in cholinergic crisis does not address the underlying cause and delays appropriate management.
explanation.b: Correct. Cholinergic signs (SLUDGE: excessive secretions, diaphoresis, miosis, bradycardia) suggest cholinergic crisis from pyridostigmine excess. Myasthenic crisis has weakness without cholinergic features. The distinction is critical — treatment is opposite: withhold anticholinesterases in cholinergic crisis vs. escalate immunotherapy in myasthenic crisis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While respiratory management is urgent (NIF -24, FVC 38%), intubation decisions can be made regardless of which crisis is present. The distinction between crises must be made because it determines whether to hold or continue pyridostigmine — giving more pyridostigmine in cholinergic crisis worsens the respiratory failure.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Increasing pyridostigmine in a patient with cholinergic signs would be dangerous. Pyridostigmine dose escalation in cholinergic crisis increases acetylcholine at all synapses, worsening secretions, bradycardia, and paradoxically worsening weakness through depolarizing blockade.

neurology.question: A 22-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay after a diving accident. He has complete loss of motor and sensory function below the nipple line bilaterally. BP is 72/46, HR 54, temperature 36.1°C. Extremities are warm and flushed. Which hemodynamic management is most appropriate?

answers.text: 2L NS bolus — likely hemorrhagic shock from associated injuries
Norepinephrine infusion targeting MAP 85-90 mmHg for spinal cord perfusion
Atropine 0.5 mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia and dopamine for BP support
Methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg IV bolus per NASCIS protocol

explanation.correct: Correct. Neurogenic shock requires vasopressor support, not primarily volume. MAP 85-90 mmHg target optimizes spinal cord perfusion in the acute injury period. Norepinephrine is preferred over phenylephrine (pure alpha — worsens bradycardia) in neurogenic shock.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Warm extremities and bradycardia distinguish neurogenic shock from hemorrhagic shock. Fluid resuscitation without vasopressors will not correct the vasodilatory component of neurogenic shock and may worsen spinal cord edema with fluid overload.
explanation.b: Correct. Neurogenic shock requires vasopressor support, not primarily volume. MAP 85-90 mmHg target optimizes spinal cord perfusion in the acute injury period. Norepinephrine is preferred over phenylephrine (pure alpha — worsens bradycardia) in neurogenic shock.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Atropine may temporarily address bradycardia but does not treat the vasodilatory component of neurogenic shock. Dopamine has unpredictable dosing requirements and tachyphylaxis. Norepinephrine provides more reliable alpha and beta support.
explanation.d: Incorrect. High-dose methylprednisolone (NASCIS protocol) is no longer recommended for acute SCI — subsequent analysis showed no meaningful neurological benefit and significant harm (infections, GI bleeding, sepsis). Major spine surgery societies have removed this recommendation from guidelines.

neurology.question: A 34-year-old man with a T4 complete spinal cord injury from 2 years ago is admitted for a surgical procedure. During recovery he suddenly develops severe headache rated 10/10, BP 218/124, HR 48, and profuse diaphoresis above the nipple line with flushed skin. He reports abdominal cramping. His Foley catheter bag is empty. Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

answers.text: Administer IV labetalol 20 mg for hypertensive urgency
Sit the patient upright and identify and remove the triggering stimulus
Administer IV morphine for pain management and BP reduction
Apply cooling blankets above the level of injury and monitor BP

explanation.correct: Correct. First-line treatment of autonomic dysreflexia is always: (1) sit patient upright, (2) identify and eliminate the triggering stimulus. Empty Foley = check for kinked tubing, blocked catheter, or bladder outlet obstruction. Relieve bladder distension with straight catheter if needed. Most episodes resolve with this approach alone.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Antihypertensives are used if trigger removal fails to normalize BP, but medication is second-line. Treating BP without removing the trigger is like treating fever without antibiotics — the cause persists. Furthermore, if the trigger is removed and medication was already given, rebound hypotension can occur.
explanation.b: Correct. First-line treatment of autonomic dysreflexia is always: (1) sit patient upright, (2) identify and eliminate the triggering stimulus. Empty Foley = check for kinked tubing, blocked catheter, or bladder outlet obstruction. Relieve bladder distension with straight catheter if needed. Most episodes resolve with this approach alone.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Morphine does not treat the underlying mechanism of autonomic dysreflexia and may mask symptoms of ongoing noxious stimulus. The trigger — not the symptoms — must be eliminated.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Cooling is not a treatment for autonomic dysreflexia. The diaphoresis and flushing above the injury level are symptoms of the dysreflexic response, not hyperthermia. Treating symptoms without removing the trigger will not resolve the episode.

neurology.question: A 45-year-old previously healthy man presents with 12 hours of fever, severe headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness. GCS is 14. CT head is normal. Which is the most appropriate sequence of management?

answers.text: Lumbar puncture first, then empiric antibiotics based on CSF results
Empiric antibiotics and dexamethasone immediately, then lumbar puncture
MRI brain, then lumbar puncture, then antibiotics based on organism identified
Empiric antibiotics alone — steroids are not indicated until organism identified

explanation.correct: Correct. CT is normal, clearing for LP. Start empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone 2g IV + vancomycin + dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV) and perform LP immediately. Dexamethasone given before or with the first antibiotic dose reduces inflammatory complications. Each hour of antibiotic delay increases mortality by approximately 7%.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While LP before antibiotics is ideal to maximize CSF yield, antibiotic initiation must not be delayed >30-45 minutes while awaiting LP. The question does not indicate any barrier to rapid LP — in this case LP and antibiotics should be nearly simultaneous, with antibiotics taking priority if any delay.
explanation.b: Correct. CT is normal, clearing for LP. Start empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone 2g IV + vancomycin + dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV) and perform LP immediately. Dexamethasone given before or with the first antibiotic dose reduces inflammatory complications. Each hour of antibiotic delay increases mortality by approximately 7%.
explanation.c: Incorrect. MRI adds no value in the acute management of suspected bacterial meningitis and introduces critical time delay. If CT is normal and there are no contraindications, LP should proceed. MRI is not required before LP in this clinical scenario.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Dexamethasone is recommended for all adults with suspected bacterial meningitis, given before or with the first antibiotic dose. Evidence from the van de Beek trial shows steroids reduce complications including deafness, particularly in pneumococcal meningitis. Waiting for organism identification defeats the purpose.

neurology.question: A 28-year-old immunocompetent woman is admitted with 5 days of fever, behavioral changes, and new-onset seizure. MRI shows T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the right temporal lobe and insular cortex. CSF: protein 88 mg/dL, WBC 62 (80% lymphocytes), glucose 62 (serum 98), RBC 12, HSV PCR pending. Which management should NOT be delayed pending HSV PCR results?

answers.text: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for bacterial meningitis coverage
Continuous EEG monitoring for subclinical seizures
Repeat MRI in 48 hours to assess for progression

explanation.correct: Correct. IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV every 8 hours (adjusted for renal function) must be started immediately in suspected HSV encephalitis. Waiting for PCR confirmation exposes the patient to preventable death and disability. The clinical, CSF, and MRI findings make HSV encephalitis the working diagnosis — treat now.
explanation.a: Correct. IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV every 8 hours (adjusted for renal function) must be started immediately in suspected HSV encephalitis. Waiting for PCR confirmation exposes the patient to preventable death and disability. The clinical, CSF, and MRI findings make HSV encephalitis the working diagnosis — treat now.
explanation.b: Incorrect. The CSF profile (lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, RBCs, temporal lobe MRI changes) is consistent with viral encephalitis, not bacterial meningitis. While empiric bacterial coverage can be included initially if there is uncertainty, it is not the most critical intervention here — acyclovir is.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Continuous EEG is important in encephalitis with seizures to detect subclinical status epilepticus, but it does not treat the underlying HSV infection. It is a monitoring tool, not a time-sensitive treatment. Acyclovir initiation takes priority.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Repeat MRI may show progression and guides prognosis, but it does not treat HSV encephalitis. Imaging is secondary to initiating antiviral therapy. A 48-hour delay in imaging while acyclovir is already running is appropriate.

neurology.question: A 44-year-old man with a massive intracerebral hemorrhage is being evaluated for brain death. Prerequisites have been confirmed: normothermia, normotension, euglycemia, and no CNS depressants. Clinical examination shows absent brainstem reflexes bilaterally. An apnea test is being performed. After disconnecting the ventilator with passive O₂ insufflation at 6L/min, PaCO₂ rises from 40 to 62 mmHg over 8 minutes. No respiratory effort is observed. Which statement is correct?

answers.text: The apnea test is inconclusive — PaCO₂ must reach 80 mmHg to confirm brain death
The apnea test confirms brain death — PaCO₂ ≥60 mmHg with no respiratory effort meets criteria
The apnea test must be repeated in 6 hours by a different examiner to confirm brain death
Ancillary testing (EEG or cerebral angiography) is required to confirm brain death before the apnea test results are valid

explanation.correct: Correct. PaCO₂ 62 mmHg (≥60 threshold met) with 22 mmHg rise above baseline (≥20 mmHg threshold met) and no respiratory effort = positive apnea test confirming absent brainstem respiratory drive. Combined with absent brainstem reflexes and confirmed prerequisites, this meets AAN criteria for brain death.
explanation.a: Incorrect. The threshold is PaCO₂ ≥60 mmHg OR ≥20 mmHg rise above baseline — not 80 mmHg. At 62 mmHg with a 22 mmHg rise and no respiratory effort, both AAN criteria are met. The apnea test is conclusive.
explanation.b: Correct. PaCO₂ 62 mmHg (≥60 threshold met) with 22 mmHg rise above baseline (≥20 mmHg threshold met) and no respiratory effort = positive apnea test confirming absent brainstem respiratory drive. Combined with absent brainstem reflexes and confirmed prerequisites, this meets AAN criteria for brain death.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Many jurisdictions and institutions require two examinations by different physicians, but this is institution-dependent and varies by state law. The apnea test itself does not need to be repeated — a single confirmatory apnea test meeting criteria is sufficient per AAN guidelines. The repeat examination requirement applies to the full neurological exam, not specifically the apnea test.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Ancillary testing (EEG, cerebral angiography, nuclear scan) is used when the clinical examination or apnea test cannot be completed due to confounders — not as a mandatory requirement when the clinical examination is complete and valid. AAN guidelines state brain death can be determined by clinical criteria alone when all prerequisites are met.

neurology.question: A 19-year-old woman is declared brain dead after a hypertensive emergency. Her family requests that life support be continued for religious reasons. She had no documented advance directive. The family states she would have wanted continued support based on their conversations. Which statement best reflects the appropriate response?

answers.text: Brain death is legal death — the team is not obligated to continue mechanical ventilation, but should engage ethics consultation and work compassionately with the family
Family consent is required before withdrawing ventilator support from a brain-dead patient
Continue ventilator support indefinitely out of respect for the family's religious beliefs
Transfer to a facility willing to continue treatment given the family's objections

explanation.correct: Correct. Brain death = legal death in the US. No obligation exists to continue mechanical ventilation after brain death declaration. However, compassion, ethics consultation, and clear communication with the family are essential. Some states have specific accommodation requirements for religious objections that should be reviewed.
explanation.a: Correct. Brain death = legal death in the US. No obligation exists to continue mechanical ventilation after brain death declaration. However, compassion, ethics consultation, and clear communication with the family are essential. Some states have specific accommodation requirements for religious objections that should be reviewed.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Family consent is not required to discontinue mechanical ventilation after brain death. The patient is legally dead — consent to withdraw support applies to living patients, not brain-dead individuals. This is a fundamental distinction between brain death and other end-of-life scenarios.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Continuing ventilator support indefinitely is not an ethically or legally required response to family wishes after brain death. It monopolizes ICU resources, may expose staff to moral distress, and does not benefit the patient (who is deceased). Time-limited accommodations may be appropriate in some institutional/state contexts.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Transfer is not an obligation when the patient is legally dead. No facility is required to provide ongoing mechanical ventilation to a brain-dead individual. While compassionate discussions about organ donation and transition of care are appropriate, mandatory transfer is not a recognized requirement.

neurology.question: A 78-year-old man is on post-operative day 2 after elective hip replacement. He becomes agitated overnight, pulling at his IV lines, and is disoriented to place and time. He passed a CAM screen pre-operatively. His temperature is 37.8°C, HR 96, BP 138/82. Urinalysis is clear. Labs are within normal limits. He is on oxycodone 5 mg q6h, metoprolol 25 mg BID, and aspirin. Which is the most appropriate initial intervention?

answers.text: Administer haloperidol 5 mg IV for rapid sedation and behavioral control
Add lorazepam 1 mg IV PRN for anxiety and agitation
Implement non-pharmacologic delirium interventions and review medications for contributing factors
Order CT head to rule out intracranial pathology before initiating treatment

explanation.correct: Correct. Non-pharmacologic interventions are first-line for post-operative delirium per PADIS guidelines. Review oxycodone (opioids commonly cause delirium in elderly), ensure adequate lighting, reorientation, early mobilization, and optimize sleep. Treat reversible causes before reaching for medications.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Haloperidol does not reduce delirium duration or severity in ICU patients per HOPE-ICU and MIND-USA trials. It should be reserved for severe agitation posing safety risk, not as routine first-line management. In the elderly, antipsychotics carry risks of QT prolongation, extrapyramidal effects, and aspiration.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Benzodiazepines are independently associated with worsening delirium and are contraindicated as first-line treatment for non-alcohol withdrawal delirium in elderly patients. Lorazepam would likely worsen the confusion.
explanation.c: Correct. Non-pharmacologic interventions are first-line for post-operative delirium per PADIS guidelines. Review oxycodone (opioids commonly cause delirium in elderly), ensure adequate lighting, reorientation, early mobilization, and optimize sleep. Treat reversible causes before reaching for medications.
explanation.d: Incorrect. CT head is not indicated in typical post-operative delirium without focal neurological signs, head trauma history, or sudden severe onset suggesting intracranial pathology. The clinical picture is classic hyperactive post-operative delirium in an elderly patient — neuroimaging would not change initial management.

neurology.question: A 48-year-old man with alcohol use disorder is admitted after being found unresponsive. He is confused and tremulous. HR 118, BP 154/96, diaphoretic. He reports his last drink was 36 hours ago. CIWA-Ar score is 18. He then has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure in the ED. Which is the most appropriate pharmacologic treatment?

answers.text: Haloperidol 5 mg IV for agitation control and seizure prevention
Lorazepam 2-4 mg IV with symptom-triggered dosing per CIWA-Ar score
Fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV for seizure prophylaxis
Propofol infusion for sedation and seizure control

explanation.correct: Correct. Benzodiazepines are the gold standard for alcohol withdrawal. Lorazepam 2-4 mg IV for acute seizure, then symptom-triggered CIWA-Ar dosing prevents progression to DTs. Thiamine 100 mg IV should be given concurrently. Phenobarbital is an alternative in refractory cases.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Haloperidol lowers the seizure threshold and does not treat the underlying GABA-A receptor dysregulation of alcohol withdrawal. It is contraindicated as monotherapy for alcohol withdrawal — it may control agitation but will not prevent seizures or DTs.
explanation.b: Correct. Benzodiazepines are the gold standard for alcohol withdrawal. Lorazepam 2-4 mg IV for acute seizure, then symptom-triggered CIWA-Ar dosing prevents progression to DTs. Thiamine 100 mg IV should be given concurrently. Phenobarbital is an alternative in refractory cases.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Phenytoin/fosphenytoin does not prevent alcohol withdrawal seizures — it acts on sodium channels, not GABA-A receptors. Multiple studies confirm phenytoin is ineffective for this indication. This is a classic exam trap.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Propofol may be used in ICU for refractory alcohol withdrawal requiring intubation, but it is not first-line for CIWA score 18 with a single seizure. Benzodiazepines remain first-line. Propofol use for alcohol withdrawal is reserved for benzodiazepine-refractory severe DTs requiring mechanical ventilation.

neurology.question: A 28-year-old man with severe TBI (GCS 6) on continuous ICP monitoring shows ICP 36 mmHg, MAP 88 mmHg (CPP 52 mmHg). He has been positioned at 30°, sedated with propofol and fentanyl, and is normocapnic (PaCO₂ 38). Serum osmolality is 298 mOsm/kg, sodium 138. His ICP has been >20 for 90 minutes. Which is the most appropriate tier 2 intervention?

answers.text: Hyperventilate to PaCO₂ 28-30 mmHg as definitive ICP treatment
Administer 23.4% hypertonic saline 30 mL IV bolus via central line
Start continuous midazolam infusion at 5 mg/hr for deeper sedation
Perform bilateral decompressive craniectomy emergently

explanation.correct: Correct. 23.4% HTS 30 mL via central line is appropriate tier 2 osmotherapy. It rapidly reduces ICP by creating a serum-brain osmotic gradient. More effective than 3% HTS bolus and avoids the intravascular volume depletion of mannitol. Monitor serum sodium and osmolality — target Na 145-155, osmolality <320.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Prophylactic hyperventilation (PaCO₂ <35) is contraindicated in TBI (BTF guidelines). It causes cerebral vasoconstriction, reducing CBF and risking cerebral ischemia. Brief hyperventilation (PaCO₂ 35) can be used as a BRIDGE during acute herniation events but is not definitive treatment and should not be maintained.
explanation.b: Correct. 23.4% HTS 30 mL via central line is appropriate tier 2 osmotherapy. It rapidly reduces ICP by creating a serum-brain osmotic gradient. More effective than 3% HTS bolus and avoids the intravascular volume depletion of mannitol. Monitor serum sodium and osmolality — target Na 145-155, osmolality <320.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Deep sedation with midazolam may reduce ICP by decreasing CMRO₂ but midazolam accumulates, prolongs mechanical ventilation, and worsens neurological assessment. Propofol is preferred for ICP sedation when deeper sedation is needed due to shorter half-life and easier titration.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Decompressive craniectomy (DC) is a tier 3 intervention for refractory ICP (ICP >20-25 mmHg despite maximal medical therapy). The DECRA trial showed DC reduced ICP but increased unfavorable outcomes. DC is reserved for cases failing all medical management, not as second-line intervention.

neurology.question: A 74-year-old man with atrial fibrillation develops a left MCA stroke. CT shows a large left hemisphere infarct involving 2/3 of the MCA territory. He is now 36 hours post-stroke with declining consciousness (GCS 9, down from 14). Repeat CT shows massive cerebral edema with 8 mm midline shift and early transtentorial herniation signs. He has a living will stating he wants 'all reasonable measures.' His family agrees. Which intervention is most appropriate?

answers.text: IV mannitol 1 g/kg and hyperventilation to PaCO₂ 30 mmHg definitively
Decompressive hemicraniectomy — should be offered to patients ≤60 years, but in a 74-year-old it is controversial and the evidence base is weaker
Full-dose anticoagulation to prevent thrombus extension
Surgical drainage of the cerebral edema via burr hole

explanation.correct: Correct. Decompressive hemicraniectomy within 48 hours is the evidence-based intervention for malignant MCA syndrome. Evidence is strongest for age <60 (HAMLET, DESTINY, DECIMAL). DESTINY II showed benefit in older patients but with more survivors having severe disability — requiring careful family counseling. Given living will and family agreement, this should be offered.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Mannitol and hyperventilation are temporizing measures for acute herniation — not definitive treatment. Prolonged hyperventilation (PaCO₂ <35) causes cerebral vasoconstriction and ischemia. These are bridges while definitive surgical decision is made, not standalone treatments.
explanation.b: Correct. Decompressive hemicraniectomy within 48 hours is the evidence-based intervention for malignant MCA syndrome. Evidence is strongest for age <60 (HAMLET, DESTINY, DECIMAL). DESTINY II showed benefit in older patients but with more survivors having severe disability — requiring careful family counseling. Given living will and family agreement, this should be offered.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Full anticoagulation in a large ischemic stroke with edema risks hemorrhagic transformation, which would be catastrophic. Anticoagulation is not indicated acutely in large MCA infarctions with edema and herniation signs.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Burr holes drain hematomas or loculated CSF collections, not diffuse cerebral edema. Cytotoxic edema (as in stroke) requires removal of the swollen brain's external constraint (craniectomy), not drainage of fluid from within the brain.

neurology.question: A 77-year-old man with chronic kidney disease (GFR 28) is admitted for hip fracture repair. On POD 3 he develops agitated delirium, picking at IV lines. His medications include meperidine 75 mg IV q4h PRN for pain (given 8 times in past 24 hours), ondansetron, and lorazepam PRN. Which medication change is the highest priority?

answers.text: Add haloperidol 2 mg IV q6h for delirium management
Discontinue meperidine — normeperidine accumulates in renal failure causing seizures and neurotoxicity
Increase lorazepam dose to achieve sedation and prevent self-harm
Add quetiapine 25 mg BID for nighttime sedation and delirium prevention

explanation.correct: Correct. Meperidine is on the Beers Criteria list as contraindicated in older adults and in renal failure. Normeperidine accumulates in CKD (GFR 28), causing CNS excitatory toxicity. Stopping meperidine is the most urgent intervention. Replace with hydromorphone 0.2-0.4 mg IV q4h PRN (dose-adjusted for CKD).
explanation.a: Incorrect. While haloperidol may be used for severe agitated delirium, the root cause here is meperidine/normeperidine toxicity. Adding haloperidol without removing the causative agent will not resolve the delirium. Treating delirium pharmacologically without identifying the iatrogenic cause is below standard of care.
explanation.b: Correct. Meperidine is on the Beers Criteria list as contraindicated in older adults and in renal failure. Normeperidine accumulates in CKD (GFR 28), causing CNS excitatory toxicity. Stopping meperidine is the most urgent intervention. Replace with hydromorphone 0.2-0.4 mg IV q4h PRN (dose-adjusted for CKD).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Benzodiazepines (lorazepam) are independently associated with worsening delirium in older adults and are on the Beers Criteria list for ICU delirium. Increasing lorazepam in a patient with agitated delirium from opioid metabolite toxicity would worsen outcomes.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Quetiapine and other antipsychotics have not been shown to reduce delirium duration or severity (MIND-USA trial). Adding a sedating antipsychotic without removing the causative agent is not appropriate first-line management.

neurology.question: A 45-year-old woman on long-term aminoglycoside therapy for osteomyelitis develops progressive weakness. EMG shows decremental response to repetitive nerve stimulation at 3 Hz. Edrophonium (Tensilon) test is positive. Which best explains the mechanism and most appropriate management change?

answers.text: Aminoglycoside-induced ototoxicity affecting vestibular-cerebellar function; discontinue aminoglycosides
Aminoglycoside-induced neuromuscular blockade by blocking presynaptic calcium channels; discontinue aminoglycoside and administer calcium gluconate
Autoimmune myasthenia gravis triggered by aminoglycoside; start pyridostigmine and prednisone
Aminoglycoside-induced Guillain-Barré syndrome; IVIG and plasmapheresis

explanation.correct: Correct. Aminoglycosides block presynaptic calcium channels at the NMJ, reducing ACh release. This creates a MG-like syndrome with decremental response and positive Tensilon test. Calcium gluconate IV reverses the presynaptic calcium channel blockade. Discontinuing the offending aminoglycoside is essential. Patients with known MG are at highest risk.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Aminoglycoside ototoxicity affects cochlear hair cells and vestibular apparatus — causing sensorineural hearing loss and vertigo, not weakness. The EMG decremental response is a neuromuscular junction finding, not a vestibular/cerebellar finding.
explanation.b: Correct. Aminoglycosides block presynaptic calcium channels at the NMJ, reducing ACh release. This creates a MG-like syndrome with decremental response and positive Tensilon test. Calcium gluconate IV reverses the presynaptic calcium channel blockade. Discontinuing the offending aminoglycoside is essential. Patients with known MG are at highest risk.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While aminoglycosides can unmask or worsen underlying MG, true de novo aminoglycoside-triggered autoimmune MG is extremely rare. The acute presentation in the context of aminoglycoside therapy, reversible with calcium, is better explained by direct pharmacological NMJ blockade rather than autoimmune disease.
explanation.d: Incorrect. GBS is an autoimmune peripheral polyneuropathy (ascending weakness, areflexia, albumino-cytologic dissociation on CSF). It shows different EMG pattern (conduction velocity slowing, F-wave abnormalities) and is not caused by aminoglycosides. IVIG/plasmapheresis are GBS treatments, not aminoglycoside NMJ blockade.

neurology.question: A patient is undergoing brain death evaluation after massive subarachnoid hemorrhage. Clinical examination shows no brainstem reflexes. During the apnea test, the patient develops hemodynamic instability — BP drops to 72/40 and SpO₂ falls to 84% before PaCO₂ reaches the target threshold. The test is aborted. Which is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Restart the apnea test immediately after brief resuscitation — it must be completed to confirm brain death
Declare brain death based on clinical examination alone — apnea test is not required if it cannot be safely completed
Proceed with ancillary testing (EEG, cerebral angiography, or nuclear medicine scan) to confirm brain death
Conclude the patient cannot be declared brain dead — incomplete apnea test means brain death determination is impossible

explanation.correct: Correct. Per AAN 2010 guidelines: if the apnea test cannot be completed safely, ancillary testing (EEG, cerebral angiography, radionuclide scan, or TCD) can confirm brain death. These tests demonstrate absence of cerebral electrical activity or cerebral blood flow, completing the determination.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Repeating an apnea test that caused hemodynamic compromise and hypoxemia is dangerous and not required by guidelines. Patient safety takes precedence — if the test cannot be completed safely, proceed to ancillary testing.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Clinical examination alone (absent brainstem reflexes) is necessary but not sufficient for brain death declaration without a completed apnea test. The apnea test demonstrates absence of medullary respiratory center function. Its absence must be compensated by ancillary testing, not ignored.
explanation.c: Correct. Per AAN 2010 guidelines: if the apnea test cannot be completed safely, ancillary testing (EEG, cerebral angiography, radionuclide scan, or TCD) can confirm brain death. These tests demonstrate absence of cerebral electrical activity or cerebral blood flow, completing the determination.
explanation.d: Incorrect. An aborted apnea test does not prevent brain death determination. AAN guidelines explicitly address this scenario — ancillary testing fills the gap when the apnea test cannot be completed. Brain death can be declared based on examination plus ancillary testing when the apnea test is not safely completable.

neurology.question: A 55-year-old woman is admitted for new-onset seizures, psychosis, and behavioral changes over 6 weeks. MRI shows subtle T2 signal in limbic areas. CSF shows 18 WBC (lymphocytic), protein 62. She is HIV-negative and has no known malignancy. Serum anti-NMDA receptor antibody is positive. Which is the most appropriate acute treatment?

answers.text: Antiepileptic drugs only — target seizure control and observe for spontaneous remission
IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 5 days and IVIG 2g/kg over 5 days; screen for occult teratoma
Empiric acyclovir for HSV limbic encephalitis pending additional testing
Rituximab immediately as first-line B-cell depleting therapy

explanation.correct: Correct. First-line: high-dose steroids AND IVIG (or plasmapheresis) simultaneously or in sequence. Additionally, screen for ovarian teratoma (pelvic ultrasound and MRI) — teratoma removal is the most powerful modifier of outcome and often leads to dramatic improvement even before immunotherapy takes full effect.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Anti-NMDA encephalitis is an autoimmune condition requiring immunotherapy, not just seizure management. Without immunosuppression, the condition can progress to coma, autonomic instability, and death. AEDs alone treat symptoms without addressing pathophysiology.
explanation.b: Correct. First-line: high-dose steroids AND IVIG (or plasmapheresis) simultaneously or in sequence. Additionally, screen for ovarian teratoma (pelvic ultrasound and MRI) — teratoma removal is the most powerful modifier of outcome and often leads to dramatic improvement even before immunotherapy takes full effect.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Acyclovir for HSV encephalitis is appropriate while awaiting HSV PCR (which may take 48-72 hours), but the positive anti-NMDA antibody test is specific for autoimmune encephalitis. HSV limbic encephalitis presents more acutely and does not have a positive NMDA receptor antibody. Empiric acyclovir may be given initially but the diagnosis is autoimmune.
