explanation.a: Incorrect. Isolated hyperreflexia and spasticity without LMN signs (no fasciculations, no atrophy) indicates a pure UMN lesion — such as stroke, MS, or cervical myelopathy. The simultaneous presence of BOTH UMN and LMN signs in multiple body regions is the key diagnostic feature of ALS that distinguishes it from pure UMN or pure LMN conditions.
explanation.b: Correct. Simultaneous UMN signs (hyperreflexia, spasticity, Babinski) AND LMN signs (fasciculations, significant atrophy, weakness) occurring together in multiple body regions is the hallmark of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS/Lou Gehrig's disease). ALS destroys BOTH upper motor neurons (in the motor cortex/corticospinal tract) and lower motor neurons (in the anterior horn cells of the spinal cord and brainstem motor nuclei) simultaneously. Sensation is characteristically INTACT — a purely motor disease. El Escorial criteria require UMN+LMN signs in ≥3 regions for definite ALS. Average survival is 3-5 years from symptom onset.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Areflexia (absence of reflexes, an LMN sign) and clonus (UMN sign) cannot coexist in the SAME reflex arc. If the lower motor neuron is destroyed, the reflex arc is interrupted and clonus cannot occur. This combination is physiologically impossible in the same muscle group. In ALS, different regions may show predominantly UMN or LMN findings, but within a single reflex arc, the dominant lesion determines the reflex response.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Normal muscle tone and normal reflexes throughout would not suggest any motor neuron disease. Motor neuron disease — whether UMN, LMN, or combined — always produces abnormal findings on the neurological exam. The absence of any motor abnormalities effectively rules out significant motor neuron pathology.

neurology.question: A 30-year-old man sustains a traumatic spinal cord injury at the C6 level. After spinal shock resolves, the neurological exam is performed. Which of the following patterns of reflexes and tone would be expected in his lower extremities below the level of injury?

answers.text: Hyporeflexia and flaccidity
Normal reflexes
Hyperreflexia and spasticity
Fasciculations with atrophy

explanation.correct: Correct. Below the level of a spinal cord injury, upper motor neuron signs develop: hyperreflexia, spasticity, positive Babinski sign, and clonus. This occurs because the descending corticospinal tract (carrying inhibitory input from the motor cortex) is interrupted at the injury level, releasing the intact spinal reflex arcs below from cortical modulation. AT the level of the lesion, LMN signs may be present (hyporeflexia, atrophy, flaccidity) if anterior horn cells are directly damaged. ABOVE the lesion, neurological findings are normal.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Below a spinal cord injury, UMN signs predominate (after the initial period of spinal shock resolves, typically 24 hours to 6 weeks). During spinal shock, reflexes may be transiently absent (mimicking LMN), but once spinal shock resolves, hyperreflexia and spasticity develop because the intact spinal reflex arcs below the lesion are released from cortical inhibition.
explanation.b: Incorrect. A C6 spinal cord injury disrupts the descending corticospinal tract, removing cortical inhibitory input to the spinal reflex arcs below the injury. This disinhibition results in UMN findings (hyperreflexia, spasticity, Babinski), not normal reflexes. Normal reflexes below a spinal cord injury would only be expected during spinal shock or with an incomplete injury.
explanation.c: Correct. Below the level of a spinal cord injury, upper motor neuron signs develop: hyperreflexia, spasticity, positive Babinski sign, and clonus. This occurs because the descending corticospinal tract (carrying inhibitory input from the motor cortex) is interrupted at the injury level, releasing the intact spinal reflex arcs below from cortical modulation. AT the level of the lesion, LMN signs may be present (hyporeflexia, atrophy, flaccidity) if anterior horn cells are directly damaged. ABOVE the lesion, neurological findings are normal.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Fasciculations and atrophy are LMN signs that occur AT the level of the spinal cord injury (where anterior horn cells are directly damaged), NOT below it. Below the lesion, UMN signs predominate. Understanding this anatomic distinction — LMN findings AT the level, UMN findings BELOW the level — is a high-yield concept for localizing spinal cord lesions.

neurology.question: A 48-year-old woman presents with a left foot drop, difficulty dorsiflexing her left ankle, decreased sensation over the lateral calf, and a diminished left ankle jerk reflex. There is atrophy of the anterior compartment of the left leg. No upper motor neuron signs are present. Which of the following types of motor neuron lesion is most consistent with these findings?

answers.text: Upper motor neuron
Lower motor neuron
Combined UMN and LMN
Cerebellar

explanation.correct: Correct. This presentation — foot drop, decreased ankle jerk reflex, and localized atrophy of the anterior compartment — is a classic LMN lesion affecting either the L4-L5 nerve root (radiculopathy) or the common peroneal (fibular) nerve (the most common cause of foot drop). LMN findings are characterized by weakness in a specific nerve/root territory, hyporeflexia, and neurogenic atrophy from denervation. The anterior compartment contains the tibialis anterior (dorsiflexion), extensor digitorum longus, and peroneus tertius — all innervated by the deep peroneal nerve (L4-L5).
explanation.a: Incorrect. UMN lesions cause hyperreflexia, spasticity, and positive Babinski sign — the OPPOSITE of this patient's findings (decreased ankle jerk reflex, focal atrophy). UMN lesions also produce weakness in a pyramidal distribution (extensors in upper extremity, flexors in lower extremity), not the specific footdrop pattern with anterior compartment atrophy seen here.
explanation.b: Correct. This presentation — foot drop, decreased ankle jerk reflex, and localized atrophy of the anterior compartment — is a classic LMN lesion affecting either the L4-L5 nerve root (radiculopathy) or the common peroneal (fibular) nerve (the most common cause of foot drop). LMN findings are characterized by weakness in a specific nerve/root territory, hyporeflexia, and neurogenic atrophy from denervation. The anterior compartment contains the tibialis anterior (dorsiflexion), extensor digitorum longus, and peroneus tertius — all innervated by the deep peroneal nerve (L4-L5).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Combined UMN and LMN disease (like ALS) would show BOTH UMN signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski) AND LMN signs (fasciculations, atrophy) in multiple body regions. This patient has isolated LMN findings in a single nerve territory without any UMN signs, making combined disease unlikely.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Cerebellar lesions cause ataxia (wide-based, uncoordinated gait), dysmetria (past-pointing on finger-to-nose testing), intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, and nystagmus — NOT foot drop with decreased reflexes and focal atrophy. Cerebellar lesions affect coordination and balance without causing weakness, reflex changes, or muscle wasting.

neurology.question: A 28-year-old woman presents with 3 days of painful vision loss in her right eye. Examination reveals an afferent pupillary defect in the right eye and decreased visual acuity. MRI brain shows multiple periventricular white matter lesions (Dawson fingers). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: Glaucoma
MS presenting with optic neuritis
GBS
Myasthenia gravis

explanation.correct: Correct. This is MS presenting with optic neuritis — the most common initial manifestation of multiple sclerosis. Key features: (1) painful monocular vision loss (typically over days); (2) afferent pupillary defect (APD/Marcus Gunn pupil — the affected pupil dilates instead of constricting with the swinging flashlight test, indicating optic nerve conduction impairment); (3) periventricular white matter lesions (Dawson fingers) on MRI supporting dissemination in space. The McDonald criteria for MS diagnosis require evidence of CNS demyelination disseminated in both SPACE (multiple anatomic locations) and TIME (lesions of different ages or new lesions on follow-up MRI).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with severe eye pain, headache, nausea/vomiting, and a mid-dilated nonreactive pupil with elevated intraocular pressure. It does NOT cause an afferent pupillary defect (APD) or white matter brain lesions on MRI. Glaucoma is an ophthalmologic emergency treated with timolol, pilocarpine, and acetazolamide — not related to demyelinating disease.
explanation.b: Correct. This is MS presenting with optic neuritis — the most common initial manifestation of multiple sclerosis. Key features: (1) painful monocular vision loss (typically over days); (2) afferent pupillary defect (APD/Marcus Gunn pupil — the affected pupil dilates instead of constricting with the swinging flashlight test, indicating optic nerve conduction impairment); (3) periventricular white matter lesions (Dawson fingers) on MRI supporting dissemination in space. The McDonald criteria for MS diagnosis require evidence of CNS demyelination disseminated in both SPACE (multiple anatomic locations) and TIME (lesions of different ages or new lesions on follow-up MRI).
explanation.c: Incorrect. GBS is a peripheral nervous system disease (LMN — ascending weakness, areflexia) that does NOT involve the CNS or optic nerve. GBS does not cause vision loss, APD, or white matter brain lesions. The optic nerve is a CNS structure (extension of the brain), making optic neuritis a central demyelinating process consistent with MS.
explanation.d: Incorrect. MG causes fatigable weakness of extraocular muscles (leading to ptosis and diplopia) but does NOT cause an afferent pupillary defect, vision loss, or white matter brain lesions. MG affects the neuromuscular junction, not the optic nerve or CNS. In MG, pupils are always normal because pupillary constriction uses smooth muscle (parasympathetic), not skeletal muscle affected by the AChR antibodies.

neurology.question: A 35-year-old woman with known relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis presents with acute onset of left leg weakness and gait instability over the past 48 hours. MRI shows a new enhancing lesion in the thoracic spinal cord. Which of the following is the first-line treatment for this acute MS relapse?

answers.text: Oral prednisone taper
IV methylprednisolone x3-5 days
IVIG x5 days
Interferon-beta

explanation.correct: Correct. IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3-5 days is the standard first-line treatment for acute MS relapses. It shortens relapse duration and speeds recovery of function, though it does NOT prevent future relapses or alter long-term disability accumulation. For steroid-refractory relapses (no improvement after 1-2 weeks), plasmapheresis (PLEX) is the established second-line therapy, with evidence showing benefit in approximately 40-50% of steroid-nonresponders. Important: disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) reduce future relapse FREQUENCY but do not treat acute relapses.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Oral prednisone tapers are less effective than IV methylprednisolone for acute MS relapses. The high-dose IV formulation achieves rapid anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in the CNS, shortening relapse duration. While some evidence supports high-dose oral methylprednisolone as a substitute, IV administration remains the standard of care for acute MS relapses.
explanation.b: Correct. IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3-5 days is the standard first-line treatment for acute MS relapses. It shortens relapse duration and speeds recovery of function, though it does NOT prevent future relapses or alter long-term disability accumulation. For steroid-refractory relapses (no improvement after 1-2 weeks), plasmapheresis (PLEX) is the established second-line therapy, with evidence showing benefit in approximately 40-50% of steroid-nonresponders. Important: disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) reduce future relapse FREQUENCY but do not treat acute relapses.
explanation.c: Incorrect. IVIG is the standard treatment for Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), NOT for MS relapses. This is one of the most important treatment distinctions in neurology: MS relapses → IV steroids (± PLEX if refractory); GBS → IVIG or PLEX. Corticosteroids are effective in MS but NOT effective in GBS.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Interferon-beta (Avonex, Betaseron, Rebif) is a disease-modifying therapy (DMT) used for LONG-TERM reduction of relapse frequency and disability progression in relapsing-remitting MS. It takes weeks to months to reach full therapeutic effect and does not treat acute relapses. DMTs prevent future attacks; steroids treat the current attack.

neurology.question: A 30-year-old woman with multiple sclerosis notices that her leg weakness and visual blurring significantly worsen whenever she takes a hot bath or exercises vigorously, then improve after cooling down. Which of the following terms describes this phenomenon?

answers.text: Lhermitte sign
Uhthoff phenomenon
Romberg sign
Brudzinski sign

explanation.correct: Correct. Uhthoff phenomenon is the transient worsening of MS symptoms with increased body temperature — typically triggered by hot baths, exercise, fever, or hot weather. Heat slows nerve conduction velocity in demyelinated axons (which have reduced safety factor for impulse propagation), causing previously compensated deficits to become clinically apparent. Critically, Uhthoff phenomenon is a PSEUDOEXACERBATION — symptoms resolve with cooling and do NOT represent new inflammatory demyelination (no new MRI lesions). This distinguishes it from a true MS relapse, which produces new neurological deficits from new/active demyelinating lesions.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Lhermitte sign is an electric shock-like sensation radiating down the spine and into the extremities upon neck flexion. It indicates demyelination of the posterior columns of the cervical spinal cord and is associated with MS (also seen with cervical spondylosis, B12 deficiency, and radiation myelopathy). Lhermitte sign is NOT related to heat sensitivity.
explanation.b: Correct. Uhthoff phenomenon is the transient worsening of MS symptoms with increased body temperature — typically triggered by hot baths, exercise, fever, or hot weather. Heat slows nerve conduction velocity in demyelinated axons (which have reduced safety factor for impulse propagation), causing previously compensated deficits to become clinically apparent. Critically, Uhthoff phenomenon is a PSEUDOEXACERBATION — symptoms resolve with cooling and do NOT represent new inflammatory demyelination (no new MRI lesions). This distinguishes it from a true MS relapse, which produces new neurological deficits from new/active demyelinating lesions.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Romberg sign (increased sway/falling with eyes closed during standing) tests proprioceptive function (posterior column/dorsal column pathways). A positive Romberg indicates proprioceptive sensory loss (peripheral neuropathy, posterior column disease, tabes dorsalis), not heat sensitivity. While Romberg can be positive in MS patients with posterior column involvement, it is unrelated to Uhthoff phenomenon.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Brudzinski sign (involuntary hip/knee flexion when the examiner passively flexes the patient's neck) indicates meningeal irritation and is a clinical sign of meningitis. It has no relationship to MS or heat-induced symptom worsening.

neurology.question: A 42-year-old man with relapsing-remitting MS has been on natalizumab for 2 years and is doing well clinically. His neurologist orders JC virus antibody testing every 6 months. Which of the following serious adverse effects is the rationale for this monitoring?

answers.text: Glatiramer acetate
Natalizumab
Dimethyl fumarate
Interferon beta

explanation.correct: Correct. Natalizumab (Tysabri) carries a significant risk of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) — a devastating, often fatal CNS infection caused by reactivation of latent JC (John Cunningham) virus. PML risk is stratified by: (1) JC virus antibody status and index value (higher index = higher risk); (2) duration of natalizumab therapy (risk increases after 24 months); (3) prior immunosuppressant use. Mandatory monitoring includes JC virus antibody testing every 6 months. If JCV antibody converts from negative to positive (or index rises above 1.5), the risk-benefit ratio must be reassessed and therapy often switched. PML presents with subacute progressive neurological deficits without MRI gadolinium enhancement.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Glatiramer acetate (Copaxone) has one of the most favorable safety profiles among MS disease-modifying therapies. It does not carry a risk of PML or significant immunosuppression. Side effects are primarily injection-site reactions and a rare immediate post-injection systemic reaction (flushing, chest tightness — benign and self-limited).
explanation.b: Correct. Natalizumab (Tysabri) carries a significant risk of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) — a devastating, often fatal CNS infection caused by reactivation of latent JC (John Cunningham) virus. PML risk is stratified by: (1) JC virus antibody status and index value (higher index = higher risk); (2) duration of natalizumab therapy (risk increases after 24 months); (3) prior immunosuppressant use. Mandatory monitoring includes JC virus antibody testing every 6 months. If JCV antibody converts from negative to positive (or index rises above 1.5), the risk-benefit ratio must be reassessed and therapy often switched. PML presents with subacute progressive neurological deficits without MRI gadolinium enhancement.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While rare cases of PML have been reported with dimethyl fumarate (Tecfidera), the association is far weaker than with natalizumab. Dimethyl fumarate-associated PML cases have been primarily in patients with prolonged, severe lymphopenia. The strongest and most clinically significant PML risk among MS therapies is with natalizumab.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Interferon-beta formulations (Avonex, Betaseron, Rebif) have no association with PML. Their main side effects include flu-like symptoms (fever, myalgias after injection — improve with time), injection-site reactions, depression, and hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs). Interferons are among the safest MS DMTs but have lower efficacy compared to newer agents.

neurology.question: A 55-year-old woman is diagnosed with primary progressive multiple sclerosis. Her neurologist discusses disease-modifying therapy options. Which of the following DMTs is FDA-approved for both relapsing forms and primary progressive MS?

answers.text: Fingolimod
Ocrelizumab
Teriflunomide
Natalizumab

explanation.correct: Correct. Ocrelizumab (Ocrevus) is an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that depletes B lymphocytes. It is the first and, for many years, the only FDA-approved DMT for primary progressive MS (PPMS) — based on the landmark ORATORIO trial showing reduced disability progression. It is also approved for relapsing MS (based on OPERA I and II trials showing superiority over interferon beta-1a). This dual indication makes ocrelizumab a landmark therapy in MS treatment. Administration: IV infusion every 6 months. Key monitoring: hepatitis B screening before initiation (risk of HBV reactivation), infection monitoring, and infusion reactions.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Fingolimod (Gilenya) is a sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulator approved only for relapsing forms of MS. It sequesters lymphocytes in lymph nodes, reducing their migration into the CNS. Fingolimod is NOT approved for primary progressive MS.
explanation.b: Correct. Ocrelizumab (Ocrevus) is an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that depletes B lymphocytes. It is the first and, for many years, the only FDA-approved DMT for primary progressive MS (PPMS) — based on the landmark ORATORIO trial showing reduced disability progression. It is also approved for relapsing MS (based on OPERA I and II trials showing superiority over interferon beta-1a). This dual indication makes ocrelizumab a landmark therapy in MS treatment. Administration: IV infusion every 6 months. Key monitoring: hepatitis B screening before initiation (risk of HBV reactivation), infection monitoring, and infusion reactions.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Teriflunomide (Aubagio) is a pyrimidine synthesis inhibitor approved only for relapsing forms of MS. It is oral (once daily), has modest efficacy, and carries teratogenic risk requiring an accelerated elimination procedure (cholestyramine) before pregnancy.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Natalizumab (Tysabri) is approved only for relapsing forms of MS. While it is one of the most efficacious DMTs for preventing relapses, it has not demonstrated benefit in primary progressive MS and carries the risk of PML from JC virus reactivation.

neurology.question: A 38-year-old man develops rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness, paresthesias in his feet, and absent deep tendon reflexes 10 days after a bout of Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis. CSF analysis shows elevated protein with a normal cell count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: MS
Guillain-Barre syndrome
MG
Transverse myelitis

explanation.correct: Correct. This is classic Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy/AIDP): post-infectious onset (1-4 weeks after Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis — the most common preceding infection), rapidly progressive ascending bilateral weakness, areflexia (LMN pattern), and CSF albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein with normal WBC). Paresthesias often precede motor symptoms. The weakness progresses to its nadir within 2-4 weeks and then gradually improves. Approximately 30% require mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Multiple sclerosis is a CENTRAL nervous system demyelinating disease with UMN signs (hyperreflexia, spasticity, Babinski), oligoclonal bands in CSF, and periventricular white matter lesions on MRI. This patient has peripheral nervous system findings (ascending weakness with AREFLEXIA — LMN pattern), post-infectious onset (Campylobacter), and albuminocytologic dissociation in CSF — all pointing to GBS, not MS.
explanation.b: Correct. This is classic Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy/AIDP): post-infectious onset (1-4 weeks after Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis — the most common preceding infection), rapidly progressive ascending bilateral weakness, areflexia (LMN pattern), and CSF albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein with normal WBC). Paresthesias often precede motor symptoms. The weakness progresses to its nadir within 2-4 weeks and then gradually improves. Approximately 30% require mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Myasthenia gravis causes FATIGABLE weakness (worsens with activity, improves with rest) — not the progressive ascending weakness seen here. MG has NORMAL reflexes, not areflexia. MG is not post-infectious and does not cause albuminocytologic dissociation. MG is caused by autoantibodies against the acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Transverse myelitis causes acute myelopathy with a sensory LEVEL (a clear dermatomal boundary of sensory loss), UMN signs below the lesion (hyperreflexia, Babinski), and bladder/bowel dysfunction. It does NOT cause ascending weakness with areflexia. Transverse myelitis is a spinal cord lesion, while GBS affects peripheral nerves.

neurology.question: A 42-year-old woman with Guillain-Barré syndrome has been in the ICU for 3 days with progressive weakness now affecting her upper extremities. The respiratory therapist reports a declining forced vital capacity (FVC) of 18 mL/kg and a negative inspiratory force (NIF) of –25 cmH₂O. She is using accessory muscles and reports difficulty lying flat. Which of the following is the most important next step?

answers.text: Continue ICU monitoring with respiratory rate checks every 4 hours
Proceed with elective intubation and mechanical ventilation based on the 20/30 rule
Place the patient on BiPAP to support ventilation and avoid intubation
Initiate high-flow nasal cannula at 40 L/min and monitor pulse oximetry closely

explanation.correct: Correct. This patient meets criteria for elective intubation based on the '20/30 rule' for GBS respiratory monitoring: FVC <20 mL/kg (her FVC is 18) and NIF less negative than –30 cmH₂O (her NIF is –25, which is weaker than the –30 threshold). Additional clinical signs supporting intubation include orthopnea (difficulty lying flat indicates diaphragm weakness) and accessory muscle use. Approximately 30% of GBS patients require mechanical ventilation, and respiratory failure is the leading cause of mortality. ELECTIVE intubation before frank respiratory arrest is critical — do NOT wait for hypoxemia or hypercarbia, as these are late and dangerous findings. After intubation, continue IVIG or plasmapheresis and monitor for autonomic instability.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While respiratory rate monitoring is part of ICU care, tachypnea is a nonspecific and late indicator of respiratory failure in GBS. By the time respiratory rate rises significantly, the patient may be near respiratory arrest. Objective bedside pulmonary function measurements (FVC and NIF) are far more sensitive for detecting impending neuromuscular respiratory failure and should drive the intubation decision.
explanation.b: Correct. This patient meets criteria for elective intubation based on the '20/30 rule' for GBS respiratory monitoring: FVC <20 mL/kg (her FVC is 18) and NIF less negative than –30 cmH₂O (her NIF is –25, which is weaker than the –30 threshold). Additional clinical signs supporting intubation include orthopnea (difficulty lying flat indicates diaphragm weakness) and accessory muscle use. Approximately 30% of GBS patients require mechanical ventilation, and respiratory failure is the leading cause of mortality. ELECTIVE intubation before frank respiratory arrest is critical — do NOT wait for hypoxemia or hypercarbia, as these are late and dangerous findings. After intubation, continue IVIG or plasmapheresis and monitor for autonomic instability.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP) is generally ineffective and potentially dangerous in GBS respiratory failure. The underlying problem is neuromuscular weakness — the patient cannot generate adequate inspiratory force or tidal volume. BiPAP does not address this and can worsen the situation by causing gastric distension, aspiration risk, and a false sense of security that delays definitive airway management. Patients with bulbar weakness (common in GBS) are particularly poor candidates for BiPAP due to inability to protect the airway.
explanation.d: Incorrect. High-flow nasal cannula and pulse oximetry monitoring are inadequate for a patient meeting intubation criteria. Pulse oximetry (SpO₂) is a LATE indicator of respiratory failure in neuromuscular disease — desaturation occurs only after significant hypoventilation has already developed. Serial FVC and NIF measurements are the appropriate monitoring tools, and this patient has already crossed both thresholds for intubation. Waiting for desaturation risks respiratory arrest.

neurology.question: A 50-year-old man is diagnosed with Guillain-Barré syndrome after presenting with ascending weakness, areflexia, and albuminocytologic dissociation on lumbar puncture. The neurology team is planning definitive treatment. Which of the following is an appropriate treatment for GBS?

answers.text: IV corticosteroids
IVIG x5 days OR plasmapheresis x5 sessions
Pyridostigmine
Interferon-beta

explanation.correct: Correct. Two treatments have proven efficacy in GBS: (1) IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 consecutive days, or (2) plasmapheresis (plasma exchange, PLEX) — typically 5 sessions over 1–2 weeks. Both are equally effective at accelerating recovery and reducing time on mechanical ventilation. Critically, they should NOT be combined — studies show no additional benefit from sequential IVIG + PLEX, and the combination may increase adverse effects. Treatment should be initiated as soon as the diagnosis is confirmed, ideally within 2 weeks of symptom onset for maximum benefit.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Corticosteroids are NOT effective in Guillain-Barré syndrome — this is one of the most important clinical distinctions between GBS and MS. Multiple randomized controlled trials have failed to demonstrate benefit of corticosteroids in GBS, and they may even delay recovery. In contrast, IV methylprednisolone is first-line treatment for acute MS relapses. Remembering this difference is high-yield for board exams.
explanation.b: Correct. Two treatments have proven efficacy in GBS: (1) IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 consecutive days, or (2) plasmapheresis (plasma exchange, PLEX) — typically 5 sessions over 1–2 weeks. Both are equally effective at accelerating recovery and reducing time on mechanical ventilation. Critically, they should NOT be combined — studies show no additional benefit from sequential IVIG + PLEX, and the combination may increase adverse effects. Treatment should be initiated as soon as the diagnosis is confirmed, ideally within 2 weeks of symptom onset for maximum benefit.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Pyridostigmine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) is the symptomatic treatment for myasthenia gravis — it improves neuromuscular junction transmission by preventing acetylcholine breakdown. It has no role in GBS, which involves autoimmune demyelination of peripheral nerves, not a neuromuscular junction defect.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Interferon-beta is a disease-modifying therapy for relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis. It reduces relapse frequency by modulating the immune response in the CNS. It has no role in GBS, which is a peripheral nervous system disorder requiring immunomodulatory therapy (IVIG or PLEX).

neurology.question: A 45-year-old woman presents with rapidly progressive ascending weakness and areflexia. Guillain-Barré syndrome is suspected, and a lumbar puncture is performed. Which of the following cerebrospinal fluid findings is most characteristic of GBS?

answers.text: Elevated WBC with low protein
Albuminocytologic dissociation
Oligoclonal bands
Low glucose

explanation.correct: Correct. Albuminocytologic dissociation — elevated CSF protein (often 50–200+ mg/dL) with a normal white blood cell count (<10 cells/mm³) — is the hallmark CSF finding in GBS. The elevated protein reflects inflammation and increased permeability of the blood-nerve barrier in the nerve roots, while the normal WBC indicates the absence of direct infection or meningeal inflammation. Important caveat: CSF may be NORMAL in the first week of GBS illness, so a normal LP early in the disease course does not exclude the diagnosis.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Elevated WBC (pleocytosis) with low protein suggests an infectious process such as bacterial or viral meningitis, not GBS. In GBS, the CSF white cell count is characteristically normal or near-normal despite markedly elevated protein — this paradoxical combination is the defining feature called albuminocytologic dissociation.
explanation.b: Correct. Albuminocytologic dissociation — elevated CSF protein (often 50–200+ mg/dL) with a normal white blood cell count (<10 cells/mm³) — is the hallmark CSF finding in GBS. The elevated protein reflects inflammation and increased permeability of the blood-nerve barrier in the nerve roots, while the normal WBC indicates the absence of direct infection or meningeal inflammation. Important caveat: CSF may be NORMAL in the first week of GBS illness, so a normal LP early in the disease course does not exclude the diagnosis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Oligoclonal bands (OCBs) in CSF are characteristic of multiple sclerosis, found in approximately 90% of MS patients. OCBs reflect intrathecal (within the CNS) immunoglobulin production. GBS is a peripheral nervous system disease and does not produce oligoclonal bands.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Low CSF glucose (hypoglycorrhachia) suggests bacterial meningitis, tuberculous meningitis, or fungal meningitis — conditions where organisms or intense inflammation consume glucose in the CSF. GBS does not significantly affect CSF glucose levels.

neurology.question: A medical student asks the ACNP to explain how to clinically distinguish Guillain-Barré syndrome from myasthenia gravis, since both cause weakness. Which of the following best describes the key clinical difference between these two conditions?

answers.text: GBS is ascending and progressive; MG is fatigable and fluctuates
GBS affects only face; MG only legs
GBS improves with rest; MG worsens
Both present identically

explanation.correct: Correct. The key clinical distinction: GBS causes rapidly ascending, symmetric, progressive weakness that reaches its nadir within 2–4 weeks, with areflexia (LMN pattern). Weakness does NOT improve with rest. In contrast, MG causes fatigable weakness that worsens with repeated use and improves after rest. MG typically begins with ocular muscles (ptosis, diplopia) and has normal reflexes. GBS follows infection (most commonly Campylobacter jejuni), while MG is caused by autoantibodies against the acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction.
explanation.a: Correct. The key clinical distinction: GBS causes rapidly ascending, symmetric, progressive weakness that reaches its nadir within 2–4 weeks, with areflexia (LMN pattern). Weakness does NOT improve with rest. In contrast, MG causes fatigable weakness that worsens with repeated use and improves after rest. MG typically begins with ocular muscles (ptosis, diplopia) and has normal reflexes. GBS follows infection (most commonly Campylobacter jejuni), while MG is caused by autoantibodies against the acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Both GBS and MG can affect multiple body regions. GBS can progress to involve cranial nerves (facial diplegia, bulbar weakness) and respiratory muscles. MG can generalize from ocular to bulbar to limb muscles. The distinguishing feature is the PATTERN of weakness (progressive vs. fatigable), not the anatomic distribution.
explanation.c: Incorrect. This reverses the key distinction. GBS weakness does NOT improve with rest — it is progressive. MG weakness characteristically IMPROVES with rest (fatigable weakness). This distinction is one of the most frequently tested board concepts.
explanation.d: Incorrect. GBS and MG have fundamentally different pathophysiology, presentation, and treatment. GBS involves autoimmune demyelination of peripheral nerves (treated with IVIG or PLEX), while MG involves autoantibodies at the neuromuscular junction (treated with pyridostigmine, immunosuppression, and thymectomy).

neurology.question: A 40-year-old woman presents with bilateral ptosis and intermittent diplopia that are minimal in the morning but progressively worsen throughout the day, particularly after prolonged reading. Deep tendon reflexes are normal and symmetric. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: MS
Myasthenia gravis
GBS
Bell palsy

explanation.correct: Correct. Bilateral ptosis and diplopia that worsen throughout the day and with sustained activity (fatigable weakness) with NORMAL deep tendon reflexes is the hallmark of myasthenia gravis. MG is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction, reducing the number of functional receptors. Repeated nerve stimulation depletes available acetylcholine, causing progressive weakness — explaining the fatigable pattern. Ocular symptoms (ptosis, diplopia) are the presenting complaint in >50% of MG patients. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies (~85% sensitivity in generalized MG), anti-MuSK antibodies if seronegative, and repetitive nerve stimulation EMG showing decremental response.
explanation.a: Incorrect. MS can cause diplopia (from internuclear ophthalmoplegia or CN III/VI palsies) but the pattern is NOT fatigable — MS deficits are constant or progressive during a relapse, not worsening with sustained use and improving with rest. MS also typically presents with UMN signs, sensory deficits, and white matter lesions on MRI, none of which are described here.
explanation.b: Correct. Bilateral ptosis and diplopia that worsen throughout the day and with sustained activity (fatigable weakness) with NORMAL deep tendon reflexes is the hallmark of myasthenia gravis. MG is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction, reducing the number of functional receptors. Repeated nerve stimulation depletes available acetylcholine, causing progressive weakness — explaining the fatigable pattern. Ocular symptoms (ptosis, diplopia) are the presenting complaint in >50% of MG patients. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies (~85% sensitivity in generalized MG), anti-MuSK antibodies if seronegative, and repetitive nerve stimulation EMG showing decremental response.
explanation.c: Incorrect. GBS causes rapidly ascending, progressive weakness with AREFLEXIA (LMN pattern), not isolated fatigable ocular symptoms with normal reflexes. GBS typically follows a gastrointestinal or respiratory infection (Campylobacter jejuni most common), progresses to nadir within 2-4 weeks, and does NOT have the fluctuating, time-of-day-dependent pattern seen in MG.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Bell palsy is an acute UNILATERAL lower motor neuron facial nerve (CN VII) paralysis causing ipsilateral facial drooping (forehead, eye, mouth). It does NOT cause bilateral ptosis or diplopia. Bell palsy is likely caused by HSV reactivation in the geniculate ganglion and is treated with corticosteroids (prednisone) ± valacyclovir within 72 hours of onset.

neurology.question: A 45-year-old man presents with progressive ptosis, diplopia, and proximal limb weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. Myasthenia gravis is suspected. Which of the following serologic tests is the most specific for confirming this diagnosis?

answers.text: MRI brain
Anti-AChR antibodies
Lumbar puncture
Standard nerve conduction velocity

explanation.correct: Correct. Anti-acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies are the most specific serologic test for MG, with approximately 85% sensitivity in generalized MG and ~50% in purely ocular MG. For seronegative patients (AChR-negative), anti-muscle-specific kinase (MuSK) antibodies should be tested, found in ~40% of AChR-negative cases. The most SENSITIVE electrophysiologic test is single-fiber EMG (showing increased jitter), while repetitive nerve stimulation showing decremental response (>10% decline at 2-3 Hz stimulation) is more widely available. The ice pack test (improvement of ptosis after applying ice for 2 minutes) is a simple bedside test — cooling improves NMJ transmission by reducing acetylcholinesterase activity.
explanation.a: Incorrect. MRI brain is the diagnostic imaging modality for multiple sclerosis (periventricular white matter lesions, Dawson fingers) and other CNS pathology. MRI has no diagnostic role in myasthenia gravis, which is a neuromuscular junction disorder. However, CT chest is indicated in MG patients to evaluate for thymoma (present in ~10-15% of MG patients).
explanation.b: Correct. Anti-acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies are the most specific serologic test for MG, with approximately 85% sensitivity in generalized MG and ~50% in purely ocular MG. For seronegative patients (AChR-negative), anti-muscle-specific kinase (MuSK) antibodies should be tested, found in ~40% of AChR-negative cases. The most SENSITIVE electrophysiologic test is single-fiber EMG (showing increased jitter), while repetitive nerve stimulation showing decremental response (>10% decline at 2-3 Hz stimulation) is more widely available. The ice pack test (improvement of ptosis after applying ice for 2 minutes) is a simple bedside test — cooling improves NMJ transmission by reducing acetylcholinesterase activity.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis is used to diagnose GBS (albuminocytologic dissociation: elevated protein with normal WBC) and MS (oligoclonal bands, elevated IgG index). CSF is NORMAL in myasthenia gravis because MG is a peripheral neuromuscular junction disorder that does not affect the central nervous system or CSF composition.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Standard motor nerve conduction studies may be completely NORMAL in MG because the resting compound muscle action potential (CMAP) is often preserved. The diagnostic electrophysiologic test is REPETITIVE nerve stimulation (RNS), which reveals a decremental response (progressive decrease in CMAP amplitude) with low-frequency stimulation (2-3 Hz). Single-fiber EMG showing increased jitter is even more sensitive but technically demanding and less widely available.

neurology.question: A 50-year-old woman with confirmed myasthenia gravis (AChR antibody positive) is found to have a 3 cm anterior mediastinal mass on CT chest. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this finding in the context of her MG?

answers.text: Radiation only
Thymectomy
Increase pyridostigmine only
Observation

explanation.correct: Correct. Thymectomy is recommended for ALL MG patients with thymoma, regardless of MG severity. The thymus plays a central role in MG pathogenesis — it contains myoid cells expressing acetylcholine receptors that may trigger the autoimmune response, and thymomas produce abnormal T cells that perpetuate the attack. Thymectomy improves MG outcomes (remission rates, medication reduction) even in NON-thymomatous generalized MG, as demonstrated by the MGTX trial. Additionally, thymomas have malignant potential (WHO classification stages) requiring surgical removal for oncologic reasons.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Radiation therapy alone is insufficient for thymoma in MG patients. Thymectomy (surgical removal) is the primary treatment for thymoma and has been shown to improve MG outcomes in the landmark MGTX randomized trial. Radiation may be used as adjunctive therapy for invasive thymoma after surgical resection but is not a substitute for surgery.
explanation.b: Correct. Thymectomy is recommended for ALL MG patients with thymoma, regardless of MG severity. The thymus plays a central role in MG pathogenesis — it contains myoid cells expressing acetylcholine receptors that may trigger the autoimmune response, and thymomas produce abnormal T cells that perpetuate the attack. Thymectomy improves MG outcomes (remission rates, medication reduction) even in NON-thymomatous generalized MG, as demonstrated by the MGTX trial. Additionally, thymomas have malignant potential (WHO classification stages) requiring surgical removal for oncologic reasons.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Increasing pyridostigmine provides only symptomatic improvement by preventing acetylcholine breakdown at the NMJ. It does NOT address the underlying autoimmune process or the thymoma itself. Furthermore, thymomas can be locally invasive and potentially malignant — observation without surgical intervention is oncologically inappropriate.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Observation of a thymoma in an MG patient is inappropriate for two reasons: (1) thymectomy provides significant therapeutic benefit for MG itself (improved remission rates, reduced immunosuppressive medication requirements); and (2) thymomas have malignant potential and require surgical excision for staging and definitive treatment. Delayed intervention risks local invasion into surrounding mediastinal structures.

neurology.question: A 55-year-old man with myasthenia gravis on pyridostigmine 60 mg QID presents with worsening weakness accompanied by excessive salivation, lacrimation, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and a heart rate of 48 bpm. Which of the following conditions is this patient most likely experiencing?

answers.text: Myasthenic crisis
Cholinergic crisis
Serotonin syndrome
NMS

explanation.correct: Correct. This is cholinergic crisis — excessive accumulation of acetylcholine at muscarinic AND nicotinic receptors from pyridostigmine overdose. Muscarinic excess produces SLUDGE/DUMBBELLS symptoms: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Diarrhea/Diaphoresis, Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Lethargy, Salivation. Treatment: (1) immediately HOLD pyridostigmine; (2) atropine for severe bradycardia or secretions (blocks muscarinic effects); (3) supportive care, airway protection. Historically, the edrophonium (Tensilon) test differentiated the two crises — worsening with edrophonium = cholinergic crisis, improvement = myasthenic crisis — but this test is rarely used today due to cardiac risks.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Myasthenic crisis is weakness caused by worsening of the underlying disease (exacerbation of MG), NOT by medication overdose. Myasthenic crisis presents with increasing weakness (respiratory, bulbar, limb) WITHOUT the excessive cholinergic symptoms (SLUDGE) seen in this patient. The key distinction: myasthenic crisis = TOO LITTLE acetylcholine effect at the NMJ → increase treatment (IVIG/PLEX); cholinergic crisis = TOO MUCH acetylcholine → reduce/hold pyridostigmine.
explanation.b: Correct. This is cholinergic crisis — excessive accumulation of acetylcholine at muscarinic AND nicotinic receptors from pyridostigmine overdose. Muscarinic excess produces SLUDGE/DUMBBELLS symptoms: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Diarrhea/Diaphoresis, Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Lethargy, Salivation. Treatment: (1) immediately HOLD pyridostigmine; (2) atropine for severe bradycardia or secretions (blocks muscarinic effects); (3) supportive care, airway protection. Historically, the edrophonium (Tensilon) test differentiated the two crises — worsening with edrophonium = cholinergic crisis, improvement = myasthenic crisis — but this test is rarely used today due to cardiac risks.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Serotonin syndrome presents with agitation, confusion, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, clonus (especially lower extremity), hyperreflexia, and myoclonus — caused by excess serotonergic activity from drug interactions (e.g., linezolid + SSRIs). Serotonin syndrome does NOT cause bradycardia or SLUDGE symptoms. The key distinguishing feature: serotonin syndrome = neuromuscular EXCITATION (clonus, hyperreflexia); cholinergic crisis = parasympathetic EXCESS (SLUDGE, bradycardia).
explanation.d: Incorrect. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is caused by dopamine receptor blockade (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, droperidol) and presents with lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, and altered mental status. NMS develops over days (slower than serotonin syndrome) and shows markedly elevated CK from rhabdomyolysis. Treatment: discontinue the offending agent + dantrolene and/or bromocriptine. NMS has no relationship to cholinergic agents.

neurology.question: An ACNP is reviewing the medication list of a 60-year-old woman with newly diagnosed myasthenia gravis before her upcoming elective surgery. Which of the following classes of medications should be specifically avoided because they can worsen neuromuscular junction transmission and precipitate myasthenic crisis?

answers.text: Acetaminophen and ibuprofen
Aminoglycosides, beta-blockers, and magnesium sulfate
PPIs and H2 blockers
Statins and ACE inhibitors

explanation.correct: Correct. Multiple medication classes can worsen MG or precipitate myasthenic crisis by impairing neuromuscular transmission: (1) AMINOGLYCOSIDES — block presynaptic calcium channels, reducing acetylcholine release; (2) BETA-BLOCKERS — interfere with NMJ transmission through unclear mechanisms; (3) MAGNESIUM SULFATE — competes with calcium at the presynaptic terminal, reducing ACh release (particularly dangerous in obstetric patients with MG receiving mag for pre-eclampsia). Other agents to avoid: fluoroquinolones, telithromycin (macrolide — strongest NMJ effect), botulinum toxin, D-penicillamine (can actually INDUCE MG), and high-dose corticosteroids (can cause initial transient worsening before improvement). Always check drug safety references before prescribing new medications to MG patients.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Acetaminophen and ibuprofen are safe in MG patients. Standard analgesics do not affect neuromuscular junction transmission and can be used without concern for exacerbating MG. Pain management in MG follows standard guidelines.
explanation.b: Correct. Multiple medication classes can worsen MG or precipitate myasthenic crisis by impairing neuromuscular transmission: (1) AMINOGLYCOSIDES — block presynaptic calcium channels, reducing acetylcholine release; (2) BETA-BLOCKERS — interfere with NMJ transmission through unclear mechanisms; (3) MAGNESIUM SULFATE — competes with calcium at the presynaptic terminal, reducing ACh release (particularly dangerous in obstetric patients with MG receiving mag for pre-eclampsia). Other agents to avoid: fluoroquinolones, telithromycin (macrolide — strongest NMJ effect), botulinum toxin, D-penicillamine (can actually INDUCE MG), and high-dose corticosteroids (can cause initial transient worsening before improvement). Always check drug safety references before prescribing new medications to MG patients.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, pantoprazole) and H2 receptor antagonists (famotidine) are safe in MG patients. These acid-suppressive medications do not affect neuromuscular junction function and can be prescribed without concern for MG exacerbation.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Statins and ACE inhibitors are generally safe in MG patients. While rare case reports have described MG-like symptoms with statins (possibly related to statin myopathy rather than true NMJ dysfunction), these medications are not contraindicated in MG and can be used when clinically indicated for cardiovascular risk management.

neurology.question: A 72-year-old man with atrial fibrillation and hypertension presents with acute-onset right-sided weakness, right facial droop, and expressive aphasia. Symptom onset was 90 minutes ago. Non-contrast CT head shows no hemorrhage. BP is 178/102 mmHg. NIHSS score is 14. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Administer IV alteplase (tPA)
Lower BP to <120/80 first
MRI before any treatment
Start heparin drip

explanation.correct: Correct. This patient meets IV tPA criteria: (1) within the 4.5-hour window (onset 90 minutes ago); (2) non-contrast CT shows no hemorrhage; (3) measurable neurological deficit (NIHSS 14); (4) BP is 178/102, below the <185/110 threshold required before tPA administration. tPA dose: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) — 10% as IV bolus over 1 minute, remaining 90% infused over 60 minutes. After tPA: maintain BP <180/105 for 24 hours, no anticoagulants or antiplatelets for 24 hours, and perform follow-up CT at 24 hours before starting aspirin.
explanation.a: Correct. This patient meets IV tPA criteria: (1) within the 4.5-hour window (onset 90 minutes ago); (2) non-contrast CT shows no hemorrhage; (3) measurable neurological deficit (NIHSS 14); (4) BP is 178/102, below the <185/110 threshold required before tPA administration. tPA dose: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) — 10% as IV bolus over 1 minute, remaining 90% infused over 60 minutes. After tPA: maintain BP <180/105 for 24 hours, no anticoagulants or antiplatelets for 24 hours, and perform follow-up CT at 24 hours before starting aspirin.
explanation.b: Incorrect. The BP threshold for tPA eligibility is <185/110 — not <120/80. Aggressively lowering BP to <120/80 in the acute stroke setting risks worsening ischemia by reducing perfusion to the penumbra (the at-risk but potentially salvageable tissue surrounding the infarct core). Permissive hypertension is standard in acute stroke management (up to 220/120 if tPA is NOT given) to maintain collateral perfusion.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Non-contrast CT is sufficient to rule out hemorrhage and determine tPA eligibility. MRI provides more detailed information (diffusion-weighted imaging shows acute ischemia within minutes) but should NOT delay tPA administration — the principle 'time is brain' means every minute of delay results in approximately 1.9 million neurons lost. MRI may be obtained after tPA for further characterization or if thrombectomy is being considered for LVO.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IV heparin is NOT part of acute ischemic stroke management and is specifically CONTRAINDICATED within 24 hours of tPA administration due to hemorrhagic transformation risk. Anticoagulation may be started 4-14 days after stroke onset (depending on infarct size) if atrial fibrillation or another cardioembolic source is identified. Aspirin 325 mg is started at 24 hours post-tPA (or immediately if tPA is not given).

neurology.question: A 70-year-old man received IV alteplase for an acute ischemic stroke 2 hours ago. The nursing staff asks the ACNP what blood pressure threshold must be maintained for the 24 hours following tPA administration. Which of the following is the correct post-tPA blood pressure target?

explanation.correct: Correct. After tPA administration, blood pressure must be maintained below 180/105 for 24 hours to minimize the risk of hemorrhagic transformation of the ischemic infarct. The tPA-lysed clot creates a vulnerable vascular bed, and uncontrolled hypertension increases the risk of intracranial hemorrhage into the infarcted tissue. Monitoring: BP checks every 15 minutes × 2 hours, then every 30 minutes × 6 hours, then hourly × 16 hours. IV antihypertensives (labetalol bolus or nicardipine drip) are used if BP exceeds 180/105.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A BP target of <120/80 is too aggressive in the immediate post-stroke/post-tPA setting. Excessive blood pressure reduction can worsen ischemia by reducing cerebral perfusion pressure to the vulnerable penumbra tissue. The <120/80 target is appropriate for chronic hypertension management, not acute post-tPA monitoring.
explanation.b: Incorrect. <140/90 is a chronic outpatient blood pressure goal. In the acute post-tPA setting, the target is <180/105 for 24 hours. After the initial 24-hour period and repeat imaging, blood pressure can be gradually liberalized or managed toward long-term targets depending on the clinical situation.
explanation.c: Correct. After tPA administration, blood pressure must be maintained below 180/105 for 24 hours to minimize the risk of hemorrhagic transformation of the ischemic infarct. The tPA-lysed clot creates a vulnerable vascular bed, and uncontrolled hypertension increases the risk of intracranial hemorrhage into the infarcted tissue. Monitoring: BP checks every 15 minutes × 2 hours, then every 30 minutes × 6 hours, then hourly × 16 hours. IV antihypertensives (labetalol bolus or nicardipine drip) are used if BP exceeds 180/105.
explanation.d: Incorrect. 220/120 is the permissive hypertension threshold for stroke patients who did NOT receive tPA. In non-tPA patients, blood pressure is only treated if >220/120 or if there is evidence of end-organ damage (hypertensive encephalopathy, aortic dissection, acute MI). For patients who received tPA, the lower threshold of <180/105 applies because of the increased hemorrhage risk from thrombolysis.

neurology.question: A 68-year-old woman presents with acute right-sided hemiplegia and global aphasia. Symptom onset was 6 hours ago. Non-contrast CT head shows no hemorrhage, and CT angiography reveals a left MCA M1 occlusion. She is outside the IV tPA window. Which of the following interventions may still be beneficial?

answers.text: IV tPA
Mechanical thrombectomy
Aspirin 325 mg PO
Observation

explanation.correct: Correct. Mechanical thrombectomy (endovascular clot retrieval) can be performed up to 24 hours from symptom onset for large vessel occlusions (LVO) in selected patients. The DAWN and DEFUSE-3 trials demonstrated dramatic benefit of late-window thrombectomy using advanced imaging to identify patients with salvageable penumbra (mismatch between infarct core and at-risk tissue on CT perfusion or MRI DWI/FLAIR). Common thrombectomy targets: ICA terminus, MCA M1/M2 segments. Number needed to treat (NNT) for functional independence: approximately 2.6 — one of the most effective interventions in medicine.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While IV tPA has a standard window of 4.5 hours, MECHANICAL THROMBECTOMY for large vessel occlusion has a much longer treatment window — up to 24 hours in selected patients based on advanced imaging (CT perfusion or MRI showing salvageable tissue). Dismissing all intervention options beyond 4.5 hours deprives this patient of a life-changing treatment.
explanation.b: Correct. Mechanical thrombectomy (endovascular clot retrieval) can be performed up to 24 hours from symptom onset for large vessel occlusions (LVO) in selected patients. The DAWN and DEFUSE-3 trials demonstrated dramatic benefit of late-window thrombectomy using advanced imaging to identify patients with salvageable penumbra (mismatch between infarct core and at-risk tissue on CT perfusion or MRI DWI/FLAIR). Common thrombectomy targets: ICA terminus, MCA M1/M2 segments. Number needed to treat (NNT) for functional independence: approximately 2.6 — one of the most effective interventions in medicine.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Aspirin 325 mg is an important secondary prevention medication but does NOT address the acute large vessel occlusion. Without mechanical clot retrieval, the occluded MCA will continue to cause progressive ischemic injury to the entire MCA territory, resulting in devastating permanent disability (dense hemiplegia, aphasia, neglect). Aspirin alone cannot dissolve an established arterial thrombus.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Observing a large vessel occlusion stroke without intervention when thrombectomy is available within the treatment window is a missed opportunity. LVO strokes carry very high morbidity and mortality — the NNT for thrombectomy to achieve functional independence is among the lowest in medicine. Observation should only be considered when thrombectomy is not available or the patient has advanced infarction without salvageable tissue.

neurology.question: A 72-year-old man presents with sudden-onset left face and arm weakness greater than leg weakness, with expressive aphasia and right gaze deviation. Non-contrast CT head shows no hemorrhage. Which of the following stroke territories is most likely affected?

answers.text: Leg > arm weakness
Contralateral face/arm > leg weakness with aphasia (dominant hemisphere)
Homonymous hemianopia only
Bilateral motor findings with cranial nerve deficits

explanation.correct: Correct. The middle cerebral artery (MCA) is the most commonly affected territory in ischemic stroke. MCA strokes present with CONTRALATERAL face and arm weakness GREATER than leg weakness (the face and arm are represented on the lateral cortical surface, which is the MCA territory on the homunculus). Additional features: aphasia if DOMINANT hemisphere (usually left) — Broca's (expressive/nonfluent) in frontal MCA or Wernicke's (receptive/fluent) in temporal MCA; hemispatial neglect if NON-DOMINANT hemisphere (usually right); homonymous hemianopia from optic radiation involvement; and conjugate eye deviation TOWARD the lesion (away from the weak side).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Leg-predominant weakness (leg > arm) is characteristic of ANTERIOR CEREBRAL ARTERY (ACA) territory strokes. The ACA supplies the medial surface of the frontal and parietal lobes, including the motor and sensory cortex for the lower extremity (represented on the medial cortical surface in the homunculus). ACA strokes may also cause personality changes, abulia, and urinary incontinence.
explanation.b: Correct. The middle cerebral artery (MCA) is the most commonly affected territory in ischemic stroke. MCA strokes present with CONTRALATERAL face and arm weakness GREATER than leg weakness (the face and arm are represented on the lateral cortical surface, which is the MCA territory on the homunculus). Additional features: aphasia if DOMINANT hemisphere (usually left) — Broca's (expressive/nonfluent) in frontal MCA or Wernicke's (receptive/fluent) in temporal MCA; hemispatial neglect if NON-DOMINANT hemisphere (usually right); homonymous hemianopia from optic radiation involvement; and conjugate eye deviation TOWARD the lesion (away from the weak side).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Isolated contralateral homonymous hemianopia (visual field loss affecting the same side of both eyes) without significant motor deficit is characteristic of POSTERIOR CEREBRAL ARTERY (PCA) territory strokes. The PCA supplies the occipital lobe (primary visual cortex) and medial temporal lobe. PCA strokes may also cause visual agnosia, alexia without agraphia, and memory deficits.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Bilateral motor/sensory findings with cranial nerve deficits and 'crossed signs' (ipsilateral cranial nerve palsy with contralateral body weakness) suggest VERTEBROBASILAR (posterior circulation) stroke. Basilar artery occlusion can cause locked-in syndrome (complete paralysis except vertical eye movements), while posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) occlusion causes lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome.

neurology.question: A 65-year-old woman is recovering from an acute ischemic stroke in the right MCA territory. She is neurologically stable at 48 hours. The ACNP is initiating secondary prevention. Which of the following medication regimens is most appropriate for long-term secondary stroke prevention?

answers.text: Antiplatelet therapy and high-intensity statin
Warfarin for all ischemic strokes
Low-dose aspirin only
Prophylactic antibiotics

explanation.correct: Correct. All ischemic stroke patients should receive: (1) ANTIPLATELET therapy — aspirin 81-325 mg, clopidogrel, or aspirin-dipyridamole; DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) × 21 days for minor stroke/high-risk TIA, then single agent; and (2) HIGH-INTENSITY STATIN — atorvastatin 40-80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg, regardless of baseline LDL (reduces stroke recurrence by ~25%). If atrial fibrillation is identified, anticoagulation with a DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban) REPLACES antiplatelet therapy. Additional measures: BP control (target <130/80 after the acute phase), diabetes management, smoking cessation, exercise, and carotid intervention for symptomatic stenosis ≥50%.
explanation.a: Correct. All ischemic stroke patients should receive: (1) ANTIPLATELET therapy — aspirin 81-325 mg, clopidogrel, or aspirin-dipyridamole; DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) × 21 days for minor stroke/high-risk TIA, then single agent; and (2) HIGH-INTENSITY STATIN — atorvastatin 40-80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg, regardless of baseline LDL (reduces stroke recurrence by ~25%). If atrial fibrillation is identified, anticoagulation with a DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban) REPLACES antiplatelet therapy. Additional measures: BP control (target <130/80 after the acute phase), diabetes management, smoking cessation, exercise, and carotid intervention for symptomatic stenosis ≥50%.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Warfarin is only indicated for CARDIOEMBOLIC strokes (atrial fibrillation, mechanical heart valves, intracardiac thrombus). For non-cardioembolic ischemic strokes, antiplatelet therapy (not anticoagulation) is the standard. Even for AF-related strokes, DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are now preferred over warfarin in most patients due to lower intracranial hemorrhage risk and no INR monitoring requirement.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While aspirin is a cornerstone of secondary stroke prevention, omitting a high-intensity statin is a significant gap. High-intensity statin therapy reduces stroke recurrence by approximately 25% through both LDL lowering and pleiotropic anti-inflammatory, plaque-stabilizing effects. The SPARCL trial established the benefit of atorvastatin 80 mg in stroke prevention regardless of baseline cholesterol levels.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Prophylactic antibiotics have no role in routine secondary stroke prevention. While stroke patients are at increased risk of aspiration pneumonia and UTIs during the acute recovery phase, prophylactic antibiotics have not shown benefit in clinical trials and promote antimicrobial resistance. Infections should be treated when they occur, not prevented empirically.

gi.question: A 58-year-old man with cirrhosis presents with hematemesis and melena. BP 84/52, HR 118. He is resuscitated with 2 units pRBC. Upper endoscopy shows actively bleeding esophageal varices with clot. Band ligation is performed. Which pharmacologic intervention should have been started at the time of presentation, before endoscopy?

answers.text: IV pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr infusion
IV octreotide 50 mcg bolus then 50 mcg/hr infusion
IV vasopressin 0.4 units/min
Oral propranolol 20 mg for portal pressure reduction

explanation.correct: Correct. Octreotide is the vasoactive agent of choice for acute variceal bleeding. It should be initiated at presentation — before endoscopy confirms the diagnosis — because it reduces active bleeding and improves visualization during endoscopy. Continue for 3-5 days post-endoscopy.
explanation.a: Incorrect. IV PPI is appropriate for non-variceal upper GI bleeding (peptic ulcer disease) to reduce rebleeding risk. It has no role in variceal bleeding — varices are not acid-related. Starting PPI instead of octreotide misses the mechanism of variceal hemorrhage.
explanation.b: Correct. Octreotide is the vasoactive agent of choice for acute variceal bleeding. It should be initiated at presentation — before endoscopy confirms the diagnosis — because it reduces active bleeding and improves visualization during endoscopy. Continue for 3-5 days post-endoscopy.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Vasopressin reduces portal pressure but has significant systemic side effects including coronary vasoconstriction, hypertension, and bowel ischemia. It is not recommended over octreotide/terlipressin and should not be used without concomitant nitroglycerin.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Non-selective beta-blockers reduce portal pressure for primary and secondary variceal prophylaxis in stable patients. Oral propranolol has no role in acute variceal hemorrhage — it is contraindicated in the setting of hemodynamic instability.

gi.question: A 52-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis is admitted for tense ascites. Diagnostic paracentesis shows: serum albumin 2.1 g/dL, ascitic fluid albumin 0.8 g/dL, ascitic fluid WBC 620 with 280 PMNs/mm³, culture pending. He has no abdominal pain or fever. Creatinine is 1.8 (baseline 1.0). Which diagnosis and management is most appropriate?

answers.text: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis; start IV cefotaxime and IV albumin 1.5 g/kg on day 1
Secondary peritonitis; emergent surgical consultation and broad-spectrum antibiotics
Malignant ascites; send cytology and hold antibiotics pending culture
Cardiac ascites; initiate IV diuresis with furosemide and spironolactone

explanation.correct: Correct. PMN ≥250 = SBP. Cefotaxime covers the gram-negative organisms most commonly responsible. IV albumin at 1.5 g/kg day 1 and 1 g/kg day 3 is guideline-recommended in SBP patients with creatinine >1.0, BUN >30, or bilirubin >4 — this patient meets creatinine criterion. Albumin significantly reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality.
explanation.a: Correct. PMN ≥250 = SBP. Cefotaxime covers the gram-negative organisms most commonly responsible. IV albumin at 1.5 g/kg day 1 and 1 g/kg day 3 is guideline-recommended in SBP patients with creatinine >1.0, BUN >30, or bilirubin >4 — this patient meets creatinine criterion. Albumin significantly reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Secondary peritonitis (bowel perforation) would show polymicrobial culture, very high WBC, multiple organisms on Gram stain, and often glucose <50 mg/dL and protein >1 g/dL in ascitic fluid. SAAG ≥1.1 also points to portal hypertension not secondary peritonitis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Malignant ascites would have a low SAAG (<1.1) and cytology positive for malignant cells. SAAG of 1.3 indicates portal hypertension. PMN count of 280 requires treatment — antibiotics must not be withheld pending culture in confirmed SBP.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Cardiac ascites would have SAAG ≥1.1 but with high protein content. More importantly, PMN ≥250 mandates treatment for SBP — diuretics alone without antibiotics in confirmed SBP is below standard of care.

gi.question: A 42-year-old woman presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting for 18 hours. Lipase is 4200 U/L. CT abdomen with contrast shows pancreatic and peripancreatic inflammation with no necrosis. HR 112, BP 98/64, RR 22, BUN 28, creatinine 1.4 (baseline 0.9). She is NPO. Which is the most important initial intervention?

answers.text: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with lactated Ringer's at 250-500 mL/hr
Start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent infected necrosis
Urgent ERCP for biliary decompression
Place nasojejunal tube and start enteral nutrition immediately

explanation.correct: Correct. Volume resuscitation with LR is the most evidence-based initial intervention in acute pancreatitis. The WATERFALL trial confirmed LR superiority over NS. Aggressive hydration in the first 24-48 hours reduces pancreatic ischemia, SIRS, and organ failure. Reassess every 2-4 hours and adjust based on urine output and hemodynamics.
explanation.a: Correct. Volume resuscitation with LR is the most evidence-based initial intervention in acute pancreatitis. The WATERFALL trial confirmed LR superiority over NS. Aggressive hydration in the first 24-48 hours reduces pancreatic ischemia, SIRS, and organ failure. Reassess every 2-4 hours and adjust based on urine output and hemodynamics.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Prophylactic antibiotics in acute pancreatitis without confirmed infected necrosis are not recommended. Multiple RCTs and meta-analyses have failed to show benefit and risk Clostridioides difficile and resistant organism selection. Antibiotics are only indicated for confirmed infected necrosis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Urgent ERCP is indicated for acute pancreatitis with concurrent cholangitis (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain, rising LFTs suggesting common bile duct obstruction). This patient has no signs of cholangitis. ERCP in uncomplicated pancreatitis is not indicated.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Early enteral nutrition is beneficial in severe pancreatitis but is typically initiated within 24-72 hours, not immediately. The most urgent intervention is IV fluid resuscitation. Enteral nutrition can begin once the patient is hemodynamically more stable and as part of a broader management plan.

gi.question: A 48-year-old man with decompensated cirrhosis develops tense ascites causing dyspnea. Paracentesis removes 6 liters. Which is the most important concurrent intervention to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction (PPCD)?

answers.text: IV normal saline 1L for every 2L removed
IV albumin 6-8 g per liter of ascites removed beyond 5 liters
Oral midodrine 10 mg after the procedure to prevent hypotension
No volume replacement needed for large volume paracentesis if BP is stable during the procedure

explanation.correct: Correct. EASL and AASLD guidelines: for large volume paracentesis (LVP) >5 liters, IV albumin 6-8 g per liter removed prevents post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction (PPCD). PPCD is defined as >50% increase in plasma renin activity 4-6 days post-procedure, predicting shortened survival. Albumin is superior to gelatin, dextran, or saline for this indication. For <5L removal, albumin is not required.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Normal saline does not prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction. PPCD occurs from rapid redistribution of portal blood flow causing splanchnic vasodilation and systemic vasoconstriction activation. Crystalloid does not adequately replace the oncotic pressure lost with albumin-rich ascites removal.
explanation.b: Correct. EASL and AASLD guidelines: for large volume paracentesis (LVP) >5 liters, IV albumin 6-8 g per liter removed prevents post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction (PPCD). PPCD is defined as >50% increase in plasma renin activity 4-6 days post-procedure, predicting shortened survival. Albumin is superior to gelatin, dextran, or saline for this indication. For <5L removal, albumin is not required.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Midodrine (oral alpha-1 agonist) is used for chronic hepatorenal syndrome management and refractory ascites. It does not prevent the acute hemodynamic changes of PPCD following LVP. Midodrine is not the primary intervention for LVP-related circulatory dysfunction.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Hemodynamic stability during the procedure does not predict against PPCD. The circulatory dysfunction occurs 4-6 days post-procedure from hormonal activation, not during the paracentesis itself. All LVP >5L requires albumin replacement regardless of intraprocedure BP.

gi.question: A 52-year-old woman is day 12 of necrotizing pancreatitis (40% necrosis). She has been improving but today develops new fever 38.9°C, increased abdominal pain, and WBC 19,000. CT shows a well-defined walled-off pancreatic necrosis (WOPN) collection of 9 cm. No air bubbles are visible. Blood cultures are pending. Which statement about this WOPN is most accurate?

answers.text: All WOPN collections >5 cm require immediate drainage regardless of symptoms
This WOPN may be sterile — intervention is indicated only for clinical deterioration or confirmed infection; if drainage is needed, minimally invasive approach is preferred
CT-guided fine needle aspiration (FNA) is no longer recommended for diagnosing infected necrosis
IV antibiotics should be started and continued indefinitely to sterilize the collection

explanation.correct: Correct. WOPN without air bubbles on CT may be sterile. Current IAP/APA guidelines: drain only for (1) confirmed/strongly suspected infection, (2) symptomatic compression causing obstruction, or (3) persistent symptoms preventing oral intake at 8+ weeks. When drainage is needed, the step-up approach applies: endoscopic transmural drainage (EUS-guided) or percutaneous drainage preferred over open surgery. Antibiotics for 4-6 weeks alone can occasionally sterilize early infected collections.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Size alone (>5 cm) is not an indication for WOPN drainage. Many large WOPNs in clinically stable patients can be managed expectantly. WOPN that is asymptomatic or causing only mild symptoms can be observed — up to 50% resolve without intervention.
explanation.b: Correct. WOPN without air bubbles on CT may be sterile. Current IAP/APA guidelines: drain only for (1) confirmed/strongly suspected infection, (2) symptomatic compression causing obstruction, or (3) persistent symptoms preventing oral intake at 8+ weeks. When drainage is needed, the step-up approach applies: endoscopic transmural drainage (EUS-guided) or percutaneous drainage preferred over open surgery. Antibiotics for 4-6 weeks alone can occasionally sterilize early infected collections.
explanation.c: Incorrect. CT-guided FNA (sampling the collection for Gram stain and culture) remains a valid diagnostic tool when infection is suspected but not confirmed by CT findings (air bubbles = virtually diagnostic). FNA is used when the clinical picture is equivocal and management will change based on sterile vs. infected result.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Indefinite antibiotic therapy does not sterilize established WOPN. Antibiotics penetrate pancreatic necrosis variably — carbapenems and fluoroquinolones have best penetration. Antibiotics are given for confirmed infection with drainage, not indefinitely as definitive monotherapy for established organized collections.

gi.question: A 72-year-old man with no prior abdominal surgery presents with 3 days of progressive abdominal distension, obstipation, and nausea. Abdominal X-ray shows massively dilated colon (cecum 12 cm) with no air in the rectum. CT confirms colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie's syndrome) without perforation or ischemia. He is NPO, on IV fluids. Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

answers.text: Emergency colostomy — cecal dilation >10 cm requires immediate surgical decompression to prevent perforation
IV neostigmine 2 mg over 3-5 minutes with continuous cardiac monitoring — reverses adrenergic inhibition of colonic motility
Colonoscopic decompression immediately without attempting pharmacologic management
Oral polyethylene glycol (GoLYTELY) 4 liters for bowel prep and decompression

explanation.correct: Correct. Neostigmine (anticholinesterase) is first-line pharmacologic treatment for Ogilvie's syndrome — Ponec 1999 NEJM trial: neostigmine 2 mg IV resulted in immediate colonic decompression in 10/11 patients vs 0/10 with placebo. Absolute contraindications: bradycardia, recent MI, bronchospasm, mechanical obstruction. Must have continuous cardiac monitoring and atropine at bedside for bradycardia. Response usually occurs within minutes. Colonoscopic decompression is second-line if neostigmine fails.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Emergency colostomy for Ogilvie's syndrome without signs of ischemia or perforation is excessive. Surgical decompression is reserved for failed medical and endoscopic management. Conservative/pharmacologic treatment is always attempted first.
explanation.b: Correct. Neostigmine (anticholinesterase) is first-line pharmacologic treatment for Ogilvie's syndrome — Ponec 1999 NEJM trial: neostigmine 2 mg IV resulted in immediate colonic decompression in 10/11 patients vs 0/10 with placebo. Absolute contraindications: bradycardia, recent MI, bronchospasm, mechanical obstruction. Must have continuous cardiac monitoring and atropine at bedside for bradycardia. Response usually occurs within minutes. Colonoscopic decompression is second-line if neostigmine fails.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Colonoscopic decompression is effective but more invasive, requires bowel prep or unprepared colon colonoscopy, and carries perforation risk. Per ASGE guidelines, pharmacologic therapy with neostigmine should be attempted before colonoscopic decompression in the absence of contraindications.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Oral PEG in massive colonic distension from pseudo-obstruction risks aspiration, worsening distension from additional volume, and does not address the underlying motility failure. It is contraindicated with cecal dilation >10 cm due to perforation risk.

gi.question: A 65-year-old man presents with acute lower GI bleed — 3 episodes of bright red blood per rectum. BP 102/68, HR 112. He received 2 units pRBC. Colonoscopy prep is underway. CT angiography (CTA) shows active extravasation from a branch of the right colic artery. Which is the most appropriate definitive management if bleeding continues?

answers.text: Emergency right hemicolectomy — surgical resection provides definitive hemostasis for right colonic arterial bleed
Interventional radiology: catheter-directed angioembolization of the bleeding vessel
Repeat colonoscopy after full prep — endoscopic hemostasis is always preferred over IR for lower GI bleed
IV vasopressin infusion to achieve vasoconstriction of the mesenteric arterial supply
