explanation.correct: Correct. CTA showing active extravasation is the key to IR success — the interventional radiologist can navigate to the identified vessel and embolize. Angioembolization achieves hemostasis in 85-95% of cases with a positive CTA. Complications: ischemia/infarction of the embolized segment (5-10% for right colon, lower for sigmoid). Surgery is reserved for IR failure.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Surgery (right hemicolectomy) for acute lower GI bleed is reserved for failed endoscopic and IR management or hemodynamic instability not responding to resuscitation. Catheter-directed angioembolization has largely replaced emergency surgery as the preferred intervention when a bleeding source is identified on CTA.
explanation.b: Correct. CTA showing active extravasation is the key to IR success — the interventional radiologist can navigate to the identified vessel and embolize. Angioembolization achieves hemostasis in 85-95% of cases with a positive CTA. Complications: ischemia/infarction of the embolized segment (5-10% for right colon, lower for sigmoid). Surgery is reserved for IR failure.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Colonoscopy is appropriate for slower bleeds or stigmata found on elective colonoscopy. When CTA has already identified active arterial extravasation, IR embolization is faster, more targeted, and does not require bowel prep. Colonoscopy in the setting of active arterial hemorrhage carries poor visualization and higher risk.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IV vasopressin infusion causes mesenteric vasoconstriction but has high rebleeding rates (50%) once discontinued and significant systemic side effects (coronary vasoconstriction, hypertension, hyponatremia). It has been largely replaced by angioembolization and is not the preferred definitive intervention when the bleeding source has been identified.

gi.question: A 58-year-old man with cirrhosis and known esophageal varices presents with massive hematemesis. BP 76/44, HR 138. He receives 2 units pRBC. Which intervention has the strongest evidence for reducing mortality in acute variceal hemorrhage?

answers.text: IV pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr infusion
IV octreotide 50 mcg bolus then 50 mcg/hr infusion plus endoscopic variceal ligation within 12 hours
IV vasopressin 0.4 units/min for vasoconstriction
Emergent TIPS procedure within 2 hours of presentation

explanation.correct: Correct. Octreotide (somatostatin analog) reduces splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure — give as early as possible, even before endoscopy. Combined with endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL), this combination reduces 5-day treatment failure and mortality. AASLD guidelines recommend this combination. Also add prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV daily) — reduces mortality by reducing bacterial infections that trigger rebleeding.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Proton pump inhibitors reduce rebleeding in peptic ulcer disease but have no mortality benefit in variceal hemorrhage. Varices bleed from portal hypertension, not acid. PPIs are not first-line for variceal hemorrhage.
explanation.b: Correct. Octreotide (somatostatin analog) reduces splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure — give as early as possible, even before endoscopy. Combined with endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL), this combination reduces 5-day treatment failure and mortality. AASLD guidelines recommend this combination. Also add prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV daily) — reduces mortality by reducing bacterial infections that trigger rebleeding.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Vasopressin reduces portal pressure but causes systemic vasoconstriction leading to myocardial ischemia, mesenteric ischemia, and stroke. It is rarely used and must be combined with nitroglycerin to reduce side effects. Octreotide/terlipressin are preferred.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Early TIPS (within 72 hours) is indicated for Child-Pugh C or B with active bleeding failing medical therapy — not as first-line before endoscopy. TIPS is a rescue/preemptive strategy for high-risk patients, not the initial intervention.

gi.question: A 44-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis (ascites, no prior SBP) undergoes diagnostic paracentesis for new abdominal distension. Ascitic fluid: WBC 680 cells/mm³, PMN 320 cells/mm³, protein 1.8 g/dL, glucose 88, LDH normal. No bacteria on Gram stain. Which is the diagnosis and treatment?

answers.text: Secondary bacterial peritonitis from bowel perforation; emergent CT abdomen and surgical consult
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP); IV cefotaxime 2g q8h for 5 days plus IV albumin 1.5 g/kg on day 1 and 1 g/kg on day 3
Tuberculous peritonitis; start RIPE therapy and repeat paracentesis in 4 weeks
Malignant ascites with peritoneal carcinomatosis; CT and tumor markers

explanation.correct: Correct. SBP diagnosis: ascitic PMN ≥250 cells/mm³ without an identifiable surgical cause. IV cefotaxime 2g q8h × 5 days is first-line (covers 95% of causative organisms). IV albumin 1.5 g/kg on day 1 and 1 g/kg on day 3 reduces renal impairment and mortality (Sort 1999 NEJM trial). After recovery, start norfloxacin 400 mg daily for secondary SBP prophylaxis.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Secondary bacterial peritonitis (bowel perforation) typically shows: PMN >250 PLUS two of three: protein >1 g/dL, glucose <50, LDH > upper limit of normal. This patient's glucose and LDH are normal, making bowel perforation less likely. SBP criteria are met (PMN ≥250).
explanation.b: Correct. SBP diagnosis: ascitic PMN ≥250 cells/mm³ without an identifiable surgical cause. IV cefotaxime 2g q8h × 5 days is first-line (covers 95% of causative organisms). IV albumin 1.5 g/kg on day 1 and 1 g/kg on day 3 reduces renal impairment and mortality (Sort 1999 NEJM trial). After recovery, start norfloxacin 400 mg daily for secondary SBP prophylaxis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Tuberculous peritonitis is rare in immunocompetent patients. It typically shows lymphocytic predominance (not PMN), high protein, high LDH, and low glucose. Treatment initiation before diagnostic confirmation is not appropriate without supporting evidence.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Malignant ascites may show high WBC but typically has lymphocytic predominance, high protein, and positive cytology. The PMN count of 320 specifically indicates an infectious/inflammatory process meeting SBP criteria.

gi.question: A 46-year-old woman presents with acute pancreatitis (lipase 4800, epigastric pain, nausea). CT shows no necrosis. BISAP score is 3. Hematocrit is 48%. BUN is 28 and rising. Which resuscitation fluid is preferred and at what rate?

answers.text: Normal saline 500 mL/hr for 4 hours then reassess
Lactated Ringer's solution 250-500 mL/hr for the first 12-24 hours targeting clinical improvement
Albumin 5% 250 mL IV over 2 hours — colloid is superior to crystalloid in pancreatitis
Restrict fluids to 1L per 24 hours to prevent fluid overload and ARDS

explanation.correct: Correct. WATERFALL trial (2022, NEJM): aggressive LR resuscitation (500 mL/hr) vs. moderate (250 mL/hr) showed no difference in outcomes, with aggressive resuscitation causing more fluid overload. Current ACG guidelines recommend LR at 250-500 mL/hr with goals: HR <120, MAP 65-85, UO 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr. LR is preferred over NS — lactate reduces inflammatory markers and acidosis.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Normal saline at high rates can worsen pancreatitis through hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis which may increase inflammatory cascade. Lactated Ringer's is now preferred over normal saline in acute pancreatitis based on WATERFALL and other trials.
explanation.b: Correct. WATERFALL trial (2022, NEJM): aggressive LR resuscitation (500 mL/hr) vs. moderate (250 mL/hr) showed no difference in outcomes, with aggressive resuscitation causing more fluid overload. Current ACG guidelines recommend LR at 250-500 mL/hr with goals: HR <120, MAP 65-85, UO 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr. LR is preferred over NS — lactate reduces inflammatory markers and acidosis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Albumin is not recommended for initial resuscitation in acute pancreatitis. No evidence supports colloid over crystalloid in this setting. Albumin is expensive and may extravasate through inflamed capillaries.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Fluid restriction in early acute pancreatitis is associated with pancreatic necrosis and organ failure. The hemoconcentration (Hct 48%, rising BUN) indicates significant third-spacing requiring aggressive resuscitation, not restriction.

gi.question: A 52-year-old man with cirrhosis develops acute kidney injury (creatinine 3.1, baseline 0.9) after starting IV contrast for abdominal CT. Urinary sodium is 6 mEq/L, FENa 0.4%, urine osmolality 510 mOsm/kg. He is euvolemic after 1.5 L albumin challenge. Which is most likely and appropriate management?

answers.text: Contrast-induced nephropathy; IV sodium bicarbonate and N-acetylcysteine
Acute tubular necrosis from hypotension; supportive care and nephrology consult
Hepatorenal syndrome type 1 (AKI-HRS); norepinephrine plus albumin 20-40 g/day
Prerenal AKI from volume depletion; additional 2L normal saline bolus

explanation.correct: Correct. This presentation meets AKI-HRS criteria: AKI in cirrhotic patient with ascites, low FENa (<1%), low urine Na, concentrated urine indicating intact tubular function, no response to volume challenge (1.5g/kg albumin already given). Norepinephrine (0.5-3 mg/hr) plus albumin 20-40 g/day is the preferred ICU regimen (midodrine+octreotide for non-ICU). Treat until creatinine returns within 0.3 mg/dL of baseline.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Contrast-induced nephropathy typically peaks at 2-3 days and resolves by 7-10 days. However, the low FENa (0.4%), low urine sodium (6), and concentrated urine (osm 510) after a volume challenge suggest intact tubular function — pointing to HRS rather than ATN or CIN. CIN shows similar indices but occurs specifically 24-72h after contrast.
explanation.b: Incorrect. ATN shows FENa >2%, urine sodium >40, urine osmolality <350, muddy brown granular casts — opposite of this patient's findings. The intact tubular function indices (low FENa, concentrated urine) indicate functional rather than structural kidney injury.
explanation.c: Correct. This presentation meets AKI-HRS criteria: AKI in cirrhotic patient with ascites, low FENa (<1%), low urine Na, concentrated urine indicating intact tubular function, no response to volume challenge (1.5g/kg albumin already given). Norepinephrine (0.5-3 mg/hr) plus albumin 20-40 g/day is the preferred ICU regimen (midodrine+octreotide for non-ICU). Treat until creatinine returns within 0.3 mg/dL of baseline.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Volume challenge with albumin 1.5 g/kg was already performed without improvement — this rules out simple prerenal AKI. Additional saline in a cirrhotic patient risks worsening ascites and hyponatremia without benefit for HRS.

gi.question: A 72-year-old man on aspirin and ibuprofen presents with melena and hemoglobin of 7.4 (baseline unknown). He is hemodynamically stable after 1 unit pRBC. Endoscopy shows a 1.2 cm duodenal ulcer with a visible vessel (Forrest Ia). Which is the most appropriate endoscopic and post-endoscopic management?

answers.text: Endoscopic injection of epinephrine alone; oral PPI indefinitely
Dual endoscopic therapy (epinephrine injection plus thermal coagulation or hemoclip); IV PPI 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr for 72 hours
Conservative management with IV PPI only; repeat endoscopy in 24 hours
Emergent surgical gastroduodenostomy — Forrest Ia carries >90% rebleeding risk

explanation.correct: Correct. Forrest Ia (active spurting) is highest risk. Dual endoscopic therapy (epinephrine + thermal/clip) reduces rebleeding vs monotherapy. Post-endoscopy: high-dose IV PPI (80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr × 72 hours) reduces rebleed and mortality in high-risk stigmata (NEJM 2001). Discontinue NSAIDs. H. pylori testing and eradication if positive.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Epinephrine injection alone is inferior to combination (dual) endoscopic therapy. Epinephrine achieves hemostasis by local vasoconstriction and tamponade but rebleeding rates are high without thermal coagulation or mechanical clip. ESGE guidelines specifically recommend dual therapy for high-risk stigmata.
explanation.b: Correct. Forrest Ia (active spurting) is highest risk. Dual endoscopic therapy (epinephrine + thermal/clip) reduces rebleeding vs monotherapy. Post-endoscopy: high-dose IV PPI (80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr × 72 hours) reduces rebleed and mortality in high-risk stigmata (NEJM 2001). Discontinue NSAIDs. H. pylori testing and eradication if positive.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Conservative management without endoscopic therapy for active arterial bleeding (Forrest Ia) has rebleeding rates >90%. Endoscopic hemostasis is mandatory. IV PPI without endoscopic therapy is insufficient for active spurting vessel.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Surgery is reserved for endoscopic failure (persistent bleeding after two attempts) or recurrent bleeding after repeat endoscopy. With modern dual endoscopic therapy, surgical rates for peptic ulcer bleeding have fallen below 5%. Immediate surgery without endoscopic attempt is not appropriate.

gi.question: A 68-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation presents with sudden severe periumbilical pain, vomiting, and watery diarrhea. She describes the pain as 'out of proportion' to exam findings. WBC 18,000, lactate 4.2 mmol/L. CT angiography shows filling defect in the superior mesenteric artery. Which is the most appropriate intervention?

answers.text: IV heparin, bowel rest, and repeat CT in 6 hours to monitor for progression
Emergent surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis/thrombectomy with laparotomy if bowel viability is compromised
IV antibiotics (pip-tazo) for presumed mesenteric ischemia with bacterial translocation
Colonoscopy to evaluate for ischemic colitis before deciding on intervention

explanation.correct: Correct. Acute SMA embolism with signs of bowel compromise (lactate 4.2, WBC 18K) requires immediate intervention. Options: endovascular (catheter-directed thrombolysis/aspiration thrombectomy) or open embolectomy. Laparotomy to assess bowel viability and resect non-viable segments is often required. 'Pain out of proportion to exam' is the classic teaching point for acute mesenteric ischemia. Time from onset to revascularization is critical — every hour increases bowel loss.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Anticoagulation alone is appropriate for non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia (NOMI) or venous thrombosis. In acute SMA embolism with elevated lactate (4.2) and WBC, the window for bowel salvage is closing. Watchful waiting with repeat imaging delays definitive treatment and risks progression to transmural infarction.
explanation.b: Correct. Acute SMA embolism with signs of bowel compromise (lactate 4.2, WBC 18K) requires immediate intervention. Options: endovascular (catheter-directed thrombolysis/aspiration thrombectomy) or open embolectomy. Laparotomy to assess bowel viability and resect non-viable segments is often required. 'Pain out of proportion to exam' is the classic teaching point for acute mesenteric ischemia. Time from onset to revascularization is critical — every hour increases bowel loss.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Antibiotics alone treat bacterial translocation but do not restore mesenteric blood flow. They may be added as adjunct therapy to cover gut organisms during/after revascularization, but they are not definitive treatment for arterial occlusion.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Colonoscopy evaluates left-sided ischemia (IMA territory). SMA occlusion affects the small bowel and right colon. Colonoscopy is not the appropriate diagnostic tool for SMA embolism and would fatally delay vascular intervention.

gi.question: A 54-year-old man with short bowel syndrome (80 cm remaining small bowel post-massive resection) is being transitioned from TPN to enteral feeds. He develops diarrhea (8 loose stools/day), bloating, and D-lactic acidosis. Which bacteria and mechanism is responsible for D-lactic acidosis?

answers.text: Clostridium difficile overgrowth producing cytotoxin B causing secretory diarrhea
Gram-positive colonic bacteria (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium) ferment unabsorbed carbohydrates producing D-lactate, which humans cannot metabolize
Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth of E. coli producing endotoxin causing mucosal injury
Candida overgrowth in a shortened bowel producing arabinose fermentation products

explanation.correct: Correct. D-lactic acidosis in short bowel syndrome: unabsorbed carbohydrates reach the colon where gram-positive anaerobes (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Streptococcus) ferment them to D-lactate. Humans lack D-lactate dehydrogenase to metabolize it. Accumulates causing encephalopathy, ataxia, and high anion gap acidosis. Treatment: limit carbohydrates, antibiotics (metronidazole or vancomycin oral), probiotics are CONTRAINDICATED.
explanation.a: Incorrect. C. difficile produces toxins A and B causing pseudomembranous colitis. It does not produce D-lactate. C. diff colitis is a different entity — diagnosed by PCR/toxin assay, not by D-lactic acidosis.
explanation.b: Correct. D-lactic acidosis in short bowel syndrome: unabsorbed carbohydrates reach the colon where gram-positive anaerobes (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Streptococcus) ferment them to D-lactate. Humans lack D-lactate dehydrogenase to metabolize it. Accumulates causing encephalopathy, ataxia, and high anion gap acidosis. Treatment: limit carbohydrates, antibiotics (metronidazole or vancomycin oral), probiotics are CONTRAINDICATED.
explanation.c: Incorrect. SIBO with gram-negatives causes different problems (bile acid deconjugation, vitamin B12 deficiency, fat malabsorption) but not D-lactic acidosis specifically. D-lactic acidosis requires colonic fermentation by gram-positive bacteria of unabsorbed substrates.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Candida overgrowth causes mucosal invasion and systemic infection in immunocompromised patients. It does not produce D-lactate. Arabinose fermentation is not the mechanism of D-lactic acidosis.

gi.question: A 38-year-old woman status post-Whipple procedure develops worsening ventilator requirements (plateau pressure 38 cmH₂O, previously 24) and oliguria on POD 3. Bladder pressure is 22 mmHg. Which is the MOST important immediate non-surgical intervention while surgical evaluation is arranged?

answers.text: Increase PEEP to 12 cmH₂O to recruit atelectatic lung from abdominal pressure
NG decompression, rectal tube, and neuromuscular blockade to reduce abdominal wall tension
IV furosemide 40 mg to reduce fluid overload contributing to abdominal hypertension
Increase vasopressor dose to maintain MAP and CPP despite elevated IAP

explanation.correct: Correct. Non-surgical measures for intra-abdominal hypertension (IAP 22 = Grade III): NG tube decompression reduces gastric/intestinal gas, rectal tube decompresses colonic gas, neuromuscular blockade reduces abdominal wall tension (can reduce IAP 10-15 mmHg acutely). These are temporizing measures while surgical decompression is arranged for IAP ≥20 with organ dysfunction.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Increasing PEEP in intra-abdominal hypertension worsens venous return and increases intrathoracic pressure — the abdominal-thoracic pressure transmission already contributes to elevated plateau pressures. Higher PEEP compounds the problem.
explanation.b: Correct. Non-surgical measures for intra-abdominal hypertension (IAP 22 = Grade III): NG tube decompression reduces gastric/intestinal gas, rectal tube decompresses colonic gas, neuromuscular blockade reduces abdominal wall tension (can reduce IAP 10-15 mmHg acutely). These are temporizing measures while surgical decompression is arranged for IAP ≥20 with organ dysfunction.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Furosemide reduces intravascular volume, worsening cardiac output and organ perfusion in IAH. The abdominal compartment syndrome is from tissue edema and bowel distension — diuresis does not reliably reduce IAP and may worsen hemodynamics.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Increasing vasopressors maintains MAP but does not address the mechanical cause of oliguria (renal vein compression from elevated IAP). The renal compression requires IAP reduction, not just pressure augmentation.

gi.question: A 62-year-old man on aspirin and naproxen presents with hematemesis and melena. HR 118 bpm, BP 86/52 mmHg, hemoglobin 6.4 g/dL. After initiating 2 large-bore IVs and fluid resuscitation, what is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Emergent upper endoscopy (EGD) within 12–24 hours after hemodynamic stabilization
Transfuse PRBCs to target hemoglobin >7 g/dL, IV PPI bolus, and urgent EGD
CT angiography of the abdomen to localize bleeding before endoscopy
Insert a Blakemore tube for immediate tamponade

explanation.correct: Correct. Management of acute upper GI hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability: (1) Resuscitate with PRBCs targeting Hb >7 g/dL (or >9 in active CAD); (2) IV PPI (pantoprazole 80 mg bolus, then 8 mg/hr drip) — reduces rebleeding by raising gastric pH; (3) Urgent EGD after hemodynamic stabilization for diagnosis and therapeutic intervention (epinephrine injection, clips, cautery). Hold NSAIDs permanently.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While EGD within 24 hours is standard for upper GI bleeds, this patient has hemodynamic instability and signs of massive hemorrhage. Blood product resuscitation must be initiated immediately along with ICU-level monitoring. EGD should be performed urgently after initial stabilization, ideally within 12 hours.
explanation.b: Correct. Management of acute upper GI hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability: (1) Resuscitate with PRBCs targeting Hb >7 g/dL (or >9 in active CAD); (2) IV PPI (pantoprazole 80 mg bolus, then 8 mg/hr drip) — reduces rebleeding by raising gastric pH; (3) Urgent EGD after hemodynamic stabilization for diagnosis and therapeutic intervention (epinephrine injection, clips, cautery). Hold NSAIDs permanently.
explanation.c: Incorrect. CTA is useful for lower GI or obscure GI bleeding when the source is unclear. In a patient with hematemesis (indicating upper GI source), EGD is both diagnostic and therapeutic. CTA delays definitive endoscopic management without adding clinical value for upper GI bleeds.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Balloon tamponade (Blakemore or Minnesota tube) is a temporizing measure specifically for variceal hemorrhage unresponsive to endoscopic therapy. It is not first-line for upper GI bleeding and carries significant risks (esophageal rupture, aspiration). There is no indication of variceal bleeding in this patient on NSAIDs.

gi.question: A 58-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) presents with confusion, asterixis, and a serum ammonia of 142 μmol/L. He has no fever, and infection workup is negative. He had a large upper GI bleed 2 days ago. What is the most appropriate treatment for his hepatic encephalopathy?

answers.text: Lactulose 30 mL PO/NG q2h until 2–3 bowel movements per day
IV flumazenil to reverse benzodiazepine-like substances
Hemodialysis to remove ammonia
Oral neomycin 1 g QID

explanation.correct: Correct. Lactulose is first-line treatment for hepatic encephalopathy. It works by: (1) osmotic catharsis clearing nitrogen-rich blood from the GI tract (especially important after GI bleed), (2) acidifying colonic pH which converts absorbable NH3 to non-absorbable NH4+, and (3) promoting bacterial metabolism of ammonia. Dose is titrated to 2–3 soft bowel movements per day. Rifaximin is added as adjunctive therapy for prevention of recurrence.
explanation.a: Correct. Lactulose is first-line treatment for hepatic encephalopathy. It works by: (1) osmotic catharsis clearing nitrogen-rich blood from the GI tract (especially important after GI bleed), (2) acidifying colonic pH which converts absorbable NH3 to non-absorbable NH4+, and (3) promoting bacterial metabolism of ammonia. Dose is titrated to 2–3 soft bowel movements per day. Rifaximin is added as adjunctive therapy for prevention of recurrence.
explanation.b: Incorrect. While the GABA hypothesis suggests that endogenous benzodiazepine-like substances may contribute to hepatic encephalopathy, flumazenil is not standard therapy. It may produce transient improvement in some patients but is not recommended for routine use and does not address ammonia metabolism.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Standard hemodialysis is not effective for ammonia removal in hepatic encephalopathy. While continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) with albumin dialysis (MARS) has been explored, lactulose remains the standard first-line therapy. Ammonia removal via GI tract manipulation is far more practical and effective.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While neomycin (a non-absorbable aminoglycoside) can reduce ammonia-producing gut bacteria, it carries risks of ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity with prolonged use. Rifaximin has replaced neomycin as the preferred adjunctive antibiotic due to its superior safety profile. Lactulose remains first-line.

gi.question: A 48-year-old woman presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting. Lipase is 1,840 U/L (normal <60). CT abdomen shows pancreatic necrosis involving >30% of the gland. On day 3, she develops fever, leukocytosis (WBC 22,000), and worsening abdominal pain. What is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Start empiric IV meropenem for infected necrosis
CT-guided fine needle aspiration (FNA) of the necrotic collection
Emergent surgical necrosectomy (open debridement)
Repeat lipase and amylase to assess disease progression

explanation.correct: Correct. When a patient with pancreatic necrosis develops signs of infection (fever, leukocytosis, clinical deterioration), CT-guided FNA with Gram stain and culture is the gold standard to distinguish sterile from infected necrosis. If infection is confirmed, targeted antibiotics (carbapenems penetrate pancreatic tissue best) are started and minimally invasive necrosectomy is planned. FNA guides management and prevents unnecessary antibiotic use.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Prophylactic antibiotics for sterile pancreatic necrosis are NOT recommended per current guidelines (AGA, ACG). Antibiotics should only be started when there is evidence of infected necrosis. The appropriate diagnostic step is to confirm infection before starting antibiotics.
explanation.b: Correct. When a patient with pancreatic necrosis develops signs of infection (fever, leukocytosis, clinical deterioration), CT-guided FNA with Gram stain and culture is the gold standard to distinguish sterile from infected necrosis. If infection is confirmed, targeted antibiotics (carbapenems penetrate pancreatic tissue best) are started and minimally invasive necrosectomy is planned. FNA guides management and prevents unnecessary antibiotic use.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Early open surgical necrosectomy (<4 weeks) is associated with high morbidity and mortality. Current guidelines favor a step-up approach: delay intervention to allow walled-off necrosis to mature (4+ weeks), then use percutaneous drainage or endoscopic necrosectomy before open surgery. FNA to confirm infection should precede any intervention.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Serial lipase/amylase values do not correlate with disease severity or predict complications in acute pancreatitis. Once pancreatitis is diagnosed, trending enzymes adds no clinical value. The concern here is infected necrosis, which requires CT-guided FNA for diagnosis.

gi.question: A 26-year-old woman with known Crohn's disease presents with severe diarrhea (10+ bloody stools/day), abdominal pain, and weight loss despite being on mesalamine. Labs show CRP 48 mg/L and fecal calprotectin >500 μg/g. Colonoscopy reveals deep ulcerations with skip lesions throughout the ileum and colon. What is the most appropriate treatment escalation?

answers.text: Switch to high-dose oral prednisone 40 mg daily
Initiate a biologic agent (anti-TNF: infliximab or adalimumab)
Increase mesalamine to maximum dose (4.8 g/day)
Add methotrexate 25 mg IM weekly as monotherapy

explanation.correct: Correct. For moderate-to-severe Crohn's disease failing first-line therapy (5-ASA), biologic agents are the standard escalation. Anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab) induce and maintain remission, promote mucosal healing, and reduce surgery rates. Current guidelines increasingly recommend early biologic therapy for aggressive disease. Combination therapy with an immunomodulator (azathioprine) may improve efficacy.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While corticosteroids (prednisone) can induce remission in acute Crohn's flares, they are NOT effective for maintenance therapy and have significant long-term side effects (osteoporosis, diabetes, adrenal suppression). This patient needs a steroid-sparing disease-modifying therapy.
explanation.b: Correct. For moderate-to-severe Crohn's disease failing first-line therapy (5-ASA), biologic agents are the standard escalation. Anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab) induce and maintain remission, promote mucosal healing, and reduce surgery rates. Current guidelines increasingly recommend early biologic therapy for aggressive disease. Combination therapy with an immunomodulator (azathioprine) may improve efficacy.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Mesalamine (5-ASA) is primarily effective for mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis and has limited efficacy in Crohn's disease, especially for ileal involvement and severe disease. Escalating a failing 5-ASA agent is insufficient for this degree of disease activity.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While methotrexate has some efficacy in Crohn's disease (particularly for steroid-dependent patients), it is generally less effective than anti-TNF biologics for moderate-to-severe disease. Current guidelines favor biologics over methotrexate as the primary escalation step for this severity of disease.

gi.question: A 52-year-old man with cirrhosis and tense ascites undergoes large-volume paracentesis (6 liters removed). His ascitic fluid shows WBC 450 cells/μL with 68% neutrophils, protein 1.1 g/dL. What TWO concerns must be addressed based on these findings?

answers.text: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) — start IV ceftriaxone; and post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction — administer IV albumin
Malignant ascites — send cytology and consult oncology
Secondary bacterial peritonitis — emergent CT and surgical consult
Chylous ascites — check triglycerides and start octreotide

explanation.correct: Correct. Two critical issues: (1) The ascitic fluid PMN count >250 cells/μL (here: 450 × 0.68 = 306, which exceeds the diagnostic threshold of ≥250 PMN/μL) meets criteria for SBP. Empiric IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily is first-line. (2) Removal of >5 L of ascites requires IV albumin (6–8 g per liter removed) to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction (PPCD), which causes renal failure and hepatorenal syndrome.
explanation.a: Correct. Two critical issues: (1) The ascitic fluid PMN count >250 cells/μL (here: 450 × 0.68 = 306, which exceeds the diagnostic threshold of ≥250 PMN/μL) meets criteria for SBP. Empiric IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily is first-line. (2) Removal of >5 L of ascites requires IV albumin (6–8 g per liter removed) to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction (PPCD), which causes renal failure and hepatorenal syndrome.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Malignant ascites typically has elevated protein (>2.5 g/dL) and positive cytology. This fluid has low protein (1.1 g/dL), consistent with portal hypertensive (cirrhotic) ascites, not malignancy. The elevated neutrophil count suggests infection (SBP), not cancer.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Secondary peritonitis (from bowel perforation, abscess) shows very high WBC counts, multiple organisms on culture, protein >1 g/dL, and glucose <50 mg/dL. While protein is elevated here, the clinical context (cirrhosis with ascites, monomicrobial pattern typical of SBP) favors primary SBP. If SBP treatment fails, then secondary peritonitis should be considered.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Chylous ascites has a milky appearance with triglycerides >200 mg/dL, seen in lymphatic obstruction or disruption. This fluid shows no features of chylous ascites. The findings (low protein, elevated PMNs in cirrhosis) are diagnostic of SBP.

gi.question: A 75-year-old man with atrial fibrillation (not anticoagulated) presents with sudden-onset severe periumbilical abdominal pain that is out of proportion to physical examination findings. Abdomen is soft and non-tender initially. Lactate is 6.8 mmol/L. WBC is 18,000/μL. What diagnosis should be suspected, and what is the diagnostic study of choice?

answers.text: Acute mesenteric ischemia — CT angiography of the abdomen
Perforated peptic ulcer — upright abdominal X-ray
Acute pancreatitis — serum lipase
Small bowel obstruction — CT abdomen with oral contrast

explanation.correct: Correct. The classic presentation of acute mesenteric ischemia is severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam in a patient with an embolic source (atrial fibrillation). The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) is the most common site of embolic occlusion. Elevated lactate indicates bowel ischemia. CT angiography is the diagnostic study of choice (sensitivity >95%) and can identify arterial occlusion. Treatment is emergent revascularization (surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis) and resection of nonviable bowel.
explanation.a: Correct. The classic presentation of acute mesenteric ischemia is severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam in a patient with an embolic source (atrial fibrillation). The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) is the most common site of embolic occlusion. Elevated lactate indicates bowel ischemia. CT angiography is the diagnostic study of choice (sensitivity >95%) and can identify arterial occlusion. Treatment is emergent revascularization (surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis) and resection of nonviable bowel.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Perforated ulcer presents with acute peritonitis (rigid abdomen, guarding, rebound tenderness) and free air on imaging. This patient's abdomen is soft and non-tender — the pain-exam discordance is the hallmark of mesenteric ischemia, not perforation.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While acute pancreatitis causes severe abdominal pain, it presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea/vomiting, and elevated lipase. The pain-exam discordance and embolic risk factor (AF) make mesenteric ischemia far more likely.
explanation.d: Incorrect. SBO presents with colicky pain, distension, vomiting, and obstipation, with a transition point on imaging. This patient's presentation (sudden-onset pain out of proportion to exam, AF as embolic source, elevated lactate) is classic for mesenteric ischemia, not mechanical obstruction.

gi.question: A 52-year-old man presents with acute gallstone pancreatitis. After initial NPO management, IV fluids, and pain control, CT shows a 1.5 cm stone impacted in the common bile duct with biliary dilation and rising bilirubin (8.2 mg/dL). What is the most appropriate intervention?

answers.text: Emergent cholecystectomy with intraoperative cholangiogram
Conservative management — most gallstones pass spontaneously
ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction
Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) and drainage

explanation.correct: Correct. ERCP with endoscopic sphincterotomy and stone extraction is the definitive procedure for impacted CBD stones causing biliary obstruction. It should be performed urgently (<24 hours) in gallstone pancreatitis with ongoing biliary obstruction or cholangitis. Cholecystectomy should then be performed during the same hospitalization (ideally within 72 hours) to prevent recurrent stone migration.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While cholecystectomy is ultimately needed to prevent recurrent gallstone pancreatitis, it does not address the impacted CBD stone causing ongoing biliary obstruction. The immediate priority is endoscopic stone removal, with cholecystectomy performed during the same hospitalization after resolution.
explanation.b: Incorrect. While small stones may pass spontaneously, this patient has an impacted 1.5 cm CBD stone with progressive biliary obstruction (rising bilirubin, biliary dilation). Impacted CBD stones require intervention — they will not pass spontaneously and risk cholangitis and worsening pancreatitis.
explanation.c: Correct. ERCP with endoscopic sphincterotomy and stone extraction is the definitive procedure for impacted CBD stones causing biliary obstruction. It should be performed urgently (<24 hours) in gallstone pancreatitis with ongoing biliary obstruction or cholangitis. Cholecystectomy should then be performed during the same hospitalization (ideally within 72 hours) to prevent recurrent stone migration.
explanation.d: Incorrect. PTC is an alternative biliary decompression method used when ERCP fails or is not feasible (e.g., altered surgical anatomy, duodenal obstruction). ERCP is the first-line approach for CBD stone extraction due to its therapeutic capability and lower complication rate.

gi.question: A 55-year-old man with known liver cirrhosis presents with massive hematemesis. He is tachycardic (HR 132) and hypotensive (BP 74/42). A nasogastric tube returns bright red blood. After initiating resuscitation with PRBCs and crystalloid, what pharmacologic agent should be started immediately for suspected variceal hemorrhage?

answers.text: IV octreotide 50 mcg bolus followed by 50 mcg/hr infusion
IV pantoprazole 80 mg bolus and 8 mg/hr drip
IV tranexamic acid (TXA) 1 g bolus
IV vasopressin 0.4 units/min

explanation.correct: Correct. In suspected variceal hemorrhage, IV octreotide (a somatostatin analog) should be started immediately — even before endoscopy. Octreotide reduces portal venous pressure by inhibiting splanchnic vasodilation and reducing portal blood flow, which helps control variceal bleeding. It is continued for 3–5 days. Additionally, IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1 g/day) should be given to cirrhotic patients with GI bleed to reduce infection risk and rebleeding. Urgent EGD for band ligation is the definitive intervention.
explanation.a: Correct. In suspected variceal hemorrhage, IV octreotide (a somatostatin analog) should be started immediately — even before endoscopy. Octreotide reduces portal venous pressure by inhibiting splanchnic vasodilation and reducing portal blood flow, which helps control variceal bleeding. It is continued for 3–5 days. Additionally, IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1 g/day) should be given to cirrhotic patients with GI bleed to reduce infection risk and rebleeding. Urgent EGD for band ligation is the definitive intervention.
explanation.b: Incorrect. IV PPI therapy is appropriate for non-variceal upper GI bleeding (peptic ulcer disease). In variceal hemorrhage, the mechanism is portal hypertension — not acid-related injury. Acid suppression does not reduce variceal bleeding. Octreotide targeting portal pressure reduction is the correct pharmacologic intervention.
explanation.c: Incorrect. The HALT-IT trial showed that tranexamic acid does NOT reduce mortality in GI bleeding and may increase thromboembolic complications. TXA is not recommended for GI hemorrhage per current guidelines.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While vasopressin reduces portal pressure and was historically used for variceal bleeding, it causes systemic vasoconstriction with significant side effects (mesenteric and coronary ischemia, arrhythmias). Octreotide has largely replaced vasopressin due to its superior safety profile and equivalent efficacy.

gi.question: A 50-year-old man with cirrhosis presents with new-onset ascites. Diagnostic paracentesis shows a serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) of 1.8 g/dL and total protein of 1.0 g/dL. He is currently on no diuretics. What is the most appropriate initial management of his ascites?

answers.text: Sodium restriction (2 g/day) and oral spironolactone 100 mg daily
Therapeutic large-volume paracentesis with albumin replacement
Fluid restriction to 1 liter per day
TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) placement

explanation.correct: Correct. Initial management of cirrhotic ascites includes dietary sodium restriction (≤2 g/day) and diuretics. Spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist) is the first-line diuretic because cirrhotic ascites is driven by secondary hyperaldosteronism. Furosemide may be added if response is inadequate (typical combination: spironolactone 100 mg + furosemide 40 mg, maintaining a 100:40 ratio). SAAG ≥1.1 confirms portal hypertension as the cause.
explanation.a: Correct. Initial management of cirrhotic ascites includes dietary sodium restriction (≤2 g/day) and diuretics. Spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist) is the first-line diuretic because cirrhotic ascites is driven by secondary hyperaldosteronism. Furosemide may be added if response is inadequate (typical combination: spironolactone 100 mg + furosemide 40 mg, maintaining a 100:40 ratio). SAAG ≥1.1 confirms portal hypertension as the cause.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Large-volume paracentesis is reserved for tense or refractory ascites causing respiratory compromise or discomfort. For new-onset moderate ascites, medical management with sodium restriction and diuretics is the appropriate first step.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Fluid restriction is indicated for hyponatremia (serum Na <125 mEq/L) in cirrhosis, not routine ascites management. Sodium restriction is the key dietary intervention. Unnecessary fluid restriction reduces quality of life without improving ascites control.
explanation.d: Incorrect. TIPS is reserved for refractory ascites that fails maximal diuretic therapy (spironolactone 400 mg + furosemide 160 mg/day) or requires frequent large-volume paracentesis. It is not first-line treatment. TIPS carries risks of hepatic encephalopathy and should be considered after medical therapy fails.

gi.question: A 68-year-old woman on anticoagulation presents with left lower quadrant pain, fever, and leukocytosis. CT shows acute diverticulitis with a 5 cm pericolic abscess. She is hemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate management?

answers.text: Emergent surgical resection (Hartmann procedure)
IV antibiotics alone and close monitoring
CT-guided percutaneous abscess drainage plus IV antibiotics
Oral metronidazole and ciprofloxacin with outpatient follow-up

explanation.correct: Correct. For complicated diverticulitis with a contained abscess ≥3 cm in a stable patient, CT-guided percutaneous drainage plus IV antibiotics (covering gram-negatives and anaerobes: piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem) is the standard initial approach. This allows source control without the morbidity of emergent surgery. Elective sigmoid colectomy can be considered 6–8 weeks later after inflammation resolves.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Emergent surgery is indicated for free perforation with peritonitis, hemodynamic instability, or failed percutaneous drainage. A contained pericolic abscess in a stable patient should first be managed with percutaneous drainage and antibiotics. Surgery can be considered electively after resolution.
explanation.b: Incorrect. While small abscesses (<3 cm) may resolve with antibiotics alone, a 5 cm abscess is unlikely to resolve without drainage. Antibiotic-only management for large abscesses has higher failure rates and may lead to sepsis or perforation.
explanation.c: Correct. For complicated diverticulitis with a contained abscess ≥3 cm in a stable patient, CT-guided percutaneous drainage plus IV antibiotics (covering gram-negatives and anaerobes: piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem) is the standard initial approach. This allows source control without the morbidity of emergent surgery. Elective sigmoid colectomy can be considered 6–8 weeks later after inflammation resolves.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Oral antibiotics with outpatient management is appropriate for uncomplicated diverticulitis. A 5 cm abscess is a complicated presentation requiring IV antibiotics, percutaneous drainage, and inpatient monitoring.

gi.question: A 70-year-old man presents with painless bright red blood per rectum. He is hemodynamically stable with hemoglobin of 10.2 g/dL. There is no nasogastric aspirate concerning for upper GI source. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic study?

answers.text: Colonoscopy after rapid bowel preparation
CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis
Tagged red blood cell scan
Emergent mesenteric angiography with embolization

explanation.correct: Correct. For hemodynamically stable acute lower GI bleeding, colonoscopy is the first-line diagnostic and therapeutic modality. It identifies the source in 70–90% of cases and allows therapeutic intervention (epinephrine injection, clips, cautery). Bowel preparation improves visualization and diagnostic yield. Common causes in this age group include diverticular bleeding, angiodysplasia, and colorectal neoplasm.
explanation.a: Correct. For hemodynamically stable acute lower GI bleeding, colonoscopy is the first-line diagnostic and therapeutic modality. It identifies the source in 70–90% of cases and allows therapeutic intervention (epinephrine injection, clips, cautery). Bowel preparation improves visualization and diagnostic yield. Common causes in this age group include diverticular bleeding, angiodysplasia, and colorectal neoplasm.
explanation.b: Incorrect. CTA is useful when bleeding is brisk (active extravasation rate >0.5 mL/min) and the patient is hemodynamically unstable or colonoscopy cannot identify the source. In a stable patient, colonoscopy provides both diagnosis and treatment, making it the preferred initial study.
explanation.c: Incorrect. RBC scintigraphy detects bleeding at rates as low as 0.1 mL/min but provides poor anatomic localization. It is used when colonoscopy is non-diagnostic and the patient has intermittent bleeding. It cannot provide therapeutic intervention. Colonoscopy is preferred in stable patients.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Angiography requires an active bleeding rate >1 mL/min for detection and is reserved for massive hemorrhage or when colonoscopy fails. It is an invasive procedure with risks of bowel ischemia from embolization. This stable patient should undergo colonoscopy first.

gi.question: A 45-year-old man with chronic hepatitis C and compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A) is found to have a 2.5 cm liver mass on surveillance ultrasound. AFP is 85 ng/mL. Contrast-enhanced MRI shows arterial phase hyperenhancement with washout on the portal venous phase. What is the diagnosis and recommended management?

answers.text: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) — refer for surgical resection or liver transplant evaluation
Benign hemangioma — no further workup needed
Indeterminate lesion — repeat imaging in 3 months
Focal nodular hyperplasia — reassurance and annual ultrasound

explanation.correct: Correct. The combination of cirrhosis, a liver mass with arterial hyperenhancement and portal venous washout on contrast-enhanced imaging, and elevated AFP is diagnostic of HCC — biopsy is NOT required per AASLD guidelines when imaging is characteristic (LI-RADS 5). In compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A) with a single lesion ≤3 cm, surgical resection is first-line. Transplant evaluation is also appropriate if within Milan criteria (single tumor ≤5 cm or up to 3 tumors each ≤3 cm).
explanation.a: Correct. The combination of cirrhosis, a liver mass with arterial hyperenhancement and portal venous washout on contrast-enhanced imaging, and elevated AFP is diagnostic of HCC — biopsy is NOT required per AASLD guidelines when imaging is characteristic (LI-RADS 5). In compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A) with a single lesion ≤3 cm, surgical resection is first-line. Transplant evaluation is also appropriate if within Milan criteria (single tumor ≤5 cm or up to 3 tumors each ≤3 cm).
explanation.b: Incorrect. Hemangiomas show peripheral nodular enhancement with centripetal fill-in on MRI — NOT arterial hyperenhancement with washout. The combination of arterial enhancement with washout in a cirrhotic liver is characteristic of HCC, not a benign vascular lesion.
explanation.c: Incorrect. When a lesion in a cirrhotic liver demonstrates both arterial hyperenhancement AND washout on contrast-enhanced MRI, the specificity for HCC is >95%. This meets LI-RADS 5 criteria (definitely HCC) and does not require additional imaging or biopsy. Delaying treatment is inappropriate.
explanation.d: Incorrect. FNH shows arterial hyperenhancement but does NOT show washout — it has a characteristic central scar and remains iso- or hyperintense on portal venous phase. The washout pattern seen here is a key distinguishing feature of HCC. FNH is also extremely rare in cirrhotic livers.

gi.question: A 60-year-old man undergoes EGD for epigastric pain and is found to have a duodenal ulcer. Biopsy is positive for Helicobacter pylori. He has no drug allergies. What is the recommended first-line eradication regimen?

answers.text: PPI monotherapy for 8 weeks
Quadruple therapy: PPI + bismuth subsalicylate + metronidazole + tetracycline for 14 days
Triple therapy: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin for 14 days
Amoxicillin monotherapy for 7 days

explanation.correct: Correct. Per 2024 ACG updated guidance, optimized 14-day bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI BID + bismuth subsalicylate 524 mg QID + metronidazole 500 mg TID-QID + tetracycline 500 mg QID) is the preferred empiric first-line regimen for H. pylori eradication when clarithromycin susceptibility is unknown — which is the case in most clinical settings. This regimen achieves eradication rates >85% regardless of clarithromycin resistance patterns. Confirm eradication with urea breath test or stool antigen ≥4 weeks after completing therapy.
explanation.a: Incorrect. PPI alone promotes ulcer healing but does NOT eradicate H. pylori. Without eradication, ulcer recurrence rates exceed 60% within 1 year. Combination antibiotic therapy is mandatory for H. pylori-positive ulcers.
explanation.b: Correct. Per 2024 ACG updated guidance, optimized 14-day bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI BID + bismuth subsalicylate 524 mg QID + metronidazole 500 mg TID-QID + tetracycline 500 mg QID) is the preferred empiric first-line regimen for H. pylori eradication when clarithromycin susceptibility is unknown — which is the case in most clinical settings. This regimen achieves eradication rates >85% regardless of clarithromycin resistance patterns. Confirm eradication with urea breath test or stool antigen ≥4 weeks after completing therapy.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Clarithromycin-based triple therapy was historically first-line but is no longer the preferred empiric regimen due to rising clarithromycin resistance (>15% in most US regions). Per 2024 ACG guidance, clarithromycin-containing regimens should only be used when local clarithromycin resistance is known to be <15% or when susceptibility testing confirms the organism is clarithromycin-sensitive. Bismuth quadruple therapy is now the preferred empiric first-line.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Single-antibiotic therapy has unacceptably low eradication rates (<40%) due to H. pylori resistance patterns. Multi-drug regimens are required to achieve eradication rates >80%. Amoxicillin alone is never appropriate for H. pylori treatment.

gi.question: A 35-year-old man with ulcerative colitis on infliximab presents with 3 days of worsening bloody diarrhea (15 stools/day), fever 38.8°C, HR 110, and hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL. Abdominal X-ray shows transverse colon diameter of 7.2 cm. Stool C. difficile is negative. What is the most concerning complication, and what is the management?

answers.text: Acute severe ulcerative colitis — increase infliximab dose and observe for 48 hours
Toxic megacolon — IV corticosteroids, NPO, NGT decompression, and urgent surgical consultation
C. difficile colitis — start oral vancomycin 125 mg QID despite negative test
Ischemic colitis — CT angiography and anticoagulation

explanation.correct: Correct. Toxic megacolon is defined as colonic dilation >6 cm with systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, anemia, leukocytosis). Management: NPO, IV methylprednisolone 60 mg/day (or equivalent), IV antibiotics (metronidazole + fluoroquinolone), nasogastric decompression, and serial abdominal X-rays q12h. Urgent surgical consultation is mandatory — if no improvement within 24–72 hours, emergent colectomy is required. Complications include perforation and sepsis.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While dose optimization of infliximab may be considered, this patient has signs of toxic megacolon (colonic dilation >6 cm with systemic toxicity), which is a surgical emergency if it fails to respond rapidly to medical therapy. Simply increasing infliximab and observing is dangerously inadequate.
explanation.b: Correct. Toxic megacolon is defined as colonic dilation >6 cm with systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, anemia, leukocytosis). Management: NPO, IV methylprednisolone 60 mg/day (or equivalent), IV antibiotics (metronidazole + fluoroquinolone), nasogastric decompression, and serial abdominal X-rays q12h. Urgent surgical consultation is mandatory — if no improvement within 24–72 hours, emergent colectomy is required. Complications include perforation and sepsis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While C. difficile should always be considered in UC flares, the test is negative. The colonic dilation of 7.2 cm with systemic toxicity indicates toxic megacolon — a complication of the underlying UC that requires IV steroids and surgical standby, not empiric CDI treatment.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Ischemic colitis typically occurs in elderly patients with vascular risk factors and presents with left-sided abdominal pain with bloody diarrhea. This young patient with known UC has an acute flare complicated by toxic megacolon, not mesenteric ischemia.

gi.question: A 50-year-old man presents for evaluation of abnormal liver enzymes. AST is 142 IU/L, ALT is 218 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase is 95 IU/L, and total bilirubin is 1.1 mg/dL. This pattern of liver enzyme elevation is best described as:

answers.text: Hepatocellular injury pattern
Cholestatic injury pattern
Mixed hepatocellular-cholestatic pattern
Infiltrative liver disease

explanation.correct: Correct. ALT and AST elevation with relatively normal alkaline phosphatase indicates a hepatocellular injury pattern. ALT is more specific for liver injury than AST. Common causes include viral hepatitis, drug-induced liver injury (acetaminophen, statins), alcoholic/non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and autoimmune hepatitis. The R-factor (ALT/ALP, both as multiples of ULN) >5 confirms hepatocellular pattern.
explanation.a: Correct. ALT and AST elevation with relatively normal alkaline phosphatase indicates a hepatocellular injury pattern. ALT is more specific for liver injury than AST. Common causes include viral hepatitis, drug-induced liver injury (acetaminophen, statins), alcoholic/non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and autoimmune hepatitis. The R-factor (ALT/ALP, both as multiples of ULN) >5 confirms hepatocellular pattern.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Cholestatic injury shows elevated alkaline phosphatase and GGT with relatively normal or mildly elevated AST/ALT. Causes include biliary obstruction (stones, tumors), primary biliary cholangitis, and drug reactions. This patient's ALP is normal, ruling out a cholestatic pattern.
explanation.c: Incorrect. A mixed pattern shows proportional elevation of both transaminases and ALP. The R-factor falls between 2 and 5. This patient has clearly dominant transaminase elevation with normal ALP — a pure hepatocellular pattern.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Infiltrative diseases (sarcoidosis, amyloidosis, lymphoma) typically elevate alkaline phosphatase disproportionately with mild transaminase elevation, similar to a cholestatic pattern. This patient's pattern is the opposite — dominant transaminase elevation.

gi.question: A 65-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation presents with 6 hours of diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, and bloody diarrhea. Abdomen is distended with diffuse tenderness and guarding. CT abdomen shows pneumatosis intestinalis and portal venous gas. Lactate is 8.4 mmol/L. What do these imaging findings indicate?

answers.text: Bowel ischemia with likely transmural necrosis — emergent surgical exploration
Benign pneumatosis — observation and repeat imaging in 24 hours
Small bowel obstruction with incarceration — nasogastric decompression and serial exams
C. difficile colitis with toxic dilation — IV vancomycin and metronidazole

explanation.correct: Correct. Pneumatosis intestinalis (air within the bowel wall) and portal venous gas are ominous findings indicating transmural bowel necrosis, most commonly from acute mesenteric ischemia. Combined with AF (embolic source), elevated lactate (tissue ischemia), and peritoneal signs, this patient requires emergent surgical exploration for bowel resection. Mortality exceeds 60–80% with delayed intervention.
explanation.a: Correct. Pneumatosis intestinalis (air within the bowel wall) and portal venous gas are ominous findings indicating transmural bowel necrosis, most commonly from acute mesenteric ischemia. Combined with AF (embolic source), elevated lactate (tissue ischemia), and peritoneal signs, this patient requires emergent surgical exploration for bowel resection. Mortality exceeds 60–80% with delayed intervention.
explanation.b: Incorrect. While pneumatosis intestinalis can be benign in some contexts (COPD patients, immunosuppression), the combination with portal venous gas, peritoneal signs, elevated lactate, and an embolic risk factor makes this a surgical emergency. Observation in this clinical context would be fatal.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While SBO can cause pneumatosis in advanced cases, the portal venous gas and clinical context (AF, bloody diarrhea, markedly elevated lactate) indicate mesenteric ischemia with bowel necrosis, not simple mechanical obstruction. This requires emergent surgery, not conservative management.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While severe C. difficile can cause toxic megacolon, it does not typically cause pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas. The acute presentation with AF as an embolic source and severely elevated lactate points to mesenteric ischemia with transmural necrosis.

gi.question: A 55-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use presents with severe retching followed by hematemesis. He is tachycardic (HR 122) and hypotensive (BP 84/50). EGD reveals a full-thickness esophageal perforation at the left posterolateral distal esophagus. What is the diagnosis and definitive management?

answers.text: Mallory-Weiss tear — endoscopic hemoclip placement
Boerhaave syndrome — emergent surgical repair with primary closure and mediastinal drainage
Esophageal variceal rupture — band ligation and octreotide
Peptic esophageal stricture with perforation — conservative management with IV antibiotics and TPN

explanation.correct: Correct. Boerhaave syndrome is spontaneous transmural esophageal perforation from forceful vomiting/retching, typically at the left posterolateral distal esophagus (the weakest point). It is a surgical emergency — untreated mortality approaches 100%. Diagnosis is confirmed by EGD or CT showing mediastinal/pleural air. Treatment within 24 hours: primary surgical repair with intercostal muscle flap reinforcement and mediastinal drainage. Delayed presentation may require esophageal diversion or stenting.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Mallory-Weiss tears are partial-thickness mucosal lacerations at the GE junction that cause hematemesis but are self-limited in 90% of cases. This patient has a full-thickness perforation — a far more dangerous condition requiring definitive surgical repair, not endoscopic management.
explanation.b: Correct. Boerhaave syndrome is spontaneous transmural esophageal perforation from forceful vomiting/retching, typically at the left posterolateral distal esophagus (the weakest point). It is a surgical emergency — untreated mortality approaches 100%. Diagnosis is confirmed by EGD or CT showing mediastinal/pleural air. Treatment within 24 hours: primary surgical repair with intercostal muscle flap reinforcement and mediastinal drainage. Delayed presentation may require esophageal diversion or stenting.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While this alcoholic patient could have varices, EGD has identified a full-thickness esophageal perforation — not variceal bleeding. Variceal rupture would show actively bleeding varices on EGD, not a transmural defect. The perforation requires surgical repair.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While select esophageal perforations may be managed conservatively (contained perforation, no sepsis, minimal contamination), this patient is hemodynamically unstable with a full-thickness tear from Boerhaave syndrome — this requires emergent surgical repair. Conservative management carries unacceptable mortality risk in this scenario.

gi.question: A 68-year-old woman who completed a course of IV ceftriaxone for pneumonia 1 week ago presents with watery diarrhea (8 stools/day), abdominal cramping, fever 38.6°C, and WBC 18,000/μL. Stool PCR is positive for C. difficile toxin gene. This is her first episode. What is the most appropriate treatment?

answers.text: Oral metronidazole 500 mg TID for 10–14 days
Oral vancomycin 125 mg QID for 10 days
IV vancomycin 1 g q12h for 14 days
Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) as first-line therapy

explanation.correct: Correct. Per 2021 IDSA/SHEA guidelines, oral vancomycin 125 mg QID for 10 days is standard first-line treatment for initial non-severe and severe CDI episodes. Fidaxomicin 200 mg BID for 10 days is an alternative first-line agent with lower recurrence rates. Key points: (1) ORAL vancomycin only — IV vancomycin does NOT reach the GI lumen; (2) Discontinue the offending antibiotic if possible; (3) Avoid antidiarrheals (loperamide) which can mask symptoms and precipitate toxic megacolon; (4) For fulminant CDI (hypotension, ileus, megacolon): oral vancomycin 500 mg QID PLUS IV metronidazole 500 mg q8h, with rectal vancomycin if ileus present.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Metronidazole is NO LONGER recommended as first-line for any severity of C. difficile infection. The 2021 IDSA/SHEA guidelines recommend oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin for all initial CDI episodes. Metronidazole has inferior cure rates compared to vancomycin, especially in moderate-to-severe disease.
explanation.b: Correct. Per 2021 IDSA/SHEA guidelines, oral vancomycin 125 mg QID for 10 days is standard first-line treatment for initial non-severe and severe CDI episodes. Fidaxomicin 200 mg BID for 10 days is an alternative first-line agent with lower recurrence rates. Key points: (1) ORAL vancomycin only — IV vancomycin does NOT reach the GI lumen; (2) Discontinue the offending antibiotic if possible; (3) Avoid antidiarrheals (loperamide) which can mask symptoms and precipitate toxic megacolon; (4) For fulminant CDI (hypotension, ileus, megacolon): oral vancomycin 500 mg QID PLUS IV metronidazole 500 mg q8h, with rectal vancomycin if ileus present.
explanation.c: Incorrect. IV vancomycin does NOT reach therapeutic concentrations in the colonic lumen and is ineffective for CDI. C. difficile infection requires ORAL (or rectal in ileus) vancomycin to achieve direct contact with the bacteria in the colon. This is a commonly tested distinction — IV vancomycin treats systemic MRSA infections, not gut infections.
explanation.d: Incorrect. FMT is reserved for RECURRENT CDI (≥2 recurrences or ≥3 episodes total) that has failed standard antibiotic therapy. For an initial first episode, oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin is first-line. FMT has >85% success rate for recurrent CDI by restoring normal gut microbiome diversity.

gi.question: A 32-year-old woman is transferred to the ICU with acute liver failure from acetaminophen overdose (ingested 3 days ago, NAC started late). She has grade III hepatic encephalopathy, INR 6.8, creatinine 2.6 mg/dL, pH 7.18 (arterial), and lactate 9.2 mmol/L. Using King's College Criteria for acetaminophen-induced ALF, which finding is the single strongest indicator for liver transplant evaluation?

answers.text: Grade III encephalopathy alone is sufficient
Arterial pH <7.30 after adequate fluid resuscitation
INR >6.5 is the most important isolated predictor
Lactate >9 mmol/L is the defining criterion for transplant listing

explanation.correct: Correct. King's College Criteria for acetaminophen-induced ALF have two pathways: (1) Arterial pH <7.30 after adequate fluid resuscitation — this ALONE is sufficient to meet criteria for transplant listing (single strongest predictor); OR (2) ALL THREE of: grade III–IV encephalopathy AND INR >6.5 AND creatinine >3.4 mg/dL. This patient's pH of 7.18 meets the first pathway independently, indicating a very poor prognosis without transplantation. The arterial lactate >3.5 at 12 hours or >3.0 at 24 hours post-overdose (Clichy criteria) also carries prognostic significance.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While hepatic encephalopathy grade III–IV is a component of the non-acetaminophen King's College Criteria, the acetaminophen-specific criteria differ. Encephalopathy alone is not the single strongest predictor for the acetaminophen pathway.
explanation.b: Correct. King's College Criteria for acetaminophen-induced ALF have two pathways: (1) Arterial pH <7.30 after adequate fluid resuscitation — this ALONE is sufficient to meet criteria for transplant listing (single strongest predictor); OR (2) ALL THREE of: grade III–IV encephalopathy AND INR >6.5 AND creatinine >3.4 mg/dL. This patient's pH of 7.18 meets the first pathway independently, indicating a very poor prognosis without transplantation. The arterial lactate >3.5 at 12 hours or >3.0 at 24 hours post-overdose (Clichy criteria) also carries prognostic significance.
explanation.c: Incorrect. INR >6.5 is a component of the second King's pathway but must be combined with grade III–IV encephalopathy AND creatinine >3.4 to meet criteria. An elevated INR alone does not meet King's criteria. In contrast, arterial pH <7.30 alone is sufficient.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While elevated lactate (>3.5 mmol/L) has been proposed as an addition to King's criteria (and is prognostically significant), it is not part of the original King's College Criteria. The arterial pH <7.30 remains the single strongest and most established predictor in the acetaminophen pathway.

gi.question: A 62-year-old man in the ICU on vasopressors and TPN develops right upper quadrant tenderness, fever, and leukocytosis on ICU day 12. Murphy's sign is equivocal due to sedation. Ultrasound shows gallbladder wall thickening (5 mm), pericholecystic fluid, and gallbladder distension without gallstones. What is the most likely diagnosis and management?

answers.text: Acalculous cholecystitis — percutaneous cholecystostomy tube placement
Choledocholithiasis with cholangitis — emergent ERCP
Hepatic abscess — CT-guided percutaneous drainage
Cholecystitis from sludge — conservative management with antibiotics alone

explanation.correct: Correct. Acalculous cholecystitis accounts for 5–10% of acute cholecystitis cases and occurs primarily in critically ill ICU patients (sepsis, TPN, prolonged NPO, vasopressor use causing gallbladder ischemia). Diagnosis: RUQ pain + fever + leukocytosis + US findings (wall thickening >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, distension, no stones). HIDA scan (hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid) showing non-filling of the gallbladder confirms the diagnosis if US is equivocal. In critically ill patients who are poor surgical candidates, percutaneous cholecystostomy (drainage tube) is the preferred initial intervention — it decompresses the gallbladder with minimal procedural risk. Definitive cholecystectomy can be performed when the patient stabilizes.
explanation.a: Correct. Acalculous cholecystitis accounts for 5–10% of acute cholecystitis cases and occurs primarily in critically ill ICU patients (sepsis, TPN, prolonged NPO, vasopressor use causing gallbladder ischemia). Diagnosis: RUQ pain + fever + leukocytosis + US findings (wall thickening >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, distension, no stones). HIDA scan (hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid) showing non-filling of the gallbladder confirms the diagnosis if US is equivocal. In critically ill patients who are poor surgical candidates, percutaneous cholecystostomy (drainage tube) is the preferred initial intervention — it decompresses the gallbladder with minimal procedural risk. Definitive cholecystectomy can be performed when the patient stabilizes.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Choledocholithiasis presents with CBD dilation and stones in the common bile duct, often with Charcot's triad (fever, RUQ pain, jaundice) or Reynolds' pentad. This patient has NO gallstones on ultrasound and the findings (wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid without stones) are characteristic of acalculous cholecystitis, not CBD obstruction.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Hepatic abscesses present as focal intrahepatic fluid collections on imaging, not gallbladder wall thickening with pericholecystic fluid. The ultrasound findings specifically localize the pathology to the gallbladder. Hepatic abscess would show a rim-enhancing collection within the liver parenchyma.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While biliary sludge can contribute to acalculous cholecystitis, conservative management alone carries a mortality rate of 30–50% due to gangrenous gallbladder and perforation risk. Source control (percutaneous drainage or surgery) is essential in addition to antibiotics. Delaying intervention in acalculous cholecystitis in ICU patients leads to poor outcomes.

gi.question: A 72-year-old woman with a history of prior abdominal surgeries presents with crampy diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, distension, and obstipation for 2 days. Abdominal X-ray shows dilated loops of small bowel with air-fluid levels and absence of colonic gas. She has no peritoneal signs and is hemodynamically stable. What is the most likely diagnosis and initial management?

answers.text: Large bowel obstruction — emergent colonoscopy for decompression
Small bowel obstruction from adhesions — NPO, NG decompression, IV fluids, and serial abdominal exams
Paralytic ileus — start IV neostigmine
Incarcerated hernia — emergent surgical exploration

explanation.correct: Correct. Adhesive small bowel obstruction (SBO) is the most common cause of SBO (65–75%), particularly in patients with prior abdominal surgery. Initial management of uncomplicated (non-strangulated) SBO: (1) NPO; (2) Nasogastric tube for decompression (relieves vomiting, reduces aspiration risk); (3) Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (third-spacing causes significant volume depletion); (4) Serial abdominal exams q4–6h to monitor for signs of strangulation (fever, peritoneal signs, tachycardia, worsening pain). Most adhesive SBOs resolve with conservative management within 24–72 hours. Surgical indications: closed-loop obstruction, strangulation (peritoneal signs, sepsis), failure to resolve after 3–5 days of conservative management.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Large bowel obstruction (LBO) presents with colonic distension on imaging, often with a competent ileocecal valve preventing retrograde decompression. This patient has dilated SMALL bowel with no colonic gas — consistent with small bowel obstruction, not LBO.
explanation.b: Correct. Adhesive small bowel obstruction (SBO) is the most common cause of SBO (65–75%), particularly in patients with prior abdominal surgery. Initial management of uncomplicated (non-strangulated) SBO: (1) NPO; (2) Nasogastric tube for decompression (relieves vomiting, reduces aspiration risk); (3) Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (third-spacing causes significant volume depletion); (4) Serial abdominal exams q4–6h to monitor for signs of strangulation (fever, peritoneal signs, tachycardia, worsening pain). Most adhesive SBOs resolve with conservative management within 24–72 hours. Surgical indications: closed-loop obstruction, strangulation (peritoneal signs, sepsis), failure to resolve after 3–5 days of conservative management.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Paralytic ileus presents with diffuse bowel distension (both small and large bowel) without a transition point. This patient has dilated small bowel with absence of colonic gas — suggesting a mechanical obstruction with a transition point, not diffuse dysmotility. Neostigmine is used for acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome), not mechanical SBO.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While incarcerated hernias are an important cause of SBO, the history of prior abdominal surgery makes adhesions the most likely etiology. Physical exam should include careful assessment of all hernia sites (inguinal, femoral, umbilical, incisional). If a hernia is identified and cannot be reduced, then surgery is emergent. Without evidence of hernia or strangulation, conservative management is appropriate initially.

gi.question: A patient with hepatorenal syndrome (from Q347) has failed 5 days of midodrine, octreotide, and IV albumin. Creatinine continues to rise to 4.8 mg/dL. He now has volume overload with pulmonary edema and potassium of 5.9 mEq/L. He is listed for liver transplantation but no donor organ is immediately available. What is the most appropriate bridge therapy?

answers.text: Start hemodialysis as definitive therapy — this replaces the need for liver transplantation
Renal replacement therapy (CRRT or intermittent HD) as a bridge to liver transplantation
TIPS placement to reduce portal pressure
Discontinue all treatment — HRS is uniformly fatal without transplant and further intervention is futile

explanation.correct: Correct. When pharmacologic therapy fails in HRS and the patient develops life-threatening complications of renal failure (volume overload, hyperkalemia, uremia), renal replacement therapy is indicated as a bridge to liver transplantation. CRRT is often preferred over intermittent HD in hemodynamically unstable cirrhotic patients. Liver transplantation remains the ONLY definitive treatment — renal function typically recovers post-transplant if HRS duration is <4–6 weeks. In patients not responding to midodrine/octreotide, escalation to ICU-based IV norepinephrine or terlipressin (where available) can also be attempted before initiating RRT.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Hemodialysis can temporarily support renal function in HRS, but it is NOT definitive therapy. HRS is a functional renal failure driven by hepatic decompensation — the kidneys are structurally normal. Renal function recovers after liver transplantation in most cases. Dialysis is a BRIDGE to transplant, not a replacement for it.
explanation.b: Correct. When pharmacologic therapy fails in HRS and the patient develops life-threatening complications of renal failure (volume overload, hyperkalemia, uremia), renal replacement therapy is indicated as a bridge to liver transplantation. CRRT is often preferred over intermittent HD in hemodynamically unstable cirrhotic patients. Liver transplantation remains the ONLY definitive treatment — renal function typically recovers post-transplant if HRS duration is <4–6 weeks. In patients not responding to midodrine/octreotide, escalation to ICU-based IV norepinephrine or terlipressin (where available) can also be attempted before initiating RRT.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While TIPS can improve renal perfusion by decompressing portal hypertension, it is generally CONTRAINDICATED in patients with severe hepatic dysfunction (Child-Pugh C, MELD >18, bilirubin >5) because it diverts portal blood away from the liver, worsening hepatic encephalopathy and liver function. This patient with decompensated Child-Pugh C cirrhosis is not a TIPS candidate.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While untreated HRS-AKI has a very poor prognosis (median survival <2 weeks), bridge therapies (RRT, vasoconstrictor escalation) can sustain the patient until transplantation. Approximately 50–70% of HRS patients recover renal function after successful liver transplant. Declaring futility is premature when a transplant-eligible patient has available bridge options.

gi.question: A 48-year-old obese woman (BMI 36) presents with 8 hours of constant, severe RUQ pain radiating to the right scapula, nausea, and two episodes of vomiting. She reports similar but milder episodes after fatty meals over the past 6 months that resolved within 2-3 hours. Temperature is 102.1°F, HR 104, BP 138/82. Exam shows RUQ tenderness with involuntary guarding and a positive Murphy sign. Labs: WBC 16,400 with 88% neutrophils, AST 52, ALT 68, total bilirubin 1.4, ALP 142, lipase 48 (normal). RUQ ultrasound shows a 1.8 cm gallstone impacted in the gallbladder neck, gallbladder wall thickening of 5 mm, pericholecystic fluid, and sonographic Murphy sign positive. Common bile duct measures 4 mm (normal). Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

answers.text: Elective outpatient cholecystectomy in 4-6 weeks with pain management and dietary counseling
Admit for NPO, IV fluids, IV antibiotics covering gram-negatives and anaerobes, and cholecystectomy within 72 hours
ERCP with sphincterotomy to relieve the obstruction, followed by interval cholecystectomy
HIDA scan to confirm the diagnosis before proceeding with surgery

explanation.correct: Correct. This is acute cholecystitis with all classic findings: persistent RUQ pain (>6 hours), fever, leukocytosis with left shift, positive Murphy sign, and US confirming gallstones with wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, and sonographic Murphy sign. Management: (1) NPO and IV fluid resuscitation; (2) IV antibiotics covering enteric gram-negatives and anaerobes (ceftriaxone + metronidazole, or piperacillin-tazobactam); (3) Early cholecystectomy within 72 hours (ideally within 24 hours if feasible) — early surgery reduces total hospital stay and complications versus delayed approach. The mildly elevated ALP and transaminases may reflect inflammation near the CBD but the normal CBD diameter and lipase make choledocholithiasis unlikely.
explanation.a: Incorrect. This patient has acute cholecystitis, not uncomplicated biliary colic. Acute cholecystitis (persistent pain >6 hours, fever, leukocytosis, positive Murphy sign, US with wall thickening and pericholecystic fluid) requires urgent surgical intervention, not delayed elective surgery.
explanation.b: Correct. This is acute cholecystitis with all classic findings: persistent RUQ pain (>6 hours), fever, leukocytosis with left shift, positive Murphy sign, and US confirming gallstones with wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, and sonographic Murphy sign. Management: (1) NPO and IV fluid resuscitation; (2) IV antibiotics covering enteric gram-negatives and anaerobes (ceftriaxone + metronidazole, or piperacillin-tazobactam); (3) Early cholecystectomy within 72 hours (ideally within 24 hours if feasible) — early surgery reduces total hospital stay and complications versus delayed approach. The mildly elevated ALP and transaminases may reflect inflammation near the CBD but the normal CBD diameter and lipase make choledocholithiasis unlikely.
explanation.c: Incorrect. ERCP is indicated for choledocholithiasis (CBD stones), not acute cholecystitis with a stone impacted in the gallbladder neck. The CBD is normal at 4 mm, and bilirubin is only mildly elevated. ERCP would not address the gallbladder pathology.
explanation.d: Incorrect.
