answers.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome — supportive care and continue allopurinol
Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) — immediately discontinue allopurinol, transfer to burn center, and begin supportive care
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS) — IV nafcillin
Pemphigus vulgaris — high-dose IV corticosteroids and rituximab

explanation.correct: Correct. TEN is defined as epidermal detachment involving >30% BSA (SJS <10%, SJS-TEN overlap 10–30%). Allopurinol is one of the most common causative drugs (along with carbamazepine, phenytoin, sulfonamides, NSAIDs). Immediate management: (1) Stop the offending drug immediately; (2) Transfer to a burn center or ICU; (3) Aggressive wound care (similar to major burns); (4) Fluid resuscitation; (5) Pain management. Mortality is 30–50% and correlates with BSA involvement (SCORTEN prognostic score).
explanation.a: Incorrect. While the description is consistent with SJS/TEN spectrum, SJS is defined by <10% BSA epidermal detachment. This patient has 35% BSA involvement, which classifies as TEN. Critically, the offending drug (allopurinol) must be IMMEDIATELY DISCONTINUED — it is one of the most common causes of SJS/TEN.
explanation.b: Correct. TEN is defined as epidermal detachment involving >30% BSA (SJS <10%, SJS-TEN overlap 10–30%). Allopurinol is one of the most common causative drugs (along with carbamazepine, phenytoin, sulfonamides, NSAIDs). Immediate management: (1) Stop the offending drug immediately; (2) Transfer to a burn center or ICU; (3) Aggressive wound care (similar to major burns); (4) Fluid resuscitation; (5) Pain management. Mortality is 30–50% and correlates with BSA involvement (SCORTEN prognostic score).
explanation.c: Incorrect. SSSS is caused by staphylococcal exfoliative toxins and primarily affects children/neonates and immunocompromised adults. It does NOT involve mucosal surfaces (a key differentiator from TEN) and the blistering is more superficial (within the epidermis). This patient's mucosal involvement and drug exposure history are classic for TEN.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Pemphigus vulgaris is a chronic autoimmune blistering disease with flaccid blisters and positive Nikolsky sign, but it has a gradual onset (weeks to months) and is not triggered by a specific drug exposure. The acute onset 3 weeks after starting allopurinol, rapid progression to 35% BSA, and mucosal involvement are classic for TEN.

integumentary.question: A 62-year-old diabetic man presents with exquisite pain, crepitus, and dusky discoloration of his left lower extremity. Fever is 39.8°C, HR 132, BP 86/48 mmHg. CT shows gas tracking along fascial planes. Labs reveal WBC 24,000, creatinine 2.8 mg/dL, and lactate 6.4 mmol/L. What is the definitive treatment?

answers.text: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin) alone
Emergent aggressive surgical debridement with planned re-exploration in 24–48 hours
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy as primary treatment
CT-guided percutaneous drainage of the fascial gas collection

explanation.correct: Correct. Necrotizing fasciitis is a surgical emergency. Definitive treatment is emergent wide surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue, with planned return to the OR every 24–48 hours for re-exploration and additional debridement until all necrotic tissue is removed. Delay in surgery (>24 hours) significantly increases mortality. Broad-spectrum antibiotics are adjunctive but NOT sufficient alone. Mortality: 25–35% even with aggressive treatment.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While broad-spectrum antibiotics are essential and should be started immediately, antibiotics ALONE cannot treat necrotizing fasciitis. The infection spreads along fascial planes with thrombosis of blood vessels, creating tissue with no blood supply — antibiotics cannot penetrate necrotic, avascular tissue. Surgical debridement is the definitive treatment.
explanation.b: Correct. Necrotizing fasciitis is a surgical emergency. Definitive treatment is emergent wide surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue, with planned return to the OR every 24–48 hours for re-exploration and additional debridement until all necrotic tissue is removed. Delay in surgery (>24 hours) significantly increases mortality. Broad-spectrum antibiotics are adjunctive but NOT sufficient alone. Mortality: 25–35% even with aggressive treatment.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While hyperbaric oxygen may have a role as adjunctive therapy in necrotizing fasciitis, it should NEVER delay surgical debridement. There is limited evidence supporting HBO as a primary intervention, and transferring a hemodynamically unstable patient for HBO instead of proceeding to the OR would be dangerous.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Percutaneous drainage is inadequate for necrotizing fasciitis. The infection involves widespread fascial necrosis — not a contained fluid collection. Wide open surgical debridement is required to visualize the extent of necrosis, remove all devitalized tissue, and establish drainage. Percutaneous approaches cannot accomplish this.

integumentary.question: A 78-year-old woman with a chronic venous stasis ulcer on her medial malleolus has been using compression stockings and local wound care for 6 weeks with no improvement. The wound bed shows good granulation tissue but wound edges are not advancing. What is the most appropriate next step to promote healing?

answers.text: Increase compression to 50 mmHg
Apply a skin substitute or bioengineered tissue graft
Prescribe oral systemic antibiotics for presumed biofilm
Apply dry gauze dressings to keep the wound bed dry

explanation.correct: Correct. For chronic venous ulcers that have adequate wound bed preparation (good granulation) but fail to progress after 4–6 weeks of standard compression and wound care, advanced therapies are indicated. Skin substitutes (Apligraf, Dermagraft) or bioengineered tissue grafts provide growth factors, extracellular matrix scaffolding, and cellular components that stimulate epithelial migration and wound closure.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Standard compression for venous ulcers is 30–40 mmHg. Increasing beyond this risks compromising arterial perfusion, especially if the patient has undiagnosed peripheral arterial disease. Before escalating compression, an ankle-brachial index (ABI) should be checked.
explanation.b: Correct. For chronic venous ulcers that have adequate wound bed preparation (good granulation) but fail to progress after 4–6 weeks of standard compression and wound care, advanced therapies are indicated. Skin substitutes (Apligraf, Dermagraft) or bioengineered tissue grafts provide growth factors, extracellular matrix scaffolding, and cellular components that stimulate epithelial migration and wound closure.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Systemic antibiotics are indicated for clinical infection (increasing pain, erythema, purulence, warmth, fever) — not for non-healing wounds without signs of infection. Biofilm, if suspected, is better addressed with sharp debridement and topical antimicrobial dressings, not systemic antibiotics.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Moist wound healing is the standard of care for chronic wounds. Dry gauze promotes tissue desiccation, impairs granulation, and disrupts the wound bed during dressing changes. Moist wound environments (hydrogels, foams, alginates) promote cell migration, angiogenesis, and autolytic debridement.

integumentary.question: A 28-year-old woman develops a widespread, symmetric, maculopapular rash 10 days after starting amoxicillin for strep pharyngitis. She has no mucosal involvement, no blisters, no facial edema, and vital signs are normal. The rash is mildly pruritic. What is the most likely diagnosis and management?

answers.text: Simple drug exanthem (morbilliform drug eruption) — discontinue amoxicillin, antihistamines for pruritus
Anaphylaxis — administer IM epinephrine 0.3 mg immediately
SJS — transfer to burn center immediately
DRESS syndrome — check eosinophil count and liver function

explanation.correct: Correct. Morbilliform (maculopapular) drug eruption is the most common type of drug reaction, typically appearing 7–14 days after drug initiation. It presents as a symmetric, pruritic, erythematous maculopapular rash WITHOUT mucosal involvement, blisters, or systemic toxicity. Management: discontinue the offending drug and provide symptomatic relief with oral antihistamines and topical corticosteroids. The rash resolves within 1–2 weeks. This is NOT drug allergy in the anaphylactic (IgE) sense, but the patient should be flagged for future reference.
explanation.a: Correct. Morbilliform (maculopapular) drug eruption is the most common type of drug reaction, typically appearing 7–14 days after drug initiation. It presents as a symmetric, pruritic, erythematous maculopapular rash WITHOUT mucosal involvement, blisters, or systemic toxicity. Management: discontinue the offending drug and provide symptomatic relief with oral antihistamines and topical corticosteroids. The rash resolves within 1–2 weeks. This is NOT drug allergy in the anaphylactic (IgE) sense, but the patient should be flagged for future reference.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Anaphylaxis presents with urticaria (wheals/hives), angioedema, bronchospasm, and hemodynamic instability — NOT a simple maculopapular rash. This patient has normal vital signs, no urticaria, no angioedema, and no respiratory symptoms. Epinephrine is not indicated.
explanation.c: Incorrect. SJS presents with targetoid lesions, skin pain, mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital), and epidermal detachment. This patient has no mucosal involvement, no blisters, and no targetoid lesions — features that reliably distinguish simple drug exanthem from SJS.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms (DRESS) presents 2–8 weeks after drug exposure with high fever, facial edema, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia, and organ involvement (hepatitis, nephritis). This patient lacks systemic features (no fever, no facial edema, no organ symptoms). While labs are reasonable to obtain, the presentation is most consistent with simple drug exanthem.

integumentary.question: A 72-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of burning pain in a band-like distribution across the left chest, followed by grouped vesicles on an erythematous base confined to the T4 dermatome. He has not received the shingles vaccine. What is the most appropriate treatment?

answers.text: Oral acyclovir 800 mg 5 times daily for 7 days, started within 72 hours of rash onset
Topical mupirocin ointment to the vesicles
IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h for all zoster cases
No antiviral treatment is needed if the rash has been present for more than 48 hours

explanation.correct: Correct. Herpes zoster (shingles) is caused by reactivation of varicella-zoster virus from dorsal root ganglia. Antiviral therapy (acyclovir, valacyclovir, or famciclovir) is most effective when started within 72 hours of rash onset. Acyclovir 800 mg 5 times/day for 7 days is standard; valacyclovir (1 g TID) has superior bioavailability and simpler dosing. Antivirals reduce duration of viral shedding, speed lesion healing, and reduce the incidence and severity of postherpetic neuralgia (PHN). Gabapentin or tricyclic antidepressants may be added for neuropathic pain. Recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) should be given after the acute episode resolves.
explanation.a: Correct. Herpes zoster (shingles) is caused by reactivation of varicella-zoster virus from dorsal root ganglia. Antiviral therapy (acyclovir, valacyclovir, or famciclovir) is most effective when started within 72 hours of rash onset. Acyclovir 800 mg 5 times/day for 7 days is standard; valacyclovir (1 g TID) has superior bioavailability and simpler dosing. Antivirals reduce duration of viral shedding, speed lesion healing, and reduce the incidence and severity of postherpetic neuralgia (PHN). Gabapentin or tricyclic antidepressants may be added for neuropathic pain. Recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) should be given after the acute episode resolves.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Mupirocin is a topical antibacterial used for impetigo and MRSA skin decolonization. It has no antiviral activity and does not treat herpes zoster. Zoster requires systemic antiviral therapy to reduce viral replication, complications, and PHN risk.
explanation.c: Incorrect. IV acyclovir is reserved for severe or complicated zoster: disseminated zoster, ophthalmic zoster (herpes zoster ophthalmicus — risk of corneal involvement and vision loss), immunocompromised patients, or zoster with CNS involvement (meningitis, encephalitis). Uncomplicated dermatomal zoster in immunocompetent patients is treated with oral antivirals.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While antivirals are most effective within 72 hours of rash onset, treatment may still be beneficial if started later — especially in patients >50 years (higher PHN risk), immunocompromised patients, or those with severe pain. The 72-hour window is a guideline, not an absolute cutoff.

integumentary.question: A 65-year-old man presents with a new 8 mm pigmented lesion on his back with asymmetric borders, color variation (brown, black, and red areas), and irregular edges. He reports the lesion has been growing over the past 3 months. What is the most appropriate initial evaluation?

answers.text: Reassurance and observation — monitor for changes at the next annual visit
Full-thickness excisional biopsy with narrow margins (1–3 mm)
Shave biopsy for quick pathologic diagnosis
Dermoscopy and photograph documentation — biopsy only if changes occur over 3 months

explanation.correct: Correct. Suspected melanoma requires full-thickness excisional biopsy (not shave biopsy) with 1–3 mm margins to provide accurate Breslow depth measurement — the single most important prognostic factor. Breslow depth determines definitive surgical margins and need for sentinel lymph node biopsy. ABCDE criteria met: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution (growing). Incisional or punch biopsy is acceptable if excisional biopsy is impractical due to location or size. Shave biopsy is AVOIDED because it may transect the deep margin, leading to inaccurate depth measurement.
explanation.a: Incorrect. This lesion has multiple features concerning for melanoma (ABCDE criteria: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution/growth). Delaying evaluation for a year risks missing a curable melanoma that could metastasize. Any suspicious lesion warrants immediate biopsy.
explanation.b: Correct. Suspected melanoma requires full-thickness excisional biopsy (not shave biopsy) with 1–3 mm margins to provide accurate Breslow depth measurement — the single most important prognostic factor. Breslow depth determines definitive surgical margins and need for sentinel lymph node biopsy. ABCDE criteria met: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution (growing). Incisional or punch biopsy is acceptable if excisional biopsy is impractical due to location or size. Shave biopsy is AVOIDED because it may transect the deep margin, leading to inaccurate depth measurement.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Shave biopsy is contraindicated for suspected melanoma because it may transect the base of the lesion, making it impossible to accurately measure Breslow depth (the most critical prognostic factor). An artificially shallow measurement could lead to inadequate surgical margins and failure to recommend sentinel lymph node biopsy.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While dermoscopy is a valuable tool for evaluating pigmented lesions, a lesion with multiple ABCDE criteria that is actively growing requires IMMEDIATE biopsy. Serial monitoring of a lesion that already meets criteria for biopsy delays diagnosis and risks progression to metastatic disease.

integumentary.question: A 58-year-old diabetic man presents with rapidly progressive perineal pain, swelling, crepitus, and erythema extending to the scrotum and anterior abdominal wall. He is febrile (39.6°C), tachycardic (HR 138), and hypotensive (BP 82/48 mmHg). CT shows subcutaneous gas tracking along fascial planes in the perineum. What is this condition and the definitive management?

answers.text: Perianal abscess — incision and drainage in the ED under local anesthesia
Fournier's gangrene — emergent wide surgical debridement plus broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and ICU admission
IV antibiotics alone for 14 days — surgical debridement is only needed if antibiotics fail
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy as the primary intervention

explanation.correct: Correct. Fournier's gangrene is a type of necrotizing fasciitis involving the perineum, genitalia, and perianal region. It carries mortality rates of 20–40% even with treatment. Management: (1) Emergent aggressive surgical debridement — all necrotic tissue must be excised; re-exploration every 24–48 hours until all necrosis is cleared; (2) Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering gram-positives (including MRSA), gram-negatives, and anaerobes: vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin (clindamycin also inhibits toxin production); (3) ICU admission for hemodynamic support; (4) Wound VAC after debridement; (5) Fecal diversion (colostomy) may be needed for perineal wounds. Risk factors: diabetes, immunosuppression, obesity, perianal abscess, urethral instrumentation.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A simple perianal abscess is a contained purulent collection that can be drained at the bedside. This patient has gas in fascial planes, hemodynamic instability, and rapidly progressive tissue involvement — hallmarks of a much more serious condition requiring emergent surgical intervention under general anesthesia.
explanation.b: Correct. Fournier's gangrene is a type of necrotizing fasciitis involving the perineum, genitalia, and perianal region. It carries mortality rates of 20–40% even with treatment. Management: (1) Emergent aggressive surgical debridement — all necrotic tissue must be excised; re-exploration every 24–48 hours until all necrosis is cleared; (2) Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering gram-positives (including MRSA), gram-negatives, and anaerobes: vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin (clindamycin also inhibits toxin production); (3) ICU admission for hemodynamic support; (4) Wound VAC after debridement; (5) Fecal diversion (colostomy) may be needed for perineal wounds. Risk factors: diabetes, immunosuppression, obesity, perianal abscess, urethral instrumentation.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Antibiotics CANNOT treat necrotizing fasciitis alone — the infection spreads along fascial planes with thrombosis of blood vessels, creating avascular necrotic tissue that antibiotics cannot penetrate. Surgical debridement is the DEFINITIVE and life-saving treatment. Every hour of surgical delay increases mortality.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While HBO may be considered as adjunctive therapy (improves tissue oxygenation, enhances neutrophil killing), it should NEVER delay surgical debridement. Transferring a hemodynamically unstable patient to an HBO chamber instead of the OR would be fatal. Surgery first, HBO as a potential adjunct after stabilization.

integumentary.question: A 72-year-old man with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) on ibrutinib presents with 2 days of severe burning pain in the left forehead followed by the eruption of grouped vesicles on an erythematous base across the left forehead, upper eyelid, and extending to the tip of the nose. He has no visual complaints yet. Temperature is 100.6°F. Which of the following is the most critical component of his management?

answers.text: Oral valacyclovir 1 g TID for 7 days and outpatient follow-up in 1 week
Admit for IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours, emergent ophthalmology consultation for slit-lamp examination, and pain management
Apply topical acyclovir cream to the affected dermatome and prescribe oral gabapentin for neuropathic pain
Administer Shingrix vaccine immediately to boost VZV immunity and start oral famciclovir

explanation.correct: Correct. This patient has herpes zoster ophthalmicus (V1 dermatome) with Hutchinson sign (vesicles on nose tip = nasociliary branch involvement). Critical management: (1) EMERGENT OPHTHALMOLOGY CONSULT — Hutchinson sign carries >75% risk of ocular complications (keratitis, uveitis, acute retinal necrosis, secondary glaucoma) even BEFORE visual symptoms. Slit-lamp exam is urgent. (2) IV ACYCLOVIR — immunocompromised patients (CLL, on ibrutinib — a BTK inhibitor causing T-cell dysfunction) require IV antiviral therapy rather than oral due to higher risk of dissemination, prolonged viral shedding, and visceral involvement. Continue for 7-10 days minimum. (3) PAIN MANAGEMENT: acute neuralgia can be severe; gabapentin/pregabalin ± opioids as needed. (4) Consider reducing immunosuppression if clinically feasible (discuss with oncology). (5) PHN prophylaxis: appropriate antiviral timing reduces but doesn't eliminate PHN risk. After resolution, vaccinate with Shingrix (now approved for immunocompromised ≥19 years).
explanation.a: Incorrect. This immunocompromised patient (CLL + ibrutinib) with herpes zoster ophthalmicus and Hutchinson sign requires IV (not oral) antiviral therapy and URGENT ophthalmology evaluation — not routine outpatient follow-up.
explanation.b: Correct. This patient has herpes zoster ophthalmicus (V1 dermatome) with Hutchinson sign (vesicles on nose tip = nasociliary branch involvement). Critical management: (1) EMERGENT OPHTHALMOLOGY CONSULT — Hutchinson sign carries >75% risk of ocular complications (keratitis, uveitis, acute retinal necrosis, secondary glaucoma) even BEFORE visual symptoms. Slit-lamp exam is urgent. (2) IV ACYCLOVIR — immunocompromised patients (CLL, on ibrutinib — a BTK inhibitor causing T-cell dysfunction) require IV antiviral therapy rather than oral due to higher risk of dissemination, prolonged viral shedding, and visceral involvement. Continue for 7-10 days minimum. (3) PAIN MANAGEMENT: acute neuralgia can be severe; gabapentin/pregabalin ± opioids as needed. (4) Consider reducing immunosuppression if clinically feasible (discuss with oncology). (5) PHN prophylaxis: appropriate antiviral timing reduces but doesn't eliminate PHN risk. After resolution, vaccinate with Shingrix (now approved for immunocompromised ≥19 years).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Topical antiviral therapy is completely inadequate for herpes zoster, especially in an immunocompromised patient with ophthalmic involvement.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Shingrix is a PREVENTIVE vaccine with no role in treating active herpes zoster. It should be administered AFTER the acute episode resolves to prevent future recurrences.

integumentary.question: A 28-year-old woman with epilepsy was started on lamotrigine 3 weeks ago (rapid titration due to break-through seizures). She now presents with a 2-day prodrome of fever (102.4°F), malaise, sore throat, and myalgias followed by the acute onset of painful dusky-red macules with central blistering on the trunk and extremities, extensive oral erosions making it painful to swallow, bilateral conjunctival injection with purulent discharge, and genital erosions. Approximately 6% of her body surface area shows full-thickness epidermal detachment with a positive Nikolsky sign. WBC is 3,200 (low). She appears acutely ill. Which of the following is the single most important immediate intervention?

answers.text: Administer IV methylprednisolone 1 g for 3 days as immunosuppressive therapy
Immediately discontinue lamotrigine; do not rechallenge
Start IV acyclovir for presumed disseminated herpes simplex causing the mucosal erosions
Apply silver sulfadiazine cream to all affected areas for wound care

explanation.correct: Correct. The single most important intervention in SJS/TEN is IMMEDIATE IDENTIFICATION AND DISCONTINUATION of the causative drug. Every hour of delay increases mortality. Lamotrigine is a well-known cause of SJS, especially with rapid dose titration (proper titration requires gradual escalation over 6-8 weeks, even slower if combined with valproate). This presentation meets SJS criteria: <10% BSA detachment with ≥2 mucosal sites involved, positive Nikolsky sign, and drug temporal correlation. Additional management: (1) Transfer to burn unit/ICU for wound care (treat like a burn patient); (2) Aggressive fluid resuscitation (significant insensible losses); (3) OPHTHALMOLOGY CONSULT urgently (conjunctival involvement can cause synechiae, corneal scarring, blindness if untreated); (4) Pain management; (5) Nutritional support (NG feeds if unable to swallow); (6) Calculate SCORTEN for prognosis; (7) Consider IVIG or cyclosporine (evidence emerging but controversial). NEVER rechallenge with lamotrigine after SJS.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Systemic corticosteroids in SJS/TEN are CONTROVERSIAL and may increase infection risk, delay wound healing, and worsen outcomes. They are NOT the most important first step.
explanation.b: Correct. The single most important intervention in SJS/TEN is IMMEDIATE IDENTIFICATION AND DISCONTINUATION of the causative drug. Every hour of delay increases mortality. Lamotrigine is a well-known cause of SJS, especially with rapid dose titration (proper titration requires gradual escalation over 6-8 weeks, even slower if combined with valproate). This presentation meets SJS criteria: <10% BSA detachment with ≥2 mucosal sites involved, positive Nikolsky sign, and drug temporal correlation. Additional management: (1) Transfer to burn unit/ICU for wound care (treat like a burn patient); (2) Aggressive fluid resuscitation (significant insensible losses); (3) OPHTHALMOLOGY CONSULT urgently (conjunctival involvement can cause synechiae, corneal scarring, blindness if untreated); (4) Pain management; (5) Nutritional support (NG feeds if unable to swallow); (6) Calculate SCORTEN for prognosis; (7) Consider IVIG or cyclosporine (evidence emerging but controversial). NEVER rechallenge with lamotrigine after SJS.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While disseminated HSV is on the differential for mucocutaneous blistering, the temporal relationship with lamotrigine initiation, typical targetoid skin lesions, multi-mucosal involvement, and Nikolsky sign are diagnostic for SJS. Tzanck smear or HSV PCR can help differentiate if uncertain.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Silver sulfadiazine is a SULFONAMIDE-based topical agent. Sulfonamides are a leading cause of SJS/TEN. Applying a drug from the same class known to cause the condition would be inappropriate, though the risk with topical agents is theoretical. Non-adherent dressings and petroleum-based products are preferred.

integumentary.question: A 35-year-old veterinarian presents 6 hours after sustaining a deep puncture wound to the right hand from a cat bite while restraining a feral cat. The wound is small (2 mm) but deep, with minimal bleeding. She has mild erythema surrounding the puncture site. Temperature is 98.8°F. She completed her tetanus series as a child and received a Tdap booster 4 years ago. She has no drug allergies. Her last meal was 2 hours ago. Which of the following represents the most appropriate comprehensive wound management?

answers.text: Copious wound irrigation, primary wound closure with sutures, and oral cephalexin for 5 days
Copious wound irrigation, leave wound open (no suturing), start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID for 5-7 days, no tetanus prophylaxis needed, arrange 24-48 hour wound check
Wound irrigation only; antibiotics are unnecessary prophylactically and should be started only if infection develops
Copious irrigation, amoxicillin-clavulanate, and administer Tdap booster plus tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG)

explanation.correct: Correct. Cat bite management: (1) IRRIGATION: copious high-pressure irrigation with NS or tap water — though deep punctures are difficult to irrigate effectively (contributing to high infection rates); (2) DO NOT CLOSE: cat bite puncture wounds have 30-50% infection rates — primary closure is contraindicated. Leave open for drainage; (3) PROPHYLACTIC ANTIBIOTICS: ALL cat bites receive prophylactic antibiotics due to the high infection rate. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is DOC — covers Pasteurella multocida (#1 pathogen), Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and anaerobes. For PCN allergy: doxycycline or clindamycin + TMP-SMX or a fluoroquinolone; (4) TETANUS: last dose was <5 years ago for a contaminated wound → no booster needed; (5) RABIES: feral cat → assess rabies risk (animal observation if possible, local health department consultation); (6) FOLLOW-UP: 24-48 hour wound recheck is essential — Pasteurella infections progress rapidly. Hand bite wounds carry additional risk of tendon sheath/joint space infection.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Cat bite puncture wounds should NEVER be closed primarily — the deep, narrow wound tract creates an anaerobic environment ideal for infection. Suturing traps bacteria and dramatically increases infection risk. Cephalexin does not adequately cover Pasteurella multocida.
explanation.b: Correct. Cat bite management: (1) IRRIGATION: copious high-pressure irrigation with NS or tap water — though deep punctures are difficult to irrigate effectively (contributing to high infection rates); (2) DO NOT CLOSE: cat bite puncture wounds have 30-50% infection rates — primary closure is contraindicated. Leave open for drainage; (3) PROPHYLACTIC ANTIBIOTICS: ALL cat bites receive prophylactic antibiotics due to the high infection rate. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is DOC — covers Pasteurella multocida (#1 pathogen), Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and anaerobes. For PCN allergy: doxycycline or clindamycin + TMP-SMX or a fluoroquinolone; (4) TETANUS: last dose was <5 years ago for a contaminated wound → no booster needed; (5) RABIES: feral cat → assess rabies risk (animal observation if possible, local health department consultation); (6) FOLLOW-UP: 24-48 hour wound recheck is essential — Pasteurella infections progress rapidly. Hand bite wounds carry additional risk of tendon sheath/joint space infection.
explanation.c: Incorrect. ALL cat bites warrant prophylactic antibiotics due to their 30-50% infection rate (the highest of any common bite type). Waiting for clinical infection to develop risks rapid progression to deep-space hand infection, septic arthritis, or osteomyelitis — especially concerning in the hand.
explanation.d: Incorrect. TIG is only indicated for contaminated wounds when the patient has <3 prior tetanus doses or unknown vaccination history. This patient has a completed primary series with a booster 4 years ago — TIG is not needed, and neither is a booster (last dose <5 years ago for dirty wound).

integumentary.question: A 56-year-old construction worker presents with a slowly enlarging, asymptomatic lesion on his left ear that has been present for approximately 8 months. Examination reveals a 1.5 cm firm, flesh-colored nodule with a central keratotic plug and surrounding induration on the left ear helix. He has extensive sun exposure history and fair complexion. He had a renal transplant 6 years ago and takes tacrolimus and mycophenolate. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

answers.text: Observation with serial photography and reassessment in 6 months
Biopsy (excisional or punch) to evaluate for squamous cell carcinoma, with subsequent definitive excision or Mohs surgery based on histology
Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen, as this is likely a benign keratoacanthoma
Apply topical 5-fluorouracil cream for 4-6 weeks for presumed actinic keratosis

explanation.correct: Correct. This is highly concerning for SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA: (1) Firm, keratotic nodule on a sun-exposed area (ear helix — high-risk location); (2) Extensive UV exposure (construction worker); (3) ORGAN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENT — SCC risk is 65-250x higher in transplant patients compared to the general population, making SCC the most common malignancy in transplant recipients. Transplant-related SCCs are more aggressive, faster-growing, and have higher metastatic rates (~5-8% vs ~2% in general population). Management: (1) Biopsy for definitive diagnosis and histologic grading; (2) If SCC confirmed: Mohs micrographic surgery is preferred for head/neck (ear is high-risk location — cosmetic, high recurrence rate); (3) Consider reducing immunosuppression (discuss with transplant team — switch from tacrolimus to sirolimus, which has anti-tumor properties); (4) Dermatology surveillance q3-6 months indefinitely; (5) Aggressive sun protection counseling. Sentinel lymph node biopsy may be indicated for high-risk features.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A growing keratotic nodule on sun-exposed skin in an immunosuppressed transplant patient cannot be observed — the risk of squamous cell carcinoma is extremely high in this population, and SCC in transplant recipients is more aggressive with higher metastatic potential.
explanation.b: Correct. This is highly concerning for SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA: (1) Firm, keratotic nodule on a sun-exposed area (ear helix — high-risk location); (2) Extensive UV exposure (construction worker); (3) ORGAN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENT — SCC risk is 65-250x higher in transplant patients compared to the general population, making SCC the most common malignancy in transplant recipients. Transplant-related SCCs are more aggressive, faster-growing, and have higher metastatic rates (~5-8% vs ~2% in general population). Management: (1) Biopsy for definitive diagnosis and histologic grading; (2) If SCC confirmed: Mohs micrographic surgery is preferred for head/neck (ear is high-risk location — cosmetic, high recurrence rate); (3) Consider reducing immunosuppression (discuss with transplant team — switch from tacrolimus to sirolimus, which has anti-tumor properties); (4) Dermatology surveillance q3-6 months indefinitely; (5) Aggressive sun protection counseling. Sentinel lymph node biopsy may be indicated for high-risk features.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While keratoacanthoma (a low-grade SCC variant) can present similarly, distinguishing it from invasive SCC requires biopsy. Cryotherapy alone is insufficient for a potentially malignant lesion in a high-risk immunosuppressed patient. Tissue diagnosis is essential.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Topical 5-FU is appropriate for superficial actinic keratoses (premalignant), not for a 1.5 cm firm keratotic nodule that has been growing for 8 months — this likely represents invasive SCC requiring tissue diagnosis and surgical excision, not topical therapy.

integumentary.question: A 42-year-old woman presents with concern about a mole on her upper back that her partner noticed has changed over the past 4 months. Examination reveals a 9 mm, asymmetric, irregularly bordered lesion with variegated colors (brown, black, and red areas) and an area of regression (white scar-like patch within the lesion). Dermoscopy shows an atypical pigment network, irregular globules, blue-white structures, and regression structures. There is no ulceration. No palpable lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Shave biopsy to remove the lesion and send for pathology
Narrow-margin excisional biopsy (1-3 mm margins) encompassing the entire lesion for full histopathologic evaluation including Breslow depth
Punch biopsy of the darkest area of the lesion for targeted sampling
Reassure the patient that the color variation and regression suggest a benign healing nevus, and schedule 6-month follow-up

explanation.correct: Correct. This lesion meets multiple ABCDE criteria for melanoma (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolving) plus concerning dermoscopic features (atypical network, blue-white structures, regression). Management: (1) EXCISIONAL BIOPSY with narrow (1-3 mm) margins through the subcutaneous fat — this preserves the full depth of the lesion for accurate BRESLOW THICKNESS measurement (the most critical prognostic factor). (2) If melanoma is confirmed: subsequent WIDE LOCAL EXCISION with margins based on Breslow depth (in situ: 0.5-1 cm; ≤1 mm: 1 cm; 1.01-2 mm: 1-2 cm; >2 mm: 2 cm). (3) SENTINEL LYMPH NODE BIOPSY if Breslow >1 mm (or 0.8 mm with adverse features like ulceration or high mitotic rate). (4) Staging workup for advanced disease (CT/PET, LDH, brain MRI if indicated). (5) For advanced/metastatic: immunotherapy (anti-PD-1: pembrolizumab/nivolumab) and targeted therapy (BRAF/MEK inhibitors if BRAF V600E mutation present). (6) Close dermatologic surveillance.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Shave biopsy is CONTRAINDICATED for suspected melanoma. It may transect the lesion at its base, preventing accurate Breslow thickness measurement — the single most important prognostic factor that determines surgical margins and staging.
explanation.b: Correct. This lesion meets multiple ABCDE criteria for melanoma (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolving) plus concerning dermoscopic features (atypical network, blue-white structures, regression). Management: (1) EXCISIONAL BIOPSY with narrow (1-3 mm) margins through the subcutaneous fat — this preserves the full depth of the lesion for accurate BRESLOW THICKNESS measurement (the most critical prognostic factor). (2) If melanoma is confirmed: subsequent WIDE LOCAL EXCISION with margins based on Breslow depth (in situ: 0.5-1 cm; ≤1 mm: 1 cm; 1.01-2 mm: 1-2 cm; >2 mm: 2 cm). (3) SENTINEL LYMPH NODE BIOPSY if Breslow >1 mm (or 0.8 mm with adverse features like ulceration or high mitotic rate). (4) Staging workup for advanced disease (CT/PET, LDH, brain MRI if indicated). (5) For advanced/metastatic: immunotherapy (anti-PD-1: pembrolizumab/nivolumab) and targeted therapy (BRAF/MEK inhibitors if BRAF V600E mutation present). (6) Close dermatologic surveillance.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Partial (punch) biopsy of a suspicious melanocytic lesion may miss the thickest area of the tumor, potentially understaging the melanoma. Excisional biopsy of the entire lesion is the standard of care.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Regression within a melanocytic lesion (white scar-like area) is a concerning feature that may indicate the immune system responding to melanoma — it is NOT reassuring. Regression can paradoxically lead to understaging (thinner Breslow depth from tumor destruction) despite aggressive biology. This lesion must be biopsied.

integumentary.question: A 70-year-old man develops a painful rash on his left flank consisting of grouped vesicles on an erythematous base distributed along the T8 dermatome. The rash is strictly unilateral and does not cross the midline. He reports burning pain that preceded the rash by 3 days. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: Contact dermatitis
Herpes zoster
HSV
Bullous pemphigoid

explanation.correct: Correct. Grouped vesicles on an erythematous base in a unilateral dermatomal distribution that does NOT cross the midline is the hallmark presentation of herpes zoster (shingles) — reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus (VZV) from the dorsal root ganglion. Thoracic dermatomes are most commonly affected. A prodrome of burning, tingling, or lancinating pain in the affected dermatome typically precedes the rash by 1-5 days. VZV remains latent in dorsal root ganglia after primary varicella (chickenpox) infection and reactivates with immunosenescence, immunosuppression, or stress.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Contact dermatitis (allergic or irritant) follows an EXPOSURE pattern — affecting skin that contacted the offending allergen/irritant (e.g., watch band, nickel jewelry, poison ivy). It does NOT follow dermatomal nerve distribution and typically produces diffuse erythema, vesicles, or eczematous changes rather than grouped vesicles on an erythematous base in a unilateral strip.
explanation.b: Correct. Grouped vesicles on an erythematous base in a unilateral dermatomal distribution that does NOT cross the midline is the hallmark presentation of herpes zoster (shingles) — reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus (VZV) from the dorsal root ganglion. Thoracic dermatomes are most commonly affected. A prodrome of burning, tingling, or lancinating pain in the affected dermatome typically precedes the rash by 1-5 days. VZV remains latent in dorsal root ganglia after primary varicella (chickenpox) infection and reactivates with immunosenescence, immunosuppression, or stress.
explanation.c: Incorrect. HSV (herpes simplex virus) causes recurrent grouped vesicles but at the SAME anatomic site each time (perioral for HSV-1, genital for HSV-2) — NOT in a dermatomal distribution. HSV recurrences are typically smaller, cluster in one location, and lack the extensive dermatomal spread characteristic of herpes zoster.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Bullous pemphigoid is an autoimmune blistering disease of the elderly, producing tense bullae (not grouped vesicles) in a GENERALIZED, non-dermatomal distribution. The bullae are typically widespread over the trunk and extremities, bilateral, and accompanied by intense pruritus. Diagnosis is confirmed by skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence showing linear IgG and C3 deposits at the dermal-epidermal junction.

integumentary.question: A 68-year-old man with herpes zoster in the left V1 (ophthalmic) trigeminal distribution is noted to have vesicles on the tip of his nose, known as Hutchinson sign. He currently denies any visual symptoms. Which of the following is the most critical concern associated with this finding?

answers.text: Bacterial superinfection risk
V1 involvement with risk of herpes zoster ophthalmicus
Impending Ramsay Hunt syndrome
PHN development

explanation.correct: Correct. Hutchinson sign — vesicles on the TIP of the nose — indicates involvement of the NASOCILIARY BRANCH of the ophthalmic division (V1) of the trigeminal nerve. This branch also supplies sensory innervation to the cornea, iris, and ciliary body, making ocular involvement highly likely (>75% when Hutchinson sign is present). Potential complications include herpes zoster keratitis (corneal ulceration, scarring), anterior uveitis, secondary glaucoma, and in severe cases, acute retinal necrosis with permanent vision loss. URGENT ophthalmology referral for slit-lamp examination is mandatory — even before ocular symptoms develop — because corneal dendrites and anterior chamber inflammation may be present subclinically.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While bacterial superinfection of herpes zoster vesicles can occur (particularly with Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes), this is a general complication of any vesicular skin eruption and is NOT the specific clinical concern raised by Hutchinson sign. The critical significance of vesicles on the nose tip is the high probability of OCULAR involvement requiring emergent ophthalmologic evaluation.
explanation.b: Correct. Hutchinson sign — vesicles on the TIP of the nose — indicates involvement of the NASOCILIARY BRANCH of the ophthalmic division (V1) of the trigeminal nerve. This branch also supplies sensory innervation to the cornea, iris, and ciliary body, making ocular involvement highly likely (>75% when Hutchinson sign is present). Potential complications include herpes zoster keratitis (corneal ulceration, scarring), anterior uveitis, secondary glaucoma, and in severe cases, acute retinal necrosis with permanent vision loss. URGENT ophthalmology referral for slit-lamp examination is mandatory — even before ocular symptoms develop — because corneal dendrites and anterior chamber inflammation may be present subclinically.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Ramsay Hunt syndrome involves reactivation of VZV in the GENICULATE GANGLION of cranial nerve VII (facial nerve), presenting with ipsilateral facial nerve palsy (LMN pattern), painful vesicles in the external ear canal and auricle, and ipsilateral sensorineural hearing loss (CN VIII involvement). Ramsay Hunt affects the EAR, not the nose. Hutchinson sign specifically implicates the nasociliary branch of V1 (ophthalmic division of trigeminal nerve).
explanation.d: Incorrect. Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) is a chronic neuropathic pain complication that can follow herpes zoster in ANY dermatome, not specifically related to nasal tip involvement. Risk factors for PHN include advanced age (>60), severe acute pain, severe rash, and immunosuppression. While PHN is an important complication, Hutchinson sign specifically signals the acute emergency of potential OCULAR involvement requiring immediate ophthalmology referral — not a chronic pain concern.

integumentary.question: A 55-year-old woman asks her provider about the shingles vaccine. She had chickenpox as a child and has no history of shingles. She is otherwise healthy with no immunocompromising conditions. Which of the following is the currently recommended vaccine for herpes zoster prevention in adults aged 50 and older?

answers.text: Zostavax (live)
Shingrix (recombinant, adjuvanted) — 2 doses, 2-6 months apart
MMR
Varivax

explanation.correct: Correct. Shingrix (recombinant, adjuvanted zoster vaccine) is the only currently recommended vaccine for herpes zoster prevention. It is a NON-LIVE vaccine (safe for immunocompromised patients, unlike the discontinued Zostavax) containing recombinant VZV glycoprotein E with AS01B adjuvant. Dosing: 2 intramuscular doses, 2-6 months apart. Efficacy: >90% across all age groups studied, with sustained protection ≥10 years. Approved for: all adults ≥50 years and immunocompromised adults ≥19 years. Common side effects: injection-site pain, myalgia, fatigue, headache (due to potent adjuvant-driven immune response).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Zostavax (live attenuated VZV vaccine) has been discontinued in the United States since November 2020 and is no longer available. It has been completely replaced by Shingrix, which provides superior efficacy and longer-lasting protection.
explanation.b: Correct. Shingrix (recombinant, adjuvanted zoster vaccine) is the only currently recommended vaccine for herpes zoster prevention. It is a NON-LIVE vaccine (safe for immunocompromised patients, unlike the discontinued Zostavax) containing recombinant VZV glycoprotein E with AS01B adjuvant. Dosing: 2 intramuscular doses, 2-6 months apart. Efficacy: >90% across all age groups studied, with sustained protection ≥10 years. Approved for: all adults ≥50 years and immunocompromised adults ≥19 years. Common side effects: injection-site pain, myalgia, fatigue, headache (due to potent adjuvant-driven immune response).
explanation.c: Incorrect. MMR vaccine protects against measles, mumps, and rubella — completely different pathogens from varicella-zoster virus. MMR is a routine childhood vaccine with catch-up dosing in adults who lack immunity, particularly healthcare workers and international travelers.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Varivax is the live attenuated varicella vaccine used for PRIMARY chickenpox prevention in children (two doses: 12-15 months and 4-6 years) and susceptible adults who have never had varicella. It does NOT prevent herpes zoster (shingles) reactivation in adults who already harbor latent VZV from prior chickenpox infection. Shingrix is the appropriate vaccine for zoster prevention in adults.

integumentary.question: A 68-year-old man develops a vesicular rash in the left T4 dermatome with severe burning pain. The rash appeared 36 hours ago. His provider plans to start antiviral therapy. Within what timeframe of rash onset should antiviral treatment be initiated for herpes zoster to achieve maximum benefit?

answers.text: 24 hours of prodrome
72 hours of rash onset
7 days
Not needed — zoster is self-limited

explanation.correct: Correct. Antiviral therapy (valacyclovir 1 g TID, acyclovir 800 mg 5×/day, or famciclovir 500 mg TID, all × 7 days) should be initiated within 72 hours of RASH ONSET for maximum benefit. Early treatment reduces: (1) duration and severity of the acute rash; (2) duration of acute pain; and (3) risk of postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) — the most debilitating complication. Treatment may still be initiated after 72 hours in immunocompromised patients, those with ophthalmic involvement, or if new vesicles are still forming (indicating active viral replication). Valacyclovir is generally preferred over acyclovir due to better bioavailability and simpler dosing.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Timing is based on RASH onset, not the prodromal symptoms. The prodrome (pain, tingling, burning) is often nonspecific and may be attributed to musculoskeletal pain or other causes before the rash appears, making it an unreliable timing marker. The 72-hour window begins when the vesicular RASH first appears.
explanation.b: Correct. Antiviral therapy (valacyclovir 1 g TID, acyclovir 800 mg 5×/day, or famciclovir 500 mg TID, all × 7 days) should be initiated within 72 hours of RASH ONSET for maximum benefit. Early treatment reduces: (1) duration and severity of the acute rash; (2) duration of acute pain; and (3) risk of postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) — the most debilitating complication. Treatment may still be initiated after 72 hours in immunocompromised patients, those with ophthalmic involvement, or if new vesicles are still forming (indicating active viral replication). Valacyclovir is generally preferred over acyclovir due to better bioavailability and simpler dosing.
explanation.c: Incorrect. After 72 hours from rash onset, antiviral benefit diminishes in immunocompetent patients with uncomplicated zoster. However, exceptions exist: antivirals may still be warranted beyond 72 hours in immunocompromised patients, patients with herpes zoster ophthalmicus, patients with new vesicles still forming, and severe cases. The 72-hour window is a guideline, not an absolute cutoff.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While herpes zoster is ultimately self-limited in immunocompetent patients (rash typically resolves in 2-4 weeks), antiviral therapy provides significant clinical benefit: shortened rash duration, reduced acute pain severity, and importantly, reduced risk of postherpetic neuralgia — a chronic neuropathic pain syndrome that can persist for months to years and significantly impairs quality of life, particularly in elderly patients.

integumentary.question: A 72-year-old woman had herpes zoster 3 months ago. The vesicles have completely healed, but she continues to experience constant burning and lancinating pain in the affected T6 dermatome. She rates her pain 7/10. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic treatment for postherpetic neuralgia?

answers.text: Oral acyclovir
Gabapentin or pregabalin
Oral prednisone
Topical ketoconazole

explanation.correct: Correct. First-line treatments for postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) include: (1) GABAPENTIN or PREGABALIN — calcium channel alpha-2-delta ligands that reduce neuropathic pain signaling (gabapentin: start 300 mg/day, titrate to 1800-3600 mg/day; pregabalin: 150-600 mg/day in divided doses); (2) TRICYCLIC ANTIDEPRESSANTS (amitriptyline, nortriptyline) — 25-150 mg at bedtime, effective for neuropathic pain through norepinephrine/serotonin reuptake inhibition; (3) TOPICAL LIDOCAINE (5% patch) — applied directly to the affected dermatome for localized pain relief; (4) CAPSAICIN cream (0.075%) — depletes substance P from sensory nerve endings. Opioids are reserved for severe, refractory cases due to dependence risk.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Oral acyclovir/valacyclovir treats ACUTE herpes zoster infection by inhibiting viral DNA polymerase during active viral replication. Once the acute infection resolves and the vesicles have crusted, the virus returns to latency — antivirals are no longer effective because they only act on actively replicating virus. Established postherpetic neuralgia represents neuronal damage from the viral infection, not ongoing viral replication.
explanation.b: Correct. First-line treatments for postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) include: (1) GABAPENTIN or PREGABALIN — calcium channel alpha-2-delta ligands that reduce neuropathic pain signaling (gabapentin: start 300 mg/day, titrate to 1800-3600 mg/day; pregabalin: 150-600 mg/day in divided doses); (2) TRICYCLIC ANTIDEPRESSANTS (amitriptyline, nortriptyline) — 25-150 mg at bedtime, effective for neuropathic pain through norepinephrine/serotonin reuptake inhibition; (3) TOPICAL LIDOCAINE (5% patch) — applied directly to the affected dermatome for localized pain relief; (4) CAPSAICIN cream (0.075%) — depletes substance P from sensory nerve endings. Opioids are reserved for severe, refractory cases due to dependence risk.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Oral prednisone during ACUTE herpes zoster may modestly reduce acute pain intensity but has NOT been shown to prevent or effectively treat established postherpetic neuralgia in clinical trials. Systemic corticosteroids are sometimes used as adjunctive therapy during the acute phase in older patients (>50) to reduce acute inflammation, but they have no role in managing PHN once it has developed.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Ketoconazole is an azole ANTIFUNGAL used for dermatophyte infections (tinea) and seborrheic dermatitis. It has no antiviral, analgesic, or neuromodulatory properties and plays no role in herpes zoster or postherpetic neuralgia management.

integumentary.question: A 30-year-old woman develops painful dusky macules with central blistering on her trunk 2 weeks after starting a new medication. She has extensive oral erosions, bilateral conjunctival injection, and approximately 8% body surface area of full-thickness epidermal detachment with a positive Nikolsky sign. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: Erythema multiforme
Stevens-Johnson syndrome
TEN
Pemphigus vulgaris

explanation.correct: Correct. Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) is defined by epidermal detachment of <10% body surface area with involvement of ≥2 mucosal sites (oral, ocular, genital, anal). SJS-TEN overlap = 10-30% BSA; TEN = >30% BSA. This patient has 8% detachment with oral and genital mucosal involvement, classifying as SJS. SJS is most commonly drug-induced (medications started 1-3 weeks prior), with positive Nikolsky sign (epidermal separation with lateral pressure on uninvolved skin). Mortality for SJS is approximately 5-10%, increasing significantly with greater BSA involvement (TEN mortality: 25-35%).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Erythema multiforme (EM) minor presents with classic TARGET lesions (three-zone pattern: dark center, pale ring, erythematous border) that are typically acral in distribution (hands, feet, extremities). EM minor has minimal-to-no mucosal involvement, no significant epidermal detachment, and is most commonly triggered by HSV infection (not drugs). The mucosal involvement and 8% BSA detachment described exceeds EM and meets SJS criteria.
explanation.b: Correct. Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) is defined by epidermal detachment of <10% body surface area with involvement of ≥2 mucosal sites (oral, ocular, genital, anal). SJS-TEN overlap = 10-30% BSA; TEN = >30% BSA. This patient has 8% detachment with oral and genital mucosal involvement, classifying as SJS. SJS is most commonly drug-induced (medications started 1-3 weeks prior), with positive Nikolsky sign (epidermal separation with lateral pressure on uninvolved skin). Mortality for SJS is approximately 5-10%, increasing significantly with greater BSA involvement (TEN mortality: 25-35%).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) requires >30% BSA detachment. With only 8% BSA involved, this patient's presentation is classified as SJS, not TEN. The SJS-TEN overlap category (10-30% BSA) bridges the gap between these two entities, which represent a clinical spectrum of the same disease process (immune-mediated keratinocyte apoptosis).
explanation.d: Incorrect. Pemphigus vulgaris is a chronic autoimmune blistering disease caused by IgG antibodies against desmoglein-3 (and sometimes desmoglein-1), resulting in intraepidermal acantholysis and FLACCID blisters/erosions. While it has a positive Nikolsky sign and mucosal involvement (oral erosions are often the FIRST manifestation), it is NOT drug-induced, follows a chronic/relapsing course, and does not present with the acute drug-temporal relationship and targetoid skin lesions seen in SJS.

integumentary.question: A 28-year-old man is admitted with suspected Stevens-Johnson syndrome after starting a new anticonvulsant 10 days ago. He has mucosal involvement of the mouth and eyes with 5% BSA epidermal detachment. The care team is prioritizing interventions. Which of the following is the single MOST important first step in management?

answers.text: IV corticosteroids
Immediately discontinue the offending drug
Silver sulfadiazine
Oral antihistamines

explanation.correct: Correct. The single MOST IMPORTANT intervention in SJS/TEN is IMMEDIATE identification and DISCONTINUATION of the causative drug. Every hour of continued drug exposure after symptom onset increases mortality. In this patient, the new anticonvulsant started 10 days ago is the most likely culprit — anticonvulsants (lamotrigine, carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) are among the highest-risk drug classes for SJS/TEN. Additional management: (1) transfer to burn unit/ICU for specialized wound care; (2) aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (insensible losses from denuded skin); (3) OPHTHALMOLOGY consult urgently (ocular involvement can cause corneal scarring, symblepharon, blindness); (4) pain management; (5) nutritional support; (6) calculate SCORTEN for prognosis.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Systemic corticosteroids in SJS/TEN are HIGHLY CONTROVERSIAL. While some retrospective studies suggest possible benefit with early high-dose pulse corticosteroids, others show increased infection risk, delayed wound healing, and no mortality benefit. Multiple dermatology and burn society guidelines do NOT recommend routine systemic corticosteroids as first-line SJS/TEN therapy. Regardless of the steroid debate, the MOST important first step is always drug discontinuation — identifying and stopping the causative medication takes priority over any pharmacologic intervention.
explanation.b: Correct. The single MOST IMPORTANT intervention in SJS/TEN is IMMEDIATE identification and DISCONTINUATION of the causative drug. Every hour of continued drug exposure after symptom onset increases mortality. In this patient, the new anticonvulsant started 10 days ago is the most likely culprit — anticonvulsants (lamotrigine, carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) are among the highest-risk drug classes for SJS/TEN. Additional management: (1) transfer to burn unit/ICU for specialized wound care; (2) aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (insensible losses from denuded skin); (3) OPHTHALMOLOGY consult urgently (ocular involvement can cause corneal scarring, symblepharon, blindness); (4) pain management; (5) nutritional support; (6) calculate SCORTEN for prognosis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Silver sulfadiazine is a topical antimicrobial containing a SULFONAMIDE moiety. Sulfonamide antibiotics (including TMP-SMX) are among the most common causative agents of SJS/TEN. While the risk of cross-reactivity between systemic sulfonamides and topical silver sulfadiazine is debated, applying a sulfonamide-containing product to a patient with a probable sulfonamide-induced SJS/TEN is inadvisable. Non-sulfonamide wound care products (petrolatum-based emollients, non-adherent dressings, mupirocin) are preferred for skin care in SJS/TEN patients.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Oral antihistamines (diphenhydramine, cetirizine, hydroxyzine) block histamine H1 receptors and are effective for urticaria and allergic reactions. However, SJS/TEN is NOT a histamine-mediated process — it is caused by immune-mediated keratinocyte apoptosis driven by cytotoxic T lymphocytes, Fas-FasL interactions, and granulysin-mediated cell death. Antihistamines cannot prevent or reverse this pathologic process and have no role in SJS/TEN management.

integumentary.question: A pharmacy student asks the ACNP which medications are most commonly implicated as causative agents of Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis. Which of the following drugs is MOST commonly associated with SJS/TEN?

answers.text: Acetaminophen
Allopurinol
Metformin
Lisinopril

explanation.correct: Correct. Allopurinol is the single most commonly implicated medication in SJS/TEN worldwide. Other high-risk drugs include: sulfonamide antibiotics (TMP-SMX), anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine — particularly with rapid dose titration), NSAID piroxicam, nevirapine (NNRTI), and antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, aminopenicillins). HLA-B*5801 testing before allopurinol initiation in at-risk populations (Southeast Asian, African American, Korean) significantly reduces SJS/TEN risk from allopurinol by identifying predisposed individuals.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Acetaminophen is one of the safest analgesic/antipyretic medications and is NOT commonly implicated in SJS/TEN. It is metabolized primarily by hepatic glucuronidation and sulfation and does not trigger the immune-mediated keratinocyte apoptosis that characterizes SJS/TEN. Acetaminophen can be safely used for pain and fever management in most clinical scenarios.
explanation.b: Correct. Allopurinol is the single most commonly implicated medication in SJS/TEN worldwide. Other high-risk drugs include: sulfonamide antibiotics (TMP-SMX), anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine — particularly with rapid dose titration), NSAID piroxicam, nevirapine (NNRTI), and antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, aminopenicillins). HLA-B*5801 testing before allopurinol initiation in at-risk populations (Southeast Asian, African American, Korean) significantly reduces SJS/TEN risk from allopurinol by identifying predisposed individuals.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Metformin is a biguanide oral hypoglycemic agent used as first-line therapy for type 2 diabetes. It is NOT associated with SJS/TEN. Metformin's primary side effects are GI (nausea, diarrhea, metallic taste) and the rare but serious risk of lactic acidosis in patients with renal impairment, hepatic dysfunction, or conditions predisposing to tissue hypoxia.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Lisinopril (an ACE inhibitor) is NOT commonly implicated in SJS/TEN. ACE inhibitors are associated with OTHER adverse drug reactions — most notably angioedema (1-7% of patients, higher in African Americans) and chronic dry cough (10-15%, mediated by bradykinin accumulation) — but these are mechanistically distinct from the immune-mediated keratinocyte destruction seen in SJS/TEN.

integumentary.question: A 35-year-old woman is hospitalized with suspected TEN after starting lamotrigine 3 weeks ago. On physical examination, the ACNP applies gentle lateral pressure to an area of apparently uninvolved skin, and the epidermis slides off easily. Which of the following clinical signs does this finding represent?

answers.text: Koebner phenomenon
Nikolsky sign
Darier sign
Auspitz sign

explanation.correct: Correct. Nikolsky sign is the epidermal separation (skin sloughing/sliding) that occurs when gentle lateral tangential pressure is applied to apparently UNINVOLVED skin. A positive Nikolsky sign indicates widespread subclinical epidermal detachment beyond the visibly affected areas — a hallmark of SJS/TEN. This occurs because the immune-mediated keratinocyte apoptosis (driven by Fas-FasL interaction, granulysin, and perforin/granzyme B) affects the entire epidermis, not just the visually apparent lesions. Nikolsky sign is also positive in pemphigus vulgaris (intraepidermal acantholysis) and staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS — superficial epidermal split from exfoliative toxins), but the clinical context usually distinguishes these conditions.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Koebner phenomenon (isomorphic response) is the development of new skin lesions at sites of cutaneous trauma or irritation, occurring in the same morphology as the patient's existing skin disease. It is classically associated with psoriasis (new psoriatic plaques at scratch/trauma sites), vitiligo, and lichen planus. Koebner phenomenon does NOT involve epidermal separation or sloughing.
explanation.b: Correct. Nikolsky sign is the epidermal separation (skin sloughing/sliding) that occurs when gentle lateral tangential pressure is applied to apparently UNINVOLVED skin. A positive Nikolsky sign indicates widespread subclinical epidermal detachment beyond the visibly affected areas — a hallmark of SJS/TEN. This occurs because the immune-mediated keratinocyte apoptosis (driven by Fas-FasL interaction, granulysin, and perforin/granzyme B) affects the entire epidermis, not just the visually apparent lesions. Nikolsky sign is also positive in pemphigus vulgaris (intraepidermal acantholysis) and staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS — superficial epidermal split from exfoliative toxins), but the clinical context usually distinguishes these conditions.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Darier sign is the development of localized urticaria (wheal and flare) when a mastocytoma (cutaneous mastocytosis lesion) is rubbed or stroked. This occurs because rubbing degranulates the mast cells within the lesion, releasing histamine and other mediators locally. Darier sign is specific to mastocytosis and is unrelated to epidermal detachment or SJS/TEN.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Auspitz sign is the appearance of pinpoint bleeding when psoriatic scales are scraped off, exposing the dilated capillaries in the dermal papillae beneath the thinned suprapapillary epidermis. It is specific to psoriasis and has no relevance to SJS/TEN or epidermal detachment disorders.

integumentary.question: A 40-year-old man is admitted with toxic epidermal necrolysis involving 35% BSA detachment. The burn team is calculating his predicted mortality using a validated scoring system specific to TEN. Which of the following severity-of-illness tools is used to predict mortality in TEN?

answers.text: APACHE II
SCORTEN
SOFA
Braden scale

explanation.correct: Correct. SCORTEN (Score of Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis) is a validated severity-of-illness scoring system specifically developed for TEN, using 7 clinical parameters assessed within the first 24 hours of admission: (1) age >40; (2) heart rate >120; (3) cancer/hematologic malignancy; (4) BSA detachment >10%; (5) BUN >28 mg/dL; (6) serum glucose >252 mg/dL; (7) serum bicarbonate <20 mEq/L. Each parameter scores 1 point, with predicted mortality ranging from ~3% (score 0-1) to >90% (score ≥5). SCORTEN guides prognosis discussions, ICU resource allocation, and treatment intensity.
explanation.a: Incorrect. APACHE II (Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation) is a general ICU severity-of-illness scoring system used across all critical care diagnoses. While it provides overall mortality prediction for ICU patients, it was NOT developed or validated specifically for TEN and does not include TEN-specific variables (such as BSA detachment percentage). SCORTEN was specifically designed and validated for TEN prognosis.
explanation.b: Correct. SCORTEN (Score of Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis) is a validated severity-of-illness scoring system specifically developed for TEN, using 7 clinical parameters assessed within the first 24 hours of admission: (1) age >40; (2) heart rate >120; (3) cancer/hematologic malignancy; (4) BSA detachment >10%; (5) BUN >28 mg/dL; (6) serum glucose >252 mg/dL; (7) serum bicarbonate <20 mEq/L. Each parameter scores 1 point, with predicted mortality ranging from ~3% (score 0-1) to >90% (score ≥5). SCORTEN guides prognosis discussions, ICU resource allocation, and treatment intensity.
explanation.c: Incorrect. SOFA (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment) is a scoring system used to quantify organ dysfunction in critically ill patients with sepsis. It evaluates 6 organ systems (respiratory, cardiovascular, hepatic, coagulation, renal, neurological) and is used to track disease progression and treatment response in sepsis/septic shock. SOFA is not specific to TEN and does not include dermatologic parameters.
explanation.d: Incorrect. The Braden scale is a validated tool for assessing PRESSURE ULCER risk in hospitalized patients, evaluating 6 subscales: sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition, and friction/shear. It has no applicability to TEN severity assessment or mortality prediction. While TEN patients ARE at high risk for skin breakdown (due to immobility and epidermal loss), the Braden scale evaluates pressure injury risk — a different clinical concern.

integumentary.question: A 35-year-old veterinarian presents 8 hours after being bitten on the hand by a stray cat. The wound is a small deep puncture with surrounding erythema. The ACNP considers which organism is most likely to cause infection. Which of the following is the most common pathogen in cat bite wound infections?

answers.text: Eikenella corrodens
Pasteurella multocida
Clostridium tetani
Staph epidermidis

explanation.correct: Correct. Pasteurella multocida is the #1 pathogen in both cat and dog bite infections, accounting for approximately 50-80% of cat bite infections and 25-50% of dog bite infections. Cat bites have HIGHER infection rates (30-50%) compared to dog bites (5-15%) because cats have thin, sharp teeth that create deep puncture wounds, effectively inoculating bacteria into deep tissue planes, tendon sheaths, and joint spaces where irrigation is difficult. Pasteurella infections characteristically develop RAPIDLY — often within 12-24 hours of the bite, with rapidly progressive erythema, swelling, and purulent drainage.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Eikenella corrodens is the hallmark pathogen specifically associated with HUMAN bite infections — particularly 'fight bite' (clenched-fist) injuries over the MCP joints. It is a facultative anaerobic gram-negative rod found in human oral flora. While Eikenella can cause serious infections (septic arthritis, osteomyelitis), it is NOT a significant pathogen in cat or dog bites, which are dominated by Pasteurella multocida.
explanation.b: Correct. Pasteurella multocida is the #1 pathogen in both cat and dog bite infections, accounting for approximately 50-80% of cat bite infections and 25-50% of dog bite infections. Cat bites have HIGHER infection rates (30-50%) compared to dog bites (5-15%) because cats have thin, sharp teeth that create deep puncture wounds, effectively inoculating bacteria into deep tissue planes, tendon sheaths, and joint spaces where irrigation is difficult. Pasteurella infections characteristically develop RAPIDLY — often within 12-24 hours of the bite, with rapidly progressive erythema, swelling, and purulent drainage.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Clostridium tetani is a legitimate concern with any puncture wound or contaminated laceration (including animal bites), and tetanus prophylaxis should be assessed for all bite wound patients. However, C. tetani is NOT the primary INFECTIOUS pathogen causing wound infection after cat bites. Pasteurella multocida is the dominant causative organism in cat bite wound infections, responsible for the rapidly progressive cellulitis that characteristically develops within 12-24 hours.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Staphylococcus epidermidis is a coagulase-negative staphylococcal species that is part of normal human skin flora. While it can cause infections of indwelling medical devices (prosthetic heart valves, orthopedic hardware, intravascular catheters) through biofilm formation, it is NOT a primary pathogen in animal bite wound infections. Cat bite infections are predominantly caused by Pasteurella multocida from the cat's oral flora, not human skin commensals.

integumentary.question: A 30-year-old woman presents 6 hours after a cat bite to her right index finger with rapidly progressive redness, swelling, and pain extending up the dorsum of her hand. Temperature is 100.2°F. The ACNP selects empiric antibiotic therapy. Which of the following is the antibiotic of choice for animal bite wound infections?

answers.text: Cephalexin
Amoxicillin-clavulanate
Metronidazole alone
Azithromycin

explanation.correct: Correct. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is the drug of choice for ALL animal and human bite wound infections. The combination covers: Pasteurella multocida (the #1 pathogen), Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus species, Eikenella corrodens (human bites), Capnocytophaga canimorsus (dog/cat bites in asplenic patients), AND oral anaerobes (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella). ALL cat bites should receive prophylactic antibiotics due to their exceptionally high infection rate (~30-50%). For PCN allergy: doxycycline (covers Pasteurella + aerobes) or clindamycin + TMP-SMX (or a fluoroquinolone) to cover both anaerobes and aerobes including Pasteurella.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Cephalexin (a 1st-generation cephalosporin) provides good coverage for Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus species but has POOR activity against Pasteurella multocida — the #1 pathogen in cat bite infections. It also lacks adequate anaerobic coverage. Using cephalexin for animal bites would leave the most common and most virulent pathogen untreated, risking rapid wound progression.
explanation.b: Correct. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is the drug of choice for ALL animal and human bite wound infections. The combination covers: Pasteurella multocida (the #1 pathogen), Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus species, Eikenella corrodens (human bites), Capnocytophaga canimorsus (dog/cat bites in asplenic patients), AND oral anaerobes (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella). ALL cat bites should receive prophylactic antibiotics due to their exceptionally high infection rate (~30-50%). For PCN allergy: doxycycline (covers Pasteurella + aerobes) or clindamycin + TMP-SMX (or a fluoroquinolone) to cover both anaerobes and aerobes including Pasteurella.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Metronidazole provides excellent anaerobic coverage (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella, Clostridium) but has NO activity against aerobic organisms including Pasteurella multocida, Staphylococcus, or Streptococcus. Bite wound infections are POLYMICROBIAL — requiring coverage of both aerobic and anaerobic organisms. Metronidazole alone would leave the aerobic pathogens untreated.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Azithromycin (a macrolide) is primarily used for respiratory tract infections (CAP, atypical PNA), STIs (chlamydia), and MAC prophylaxis in HIV. It has inconsistent and unreliable activity against the polymicrobial flora of bite wounds — particularly Pasteurella multocida, anaerobes, and Staphylococcus species. Azithromycin is NOT recommended for bite wound prophylaxis or treatment by any current guideline.

integumentary.question: A 22-year-old man presents with a laceration over the dorsum of his right 3rd MCP joint sustained during an altercation ('fight bite'). The wound is small but deep, with concern for joint capsule penetration. Which of the following organisms is specifically associated with human bite infections and clenched-fist injuries?

answers.text: Pasteurella
Eikenella corrodens
Capnocytophaga
Bartonella

explanation.correct: Correct. Eikenella corrodens is the hallmark pathogen specifically associated with HUMAN bite infections and 'fight bite' (clenched-fist) injuries. Fight bites occur when a fist strikes teeth during an altercation, creating a small but deep laceration over the MCP joint that penetrates the extensor tendon, joint capsule, and/or bone. These wounds are notoriously high-risk for septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, and deep-space hand infections. Management requires: hand surgery consultation, IV antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or piperacillin-tazobactam), operative exploration/washout if joint space penetration is suspected, and wound should NOT be closed primarily.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Pasteurella multocida is the dominant pathogen in CAT and DOG bite infections, not human bites. While Pasteurella is present in the oral flora of cats and dogs, it is NOT a significant component of human oral flora. Human bite infections are characterized by Eikenella corrodens, oral streptococci, Staphylococcus aureus, and anaerobic bacteria (Bacteroides, Prevotella, Fusobacterium).
explanation.b: Correct. Eikenella corrodens is the hallmark pathogen specifically associated with HUMAN bite infections and 'fight bite' (clenched-fist) injuries. Fight bites occur when a fist strikes teeth during an altercation, creating a small but deep laceration over the MCP joint that penetrates the extensor tendon, joint capsule, and/or bone. These wounds are notoriously high-risk for septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, and deep-space hand infections. Management requires: hand surgery consultation, IV antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or piperacillin-tazobactam), operative exploration/washout if joint space penetration is suspected, and wound should NOT be closed primarily.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Capnocytophaga canimorsus is a gram-negative rod found in DOG and CAT saliva that causes fulminant sepsis with DIC and purpura specifically in ASPLENIC, alcoholic, and immunocompromised patients. It is NOT associated with human bites. Capnocytophaga is a zoonotic pathogen transmitted exclusively through dog/cat saliva contact.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Bartonella henselae is the causative agent of cat-scratch disease (CSD) — a self-limited lymphadenitis that develops 1-3 weeks after a cat scratch or bite, presenting with regional lymphadenopathy proximal to the inoculation site. While Bartonella is cat-associated, it causes a distinct clinical entity (granulomatous lymphadenitis) rather than the acute wound infection pattern described in this question. Additionally, the question specifically asks about HUMAN bite infections, not cat-associated pathogens.

integumentary.question: An emergency medicine resident asks the ACNP which type of mammalian bite carries the highest rate of wound infection, necessitating prophylactic antibiotics for all patients. Which of the following bite types has the HIGHEST infection rate?

answers.text: Dog
Cat
Human
All equal

explanation.correct: Correct. Cat bites have the highest infection rate (~30-50%) among common mammalian bites, significantly higher than dog bites (~5-15%) and human bites (~10-20%). This is directly attributable to the cat's dental anatomy: thin, sharp, needle-like teeth penetrate deeply into tissue, effectively inoculating bacteria (particularly Pasteurella multocida) into deep tissue planes, tendon sheaths, and joint spaces. The small puncture wounds are difficult to irrigate adequately. For this reason, ALL cat bites require prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate), and cat bite puncture wounds should NOT be sutured closed.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Dog bites have a relatively LOW infection rate of approximately 5-15%, despite being the most common type of mammalian bite. Dog bites typically produce crush/tear (avulsion) injuries with wider, shallower wound morphology that is more amenable to thorough irrigation and debridement — reducing bacterial inoculation into deep tissues. Prophylactic antibiotics are recommended selectively for dog bites (deep punctures, hand bites, immunocompromised patients), not universally.
explanation.b: Correct. Cat bites have the highest infection rate (~30-50%) among common mammalian bites, significantly higher than dog bites (~5-15%) and human bites (~10-20%).
