This is directly attributable to the cat's dental anatomy: thin, sharp, needle-like teeth penetrate deeply into tissue, effectively inoculating bacteria (particularly Pasteurella multocida) into deep tissue planes, tendon sheaths, and joint spaces. The small puncture wounds are difficult to irrigate adequately. For this reason, ALL cat bites require prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate), and cat bite puncture wounds should NOT be sutured closed.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Human bite infections carry a significant infection rate (approximately 10-20%) but this is still lower than cat bites overall. However, the specific subset of 'fight bites' (clenched-fist injuries over the MCP joint) have very high complication rates because the wound penetrates the thin dorsal skin directly into the joint capsule and extensor tendon, creating a high-risk environment for septic arthritis and osteomyelitis. All human bites should receive prophylactic antibiotics and should not be sutured closed.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Bite wound infection rates vary significantly by animal type due to differences in tooth morphology, bite mechanics, and oral bacterial flora. Cat bites have the highest rate (~30-50%) due to deep puncture wound mechanics; dog bites are moderate (~5-15%); human bites vary by location (~10-20% overall, higher for fight bites). Understanding these differences guides clinical decisions about prophylactic antibiotics, wound closure, and follow-up intensity.

integumentary.question: A 28-year-old man sustains a large contaminated dog bite wound to his forearm. His immunization records confirm a completed primary tetanus vaccination series with a Tdap booster 3 years ago. Which of the following is the appropriate tetanus prophylaxis for this patient?

answers.text: Tdap + TIG
No tetanus prophylaxis needed
TIG alone
Tdap booster only

explanation.correct: Correct. This patient has a completed primary tetanus series (≥3 documented doses) with the most recent booster 3 years ago. For DIRTY/contaminated wounds (animal bites, punctures, soil contamination, devitalized tissue), a Td/Tdap booster is indicated if the last dose was ≥5 years ago. Since this patient's last dose was only 3 years ago, NO tetanus prophylaxis (neither booster nor TIG) is needed. TIG (tetanus immunoglobulin) is only indicated for dirty wounds in patients with <3 prior doses or UNKNOWN vaccination history — to provide immediate passive immunity while the active vaccination series is initiated.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) is indicated ONLY for contaminated/dirty wounds in patients with FEWER than 3 prior tetanus vaccine doses or UNKNOWN vaccination history. This patient has ≥3 documented doses with his most recent booster only 3 years ago — well within the 5-year window for dirty wounds. Administering TIG to a fully vaccinated patient with a recent booster provides no additional protection and is an unnecessary use of a blood-derived product.
explanation.b: Correct. This patient has a completed primary tetanus series (≥3 documented doses) with the most recent booster 3 years ago. For DIRTY/contaminated wounds (animal bites, punctures, soil contamination, devitalized tissue), a Td/Tdap booster is indicated if the last dose was ≥5 years ago. Since this patient's last dose was only 3 years ago, NO tetanus prophylaxis (neither booster nor TIG) is needed. TIG (tetanus immunoglobulin) is only indicated for dirty wounds in patients with <3 prior doses or UNKNOWN vaccination history — to provide immediate passive immunity while the active vaccination series is initiated.
explanation.c: Incorrect. TIG (tetanus immunoglobulin) should NEVER be administered alone without concurrent active immunization (Td/Tdap). TIG provides only PASSIVE, temporary immunity (preformed anti-tetanus antibodies with a half-life of approximately 28 days). Without active vaccination to stimulate the patient's own immune response, protection wanes completely within weeks. When TIG is indicated, it is always given WITH Td/Tdap at a different injection site.
explanation.d: Incorrect. A Tdap booster is NOT needed when the last dose was within 5 years for a dirty/contaminated wound (or within 10 years for a clean wound). This patient received a Tdap booster only 3 years ago, which is well within the 5-year window. Administering an unnecessary booster wastes resources and exposes the patient to avoidable injection-site reactions without providing additional protective benefit.

integumentary.question: A 45-year-old construction worker steps on a rusty nail, sustaining a deep puncture wound to his right foot. He does not know his tetanus vaccination history. The wound is contaminated with soil and debris. Which of the following is the most appropriate tetanus prophylaxis?

answers.text: Tdap only
Tdap + TIG
TIG alone
No prophylaxis needed

explanation.correct: Correct. For a contaminated wound in a patient with UNKNOWN or INCOMPLETE (<3 doses) tetanus vaccination history, BOTH Td/Tdap (active immunization to stimulate long-term antibody production) AND TIG (tetanus immunoglobulin — passive immunization providing immediate preformed anti-tetanus antibodies) are required. TIG provides temporary protection (half-life ~28 days) while the patient's immune system responds to the active vaccine over 2-4 weeks. This combination ensures both immediate and long-term protection against Clostridium tetani infection. TIG and Td/Tdap should be administered at different injection sites using different syringes.
explanation.a: Incorrect. When a patient has UNKNOWN vaccination history AND a contaminated wound, a Td/Tdap booster ALONE is insufficient. Active immunization (Td/Tdap) requires 2-4 weeks to stimulate adequate antibody production — during which time the patient remains vulnerable to tetanus toxin if Clostridium tetani spores germinate in the wound. TIG must be co-administered to provide IMMEDIATE passive protection during this critical window.
explanation.b: Correct. For a contaminated wound in a patient with UNKNOWN or INCOMPLETE (<3 doses) tetanus vaccination history, BOTH Td/Tdap (active immunization to stimulate long-term antibody production) AND TIG (tetanus immunoglobulin — passive immunization providing immediate preformed anti-tetanus antibodies) are required. TIG provides temporary protection (half-life ~28 days) while the patient's immune system responds to the active vaccine over 2-4 weeks. This combination ensures both immediate and long-term protection against Clostridium tetani infection. TIG and Td/Tdap should be administered at different injection sites using different syringes.
explanation.c: Incorrect. TIG alone provides only temporary passive immunity (preformed antibodies) without stimulating the patient's own immune system. Once the exogenous antibodies are catabolized (half-life ~28 days), the patient has NO residual tetanus protection. Active immunization with Td/Tdap is always required concurrently to initiate the patient's own long-term immune response. TIG and vaccine are given at different anatomic sites using separate syringes to prevent interference.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Tetanus is a real and potentially fatal risk with any contaminated wound. Clostridium tetani spores are ubiquitous in soil, dust, and animal feces worldwide. When introduced into wounds (particularly deep puncture wounds, crush injuries, and devitalized tissue), spores can germinate in the anaerobic wound environment and produce tetanospasmin — one of the most potent biological toxins known. Tetanus carries a mortality rate of 10-20% even with modern ICU care, and prevention through vaccination is far more effective than treatment.

integumentary.question: A 30-year-old presents 18 hours after sustaining a 4 cm laceration to the volar forearm from a kitchen knife. The wound was immediately cleaned at home. On exam, the wound edges are clean and well-approximated with no signs of infection, no foreign body, and no tendon involvement. What is the most appropriate closure approach?

answers.text: Primary closure immediately
Delayed primary closure at 3-5 days after irrigation, wound packing, and monitoring for infection
Skin grafting
Leave open without follow-up

explanation.correct: Correct. For an extremity laceration presenting beyond the traditional 6-12 hour window, delayed primary closure (DPC) is a conservative and safe approach — irrigate, loosely pack the wound, and close at 3-5 days if no infection develops. However, evidence increasingly supports that clean, well-perfused wounds without contamination can be safely closed beyond traditional time cutoffs; clinical judgment based on contamination, blood supply, and infection risk is more important than a rigid hourly rule.
explanation.a: Incorrect. For extremity lacerations, the traditional 'golden period' for primary closure is 6-12 hours (some sources extend to 18 hours for clean wounds with minimal contamination). At 18 hours, primary closure carries a higher infection risk because bacteria have had time to colonize the wound bed. However, evidence increasingly suggests that CLEAN wounds on well-perfused tissue can be safely closed beyond these traditional cutoffs — clinical judgment about contamination level, wound appearance, and tissue vascularity matters more than strict hourly cutoffs.
explanation.b: Correct. For an extremity laceration presenting beyond the traditional 6-12 hour window, delayed primary closure (DPC) is a conservative and safe approach — irrigate, loosely pack the wound, and close at 3-5 days if no infection develops. However, evidence increasingly supports that clean, well-perfused wounds without contamination can be safely closed beyond traditional time cutoffs; clinical judgment based on contamination, blood supply, and infection risk is more important than a rigid hourly rule.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Skin grafting (split-thickness or full-thickness) is reserved for large tissue defects where primary closure or delayed primary closure is not possible — typically after burns, degloving injuries, large excisions, or chronic wounds with significant tissue loss. A 4 cm linear laceration can be closed with sutures or staples and does not require the complexity of skin grafting.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Leaving a wound completely open to heal by secondary intention WITHOUT follow-up is inappropriate management. If delayed primary closure is chosen, the wound must be irrigated, loosely packed, and monitored closely at 24-48 hour intervals for signs of infection (increasing erythema, purulence, fever, wound deterioration). If no infection develops by day 3-5, the wound can be closed. Abandoning the wound without follow-up risks missed infection, delayed healing, and suboptimal cosmetic outcome.

integumentary.question: A 5-year-old boy sustains a 3 cm laceration to his forehead after falling against a coffee table. The wound is cleaned and closed with sutures in the emergency department. The mother asks when the sutures should be removed. What is the recommended suture removal timing for facial lacerations?

answers.text: 1-2 days
3-5 days
10-14 days
21 days

explanation.correct: Correct. Facial sutures should be removed early — at 3-5 days — because: (1) the face has excellent blood supply, promoting rapid wound healing and reducing infection risk; (2) early removal minimizes permanent suture track marks (epithelialized 'railroad tracks' that form when sutures remain too long in thin, well-vascularized facial skin); (3) Steri-strips (adhesive wound closure strips) can be applied after suture removal to provide additional support during continued healing. Comparison of suture removal timing by location: face 3-5 days, scalp 7-10 days, trunk/extremities 7-14 days, joints (over flexion surfaces) 10-14 days.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Removing sutures at 1-2 days is too early — the wound has not developed sufficient tensile strength to maintain closure without suture support. Premature suture removal risks wound dehiscence (opening), requiring re-suturing or healing by secondary intention with a worse cosmetic result.
explanation.b: Correct. Facial sutures should be removed early — at 3-5 days — because: (1) the face has excellent blood supply, promoting rapid wound healing and reducing infection risk; (2) early removal minimizes permanent suture track marks (epithelialized 'railroad tracks' that form when sutures remain too long in thin, well-vascularized facial skin); (3) Steri-strips (adhesive wound closure strips) can be applied after suture removal to provide additional support during continued healing. Comparison of suture removal timing by location: face 3-5 days, scalp 7-10 days, trunk/extremities 7-14 days, joints (over flexion surfaces) 10-14 days.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Leaving facial sutures in place for 10-14 days is too long and is the timing appropriate for EXTREMITY wounds. On the face, prolonged suture retention causes permanent epithelialized suture tracks ('railroad tracks' or 'cross-hatching') — cosmetically unacceptable scars from the suture material itself. The face's excellent blood supply allows faster healing, enabling earlier suture removal (3-5 days) without compromising wound integrity.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Twenty-one days is far too long for suture retention in any body location. Even for joints and high-tension areas (which have the longest recommended retention at 10-14 days), 21 days would cause significant suture-track scarring, tissue reaction, and increased infection risk from the foreign body presence of the suture material.

integumentary.question: An ACNP in the emergency department is evaluating several patients with traumatic wounds and determining which are appropriate for primary closure. Which of the following wound types should NOT be closed with primary suturing due to unacceptably high infection risk?

answers.text: Clean facial laceration at 12 hours
Human bite on the hand
Clean scalp laceration at 6 hours
Clean linear leg laceration at 4 hours

explanation.correct: Correct. Human bite wounds on the hand should NEVER be closed primarily due to extremely high infection rates. The human oral cavity contains >400 bacterial species, and hand wounds (especially fight bites over the MCP joint) are at particular risk for deep-space infection, septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, and flexor tenosynovitis. Management: copious irrigation, wound exploration for tendon/joint involvement, prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate), tetanus and hepatitis B/HIV risk assessment, hand surgery consultation for fight bites, and close 24-48 hour follow-up. Cat bite puncture wounds are also generally left open for the same infection-risk rationale.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Facial lacerations can generally be closed up to 24 hours after injury (and sometimes longer) because the face has an exceptionally rich blood supply that promotes rapid healing and provides strong resistance to wound infection. The excellent facial vasculature maintains robust immune cell delivery and tissue oxygenation, allowing safe primary closure well beyond the typical 6-12 hour window used for extremity wounds.
explanation.b: Correct. Human bite wounds on the hand should NEVER be closed primarily due to extremely high infection rates. The human oral cavity contains >400 bacterial species, and hand wounds (especially fight bites over the MCP joint) are at particular risk for deep-space infection, septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, and flexor tenosynovitis. Management: copious irrigation, wound exploration for tendon/joint involvement, prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate), tetanus and hepatitis B/HIV risk assessment, hand surgery consultation for fight bites, and close 24-48 hour follow-up. Cat bite puncture wounds are also generally left open for the same infection-risk rationale.
explanation.c: Incorrect. A clean scalp laceration at 6 hours is well within the safe window for primary closure. Like the face, the scalp has excellent blood supply, and scalp lacerations can typically be closed up to 24 hours after injury with low infection rates. Scalp wounds bleed profusely due to the rich vascular network and often require closure primarily for hemostasis.
explanation.d: Incorrect. A clean, linear leg laceration presenting at 4 hours is well within the standard primary closure window (6-12 hours for extremity wounds). With adequate irrigation, debridement of any contaminated tissue, and appropriate wound edge apposition, this wound can be safely closed with an expected low infection rate.

integumentary.question: A 40-year-old woman presents with a clean 4 cm laceration to her forearm that is being dressed after primary closure. She asks the ACNP whether the wound should be kept dry or covered with ointment. Which of the following wound dressing principles promotes the fastest wound healing?

answers.text: Keep wound dry and exposed
Moist wound healing
Hydrogen peroxide with each change
Change dressings every hour

explanation.correct: Correct. MOIST wound healing is the evidence-based standard, producing: faster epithelialization (40-50% faster than dry healing), reduced pain (moist wounds have less nerve exposure), less scarring, and improved overall wound cosmesis. The optimal moist environment is maintained by applying topical antibiotic ointment (bacitracin, mupirocin) plus a non-adherent dressing covered with gauze. For deeper or draining wounds, moisture-retentive dressings (calcium alginate, hydrofiber, foam) absorb excess exudate while maintaining the moist wound bed. Dressing changes every 1-2 days (or when saturated) are standard — more frequent changes disrupt the healing microenvironment.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Keeping wounds dry and exposed to air is an outdated wound care practice that has been disproven by decades of research. Dry wound healing results in desiccation (drying) of the wound surface, formation of a hard eschar (scab) that actually IMPEDES epithelial cell migration, increased pain from exposed nerve endings, and more prominent scarring. George Winter's landmark 1962 study demonstrated that moist wounds epithelialize 40-50% faster than dry wounds.
explanation.b: Correct. MOIST wound healing is the evidence-based standard, producing: faster epithelialization (40-50% faster than dry healing), reduced pain (moist wounds have less nerve exposure), less scarring, and improved overall wound cosmesis. The optimal moist environment is maintained by applying topical antibiotic ointment (bacitracin, mupirocin) plus a non-adherent dressing covered with gauze. For deeper or draining wounds, moisture-retentive dressings (calcium alginate, hydrofiber, foam) absorb excess exudate while maintaining the moist wound bed. Dressing changes every 1-2 days (or when saturated) are standard — more frequent changes disrupt the healing microenvironment.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) is CYTOTOXIC to fibroblasts, keratinocytes, and other cells essential for wound healing. While it has mild antimicrobial properties through oxygen free radical generation, the tissue damage it causes significantly outweighs any antimicrobial benefit. Current wound care guidelines recommend against using hydrogen peroxide, povidone-iodine (full strength), or Dakin solution directly on healing wounds. Gentle irrigation with normal saline or clean tap water is preferred for wound cleansing.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Changing wound dressings every hour is unnecessary, disruptive to the healing process, and potentially harmful. Each dressing change disturbs the moist wound healing microenvironment, disrupts newly forming epithelium and granulation tissue, causes pain, and increases contamination risk. Standard wound dressing changes are performed every 1-2 days (or when saturated/soiled). Less frequent dressing changes with moisture-retentive dressings actually promote faster healing.

integumentary.question: A 72-year-old fair-skinned man with a history of extensive sun exposure presents with a slowly growing, painless nodule on the left side of his nose. Examination reveals a 1 cm pearly, translucent papule with visible surface telangiectasias and rolled, well-defined borders. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: SCC
Basal cell carcinoma
Melanoma
Actinic keratosis

explanation.correct: Correct. This is the classic presentation of BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (BCC) — the most common skin cancer (accounting for ~80% of all skin cancers). Classic features: pearly/translucent papule or nodule with surface telangiectasias, rolled borders, and possible central ulceration ('rodent ulcer') on chronically sun-exposed areas (face, nose, ears, neck). BCC is locally destructive (can invade cartilage, bone) but RARELY metastasizes (<0.1%), distinguishing it from SCC and melanoma. Risk factors: chronic UV exposure, fair skin (Fitzpatrick I-II), prior radiation therapy, immunosuppression, and genetic syndromes (Gorlin/basal cell nevus syndrome).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) presents as a firm, erythematous, scaly plaque or nodule that may ulcerate and bleed, often arising from a preexisting actinic keratosis. SCC typically has rough, keratotic surface texture and ill-defined borders — distinctly different from the pearly, translucent appearance with telangiectasias and smooth, rolled borders characteristic of BCC. SCC has greater metastatic potential (2-5%) compared to BCC (<0.1%).
explanation.b: Correct. This is the classic presentation of BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (BCC) — the most common skin cancer (accounting for ~80% of all skin cancers). Classic features: pearly/translucent papule or nodule with surface telangiectasias, rolled borders, and possible central ulceration ('rodent ulcer') on chronically sun-exposed areas (face, nose, ears, neck). BCC is locally destructive (can invade cartilage, bone) but RARELY metastasizes (<0.1%), distinguishing it from SCC and melanoma. Risk factors: chronic UV exposure, fair skin (Fitzpatrick I-II), prior radiation therapy, immunosuppression, and genetic syndromes (Gorlin/basal cell nevus syndrome).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Melanoma presents as an asymmetric, irregularly bordered lesion with color variation (brown, black, red, white, blue), typically >6 mm in diameter and changing over time (ABCDEs). Melanoma does NOT have the pearly/translucent appearance, telangiectasias, or rolled borders characteristic of BCC. Melanoma has the highest metastatic potential and mortality of all skin cancers and requires excisional biopsy for accurate Breslow depth staging.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Actinic keratosis (AK) presents as rough, scaly, sandpaper-textured patches on chronically sun-exposed skin — they are FLAT lesions, not nodules. AKs are PREmalignant (not cancer) and represent dysplastic keratinocytes that may progress to SCC if untreated (estimated 1-10% lifetime progression risk per lesion). Treatment: cryotherapy, topical 5-fluorouracil, topical imiquimod, or photodynamic therapy. AKs do not have the nodular, pearly morphology of BCC.

integumentary.question: A 48-year-old woman has a 12 mm asymmetric, irregularly bordered pigmented lesion on her trunk. What type of biopsy is recommended for a suspected melanoma when anatomically feasible?

answers.text: Shave biopsy
Excisional biopsy with narrow margins
FNA
Punch biopsy of center only

explanation.correct: Correct. Excisional biopsy with 1-3 mm narrow margins encompassing the ENTIRE lesion through the subcutaneous fat is the gold standard for suspected melanoma. This preserves the complete tumor architecture for accurate BRESLOW THICKNESS measurement — the single most important prognostic factor in melanoma that determines surgical excision margins and staging: melanoma in situ → 0.5-1 cm margins; ≤1 mm Breslow → 1 cm margins; 1.01-2 mm → 1-2 cm margins; >2 mm → 2 cm margins. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is recommended for Breslow depth >1 mm (or >0.8 mm with adverse features: ulceration, high mitotic rate, lymphovascular invasion).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Shave biopsy is CONTRAINDICATED for suspected melanoma because it may transect (cut through) the base of the tumor, preventing accurate measurement of BRESLOW THICKNESS — the single most important prognostic and staging factor in melanoma. If the deepest portion of the melanoma is shaved off and discarded, the pathologist cannot determine the true tumor depth, leading to potential understaging and inadequate surgical margins. Shave biopsies are appropriate for suspected BCC, SCC, and benign lesions — but NEVER for melanoma.
explanation.b: Correct. Excisional biopsy with 1-3 mm narrow margins encompassing the ENTIRE lesion through the subcutaneous fat is the gold standard for suspected melanoma. This preserves the complete tumor architecture for accurate BRESLOW THICKNESS measurement — the single most important prognostic factor in melanoma that determines surgical excision margins and staging: melanoma in situ → 0.5-1 cm margins; ≤1 mm Breslow → 1 cm margins; 1.01-2 mm → 1-2 cm margins; >2 mm → 2 cm margins. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is recommended for Breslow depth >1 mm (or >0.8 mm with adverse features: ulceration, high mitotic rate, lymphovascular invasion).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) samples individual cells but does NOT preserve tissue architecture, which is essential for melanoma diagnosis and staging. FNA cannot provide Breslow depth measurement, assess for ulceration, determine mitotic rate, or evaluate for lymphovascular invasion — all critical pathologic features that guide melanoma staging and treatment planning. FNA is used for evaluating LYMPH NODES (e.g., assessing suspicious nodes in known melanoma patients), not for primary skin lesion diagnosis.
explanation.d: Incorrect. A punch biopsy of the center only (partial/incisional biopsy) may MISS the thickest area of the melanoma, leading to inaccurate Breslow depth measurement and potential understaging. Melanomas often have variable depth across the lesion, and the thickest area may be at the periphery, not the center. EXCISIONAL biopsy of the entire lesion is the standard of care to ensure that the maximum depth is captured. Punch biopsy may be acceptable only when the lesion is too large for excisional biopsy (e.g., large facial lesion) or in anatomically constrained locations.

integumentary.question: A nurse practitioner student asks the ACNP which type of skin cancer has the greatest potential for distant metastasis and the highest mortality rate. Which of the following skin cancers has the highest metastatic potential?

answers.text: BCC
Melanoma
SCC in situ
Actinic keratosis

explanation.correct: Correct. Melanoma is the most lethal skin cancer despite being less common than BCC and SCC. It has the highest metastatic potential of all skin cancers — it can spread to virtually any organ including brain, lungs, liver, and bone. Prognosis is directly related to Breslow thickness (depth of invasion), ulceration, mitotic rate, and lymph node status. Thin melanomas (<1 mm) detected early have >95% 5-year survival, while thick melanomas (>4 mm) or those with distant metastases have dramatically worse prognosis. Advanced/metastatic melanoma treatment has been revolutionized by immunotherapy (anti-PD-1: pembrolizumab, nivolumab; anti-CTLA-4: ipilimumab) and targeted therapy (BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF V600E mutant tumors).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is locally destructive — it can erode through skin, cartilage, and bone if neglected — but it very RARELY metastasizes (<0.1% of cases). BCC is the most COMMON skin cancer but NOT the most deadly. Most BCC-related morbidity comes from local tissue destruction, particularly in cosmetically sensitive areas (nose, periorbital region), rather than distant metastasis.
explanation.b: Correct. Melanoma is the most lethal skin cancer despite being less common than BCC and SCC. It has the highest metastatic potential of all skin cancers — it can spread to virtually any organ including brain, lungs, liver, and bone. Prognosis is directly related to Breslow thickness (depth of invasion), ulceration, mitotic rate, and lymph node status. Thin melanomas (<1 mm) detected early have >95% 5-year survival, while thick melanomas (>4 mm) or those with distant metastases have dramatically worse prognosis. Advanced/metastatic melanoma treatment has been revolutionized by immunotherapy (anti-PD-1: pembrolizumab, nivolumab; anti-CTLA-4: ipilimumab) and targeted therapy (BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF V600E mutant tumors).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Squamous cell carcinoma IN SITU (Bowen disease) is, by definition, confined to the epidermis — the dysplastic cells have NOT invaded through the basement membrane into the dermis. Without dermal invasion, there is no access to lymphatic or vascular channels, making metastasis impossible. SCC in situ is treated with excision, topical 5-FU, or imiquimod. Once SCC invades the dermis (becomes invasive SCC), metastatic potential increases to approximately 2-5%.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Actinic keratosis (AK) is a PREmalignant lesion — it is NOT cancer and cannot metastasize. AKs represent dysplastic keratinocytes in the epidermis that have the potential to progress to invasive squamous cell carcinoma over time (estimated 1-10% lifetime risk per lesion). AKs are treated to prevent this progression, not because they themselves can spread. Treatment options include cryotherapy, topical 5-FU, topical imiquimod, and photodynamic therapy.

integumentary.question: A dermatology preceptor asks the ACNP student to describe the clinical warning signs used to evaluate pigmented skin lesions for possible melanoma. Which of the following correctly describes the ABCDEs of melanoma assessment?

answers.text: Age, Border, Color, Depth, Excision
Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolving
Asymmetry, Bleeding, Crusting, Drainage, Erythema
Atypical, Benign, Congenital, Dermal, Epidermal

explanation.correct: Correct. The ABCDEs of melanoma assessment: (A) Asymmetry — one half does not mirror the other; (B) Border irregularity — edges are ragged, scalloped, or blurred; (C) Color variation — multiple shades of brown, black, red, white, or blue within the same lesion; (D) Diameter >6 mm — larger than a pencil eraser (though melanomas can be smaller at diagnosis); (E) Evolving — any change in size, shape, color, elevation, or new symptoms (itching, bleeding, crusting). The 'ugly duckling' sign is an additional clinical clue: a mole that looks distinctly DIFFERENT from the patient's other nevi warrants biopsy regardless of ABCDE criteria.
explanation.a: Incorrect. The correct ABCDE mnemonic starts with 'A' for Asymmetry (one half of the mole does not match the other), not 'Age.' Additionally, 'D' stands for Diameter (>6 mm), not 'Depth,' and 'E' stands for Evolving (changing over time), not 'Excision.'
explanation.b: Correct. The ABCDEs of melanoma assessment: (A) Asymmetry — one half does not mirror the other; (B) Border irregularity — edges are ragged, scalloped, or blurred; (C) Color variation — multiple shades of brown, black, red, white, or blue within the same lesion; (D) Diameter >6 mm — larger than a pencil eraser (though melanomas can be smaller at diagnosis); (E) Evolving — any change in size, shape, color, elevation, or new symptoms (itching, bleeding, crusting). The 'ugly duckling' sign is an additional clinical clue: a mole that looks distinctly DIFFERENT from the patient's other nevi warrants biopsy regardless of ABCDE criteria.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While some of these features may be seen in skin cancers, this is NOT the standardized ABCDE mnemonic taught in dermatology and tested on board examinations. The correct mnemonic specifically refers to Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, and Evolving.
explanation.d: Incorrect. These terms describe histologic or anatomic classifications of lesions, not the clinical warning signs used for melanoma screening. The ABCDE mnemonic is a clinical assessment tool designed for visual examination by providers and patients, not a pathologic classification system.

integumentary.question: A 65-year-old man is diagnosed with a biopsy-confirmed basal cell carcinoma on the ala of his nose, a cosmetically sensitive and functionally important area. The ACNP and dermatologist discuss treatment options. Which of the following is the preferred treatment approach for BCC in cosmetically sensitive areas?

answers.text: Wide excision with 2 cm margins
Mohs micrographic surgery
Observation
Oral isotretinoin

explanation.correct: Correct. Mohs micrographic surgery is the preferred treatment for BCC in cosmetically and functionally sensitive areas (nose, ears, periorbital, lips, digits). Mohs provides the HIGHEST cure rate (~99% for primary BCC) while maximizing tissue conservation through real-time microscopic margin assessment during surgery. During Mohs, thin layers of tissue are sequentially removed and immediately examined under microscopy — if tumor cells are found at any margin, additional tissue is removed from that specific area only. This precise, tissue-sparing approach is critical for facial structures where every millimeter of preserved tissue affects cosmetic and functional outcomes.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Wide local excision with 2 cm margins is inappropriate for BCC, particularly on the face. Standard surgical excision margins for BCC are 3-5 mm for well-defined lesions and 5-10 mm for aggressive subtypes — far smaller than 2 cm. Wide 2 cm margins would remove excessive healthy tissue from a cosmetically and functionally critical area (the nose), causing unnecessary disfigurement. Mohs surgery achieves superior cure rates with tissue-sparing precision, making it the preferred approach for facial BCC.
explanation.b: Correct. Mohs micrographic surgery is the preferred treatment for BCC in cosmetically and functionally sensitive areas (nose, ears, periorbital, lips, digits). Mohs provides the HIGHEST cure rate (~99% for primary BCC) while maximizing tissue conservation through real-time microscopic margin assessment during surgery. During Mohs, thin layers of tissue are sequentially removed and immediately examined under microscopy — if tumor cells are found at any margin, additional tissue is removed from that specific area only. This precise, tissue-sparing approach is critical for facial structures where every millimeter of preserved tissue affects cosmetic and functional outcomes.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Observation (watchful waiting) is NOT appropriate for BCC. While BCC grows slowly and rarely metastasizes, it is LOCALLY DESTRUCTIVE — untreated BCC will continue to invade surrounding tissue, eroding through skin, cartilage, and eventually bone. On the nose, untreated BCC can destroy the alar cartilage, invade the nasal cavity, and cause severe cosmetic and functional deformity. All confirmed BCCs require definitive treatment.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Oral isotretinoin (Accutane) is a systemic retinoid used for severe nodulocystic acne — it has NO role in BCC treatment. Isotretinoin reduces sebaceous gland activity and normalizes follicular keratinization in acne, but does not have antineoplastic activity against BCC. Some evidence supports oral retinoids for chemoprevention in patients with basal cell nevus syndrome (Gorlin syndrome) who develop numerous BCCs, but this is a specialized preventive strategy, not a treatment for established BCC.

musculoskeletal.question: A 22-year-old man is found down after suspected drug use. CK is 68,000 U/L, creatinine 2.8, K 5.9, urine is dark brown. Urine dipstick is positive for blood but microscopy shows 0-2 RBCs/hpf. Which is the most appropriate initial intervention?

answers.text: IV sodium bicarbonate infusion to alkalinize urine to pH >6.5
Aggressive IV isotonic fluid resuscitation targeting urine output 200-300 mL/hr
Emergent hemodialysis for hyperkalemia and AKI
IV mannitol to promote osmotic diuresis and myoglobin clearance

explanation.correct: Correct. Aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation (LR or NS) is the cornerstone of rhabdomyolysis treatment. Target UO 200-300 mL/hr to flush myoglobin through the tubules. Start at 1-1.5 L/hr initially and titrate. This is the most effective intervention for preventing AKI progression.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Urine alkalinization with bicarbonate theoretically reduces myoglobin precipitation in tubules (myoglobin is less soluble in acidic urine), but clinical evidence is weak and bicarbonate can worsen hypocalcemia (common in rhabdomyolysis) and cause metabolic alkalosis. Aggressive fluid resuscitation is the priority; bicarbonate is not routinely recommended.
explanation.b: Correct. Aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation (LR or NS) is the cornerstone of rhabdomyolysis treatment. Target UO 200-300 mL/hr to flush myoglobin through the tubules. Start at 1-1.5 L/hr initially and titrate. This is the most effective intervention for preventing AKI progression.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Hemodialysis is not indicated at this stage. K 5.9 with preserved urine output (if diuresis achieved) should be managed medically. Dialysis is reserved for anuric AKI with refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, fluid overload, or uremia. Initiating dialysis before aggressive fluid resuscitation fails is premature.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Mannitol may increase urine flow but has not been shown superior to isotonic fluid resuscitation alone, and risks intravascular volume depletion and osmotic nephropathy if overused. It is not recommended as initial therapy over simple aggressive IV hydration.

musculoskeletal.question: A 19-year-old man sustains a tibial fracture in a football injury. Six hours after splinting, he develops severe pain with passive stretch of the toes and a tense calf. He has intact pulses. Compartment pressure is measured at 38 mmHg. Diastolic BP is 72 mmHg. Which is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Elevate the extremity, remove constrictive dressings, and repeat pressure in 2 hours
Emergent four-compartment fasciotomy
MRI of the lower leg to evaluate muscle viability before surgical decision
IV mannitol to reduce tissue swelling and reassess

explanation.correct: Correct. Delta pressure = diastolic (72) - compartment pressure (38) = 34 mmHg. Threshold for fasciotomy is ≤30 mmHg, and clinical signs are present. Emergent four-compartment leg fasciotomy is the definitive treatment. Intact pulses do not exclude compartment syndrome — neuromuscular injury precedes vascular compromise.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Removing constrictive dressings and elevating the limb are initial measures for suspected compartment syndrome, but with compartment pressure 38 and delta P of 34 mmHg combined with clinical signs, medical management is insufficient. The window for fasciotomy to prevent irreversible muscle necrosis is narrow — 6-8 hours. Delay risks permanent damage.
explanation.b: Correct. Delta pressure = diastolic (72) - compartment pressure (38) = 34 mmHg. Threshold for fasciotomy is ≤30 mmHg, and clinical signs are present. Emergent four-compartment leg fasciotomy is the definitive treatment. Intact pulses do not exclude compartment syndrome — neuromuscular injury precedes vascular compromise.
explanation.c: Incorrect. MRI is not part of acute compartment syndrome management — it delays definitive treatment and is not necessary to make the surgical decision. Compartment pressure measurement and clinical exam are the diagnostic standards. MRI-induced delay risks irreversible ischemic muscle necrosis.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IV mannitol is used for cerebral edema and rhabdomyolysis — not for limb compartment syndrome. It will not adequately reduce compartment pressure to safe levels and delays fasciotomy.

musculoskeletal.question: A 62-year-old man with diabetes presents with 3 weeks of right foot pain and a non-healing plantar ulcer. He probes to bone with a sterile swab. Plain film shows cortical erosion of the second metatarsal. WBC is 11,200, ESR 88 mm/hr, CRP 64 mg/L. Which is the most sensitive test to confirm osteomyelitis and what is first-line treatment?

answers.text: CT scan of the foot; IV vancomycin monotherapy for 6 weeks
Bone biopsy for culture and pathology; pathogen-directed antibiotics for 6 weeks guided by bone biopsy culture
MRI of the foot; empiric oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for 6 weeks
Leukocyte scintigraphy (tagged WBC scan); IV cefazolin for 6 weeks

explanation.correct: Correct. Bone biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard — it confirms osteomyelitis histologically and identifies the causative organism(s) for targeted therapy. Diabetic foot osteomyelitis is often polymicrobial. Culture-directed antibiotics for 6 weeks (IV or highly bioavailable oral agents such as fluoroquinolones, linezolid) is guideline-recommended. Surgical debridement may be needed concurrently.
explanation.a: Incorrect. CT detects cortical destruction but is less sensitive than MRI for early marrow involvement. More importantly, vancomycin monotherapy covers MRSA but misses gram-negative organisms frequently involved in diabetic foot osteomyelitis — polymicrobial infection is the rule. Empiric monotherapy without culture guidance is suboptimal.
explanation.b: Correct. Bone biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard — it confirms osteomyelitis histologically and identifies the causative organism(s) for targeted therapy. Diabetic foot osteomyelitis is often polymicrobial. Culture-directed antibiotics for 6 weeks (IV or highly bioavailable oral agents such as fluoroquinolones, linezolid) is guideline-recommended. Surgical debridement may be needed concurrently.
explanation.c: Incorrect. MRI is the most sensitive imaging study for osteomyelitis (sensitivity >90%) and can confirm the diagnosis, but it is not the gold standard for microbiological diagnosis. Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate is inadequate for MRSA and many gram-negative organisms found in diabetic foot osteomyelitis. Culture-directed therapy is essential.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Tagged WBC scan (leukocyte scintigraphy) is used when MRI is contraindicated but has lower sensitivity/specificity than MRI. Cefazolin covers MSSA but not MRSA or gram-negative organisms. Starting empiric cefazolin without cultures risks treatment failure in polymicrobial infection.

musculoskeletal.question: A 24-year-old man is admitted with rhabdomyolysis from prolonged immobility after a seizure. CK is 125,000 U/L. After 24 hours of aggressive IV NS hydration at 300 mL/hr, urine output is 80 mL/hr (target 200-300) and creatinine has risen from 1.2 to 3.8. Urine remains dark. Which is the most appropriate next step?

answers.text: Increase NS rate to 500 mL/hr — aggressive volume is the only treatment
Start continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) — CK >100,000 requires dialysis regardless of other parameters
Assess volume status (exam, CVP, or bedside echo); if euvolemic or hypervolemic with oliguria — initiate CRRT; if still volume-depleted — continue aggressive resuscitation
IV furosemide 80 mg to force urine output and flush myoglobin through the tubules

explanation.correct: Correct. After 24 hours of aggressive resuscitation with ongoing oliguria and worsening creatinine, clinical reassessment is essential: (1) If volume-depleted (dry mucous membranes, low CVP, flat IVC) — continue/increase fluids; (2) If euvolemic or hypervolemic (crackles, elevated CVP, full IVC) with oliguria → established AKI requiring RRT. CRRT is preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients. The goal is to remove myoglobin and uremic toxins while managing fluid balance.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Blindly increasing IV fluids without assessing volume status risks fluid overload and pulmonary edema. After 24 hours of aggressive resuscitation, the patient may be euvolemic or hypervolemic — additional fluid without checking volume status is potentially harmful.
explanation.b: Incorrect. CK level alone does not determine dialysis need. CK 125,000 with appropriate urine output might not require dialysis. The indications for RRT are: oliguria/AKI despite adequate volume, refractory hyperkalemia, severe metabolic acidosis, or fluid overload — not CK level per se.
explanation.c: Correct. After 24 hours of aggressive resuscitation with ongoing oliguria and worsening creatinine, clinical reassessment is essential: (1) If volume-depleted (dry mucous membranes, low CVP, flat IVC) — continue/increase fluids; (2) If euvolemic or hypervolemic (crackles, elevated CVP, full IVC) with oliguria → established AKI requiring RRT. CRRT is preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients. The goal is to remove myoglobin and uremic toxins while managing fluid balance.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Loop diuretics in rhabdomyolysis are not evidence-based and potentially harmful. Furosemide concentrates tubular myoglobin, potentially worsening tubular obstruction. It may transiently increase urine output in a volume-replete patient but does not improve outcomes. Furosemide should not be used to 'flush' myoglobin.

musculoskeletal.question: An 18-year-old woman undergoes fasciotomy for forearm compartment syndrome after a crush injury. Post-fasciotomy, the wounds are left open. On day 4, the wounds show pink granulation tissue, minimal edema, and the skin edges have retracted 4 cm bilaterally. Which is the most appropriate wound management strategy?

answers.text: Primary closure on day 4 — fasciotomy wounds should always be closed as soon as possible to prevent infection
Split-thickness skin grafting immediately — granulation tissue indicates readiness for definitive closure
Continue open wound management; plan delayed primary closure (vessel loops or skin staples for gradual approximation) or split-thickness skin graft when wound is ready (typically day 5-14)
Wound VAC application at -125 mmHg to rapidly reduce edema and prepare for early closure

explanation.correct: Correct. Post-fasciotomy wound management: keep wounds open initially, dress with moist saline dressings or negative pressure wound therapy. When swelling subsides and granulation tissue appears (days 5-14), options include: (1) Delayed primary closure using vessel loops across wound edges with serial tightening ('shoelace' technique); (2) Split-thickness skin grafting for wounds that cannot be closed primarily due to skin loss/retraction. Most forearm wounds can achieve delayed primary closure with gradual approximation before resorting to grafting.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Primary closure on day 4 is premature and may not be possible given 4 cm skin edge retraction. Forced primary closure over compartments with residual swelling risks recurrent compartment syndrome and wound dehiscence. Gradual approximation or grafting is appropriate.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Split-thickness skin grafting on day 4 is possible but not necessarily required yet. Wound closure techniques should be chosen based on wound readiness (typically days 5-14). Many forearm fasciotomy wounds can achieve delayed primary closure with gradual skin approximation techniques before requiring grafting.
explanation.c: Correct. Post-fasciotomy wound management: keep wounds open initially, dress with moist saline dressings or negative pressure wound therapy. When swelling subsides and granulation tissue appears (days 5-14), options include: (1) Delayed primary closure using vessel loops across wound edges with serial tightening ('shoelace' technique); (2) Split-thickness skin grafting for wounds that cannot be closed primarily due to skin loss/retraction. Most forearm wounds can achieve delayed primary closure with gradual approximation before resorting to grafting.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While wound VAC is used for fasciotomy wounds, -125 mmHg is a standard pressure used in wound management. VAC can prepare the wound bed and reduce edema. However, it is one option — not mandatory — and the comprehensive answer addresses the overall wound management strategy including closure planning, which VAC alone does not address.

musculoskeletal.question: A 25-year-old man is brought in after a high-speed motorcycle collision with pelvic instability on physical exam. BP 82/50 despite 2L crystalloid. Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma (FAST) is negative for intraperitoneal free fluid. Pelvic X-ray shows an open book pelvic fracture. Which is the most appropriate immediate hemorrhage control strategy?

answers.text: Emergent laparotomy — open book fracture with shock requires immediate surgical control
Pelvic binder or sheet application to reduce pelvic volume and tamponade venous bleeding; activate massive transfusion protocol
CT angiography of the pelvis first to identify arterial bleeding before intervention
IV tranexamic acid 1g over 10 minutes; no mechanical stabilization needed

explanation.correct: Correct. Open book pelvic fracture hemorrhage management: (1) Immediate pelvic binder or circumferential sheet tied at level of greater trochanters — reduces pelvic volume by closing the anterior ring, compressing pelvic veins (85% of pelvic fracture bleeding is venous), and provides tamponade; (2) Activate massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 pRBC:FFP:platelets); (3) If binder + MTP fails → IR angioembolization for arterial bleeding; (4) REBOA (Resuscitative Endovascular Balloon Occlusion of the Aorta) as adjunct. Do not delay binder application for imaging in an unstable patient.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Laparotomy for isolated pelvic fracture hemorrhage (FAST negative — no intraperitoneal blood) is not indicated. Pelvic hematomas are retroperitoneal — opening the abdomen releases the tamponade effect and worsens retroperitoneal bleeding. Laparotomy is indicated when FAST is positive indicating intraperitoneal hemorrhage.
explanation.b: Correct. Open book pelvic fracture hemorrhage management: (1) Immediate pelvic binder or circumferential sheet tied at level of greater trochanters — reduces pelvic volume by closing the anterior ring, compressing pelvic veins (85% of pelvic fracture bleeding is venous), and provides tamponade; (2) Activate massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 pRBC:FFP:platelets); (3) If binder + MTP fails → IR angioembolization for arterial bleeding; (4) REBOA (Resuscitative Endovascular Balloon Occlusion of the Aorta) as adjunct. Do not delay binder application for imaging in an unstable patient.
explanation.c: Incorrect. CT angiography requires hemodynamic stability the patient does not have (BP 82/50). Imaging in an unstable trauma patient with pelvic fracture delays lifesaving hemorrhage control. Per ATLS: hemodynamically unstable pelvic fracture → binder + MTP NOW, angio if needed after stabilization or simultaneously in hybrid OR.
explanation.d: Incorrect. IV tranexamic acid (1g over 10 min within 3 hours of injury, per CRASH-2 trial) is an important adjunct for trauma hemorrhage reducing fibrinolysis-related bleeding mortality. However, TXA alone without mechanical stabilization of an open book fracture is insufficient hemorrhage control. Pelvic binder is the priority mechanical intervention.

musculoskeletal.question: A 42-year-old man with sickle cell disease presents with fever 39.2°C, severe right tibial pain and swelling, and WBC 18,000. MRI confirms osteomyelitis of the right tibia. Which organism is most likely causative in sickle cell osteomyelitis AND what is the appropriate empiric antibiotic?

answers.text: Staphylococcus aureus is still most common; empiric vancomycin for MRSA coverage

Salmonella species is the classic sickle cell osteomyelitis organism; cover with a fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) OR third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone) — but S. aureus remains common so dual coverage is often needed
Streptococcus pneumoniae; empiric penicillin G
Pseudomonas aeruginosa from functional asplenia; antipseudomonal beta-lactam (pip-tazo)

explanation.correct: Correct. Sickle cell osteomyelitis: Salmonella species (20-50% of cases — uniquely elevated compared to the general population) AND S. aureus (also common). Mechanism: functional asplenia from repeated splenic infarction impairs opsonization of encapsulated gram-negative organisms. Empiric coverage should address both: ciprofloxacin or ceftriaxone (Salmonella) plus antistaphylococcal coverage. Bone biopsy/aspiration for definitive diagnosis guides narrowing.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While S. aureus is the most common cause of osteomyelitis overall, in sickle cell disease Salmonella is the classic and high-yield organism — it is the most commonly cited differentiator. The association is so strong that any board exam question about sickle cell + osteomyelitis points to Salmonella first.
explanation.b: Correct. Sickle cell osteomyelitis: Salmonella species (20-50% of cases — uniquely elevated compared to the general population) AND S. aureus (also common). Mechanism: functional asplenia from repeated splenic infarction impairs opsonization of encapsulated gram-negative organisms. Empiric coverage should address both: ciprofloxacin or ceftriaxone (Salmonella) plus antistaphylococcal coverage. Bone biopsy/aspiration for definitive diagnosis guides narrowing.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Streptococcus pneumoniae causes invasive disease (meningitis, pneumonia, bacteremia) in sickle cell patients from functional asplenia — but not classically osteomyelitis. Penicillin G alone is inadequate for sickle cell osteomyelitis coverage.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is associated with osteomyelitis from puncture wounds (e.g., nail through sneaker in children) and IV drug use — not specifically with sickle cell disease. Functional asplenia primarily predisposes to encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, Salmonella) — not Pseudomonas.

musculoskeletal.question: A 22-year-old motorcyclist has a tibial fracture after a collision. Four hours post-splinting, he complains of severe pain with passive toe dorsiflexion, a tense anterior compartment, and paresthesias in the first web space. Measured anterior compartment pressure is 48 mmHg; his diastolic BP is 74 mmHg. Which is the diagnosis and treatment threshold?

answers.text: Compartment pressure 48 mmHg is elevated, but observation is appropriate as long as pulses are present
Acute compartment syndrome; emergent fasciotomy is indicated because delta pressure is 26 mmHg
Neurovascular compromise from splinting; loosen the splint and reassess in 4 to 6 hours before deciding on fasciotomy
Normal post-fracture pain; manage with opioids and repeat exam tomorrow

explanation.correct: Correct. Acute compartment syndrome is primarily a clinical diagnosis. Classic findings include pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch, a tense compartment, and paresthesias. Pressure measurements can support the diagnosis. Two commonly used thresholds are an absolute compartment pressure greater than 30 mmHg or a delta pressure of 30 mmHg or less. In this patient, the delta pressure is 74 − 48 = 26 mmHg, which meets the treatment threshold. Emergent fasciotomy is indicated to prevent irreversible muscle and nerve ischemia.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Presence of distal pulses does not rule out compartment syndrome. The delta pressure is calculated as diastolic BP minus compartment pressure: 74 − 48 = 26 mmHg. A delta pressure of 30 mmHg or less is concerning for compartment syndrome and supports urgent fasciotomy, especially when classic clinical findings are present.
explanation.b: Correct. Acute compartment syndrome is primarily a clinical diagnosis. Classic findings include pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch, a tense compartment, and paresthesias. Pressure measurements can support the diagnosis. Two commonly used thresholds are an absolute compartment pressure greater than 30 mmHg or a delta pressure of 30 mmHg or less. In this patient, the delta pressure is 74 − 48 = 26 mmHg, which meets the treatment threshold. Emergent fasciotomy is indicated to prevent irreversible muscle and nerve ischemia.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Loosening or removing restrictive dressings may be a brief temporizing step, but it is not definitive management when the clinical picture and pressure data support compartment syndrome. Delaying fasciotomy risks permanent tissue injury.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch, tense compartment, and paresthesias are red flags for compartment syndrome and require urgent intervention, not routine pain control and delayed reassessment.

musculoskeletal.question: A 32-year-old man collapses during a marathon in hot weather. CK is 68,000 U/L, creatinine 2.4, potassium 6.1, urine is dark brown (myoglobinuria). Temperature 40.1°C. Which is the most important intervention to prevent AKI?

answers.text: IV sodium bicarbonate infusion to alkalinize urine and prevent myoglobin precipitation
Aggressive IV normal saline to achieve urine output of 200-300 mL/hr until CK trending down
IV furosemide to force diuresis and flush myoglobin through the tubules
Hemodialysis immediately for CK >50,000 regardless of renal function

explanation.correct: Correct. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation is the cornerstone of rhabdomyolysis management. Target: urine output 200-300 mL/hr until CK trending down and urine clears. This dilutes myoglobin in tubular fluid, maintains GFR, and prevents tubular obstruction. Correct electrolytes (K 6.1 needs treatment). Treat hyperthermia aggressively. Normal saline is preferred — avoid LR (contains potassium).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Urine alkalinization (sodium bicarbonate) to prevent myoglobin precipitation is theoretically appealing but has NOT been shown to be superior to normal saline alone in clinical studies. It also risks metabolic alkalosis and hypocalcemia. Aggressive NS resuscitation is+I32:K32 the evidence-based cornerstone.
explanation.b: Correct. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation is the cornerstone of rhabdomyolysis management. Target: urine output 200-300 mL/hr until CK trending down and urine clears. This dilutes myoglobin in tubular fluid, maintains GFR, and prevents tubular obstruction. Correct electrolytes (K 6.1 needs treatment). Treat hyperthermia aggressively. Normal saline is preferred — avoid LR (contains potassium).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Loop diuretics in rhabdomyolysis are controversial and potentially harmful — they concentrate the tubular myoglobin rather than diluting it. IV fluids establish adequate urine output naturally if volume is sufficient. Furosemide is only used if volume-overloaded and oliguria persists despite adequate resuscitation.
explanation.d: Incorrect. CK level alone does not determine dialysis need. Dialysis is indicated for: oliguria/AKI not responding to fluids, hyperkalemia refractory to medical management, severe acidosis, or fluid overload. A CK of 68,000 with adequate urine output can be managed with aggressive IV fluids without immediate dialysis.

musculoskeletal.question: A 58-year-old diabetic man presents with a chronic foot ulcer at the plantar surface of the first metatarsal. Probing the ulcer reaches bone. ESR 88, CRP 62. Plain X-ray shows subtle periosteal reaction at the first metatarsal head. MRI confirms osteomyelitis. Wound culture grows MSSA. Which is the most appropriate antibiotic therapy?

answers.text: IV vancomycin for 6 weeks — MRSA must be covered empirically in diabetic foot osteomyelitis
IV cefazolin 2g q8h (or IV nafcillin/oxacillin) for 4-6 weeks OR transition to oral TMP-SMX plus rifampin after initial IV response
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for 12 weeks — adequate bioavailability for diabetic foot osteomyelitis
Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID for 6 weeks — fluoroquinolone with excellent bone penetration

explanation.correct: Correct. MSSA osteomyelitis: IV anti-staphylococcal beta-lactam (cefazolin, nafcillin, or oxacillin) is first-line with proven superiority to vancomycin for MSSA. Duration: 4-6 weeks for osteomyelitis. Oral step-down options for MSSA after initial IV response: TMP-SMX + rifampin (rifampin provides biofilm penetration; TMP-SMX has excellent bone penetration). IDSA supports early oral step-down for stable patients.
explanation.a: Incorrect. MSSA is confirmed — vancomycin is inferior to beta-lactams for MSSA. Empiric MRSA coverage is not indicated when MSSA susceptibilities are confirmed. Using vancomycin unnecessarily exposes the patient to nephrotoxicity and inferior outcomes.
explanation.b: Correct. MSSA osteomyelitis: IV anti-staphylococcal beta-lactam (cefazolin, nafcillin, or oxacillin) is first-line with proven superiority to vancomycin for MSSA. Duration: 4-6 weeks for osteomyelitis. Oral step-down options for MSSA after initial IV response: TMP-SMX + rifampin (rifampin provides biofilm penetration; TMP-SMX has excellent bone penetration). IDSA supports early oral step-down for stable patients.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Amoxicillin-clavulanate covers MSSA but also provides unnecessary broad-spectrum coverage. More importantly, 12 weeks is longer than standard osteomyelitis treatment duration (4-6 weeks) without evidence of benefit. Oral therapy from day 1 without initial IV is generally not recommended for severe/deep osteomyelitis.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Ciprofloxacin monotherapy for MSSA osteomyelitis has high resistance emergence rates — Staphylococcus rapidly develops fluoroquinolone resistance during monotherapy. Fluoroquinolone monotherapy for S. aureus osteomyelitis is not recommended. If fluoroquinolones are used, they must be combined with another agent (e.g., rifampin).

musculoskeletal.question: A 78-year-old woman on aspirin and warfarin (INR 2.4) falls and sustains a right femoral neck fracture. She is hemodynamically stable. Orthopedics wants to take her to the OR within 24-48 hours. Which perioperative management decision has the best evidence for improving outcomes in hip fracture?

answers.text: Delay surgery until INR <1.5; hold aspirin for 5-7 days before surgery to minimize bleeding
Reverse warfarin with 4-factor PCC to INR <1.5 and proceed to surgery within 24-48 hours; continue aspirin
Hold surgery for 7-10 days until all antithrombotic effects resolve to minimize intraoperative bleeding
Proceed with surgery at INR 2.4 — studies show no increased bleeding at therapeutic INR

explanation.correct: Correct. Time to surgery is a quality metric for hip fracture — mortality increases significantly with delays beyond 24-48 hours. Warfarin reversal with 4-factor PCC allows urgent INR normalization within hours. Aspirin continuation is generally safe for orthopedic surgery and its cardiovascular benefit outweighs the modest bleeding increase. NHFD (National Hip Fracture Database) data supports surgery within 24 hours.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Waiting for INR to drift down spontaneously delays surgery. Hip fracture surgery within 24-48 hours significantly reduces mortality, pain, delirium, DVT, and pneumonia. The INR must be reversed urgently with 4-factor PCC, not passively.
explanation.b: Correct. Time to surgery is a quality metric for hip fracture — mortality increases significantly with delays beyond 24-48 hours. Warfarin reversal with 4-factor PCC allows urgent INR normalization within hours. Aspirin continuation is generally safe for orthopedic surgery and its cardiovascular benefit outweighs the modest bleeding increase. NHFD (National Hip Fracture Database) data supports surgery within 24 hours.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Delaying hip fracture surgery 7-10 days significantly increases: mortality (OR 1.41 for >48h delay), DVT, pressure injuries, pneumonia, and delirium. Modern perioperative anticoagulation management allows rapid reversal and early surgery — delaying is not standard of care.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While some studies suggest therapeutic anticoagulation doesn't dramatically worsen operative bleeding for some procedures, hip fracture surgery with INR 2.4 carries increased intraoperative bleeding risk. Guidelines recommend INR <1.5 for orthopedic surgery. PCC allows rapid reversal without the delay of vitamin K alone.

musculoskeletal.question: A 65-year-old man with critical limb ischemia and dry gangrene of the right foot is scheduled for below-knee amputation. 48 hours post-amputation he develops fever 38.8°C, erythema spreading up the residual limb, pain at the surgical site, and foul-smelling drainage. WBC 24,000. Which is the most appropriate management?

answers.text: IV cefazolin for 7 days for presumed wound cellulitis — most common post-amputation infection
Emergent surgical exploration and debridement; wound culture; broad-spectrum antibiotics covering polymicrobial infection including anaerobes
Wound VAC application to promote healing; IV vancomycin for MRSA coverage
MRI of residual limb before any intervention to confirm depth of infection

explanation.correct: Correct. Post-amputation wound infection with systemic signs (fever, WBC 24K), foul-smelling drainage, and rapid progression within 48 hours requires: (1) emergent surgical re-exploration to identify residual necrosis, dehiscence, deep space infection, or gas-forming organisms; (2) broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-positives (MRSA), gram-negatives, and anaerobes (e.g., vancomycin + pip-tazo or meropenem); (3) wound cultures. Early intervention prevents gas gangrene and ascending infection.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Cefazolin alone provides narrow-spectrum coverage (predominantly gram-positive). Post-amputation wound infection in a diabetic/vascular patient is frequently polymicrobial with anaerobes, gram-negatives, and gram-positives. Fever, spreading erythema, foul odor, and high WBC in the first 48 hours suggests a serious deep wound infection potentially including residual necrosis or gas-forming organisms.
explanation.b: Correct. Post-amputation wound infection with systemic signs (fever, WBC 24K), foul-smelling drainage, and rapid progression within 48 hours requires: (1) emergent surgical re-exploration to identify residual necrosis, dehiscence, deep space infection, or gas-forming organisms; (2) broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-positives (MRSA), gram-negatives, and anaerobes (e.g., vancomycin + pip-tazo or meropenem); (3) wound cultures. Early intervention prevents gas gangrene and ascending infection.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Wound VAC is contraindicated over infected wounds without surgical debridement — it creates an anaerobic environment promoting gas-forming organisms. Additionally, the decision to apply VAC comes after debridement, not as initial management of fever and spreading cellulitis.
explanation.d: Incorrect. MRI is useful for osteomyelitis diagnosis but its use before any intervention in an acutely ill patient with rapidly evolving post-amputation wound infection wastes critical time. Clinical diagnosis of serious post-surgical wound infection requires immediate surgical and antibiotic management.

musculoskeletal.question: A 25-year-old man is brought to the ED after a motorcycle accident with a closed tibial shaft fracture. He complains of severe, worsening pain in his anterior leg that is disproportionate to the visible injury. Passive dorsiflexion of the toes elicits excruciating pain. The anterior compartment is tense and firm. Pedal pulses are still palpable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: Deep vein thrombosis of the lower extremity
Acute compartment syndrome — emergent fasciotomy required
Fracture-related pain — increase IV morphine and splint the extremity
Arterial injury — CT angiography of the lower extremity

explanation.correct: Correct. Compartment syndrome occurs when pressure within a closed fascial compartment exceeds perfusion pressure, causing ischemia. The 6 P's (Pain out of proportion, Pain with passive stretch, Pressure/tense compartment, Paresthesias, Paralysis, Pulselessness) are classic but late findings. CRITICAL: Pulses may be PRESENT until very late because compartment pressure can compromise microvascular flow before occluding larger arteries. Diagnosis is clinical; compartment pressures >30 mmHg or within 30 mmHg of diastolic BP (delta pressure) confirm it. Emergent fasciotomy is the only definitive treatment — delay beyond 6 hours causes irreversible muscle necrosis.
explanation.a: Incorrect. DVT presents with calf swelling, warmth, and tenderness — not a tense compartment with pain on passive stretch. DVT does not cause the severe, disproportionate pain and firmness seen here. The mechanism (acute fracture with compartment findings) points to compartment syndrome.
explanation.b: Correct. Compartment syndrome occurs when pressure within a closed fascial compartment exceeds perfusion pressure, causing ischemia. The 6 P's (Pain out of proportion, Pain with passive stretch, Pressure/tense compartment, Paresthesias, Paralysis, Pulselessness) are classic but late findings. CRITICAL: Pulses may be PRESENT until very late because compartment pressure can compromise microvascular flow before occluding larger arteries. Diagnosis is clinical; compartment pressures >30 mmHg or within 30 mmHg of diastolic BP (delta pressure) confirm it. Emergent fasciotomy is the only definitive treatment — delay beyond 6 hours causes irreversible muscle necrosis.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While fracture pain is expected, pain DISPROPORTIONATE to the injury with pain on passive stretch and a tense compartment is a red flag for compartment syndrome — a surgical emergency. Simply increasing analgesics and applying a splint (which may actually worsen compartment pressure) could lead to permanent limb damage.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Arterial injury would present with absent distal pulses, expanding hematoma, and signs of limb ischemia. This patient has PALPABLE pedal pulses. The tense compartment with pain on passive stretch indicates compartment syndrome, not arterial disruption. CTA delays the emergent fasciotomy that is needed.

musculoskeletal.question: A 58-year-old man presents with acute onset of severe pain, swelling, and erythema of his right first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint. He has a history of CKD stage 4 (GFR 18 mL/min). Synovial fluid analysis shows negatively birefringent, needle-shaped crystals under polarized microscopy. Which acute treatment is most appropriate given his renal function?

answers.text: Colchicine 1.2 mg followed by 0.6 mg one hour later
Indomethacin 50 mg TID
Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day for 5–7 days
Allopurinol 300 mg daily to lower uric acid urgently

explanation.correct: Correct. In patients with CKD where NSAIDs are contraindicated and colchicine carries increased toxicity risk, systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day for 5–7 days, then taper) are the preferred acute gout treatment. Alternatively, intra-articular corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone) can be used for monoarticular gout with fewer systemic effects.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While low-dose colchicine is an option for acute gout, it requires dose reduction in severe renal impairment (GFR <30) and is best used within 36 hours of flare onset. In CKD stage 4, the risk of toxicity (GI, neuromyopathy) is increased. Corticosteroids are preferred in this clinical scenario.
explanation.b: Incorrect. NSAIDs are CONTRAINDICATED in CKD stage 4 (GFR 18 mL/min). They inhibit prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation, reducing renal blood flow and worsening kidney function. NSAIDs can precipitate AKI in patients with pre-existing CKD.
explanation.c: Correct. In patients with CKD where NSAIDs are contraindicated and colchicine carries increased toxicity risk, systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day for 5–7 days, then taper) are the preferred acute gout treatment. Alternatively, intra-articular corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone) can be used for monoarticular gout with fewer systemic effects.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol, febuxostat) should NEVER be initiated during an acute gout flare — it can paradoxically worsen the attack by mobilizing urate crystals from tissue deposits. ULT is started 2–4 weeks after the acute flare has resolved, with concurrent prophylaxis (low-dose colchicine or prednisone).

musculoskeletal.question: A 68-year-old woman presents with acute onset of severe midthoracic back pain after lifting a heavy object. She has a history of osteoporosis and is not on any bone-protective therapy. X-ray shows a T11 vertebral compression fracture with 40% height loss. She has no neurological deficits. What is the most important initial management in addition to pain control?

answers.text: Emergent surgical spinal fusion
Initiate osteoporosis treatment (bisphosphonate or denosumab) and consider vertebral augmentation if pain persists
Strict bed rest for 6 weeks with a thoracolumbar brace
MRI to rule out malignancy before any treatment

explanation.correct: Correct. Immediate priorities: (1) Pain management (acetaminophen, short-course opioids, calcitonin nasal spray may provide analgesic benefit); (2) Initiate osteoporosis pharmacotherapy — bisphosphonates (alendronate, zoledronic acid) or denosumab are first-line to reduce future fracture risk (this patient has an untreated osteoporotic fracture); (3) Calcium 1200 mg + vitamin D 800–1000 IU daily; (4) Early mobilization to prevent deconditioning; (5) Vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty may be considered for severe pain refractory to 2–4 weeks of conservative management.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Surgical fixation is not indicated for stable osteoporotic compression fractures without neurological compromise. Most osteoporotic VCFs are managed conservatively. Surgery is reserved for unstable fractures, progressive neurological deficits, or failure of conservative management.
explanation.b: Correct. Immediate priorities: (1) Pain management (acetaminophen, short-course opioids, calcitonin nasal spray may provide analgesic benefit); (2) Initiate osteoporosis pharmacotherapy — bisphosphonates (alendronate, zoledronic acid) or denosumab are first-line to reduce future fracture risk (this patient has an untreated osteoporotic fracture); (3) Calcium 1200 mg + vitamin D 800–1000 IU daily; (4) Early mobilization to prevent deconditioning; (5) Vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty may be considered for severe pain refractory to 2–4 weeks of conservative management.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Prolonged bed rest is detrimental — it accelerates bone loss, increases DVT risk, causes deconditioning, and worsens osteoporosis. Early mobilization with appropriate pain management is recommended. A brace may provide comfort but is not mandatory, and prolonged immobilization is harmful.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While pathologic fractures from metastatic disease should be considered, this patient has known untreated osteoporosis, and the mechanism (lifting injury) and location (thoracolumbar junction) are typical for osteoporotic VCFs. MRI may be obtained if there are red flags (unexplained weight loss, history of cancer, multilevel involvement), but it should not delay pain management and osteoporosis treatment initiation.

musculoskeletal.question: A 45-year-old woman presents with symmetric joint pain and swelling of her bilateral MCPs, PIPs, and wrists for 8 weeks. She has morning stiffness lasting 2 hours daily. Labs show positive anti-CCP antibody, elevated ESR (68 mm/hr), and CRP (3.4 mg/dL). X-rays show periarticular osteopenia but no erosions. What is the diagnosis and first-line disease-modifying therapy?

answers.text: Osteoarthritis — acetaminophen and physical therapy
Rheumatoid arthritis — start methotrexate as first-line DMARD
Systemic lupus erythematosus — start hydroxychloroquine
Psoriatic arthritis — start a TNF inhibitor

explanation.correct: Correct. This patient meets criteria for RA: symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis of small joints (MCPs, PIPs, wrists) lasting >6 weeks with morning stiffness >1 hour, positive anti-CCP (highly specific for RA, ~95%), and elevated inflammatory markers. Methotrexate (15–25 mg weekly) is the first-line DMARD per ACR/EULAR guidelines. Early initiation of DMARDs within 3 months of symptom onset significantly improves outcomes and prevents irreversible joint erosion. Folic acid supplementation reduces MTX side effects.
explanation.a: Incorrect. OA affects weight-bearing joints (knees, hips) and DIPs (Heberden's nodes), NOT MCPs and PIPs symmetrically. OA has brief morning stiffness (<30 minutes), negative autoantibodies, and normal inflammatory markers. This patient's presentation is classic for an inflammatory arthritis.
explanation.b: Correct. This patient meets criteria for RA: symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis of small joints (MCPs, PIPs, wrists) lasting >6 weeks with morning stiffness >1 hour, positive anti-CCP (highly specific for RA, ~95%), and elevated inflammatory markers. Methotrexate (15–25 mg weekly) is the first-line DMARD per ACR/EULAR guidelines. Early initiation of DMARDs within 3 months of symptom onset significantly improves outcomes and prevents irreversible joint erosion. Folic acid supplementation reduces MTX side effects.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While SLE can cause inflammatory arthritis, it is typically non-erosive and presents with multisystem features (malar rash, serositis, cytopenias, nephritis, anti-dsDNA/ANA). Anti-CCP is specific for RA, not SLE. The isolated symmetric polyarthritis with anti-CCP is diagnostic of RA.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Psoriatic arthritis can involve small joints but typically presents asymmetrically with dactylitis (sausage digits), enthesitis, nail pitting, and skin psoriasis. Anti-CCP is usually negative in PsA. This patient's symmetric MCP/PIP/wrist involvement with positive anti-CCP is classic for RA.

musculoskeletal.question: A 75-year-old woman falls on an outstretched hand. X-ray shows a displaced distal radius fracture (Colles fracture). Exam reveals numbness in the thumb, index finger, and middle finger with weakness of thumb opposition. What nerve is most likely injured?

answers.text: Ulnar nerve
Median nerve
Radial nerve
Musculocutaneous nerve

explanation.correct: Correct. The median nerve passes through the carpal tunnel and is vulnerable to injury in distal radius fractures. Its sensory distribution includes the palmar thumb, index finger, middle finger, and radial half of the ring finger. Motor function includes thumb opposition (opponens pollicis) and thenar muscles. Acute carpal tunnel syndrome from fracture fragment displacement or swelling requires urgent reduction to relieve nerve compression.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Ulnar nerve injury would cause numbness of the ring and little fingers (ulnar 1.5 digits) and weakness of finger abduction/adduction (interossei) and the hypothenar muscles. The distribution described (thumb, index, middle finger) is median nerve territory.
explanation.b: Correct. The median nerve passes through the carpal tunnel and is vulnerable to injury in distal radius fractures. Its sensory distribution includes the palmar thumb, index finger, middle finger, and radial half of the ring finger. Motor function includes thumb opposition (opponens pollicis) and thenar muscles. Acute carpal tunnel syndrome from fracture fragment displacement or swelling requires urgent reduction to relieve nerve compression.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Radial nerve injury causes wrist drop (inability to extend the wrist and fingers) and sensory loss over the dorsal first web space. It does not affect thumb opposition or palmar sensation of the first three digits. Radial nerve is more commonly injured in humeral shaft fractures.
