explanation.d: Incorrect. The musculocutaneous nerve innervates the biceps and brachialis (elbow flexion) and provides sensation to the lateral forearm. It is not involved in hand sensation or thumb opposition, and it is not at risk from distal radius fractures.

musculoskeletal.question: A 32-year-old man presents with acute back pain, bilateral sacroiliitis on MRI, and morning stiffness >1 hour that improves with exercise. He has a history of recurrent anterior uveitis. HLA-B27 is positive. ESR is elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what extraarticular manifestation should be screened for?

answers.text: Ankylosing spondylitis — screen for inflammatory bowel disease
Reactive arthritis — screen for sexually transmitted infections
Rheumatoid arthritis — screen for pulmonary fibrosis
Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH) — screen for diabetes

explanation.correct: Correct. Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy characterized by inflammatory back pain (onset <40 years, insidious onset, improvement with exercise, no improvement with rest, morning stiffness >30 min), bilateral sacroiliitis, and HLA-B27 positivity. Key extraarticular manifestations: anterior uveitis (most common, 25–40%), inflammatory bowel disease (up to 10% have concurrent Crohn's or UC), psoriasis, and cardiac involvement (aortitis, conduction abnormalities). Screening for IBD is important because subclinical gut inflammation is present in up to 60% of AS patients.
explanation.a: Correct. Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy characterized by inflammatory back pain (onset <40 years, insidious onset, improvement with exercise, no improvement with rest, morning stiffness >30 min), bilateral sacroiliitis, and HLA-B27 positivity. Key extraarticular manifestations: anterior uveitis (most common, 25–40%), inflammatory bowel disease (up to 10% have concurrent Crohn's or UC), psoriasis, and cardiac involvement (aortitis, conduction abnormalities). Screening for IBD is important because subclinical gut inflammation is present in up to 60% of AS patients.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter syndrome) presents with the triad of arthritis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis following a GI or genitourinary infection. While it is HLA-B27 associated and causes sacroiliitis, the chronic bilateral sacroiliitis and recurrent uveitis without preceding infection or urethritis point to AS.
explanation.c: Incorrect. RA is a symmetric peripheral arthritis affecting small joints (MCPs, PIPs, wrists) with positive RF/anti-CCP. It does NOT cause sacroiliitis or bilateral SI joint involvement. RA is NOT associated with HLA-B27. This patient's axial skeleton involvement is characteristic of spondyloarthropathy.
explanation.d: Incorrect. DISH is a non-inflammatory condition causing flowing calcification along the anterolateral spine, typically in older adults (>50) with metabolic syndrome. It does not cause sacroiliitis, uveitis, or inflammatory back pain. This young patient with HLA-B27, bilateral sacroiliitis, and uveitis has ankylosing spondylitis.

musculoskeletal.question: A 68-year-old woman has a DEXA scan showing a T-score of -3.1 at the femoral neck. She has no history of fragility fracture. Her calcium is normal, vitamin D is 18 ng/mL (low), and TSH is normal. She has no contraindications to bisphosphonates. What is the most appropriate treatment plan?

answers.text: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation alone — recheck DEXA in 2 years
Alendronate 70 mg weekly plus calcium 1200 mg/day plus vitamin D 1000 IU/day
Teriparatide (PTH analog) as first-line therapy
Denosumab 60 mg SC every 6 months

explanation.correct: Correct. For postmenopausal women with osteoporosis (T-score ≤ -2.5), bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid) are first-line therapy. They inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, increasing BMD and reducing fracture risk by 40–50%. Vitamin D deficiency must be corrected (goal 25-OH vitamin D ≥30 ng/mL) and calcium supplemented to 1200 mg total daily intake. Alendronate must be taken on an empty stomach with a full glass of water, remaining upright for 30 minutes to prevent esophageal irritation.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A T-score ≤ -2.5 at the femoral neck meets the WHO definition of osteoporosis and requires pharmacologic treatment beyond supplementation alone. Calcium and vitamin D are adjunctive, not sufficient as monotherapy for established osteoporosis.
explanation.b: Correct. For postmenopausal women with osteoporosis (T-score ≤ -2.5), bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid) are first-line therapy. They inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, increasing BMD and reducing fracture risk by 40–50%. Vitamin D deficiency must be corrected (goal 25-OH vitamin D ≥30 ng/mL) and calcium supplemented to 1200 mg total daily intake. Alendronate must be taken on an empty stomach with a full glass of water, remaining upright for 30 minutes to prevent esophageal irritation.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Teriparatide is an anabolic agent (stimulates osteoblasts) reserved for severe osteoporosis with very high fracture risk, multiple prior fractures, or failure/intolerance of bisphosphonates. It is not first-line for uncomplicated osteoporosis and has a maximum treatment duration of 2 years due to concerns about osteosarcoma (from animal studies).
explanation.d: Incorrect. Denosumab (RANKL inhibitor) is an effective alternative when bisphosphonates are contraindicated (severe CKD with GFR <30, esophageal disorders) or not tolerated. However, for a patient with no contraindications to bisphosphonates, oral bisphosphonates are the recommended first-line therapy due to established long-term safety data, cost-effectiveness, and ease of discontinuation.

musculoskeletal.question: A 55-year-old man with diabetes presents with acute onset of severe right knee pain, swelling, warmth, and inability to bear weight. He has fever of 38.8°C. Synovial fluid analysis reveals WBC 68,000/μL (92% neutrophils), no crystals seen on polarized microscopy, and Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters. What is the diagnosis and management?

answers.text: Gout flare — prednisone and colchicine
Septic arthritis from Staphylococcus aureus — IV vancomycin plus urgent joint aspiration and lavage
Reactive arthritis — NSAIDs and physical therapy
Pseudogout (CPPD disease) — NSAIDs and colchicine

explanation.correct: Correct. Septic arthritis diagnostic criteria: synovial WBC >50,000 (often >80,000) with >90% neutrophils, positive Gram stain or culture. Gram-positive cocci in clusters = Staph aureus (most common cause overall). Management: (1) IV antibiotics immediately — vancomycin for empiric MRSA coverage until sensitivities return (de-escalate to nafcillin/cefazolin if MSSA); (2) Urgent surgical drainage — either arthroscopic lavage or open arthrotomy to remove purulent material and reduce bacterial load; (3) Serial joint aspirations if surgical drainage is delayed. WITHOUT prompt drainage and antibiotics, cartilage destruction occurs within 24–48 hours, leading to permanent joint damage.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While gout can present with acute monoarticular inflammation, polarized microscopy shows NO crystals, and Gram stain reveals bacteria — these findings rule out crystal arthropathy and confirm septic arthritis. Treating for gout while missing a septic joint would have devastating consequences (joint destruction within 24–48 hours).
explanation.b: Correct. Septic arthritis diagnostic criteria: synovial WBC >50,000 (often >80,000) with >90% neutrophils, positive Gram stain or culture. Gram-positive cocci in clusters = Staph aureus (most common cause overall). Management: (1) IV antibiotics immediately — vancomycin for empiric MRSA coverage until sensitivities return (de-escalate to nafcillin/cefazolin if MSSA); (2) Urgent surgical drainage — either arthroscopic lavage or open arthrotomy to remove purulent material and reduce bacterial load; (3) Serial joint aspirations if surgical drainage is delayed. WITHOUT prompt drainage and antibiotics, cartilage destruction occurs within 24–48 hours, leading to permanent joint damage.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Reactive arthritis is a sterile inflammatory arthritis (negative Gram stain, no organisms, WBC usually <50,000) that occurs 2–4 weeks after a GI or GU infection. This patient has bacteria on Gram stain and a highly inflammatory synovial fluid — this is septic arthritis requiring emergent treatment.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Pseudogout (CPPD) would show positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped calcium pyrophosphate crystals on polarized microscopy. This patient has no crystals and has gram-positive cocci on Gram stain — confirming bacterial infection, not crystal arthropathy.

musculoskeletal.question: A 62-year-old diabetic man presents with a non-healing plantar foot ulcer that has been present for 8 weeks. The ulcer probes to bone (positive probe-to-bone test). ESR is 92 mm/hr, CRP is 6.8 mg/dL, and WBC is 12,400/μL. Plain X-rays show cortical destruction of the second metatarsal head. What is the most appropriate diagnostic and treatment approach?

answers.text: Treat with oral ciprofloxacin for 2 weeks based on the positive probe-to-bone test
MRI of the foot for staging, bone biopsy for culture and pathology, then targeted IV antibiotics for 4–6 weeks plus surgical debridement
Nuclear bone scan (technetium-99m) as first-line imaging
Empiric IV vancomycin plus piperacillin-tazobactam for 2 weeks without bone biopsy

explanation.correct: Correct. Optimal management: (1) MRI is the best imaging modality for osteomyelitis — it detects marrow edema, cortical disruption, and soft tissue extent with >90% sensitivity and specificity; (2) Bone biopsy with culture is the gold standard for identifying the causative organism and guiding antibiotic therapy (polymicrobial infections are common in diabetic foot osteomyelitis — Staph aureus, Enterococcus, gram-negatives, anaerobes); (3) IV antibiotics for 4–6 weeks based on culture sensitivities; (4) Surgical debridement of necrotic bone ± partial amputation. The IDSA guidelines emphasize that bone culture — NOT wound swab culture — should guide antibiotic selection.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A positive probe-to-bone test in a diabetic foot ulcer has high specificity (~90%) for osteomyelitis, but oral ciprofloxacin for 2 weeks is grossly inadequate. Osteomyelitis requires prolonged IV antibiotic therapy (4–6 weeks) guided by bone culture, and often surgical debridement. Short-course oral antibiotics cannot sterilize infected bone.
explanation.b: Correct. Optimal management: (1) MRI is the best imaging modality for osteomyelitis — it detects marrow edema, cortical disruption, and soft tissue extent with >90% sensitivity and specificity; (2) Bone biopsy with culture is the gold standard for identifying the causative organism and guiding antibiotic therapy (polymicrobial infections are common in diabetic foot osteomyelitis — Staph aureus, Enterococcus, gram-negatives, anaerobes); (3) IV antibiotics for 4–6 weeks based on culture sensitivities; (4) Surgical debridement of necrotic bone ± partial amputation. The IDSA guidelines emphasize that bone culture — NOT wound swab culture — should guide antibiotic selection.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While bone scans are sensitive for osteomyelitis, they have poor specificity in diabetic feet (Charcot arthropathy, recent fractures, and soft tissue infection all cause uptake). MRI is preferred for diabetic foot osteomyelitis because it provides superior anatomic detail, differentiates bone from soft tissue infection, and guides surgical planning.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics without bone culture is suboptimal — diabetic foot osteomyelitis is often polymicrobial, and cultures are essential to guide targeted therapy and identify resistant organisms. Additionally, 2 weeks is insufficient — standard osteomyelitis treatment duration is 4–6 weeks of IV antibiotics.

musculoskeletal.question: A 30-year-old man with bilateral femur fractures develops confusion, tachypnea, tachycardia, and a petechial rash on the chest and axillae 48 hours after injury. SpO2 is 84% on room air. CXR shows bilateral diffuse infiltrates. Platelet count has dropped to 98,000/μL. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answers.text: Pulmonary embolism from deep vein thrombosis
Fat embolism syndrome
ARDS from traumatic lung contusion
Disseminated intravascular coagulation from trauma

explanation.correct: Correct. Fat embolism syndrome (FES) classically presents 24–72 hours after long bone fractures with a triad: (1) Respiratory distress (hypoxemia, bilateral infiltrates — fat globules obstruct pulmonary microvasculature); (2) Neurological changes (confusion, agitation — cerebral fat emboli); (3) Petechial rash (pathognomonic — fat emboli damage dermal capillaries; appears on chest, axillae, conjunctivae). Additional findings: thrombocytopenia, anemia, elevated lipase. Diagnosis is clinical (Gurd's criteria). Treatment is supportive: oxygen, mechanical ventilation if needed, fluids, and supportive care. Prevention: early fixation of long bone fractures within 24 hours reduces FES incidence.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While DVT/PE is a concern after long bone fractures, the classic triad (respiratory distress, neurological changes, petechial rash) occurring 24–72 hours after long bone fracture is pathognomonic for fat embolism syndrome. PE typically does not cause petechiae or the specific neurological changes seen in FES.
explanation.b: Correct. Fat embolism syndrome (FES) classically presents 24–72 hours after long bone fractures with a triad: (1) Respiratory distress (hypoxemia, bilateral infiltrates — fat globules obstruct pulmonary microvasculature); (2) Neurological changes (confusion, agitation — cerebral fat emboli); (3) Petechial rash (pathognomonic — fat emboli damage dermal capillaries; appears on chest, axillae, conjunctivae). Additional findings: thrombocytopenia, anemia, elevated lipase. Diagnosis is clinical (Gurd's criteria). Treatment is supportive: oxygen, mechanical ventilation if needed, fluids, and supportive care. Prevention: early fixation of long bone fractures within 24 hours reduces FES incidence.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While traumatic ARDS can occur after blunt trauma, it typically presents earlier (within hours) and is associated with direct chest injury. The 48-hour delay, classic triad (respiratory + neurological + petechiae), and bilateral femur fractures without chest trauma are specific for fat embolism syndrome.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While DIC can cause thrombocytopenia and petechiae, DIC from trauma presents with prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, and often bleeding from multiple sites. The petechial distribution (chest, axillae, conjunctivae), neurological changes, and temporal relationship to long bone fractures distinguish FES from DIC.

musculoskeletal.question: A 50-year-old man with a recent history of IV drug use presents with 5 days of progressive severe back pain, fever 39.4°C, and now bilateral lower extremity weakness developing over the past 12 hours. ESR is 110 mm/hr, CRP is 14.2 mg/dL, WBC is 19,800/μL. MRI thoracic spine shows a posterior epidural collection compressing the spinal cord at T8–T10 with cord edema. Blood cultures are pending. What is the management priority?

answers.text: IV antibiotics alone for 6–8 weeks — surgery is only needed if antibiotics fail
Emergent surgical decompression (laminectomy and abscess drainage) plus empiric IV antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftriaxone)
CT-guided percutaneous needle aspiration of the abscess
High-dose IV dexamethasone to reduce cord edema, then plan elective surgery in 1–2 weeks

explanation.correct: Correct. Spinal epidural abscess with neurological deficit is a neurosurgical emergency. The degree of recovery correlates directly with the severity and duration of neurological deficit at the time of surgery. Management: (1) Emergent posterior laminectomy with abscess drainage and culture; (2) Empiric IV antibiotics — vancomycin (MRSA coverage, most common organism in IVDU) plus a third-generation cephalosporin (gram-negative coverage); (3) Narrow antibiotics based on intraoperative culture results; (4) Total IV antibiotic duration: 4–8 weeks. Risk factors: IVDU (hematogenous seeding), diabetes, immunosuppression, recent epidural procedures, spinal surgery.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Conservative antibiotic-only management is considered only for patients WITHOUT neurological deficits. This patient has progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness — a neurological emergency requiring both antibiotics AND urgent surgical decompression. Delaying surgery for a trial of antibiotics risks permanent paralysis.
explanation.b: Correct. Spinal epidural abscess with neurological deficit is a neurosurgical emergency. The degree of recovery correlates directly with the severity and duration of neurological deficit at the time of surgery. Management: (1) Emergent posterior laminectomy with abscess drainage and culture; (2) Empiric IV antibiotics — vancomycin (MRSA coverage, most common organism in IVDU) plus a third-generation cephalosporin (gram-negative coverage); (3) Narrow antibiotics based on intraoperative culture results; (4) Total IV antibiotic duration: 4–8 weeks. Risk factors: IVDU (hematogenous seeding), diabetes, immunosuppression, recent epidural procedures, spinal surgery.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Percutaneous aspiration may obtain culture material but does NOT provide adequate decompression of the spinal cord. When neurological deficits are present, open surgical laminectomy is required to decompress the cord and drain the abscess completely. Percutaneous approaches are insufficient for this emergency.
explanation.d: Incorrect. While corticosteroids are used for metastatic epidural cord compression, their role in epidural abscess is controversial — steroids may impair immune response and worsen infection. More importantly, delaying surgery 1–2 weeks in a patient with progressive neurological deficit from cord compression would likely result in irreversible paralysis. Emergent decompression is required.

musculoskeletal.question: A 62-year-old man with hypertension (on hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily), CKD stage 3 (GFR 48), and obesity presents with acute onset of excruciating pain, erythema, and swelling of the right first MTP joint over the past 12 hours. He consumed several beers at a cookout yesterday. Vital signs: T 100.4°F, HR 94, BP 152/88. The joint is exquisitely tender with overlying erythema and warmth. Serum uric acid is 6.2 mg/dL. WBC is 11,400. Which of the following is the most important next diagnostic step?

answers.text: The serum uric acid of 6.2 is normal, effectively ruling out gout — pursue other diagnoses
Arthrocentesis of the first MTP joint for synovial fluid crystal analysis and Gram stain/culture
Start empiric colchicine and NSAIDs immediately without arthrocentesis since the presentation is classic for gout
Obtain X-rays of the right foot and serum rheumatoid factor to differentiate gout from RA

explanation.correct: Correct. Arthrocentesis is the GOLD STANDARD for diagnosing gout AND ruling out septic arthritis, which can present identically (acute monoarticular arthritis with fever, erythema, leukocytosis). Synovial fluid findings in gout: needle-shaped, negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals under polarized light microscopy. The fluid must also be sent for Gram stain and culture because gout and septic arthritis can COEXIST. Key teaching points: (1) Serum uric acid may be normal during flares; (2) The patient has multiple gout risk factors: HCTZ (decreases uric acid excretion), CKD, alcohol, obesity, male sex; (3) Acute gout can present with low-grade fever and elevated WBC, mimicking infection.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Serum uric acid is frequently NORMAL or even LOW during an acute gout flare (paradoxical decrease due to inflammatory cytokine-mediated increased renal excretion). A normal uric acid during an acute attack does NOT rule out gout. This is one of the most commonly tested board concepts.
explanation.b: Correct. Arthrocentesis is the GOLD STANDARD for diagnosing gout AND ruling out septic arthritis, which can present identically (acute monoarticular arthritis with fever, erythema, leukocytosis). Synovial fluid findings in gout: needle-shaped, negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals under polarized light microscopy. The fluid must also be sent for Gram stain and culture because gout and septic arthritis can COEXIST. Key teaching points: (1) Serum uric acid may be normal during flares; (2) The patient has multiple gout risk factors: HCTZ (decreases uric acid excretion), CKD, alcohol, obesity, male sex; (3) Acute gout can present with low-grade fever and elevated WBC, mimicking infection.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While the clinical presentation is suggestive, empiric treatment without arthrocentesis fails to rule out septic arthritis — a surgical emergency. In a patient with fever, erythema, and a hot joint, infection must be excluded by synovial fluid analysis before presuming crystal disease.
explanation.d: Incorrect. X-rays are normal in early gout (erosive changes develop only in chronic tophaceous gout). RF is irrelevant to the acute presentation. Arthrocentesis provides the definitive diagnosis.

musculoskeletal.question: A 58-year-old man with confirmed gout (3 flares in the past year, most recent 2 weeks ago now resolved) and stage 3 CKD (GFR 42) presents to discuss starting urate-lowering therapy (ULT). His serum uric acid is 9.4 mg/dL. He is of Korean descent. He takes lisinopril and amlodipine for hypertension. Which of the following is the most appropriate approach to initiating ULT?

answers.text: Start allopurinol 300 mg daily to reach the target uric acid of <6 mg/dL as quickly as possible
Check HLA-B*5801 before starting allopurinol; if negative, start at 100 mg daily (or lower given CKD), add colchicine prophylaxis, and titrate to serum uric acid <6 mg/dL
Start febuxostat 80 mg daily without HLA testing since HLA-B*5801 is only relevant for allopurinol
Defer ULT since the patient's CKD makes all available options too risky

explanation.correct: Correct. This Korean patient requires HLA-B*5801 testing before allopurinol (prevalence ~12% in Korean populations). If HLA-B*5801 POSITIVE → do NOT use allopurinol (use febuxostat instead). If NEGATIVE → start allopurinol LOW (≤100 mg daily, even lower in CKD) and titrate by 100 mg increments every 2-4 weeks to target uric acid <6 mg/dL. CRITICAL: add colchicine 0.6 mg daily (or BID) as flare prophylaxis for the first 3-6 months of ULT — initiating ULT mobilizes urate crystals and paradoxically triggers flares. Also counsel: (1) Avoid starting ULT during an acute flare; (2) Consider switching HCTZ (if applicable) to losartan (uricosuric) or amlodipine; (3) Dietary counseling (limit alcohol, purine-rich foods).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Starting allopurinol at a high dose increases the risk of allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome (AHS), which includes severe cutaneous adverse reactions (DRESS, SJS/TEN). Furthermore, in CKD, allopurinol should be started at 100 mg daily (or lower) and titrated slowly.
explanation.b: Correct. This Korean patient requires HLA-B*5801 testing before allopurinol (prevalence ~12% in Korean populations). If HLA-B*5801 POSITIVE → do NOT use allopurinol (use febuxostat instead). If NEGATIVE → start allopurinol LOW (≤100 mg daily, even lower in CKD) and titrate by 100 mg increments every 2-4 weeks to target uric acid <6 mg/dL. CRITICAL: add colchicine 0.6 mg daily (or BID) as flare prophylaxis for the first 3-6 months of ULT — initiating ULT mobilizes urate crystals and paradoxically triggers flares. Also counsel: (1) Avoid starting ULT during an acute flare; (2) Consider switching HCTZ (if applicable) to losartan (uricosuric) or amlodipine; (3) Dietary counseling (limit alcohol, purine-rich foods).
explanation.c: Incorrect. While febuxostat does not require HLA-B*5801 testing, febuxostat carries an FDA Black Box Warning for increased cardiovascular mortality compared to allopurinol (CARES trial). It is generally reserved for patients who cannot take allopurinol. Additionally, starting at the full dose without titration increases flare risk.
explanation.d: Incorrect. CKD is actually an INDICATION for ULT (not a contraindication), as hyperuricemia accelerates CKD progression. Allopurinol and febuxostat are both safe in CKD with appropriate dosing adjustments.

musculoskeletal.question: A 52-year-old woman presents with 8 months of symmetric joint pain and morning stiffness lasting 90 minutes affecting the MCP joints, PIP joints, and wrists bilaterally. She reports fatigue, low-grade fevers, and difficulty opening jars. Exam shows swelling and tenderness of the 2nd-4th MCP and PIP joints bilaterally with synovial thickening, ulnar deviation of the fingers, and subcutaneous nodules at the elbows. DIP joints are spared. Labs: RF 182 IU/mL (positive), anti-CCP 240 U/mL (strongly positive), ESR 68, CRP 4.2, Hgb 10.8 (normocytic). Hand X-rays show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions at multiple MCP joints. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment strategy?

answers.text: Start ibuprofen 600 mg TID for pain control and refer to rheumatology in 6 weeks
Start methotrexate 15 mg weekly with folic acid 1 mg daily, initiate low-dose prednisone as bridge therapy, and refer to rheumatology urgently
Start hydroxychloroquine 200 mg BID as first-line DMARD since it is the safest option
Start adalimumab (TNF inhibitor) as first-line monotherapy without methotrexate

explanation.correct: Correct. This is seropositive, erosive RA with poor prognostic features (strongly positive anti-CCP, erosions already present, rheumatoid nodules, anemia of chronic disease). Treatment: (1) METHOTREXATE — the cornerstone DMARD, started immediately (weekly PO or SQ dosing). Folic acid 1 mg daily reduces MTX side effects (mucositis, GI upset, cytopenias). Monitor CBC, LFTs, and creatinine. Contraindications: pregnancy (teratogenic), active liver disease, significant cytopenias. (2) LOW-DOSE PREDNISONE (≤10 mg/day) as bridge therapy while waiting for MTX to reach full effect (6-12 weeks). Taper steroids as rapidly as possible. (3) If inadequate response to MTX after 3 months → add biologic DMARD (TNF inhibitor, IL-6 inhibitor, or JAK inhibitor). Screen for TB and Hep B before biologics. (4) Target: remission or low disease activity (DAS28, CDAI scores).
explanation.a: Incorrect. NSAIDs provide symptom relief but do NOT modify disease progression or prevent joint destruction. Delaying DMARD therapy allows irreversible erosive damage to continue. The 'treat-to-target' approach requires early DMARD initiation.
explanation.b: Correct. This is seropositive, erosive RA with poor prognostic features (strongly positive anti-CCP, erosions already present, rheumatoid nodules, anemia of chronic disease). Treatment: (1) METHOTREXATE — the cornerstone DMARD, started immediately (weekly PO or SQ dosing). Folic acid 1 mg daily reduces MTX side effects (mucositis, GI upset, cytopenias). Monitor CBC, LFTs, and creatinine. Contraindications: pregnancy (teratogenic), active liver disease, significant cytopenias. (2) LOW-DOSE PREDNISONE (≤10 mg/day) as bridge therapy while waiting for MTX to reach full effect (6-12 weeks). Taper steroids as rapidly as possible. (3) If inadequate response to MTX after 3 months → add biologic DMARD (TNF inhibitor, IL-6 inhibitor, or JAK inhibitor). Screen for TB and Hep B before biologics. (4) Target: remission or low disease activity (DAS28, CDAI scores).
explanation.c: Incorrect. Hydroxychloroquine is appropriate for mild RA without erosive disease. This patient has aggressive, erosive, seropositive RA with poor prognostic markers — HCQ alone is insufficiently potent. Methotrexate is the appropriate first-line agent for moderate-severe RA.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Biologic DMARDs are not first-line; they are added when conventional DMARDs (primarily methotrexate) fail. Furthermore, TNF inhibitors are more effective when combined with methotrexate than as monotherapy. Starting biologics requires TB and Hep B screening first.

musculoskeletal.question: A 71-year-old postmenopausal woman with a history of breast cancer (in remission, previously treated with aromatase inhibitors) presents after a fall resulting in a distal radius fracture from low-energy trauma (fell from standing height). She also reports 2 inches of height loss over 5 years. PMH includes GERD treated with omeprazole for 8 years. DEXA scan: lumbar spine T-score -3.1, femoral neck T-score -2.8. Vitamin D level is 18 ng/mL (low). Calcium is 9.2 (normal). TSH and PTH are normal. Which of the following is the most comprehensive treatment plan?

answers.text: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation with weight-bearing exercise only, since pharmacotherapy is not yet indicated
Replete vitamin D, start calcium supplementation, initiate IV zoledronic acid (or denosumab), address fall risk, and discontinue omeprazole if possible
Start oral alendronate 70 mg weekly with careful esophageal precautions
Start teriparatide (PTH analog) indefinitely for maximum bone building

explanation.correct: Correct. This patient has severe osteoporosis (T-score ≤-2.5 with fragility fracture) requiring comprehensive management: (1) VITAMIN D REPLETION: 50,000 IU weekly × 8-12 weeks, then 1000-2000 IU daily maintenance (target 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL); (2) CALCIUM: 1000-1200 mg/day total (dietary + supplement); (3) PHARMACOTHERAPY: Given her fragility fracture and aromatase inhibitor history, first-line options include IV zoledronic acid (5 mg annually — ensures adherence, avoids esophageal issues from oral bisphosphonates) or denosumab (60 mg SQ q6 months). For SEVERE osteoporosis with fracture, anabolic agents (teriparatide, romosozumab) may be considered before transitioning to anti-resorptive therapy. (4) FALL PREVENTION: home safety, PT for balance/strength, review medications for fall risk; (5) ADDRESS CONTRIBUTING FACTORS: Chronic PPI use (>1 year) is associated with calcium malabsorption and fracture risk — reassess necessity and consider step-down or discontinuation; aromatase inhibitors accelerate bone loss. (6) DEXA monitoring every 2 years.
explanation.a: Incorrect. This patient has SEVERE osteoporosis with a fragility fracture, T-scores well below -2.5, and multiple risk factors. Pharmacotherapy is absolutely indicated — not optional. Calcium and vitamin D alone are insufficient.
explanation.b: Correct. This patient has severe osteoporosis (T-score ≤-2.5 with fragility fracture) requiring comprehensive management: (1) VITAMIN D REPLETION: 50,000 IU weekly × 8-12 weeks, then 1000-2000 IU daily maintenance (target 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL); (2) CALCIUM: 1000-1200 mg/day total (dietary + supplement); (3) PHARMACOTHERAPY: Given her fragility fracture and aromatase inhibitor history, first-line options include IV zoledronic acid (5 mg annually — ensures adherence, avoids esophageal issues from oral bisphosphonates) or denosumab (60 mg SQ q6 months). For SEVERE osteoporosis with fracture, anabolic agents (teriparatide, romosozumab) may be considered before transitioning to anti-resorptive therapy. (4) FALL PREVENTION: home safety, PT for balance/strength, review medications for fall risk; (5) ADDRESS CONTRIBUTING FACTORS: Chronic PPI use (>1 year) is associated with calcium malabsorption and fracture risk — reassess necessity and consider step-down or discontinuation; aromatase inhibitors accelerate bone loss. (6) DEXA monitoring every 2 years.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While alendronate is first-line for many patients, this patient has GERD requiring chronic PPI therapy, which suggests esophageal pathology — a relative contraindication to oral bisphosphonates (risk of esophageal ulceration/erosion). IV zoledronic acid or denosumab bypasses the GI tract entirely.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Teriparatide is an excellent anabolic option for severe osteoporosis, but it is limited to ≤2 years of use (theoretical risk of osteosarcoma based on animal data) and must be followed by anti-resorptive therapy to maintain gains. Additionally, teriparatide is contraindicated in patients with a history of bone metastases (relevant given breast cancer history, though in remission). Romosozumab carries a cardiovascular risk warning.

musculoskeletal.question: A 22-year-old college soccer player sustains a non-contact pivoting injury to his left knee during a game. He reports hearing a 'pop' followed by immediate swelling. In the emergency department 2 hours later, the knee is significantly swollen with a tense effusion. He is unable to fully extend the knee. Exam: positive Lachman test with no firm endpoint, positive anterior drawer test, negative valgus and varus stress tests, and equivocal McMurray test (limited by pain and swelling). He has no bony tenderness. Ottawa Knee Rules are negative for fracture criteria except inability to flex to 90°. Which of the following is the most appropriate evaluation and management plan?

answers.text: Clinical diagnosis of ACL tear is sufficient — refer directly to orthopedic surgery for reconstruction without further imaging
Obtain knee X-rays (per Ottawa criteria for inability to flex to 90°), followed by MRI for soft tissue evaluation; immobilize, ice, refer to orthopedics
Aspirate the knee effusion, inject corticosteroid for pain relief, and discharge with follow-up in 2 weeks
Apply a hinged knee brace and allow return to play once swelling resolves, as this is likely a minor sprain

explanation.correct: Correct. Systematic approach: (1) X-RAYS first — Ottawa Knee Rules are met (inability to flex to 90°) to rule out fracture (including Segond fracture — pathognomonic avulsion for ACL tear, tibial plateau fracture). (2) MRI — gold standard for soft tissue evaluation: confirms ACL tear, evaluates for concomitant meniscal tears (present in >50% of ACL injuries), collateral ligament damage, bone bruises, and cartilage injury. (3) ACUTE MANAGEMENT: RICE, knee immobilizer, crutches, NSAIDs for pain/swelling. (4) ORTHOPEDIC REFERRAL: ACL reconstruction is typically recommended for active/athletic patients. Physical therapy for prehabilitation before surgery (restore ROM and quad strength). The rapid hemarthrosis (swelling within 2 hours) is >70% specific for ACL tear — meniscal tears typically cause slower effusion development.
explanation.a: Incorrect. While the clinical exam strongly suggests ACL tear, MRI is recommended to evaluate for concomitant injuries (meniscal tears occur in 50-65% of ACL tears, and MCL/bone bruises are common). MRI guides surgical planning.
explanation.b: Correct. Systematic approach: (1) X-RAYS first — Ottawa Knee Rules are met (inability to flex to 90°) to rule out fracture (including Segond fracture — pathognomonic avulsion for ACL tear, tibial plateau fracture). (2) MRI — gold standard for soft tissue evaluation: confirms ACL tear, evaluates for concomitant meniscal tears (present in >50% of ACL injuries), collateral ligament damage, bone bruises, and cartilage injury. (3) ACUTE MANAGEMENT: RICE, knee immobilizer, crutches, NSAIDs for pain/swelling. (4) ORTHOPEDIC REFERRAL: ACL reconstruction is typically recommended for active/athletic patients. Physical therapy for prehabilitation before surgery (restore ROM and quad strength). The rapid hemarthrosis (swelling within 2 hours) is >70% specific for ACL tear — meniscal tears typically cause slower effusion development.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection in an acute traumatic effusion (likely hemarthrosis from ACL tear) is inappropriate — it does not address the structural injury and may mask symptoms. Aspiration may provide symptom relief but is not a definitive management step without imaging.
explanation.d: Incorrect. An audible pop with immediate hemarthrosis and positive Lachman test indicates a significant ligamentous injury (ACL tear), not a minor sprain. Return to play without proper evaluation risks further damage (meniscal tears, cartilage injury, pivot-shift episodes).

musculoskeletal.question: A 60-year-old man with hypertension and CKD stage 3 presents with acute onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint over the past 8 hours. He consumed a large meal of shellfish and beer the night before. Arthrocentesis reveals needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals. Which of the following is the diagnosis?

answers.text: Pseudogout
Gout
Septic arthritis
Rheumatoid arthritis

explanation.correct: Correct. Needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals (yellow when parallel to the compensator on polarized light microscopy) are monosodium urate crystals, diagnostic of gout. The 1st MTP joint (podagra) is the single most common site, involved in >50% of initial gout attacks. Arthrocentesis is the gold standard because it simultaneously confirms gout AND rules out septic arthritis (send fluid for Gram stain and culture) — critical since both can present with an acutely hot, red, swollen joint.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate deposition/CPPD disease) produces RHOMBOID-shaped, POSITIVELY birefringent crystals (blue when parallel to the compensator on polarized microscopy). This patient has NEEDLE-shaped, NEGATIVELY birefringent crystals — the defining feature of monosodium urate (MSU), diagnostic of gout. Pseudogout also tends to affect larger joints (knee, wrist) more than the 1st MTP.
explanation.b: Correct. Needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals (yellow when parallel to the compensator on polarized light microscopy) are monosodium urate crystals, diagnostic of gout. The 1st MTP joint (podagra) is the single most common site, involved in >50% of initial gout attacks. Arthrocentesis is the gold standard because it simultaneously confirms gout AND rules out septic arthritis (send fluid for Gram stain and culture) — critical since both can present with an acutely hot, red, swollen joint.
explanation.c: Incorrect. While septic arthritis must always be considered in an acutely inflamed joint (and can even coexist with gout), the presence of MSU crystals on polarized microscopy is diagnostic of gout. Synovial fluid should always be sent for BOTH crystal analysis AND culture to evaluate for concurrent infection, especially if the patient is febrile or immunocompromised.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic, symmetric, inflammatory polyarthritis diagnosed by clinical criteria (symmetric small joint involvement sparing DIPs), serologic markers (RF, anti-CCP antibodies), and imaging (marginal erosions, periarticular osteopenia). RA does NOT present as acute monoarticular arthritis with crystal findings on synovial fluid analysis.

musculoskeletal.question: A 62-year-old man with chronic gout and a serum uric acid of 9.8 mg/dL is being evaluated for initiation of urate-lowering therapy (ULT). He is currently experiencing a mild gout flare in his left ankle that began 2 days ago. Which of the following medications should NOT be started or adjusted during an acute gout flare?

answers.text: Colchicine — should not be started during a flare
Allopurinol — should not be started during a flare
Prednisone — should not be started during a flare
Indomethacin — should not be started during a flare

explanation.correct: Correct. Urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol, febuxostat, probenecid) should NOT be started during an acute gout flare because acute changes in serum uric acid levels — whether increasing OR decreasing — can destabilize existing monosodium urate crystal deposits in joints, worsening or prolonging the attack. ULT should be initiated only AFTER the acute flare has fully resolved. However, if a patient is ALREADY on ULT when a flare occurs, the ULT should be CONTINUED at the same dose — do not stop or adjust. When eventually starting ULT, colchicine prophylaxis should be co-prescribed for 3-6 months to prevent mobilization flares during the initial uric acid reduction phase.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Colchicine IS appropriate during an acute gout flare and is one of the three first-line acute treatment options (along with NSAIDs and corticosteroids). Colchicine is most effective when started within 12-36 hours of flare onset. The low-dose regimen (1.2 mg then 0.6 mg one hour later) is preferred over the older high-dose regimen due to equivalent efficacy with fewer GI side effects (diarrhea, nausea, abdominal cramping). Colchicine is also used as PROPHYLAXIS when initiating ULT (0.6 mg daily or BID × 3-6 months) to prevent mobilization flares.
explanation.b: Correct. Urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol, febuxostat, probenecid) should NOT be started during an acute gout flare because acute changes in serum uric acid levels — whether increasing OR decreasing — can destabilize existing monosodium urate crystal deposits in joints, worsening or prolonging the attack. ULT should be initiated only AFTER the acute flare has fully resolved. However, if a patient is ALREADY on ULT when a flare occurs, the ULT should be CONTINUED at the same dose — do not stop or adjust. When eventually starting ULT, colchicine prophylaxis should be co-prescribed for 3-6 months to prevent mobilization flares during the initial uric acid reduction phase.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Corticosteroids (oral prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day × 5-10 days with taper, intra-articular triamcinolone injection, or IM/IV methylprednisolone) are appropriate first-line treatment for acute gout flares — particularly in patients who cannot take NSAIDs (CKD, GI bleeding history, heart failure) or colchicine (drug interactions, intolerance). Corticosteroids reduce inflammation rapidly and are safe in renal impairment. Intra-articular injection is preferred for monoarticular flares as it provides rapid, localized relief with minimal systemic effects.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Indomethacin and other NSAIDs at full anti-inflammatory doses are first-line treatment for acute gout flares. Indomethacin (50 mg TID initially, tapering over 5-7 days) has traditionally been the most commonly used NSAID for gout due to its potent anti-inflammatory properties, though any NSAID at adequate dosing (naproxen 500 mg BID, sulindac, celecoxib) is effective. NSAIDs should be AVOIDED in patients with CKD (GFR <30), active peptic ulcer disease, GI bleeding, decompensated heart failure, or concurrent anticoagulation due to bleeding risk.

musculoskeletal.question: A 58-year-old man of Korean descent with recurrent gout is being evaluated for initiation of allopurinol. Before prescribing, his provider orders a specific pharmacogenomic test to assess the risk of a severe hypersensitivity reaction. Which of the following tests is most appropriate?

answers.text: G6PD
HLA-B*5801
CYP2D6
HLA-B*5701

explanation.correct: Correct. HLA-B*5801 testing is recommended before starting allopurinol in at-risk populations — Southeast Asian (especially Korean, Thai, Han Chinese: prevalence ~6-8%), African American (~3-4%), and Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander descent. HLA-B*5801 positivity carries a high risk of severe allopurinol hypersensitivity including DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) and SJS/TEN — potentially fatal reactions. If HLA-B*5801 is positive, allopurinol must NOT be used, and febuxostat (which does not require HLA testing) is the appropriate alternative xanthine oxidase inhibitor.
explanation.a: Incorrect. G6PD (glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase) testing is relevant before prescribing dapsone (PJP prophylaxis alternative) or rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase for tumor lysis syndrome). G6PD deficiency increases the risk of hemolytic anemia with these agents. G6PD is NOT relevant to allopurinol safety screening.
explanation.b: Correct. HLA-B*5801 testing is recommended before starting allopurinol in at-risk populations — Southeast Asian (especially Korean, Thai, Han Chinese: prevalence ~6-8%), African American (~3-4%), and Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander descent. HLA-B*5801 positivity carries a high risk of severe allopurinol hypersensitivity including DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) and SJS/TEN — potentially fatal reactions. If HLA-B*5801 is positive, allopurinol must NOT be used, and febuxostat (which does not require HLA testing) is the appropriate alternative xanthine oxidase inhibitor.
explanation.c: Incorrect. CYP2D6 genotyping is relevant for drugs metabolized by this enzyme, including codeine (which requires CYP2D6 conversion to morphine for efficacy — poor metabolizers get no analgesic effect, ultrarapid metabolizers risk toxicity) and tamoxifen (requires CYP2D6 conversion to active metabolite endoxifen). CYP2D6 has no relevance to allopurinol metabolism or safety.
explanation.d: Incorrect. HLA-B*5701 testing is required before starting ABACAVIR (an NRTI used in HIV ART regimens). HLA-B*5701 positivity predicts severe abacavir hypersensitivity reaction. The question asks about HLA-B*5801 (for allopurinol), not HLA-B*5701 (for abacavir). These are different HLA alleles with different clinical applications — a common board distractor.

musculoskeletal.question: A 55-year-old man presents with acute monoarthritis of the right knee with significant swelling and erythema. Arthrocentesis shows monosodium urate crystals. His serum uric acid drawn at the time of the flare is 5.8 mg/dL, which is within the normal reference range. Which of the following is the correct interpretation of this finding?

answers.text: Yes
No — uric acid may be normal or low during acute flares
Must be >8 for gout
Repeat in 24 hours

explanation.correct: Correct. Serum uric acid is unreliable during an acute gout flare and should NOT be used to diagnose or exclude gout. The gold standard for diagnosis is ARTHROCENTESIS with synovial fluid crystal analysis (needle-shaped, negatively birefringent MSU crystals under polarized microscopy). Serum uric acid should be rechecked 2-4 weeks after the flare resolves for an accurate baseline to guide urate-lowering therapy decisions. Remember: hyperuricemia is a risk factor for gout, but many people with elevated uric acid never develop gout, and many gout flares occur with 'normal' serum levels.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A normal serum uric acid does NOT rule out gout. During an acute flare, serum uric acid may paradoxically DECREASE due to the acute inflammatory response (IL-6 and other cytokines increase renal uric acid excretion) and hemodilution from inflammatory edema. Studies show that up to 40% of patients with crystal-proven acute gout have a normal serum uric acid during the attack. This is one of the most commonly tested and most commonly missed clinical concepts.
explanation.b: Correct. Serum uric acid is unreliable during an acute gout flare and should NOT be used to diagnose or exclude gout. The gold standard for diagnosis is ARTHROCENTESIS with synovial fluid crystal analysis (needle-shaped, negatively birefringent MSU crystals under polarized microscopy). Serum uric acid should be rechecked 2-4 weeks after the flare resolves for an accurate baseline to guide urate-lowering therapy decisions. Remember: hyperuricemia is a risk factor for gout, but many people with elevated uric acid never develop gout, and many gout flares occur with 'normal' serum levels.
explanation.c: Incorrect. There is no absolute serum uric acid threshold above which gout is diagnosed or below which it is excluded. The solubility limit of MSU is approximately 6.8 mg/dL at physiologic temperature and pH, but crystal formation depends on local tissue factors (temperature, pH, trauma, dehydration) in addition to serum concentration. Some patients develop gout at levels below 8 mg/dL, while others with levels >10 may never have an attack.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Repeating the uric acid level in 24 hours will not change the clinical decision-making. The flare should be treated based on clinical presentation and crystal analysis, not serum uric acid levels. Serial uric acid measurements during an acute attack are unnecessary and potentially misleading.

musculoskeletal.question: A 60-year-old man with a history of 4 gout flares per year and bilateral tophi on his ears has been on allopurinol for 6 months. His current serum uric acid is 7.2 mg/dL. The ACNP is adjusting his dose. What is the target serum uric acid goal for patients on urate-lowering therapy?

answers.text: <8 mg/dL
<6 mg/dL
<10 mg/dL
<2 mg/dL

explanation.correct: Correct. The treat-to-target goal for urate-lowering therapy is serum uric acid <6 mg/dL — approximately 0.8 mg/dL below the saturation threshold. Maintaining levels below 6 mg/dL promotes gradual dissolution of existing MSU crystal deposits (including tophi), prevents new crystal formation, and reduces flare frequency over time. For patients with severe tophaceous gout, an even lower target of <5 mg/dL may be pursued to accelerate tophus dissolution. ULT should be titrated (allopurinol by 100 mg increments every 2-4 weeks) with uric acid level monitoring to reach and maintain the target.
explanation.a: Incorrect. A target of <8 mg/dL is insufficient. The monosodium urate saturation point in body fluids is approximately 6.8 mg/dL at physiologic temperature — levels above this promote crystal formation and deposition. To dissolve existing urate crystal deposits and prevent new crystal formation, the serum uric acid must be maintained well BELOW the saturation point.
explanation.b: Correct. The treat-to-target goal for urate-lowering therapy is serum uric acid <6 mg/dL — approximately 0.8 mg/dL below the saturation threshold. Maintaining levels below 6 mg/dL promotes gradual dissolution of existing MSU crystal deposits (including tophi), prevents new crystal formation, and reduces flare frequency over time. For patients with severe tophaceous gout, an even lower target of <5 mg/dL may be pursued to accelerate tophus dissolution. ULT should be titrated (allopurinol by 100 mg increments every 2-4 weeks) with uric acid level monitoring to reach and maintain the target.
explanation.c: Incorrect. 10 mg/dL is well above the MSU saturation point and would not prevent crystal formation or gout flares. This level is associated with ongoing crystal deposition, tophus growth, and continued gouty arthritis attacks.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Targeting serum uric acid <2 mg/dL is unnecessarily aggressive and may have adverse consequences. Uric acid functions as an endogenous antioxidant, and extremely low levels have been associated (in observational studies) with neurodegenerative conditions. The established therapeutic target of <6 mg/dL (or <5 for tophaceous gout) provides the optimal balance of crystal dissolution with physiologic safety.

musculoskeletal.question: A 55-year-old woman presents with 6 months of symmetric joint pain and swelling affecting the MCP and PIP joints bilaterally. Her DIP joints are uninvolved. She has prolonged morning stiffness lasting 90 minutes. Which of the following joint involvement patterns is most characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis and helps distinguish it from osteoarthritis?

answers.text: Heberden nodes at DIP
Symmetric MCP/PIP involvement with ulnar deviation
Crepitus
Weight-bearing joint predominance

explanation.correct: Correct. Symmetric involvement of the MCP and PIP joints with ulnar deviation is the hallmark of rheumatoid arthritis. RA produces synovial inflammation and pannus formation that destroys articular cartilage and bone, leading to characteristic deformities: ulnar deviation at MCP joints, swan-neck deformity (PIP hyperextension + DIP flexion), boutonnière deformity (PIP flexion + DIP hyperextension), and rheumatoid nodules at pressure points. RA SPARES the DIP joints — involvement of DIP joints suggests OA (Heberden nodes) or psoriatic arthritis instead.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Heberden nodes (bony enlargements at the DIP joints) are a classic finding of osteoarthritis, caused by osteophyte formation at the DIP joint margins. RA characteristically SPARES the DIP joints — this is one of the most important distinguishing features between OA and RA. RA involves MCP, PIP, and wrists symmetrically.
explanation.b: Correct. Symmetric involvement of the MCP and PIP joints with ulnar deviation is the hallmark of rheumatoid arthritis. RA produces synovial inflammation and pannus formation that destroys articular cartilage and bone, leading to characteristic deformities: ulnar deviation at MCP joints, swan-neck deformity (PIP hyperextension + DIP flexion), boutonnière deformity (PIP flexion + DIP hyperextension), and rheumatoid nodules at pressure points. RA SPARES the DIP joints — involvement of DIP joints suggests OA (Heberden nodes) or psoriatic arthritis instead.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Crepitus (a grinding or cracking sensation with joint movement) is a nonspecific finding more commonly associated with osteoarthritis, caused by roughened articular cartilage surfaces and loose cartilage fragments within the joint space. While crepitus can occur in RA from joint destruction, it is not a distinguishing feature of RA versus OA.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Weight-bearing joint predominance (knees, hips, lumbar spine) is characteristic of OSTEOARTHRITIS, which results from mechanical wear-and-tear and cartilage degeneration. The first CMC (base of thumb) joint is also commonly affected by OA. In contrast, RA preferentially affects small joints of the hands (MCPs, PIPs), wrists, and MTPs in a symmetric pattern.

musculoskeletal.question: A 48-year-old woman presents with symmetric polyarthritis affecting her wrists and MCP joints. Her provider orders serologic testing to evaluate for rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatoid factor is mildly elevated but the provider wants a more specific confirmatory test. Which of the following laboratory tests is the MOST specific for rheumatoid arthritis?

answers.text: Rheumatoid factor
Anti-CCP antibodies
ESR
ANA

explanation.correct: Correct. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies have approximately 95% specificity for RA — making them the most specific serologic marker available. Anti-CCP can be positive YEARS before clinical disease onset (in the preclinical/asymptomatic phase) and is associated with more aggressive, erosive disease course. When both RF and anti-CCP are positive ('seropositive RA'), the diagnostic certainty is very high and the prognosis tends to be worse, with more rapid joint destruction requiring early aggressive DMARD therapy.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Rheumatoid factor (RF) is present in 70-80% of RA patients but has low specificity — it is also found in hepatitis C, Sjögren syndrome, SLE, endocarditis, sarcoidosis, and even in up to 5-10% of healthy elderly adults. While RF supports the diagnosis, it cannot confirm RA alone.
explanation.b: Correct. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies have approximately 95% specificity for RA — making them the most specific serologic marker available. Anti-CCP can be positive YEARS before clinical disease onset (in the preclinical/asymptomatic phase) and is associated with more aggressive, erosive disease course. When both RF and anti-CCP are positive ('seropositive RA'), the diagnostic certainty is very high and the prognosis tends to be worse, with more rapid joint destruction requiring early aggressive DMARD therapy.
explanation.c: Incorrect. ESR (erythrocyte sedimentation rate) is a nonspecific marker of systemic inflammation that is elevated in many conditions — infections, malignancies, autoimmune diseases, pregnancy, and aging. While ESR is useful for monitoring RA disease activity and treatment response, it cannot specifically diagnose RA or distinguish it from other inflammatory conditions.
explanation.d: Incorrect. ANA (antinuclear antibody) is primarily associated with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), where it is positive in >95% of patients. While ANA can be weakly positive in some RA patients, it is not a diagnostic marker for RA and has no role in differentiating RA from OA. High-titer ANA should prompt evaluation for SLE or other connective tissue diseases.

musculoskeletal.question: A 52-year-old woman is diagnosed with moderate-severe rheumatoid arthritis with evidence of early erosive changes on hand X-rays. Her rheumatologist plans to initiate disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) therapy. Which of the following is the gold-standard first-line DMARD for RA?

answers.text: Hydroxychloroquine
Methotrexate
Prednisone
Ibuprofen

explanation.correct: Correct. Methotrexate is the anchor/cornerstone DMARD for RA and should be started as early as possible to prevent irreversible joint destruction. Dosing: typically 15-25 mg weekly (PO or subcutaneous), always with folic acid (1 mg daily or 5 mg weekly) to reduce side effects including mucositis, GI upset, hepatotoxicity, and cytopenias. Required monitoring: CBC, LFTs, and creatinine at baseline and every 8-12 weeks. Key contraindications: pregnancy (Category X — teratogenic), significant hepatic disease, active infection, and significant cytopenias. If methotrexate fails after 3 months of optimal dosing, a biologic DMARD (TNF inhibitor, IL-6 inhibitor, JAK inhibitor) is added.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Hydroxychloroquine (Plaquenil) is a mild DMARD appropriate for very mild RA without erosive disease or as adjunctive therapy in combination with methotrexate. For moderate-severe RA with erosive changes already present (as in this patient), HCQ alone is insufficiently potent to prevent further joint destruction. The treat-to-target strategy for moderate-severe RA requires methotrexate as the foundational agent.
explanation.b: Correct. Methotrexate is the anchor/cornerstone DMARD for RA and should be started as early as possible to prevent irreversible joint destruction. Dosing: typically 15-25 mg weekly (PO or subcutaneous), always with folic acid (1 mg daily or 5 mg weekly) to reduce side effects including mucositis, GI upset, hepatotoxicity, and cytopenias. Required monitoring: CBC, LFTs, and creatinine at baseline and every 8-12 weeks. Key contraindications: pregnancy (Category X — teratogenic), significant hepatic disease, active infection, and significant cytopenias. If methotrexate fails after 3 months of optimal dosing, a biologic DMARD (TNF inhibitor, IL-6 inhibitor, JAK inhibitor) is added.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Prednisone and NSAIDs are used as 'bridge therapy' to control symptoms while waiting for DMARD onset of action (methotrexate takes 6-12 weeks for full effect). However, prednisone is NOT a disease-modifying agent — it does not prevent joint erosion or alter the disease course when used alone. Long-term prednisone carries significant side effects (osteoporosis, diabetes, infections, adrenal suppression) and should be tapered as quickly as possible once the DMARD takes effect.
explanation.d: Incorrect. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) provide symptomatic pain and inflammation relief but do NOT modify disease progression. RA patients on NSAIDs alone continue to develop progressive joint erosions and irreversible deformity. DMARDs are the only drug class that slows or halts the autoimmune-mediated joint destruction in RA. Relying on NSAIDs alone for RA management is considered substandard care.

musculoskeletal.question: A 58-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider complaining of joint stiffness each morning that takes over an hour to improve and gets better as she moves around during the day. Her 70-year-old husband also has joint stiffness, but his lasts only 15-20 minutes and worsens with activity throughout the day. Prolonged morning stiffness exceeding 60 minutes that improves with activity is most characteristic of which condition?

answers.text: OA
RA
Gout
Fibromyalgia

explanation.correct: Correct. Prolonged morning stiffness exceeding 60 minutes that IMPROVES with activity is a hallmark of inflammatory arthritis — most commonly RA. The stiffness reflects overnight accumulation of inflammatory mediators and synovial fluid in the affected joints. Physical activity increases synovial circulation, clears inflammatory cytokines, and improves lubrication, gradually reducing stiffness. This pattern contrasts sharply with OA stiffness (<30 min, worsens with use) and is a reliable clinical differentiator. Duration of morning stiffness is included in the 2010 ACR/EULAR RA classification criteria.
explanation.a: Incorrect. OA stiffness typically lasts <30 minutes in the morning, is described as 'gelling' after periods of inactivity, and WORSENS with prolonged use throughout the day (activity exacerbates pain from damaged cartilage). This is the opposite of the inflammatory pattern described in RA, where stiffness improves with activity as synovial fluid lubricates the inflamed joint.
explanation.b: Correct. Prolonged morning stiffness exceeding 60 minutes that IMPROVES with activity is a hallmark of inflammatory arthritis — most commonly RA. The stiffness reflects overnight accumulation of inflammatory mediators and synovial fluid in the affected joints. Physical activity increases synovial circulation, clears inflammatory cytokines, and improves lubrication, gradually reducing stiffness. This pattern contrasts sharply with OA stiffness (<30 min, worsens with use) and is a reliable clinical differentiator. Duration of morning stiffness is included in the 2010 ACR/EULAR RA classification criteria.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Gout presents as ACUTE monoarticular attacks (severe pain, erythema, swelling in a single joint — classically the 1st MTP) with pain-free intervals between flares, not chronic daily morning stiffness of multiple joints. Gout attacks peak within 12-24 hours and resolve within 1-2 weeks, unlike the persistent daily stiffness pattern of RA.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Fibromyalgia causes widespread body pain, fatigue, cognitive dysfunction ('fibro fog'), and sleep disturbance, but NOT the specific joint swelling, synovitis, or prolonged morning stiffness >60 minutes characteristic of RA. Fibromyalgia is a central sensitization/nociplastic pain syndrome without joint inflammation, and examination shows tender points without synovitis or joint swelling.

musculoskeletal.question: A 50-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis has failed methotrexate monotherapy and his rheumatologist plans to start adalimumab, a TNF inhibitor. Before initiating this biologic DMARD, which of the following screening evaluations is required?

answers.text: Uric acid and G6PD
TB screening and hepatitis B serology
HIV testing only
No screening needed

explanation.correct: Correct. Before initiating ANY biologic immunosuppressive therapy (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, JAK inhibitors, anti-CD20 agents), two mandatory screening evaluations are required: (1) TUBERCULOSIS screening (IGRA or TST) — TNF-alpha is critical for granuloma formation and containment of M. tuberculosis; TNF inhibition can reactivate latent TB, causing disseminated or miliary TB. If LTBI is detected, treatment must be initiated before starting the biologic. (2) HEPATITIS B serology (HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc) — TNF inhibitors and other biologics can cause HBV reactivation with fulminant hepatitis. Additional considerations: hepatitis C screening, baseline CBC/LFTs, and live vaccine administration (complete before starting immunosuppression).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Serum uric acid and G6PD are relevant to gout management (uric acid) and dapsone/rasburicase safety (G6PD), respectively. Neither has any relevance to TNF inhibitor safety screening.
explanation.b: Correct. Before initiating ANY biologic immunosuppressive therapy (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, JAK inhibitors, anti-CD20 agents), two mandatory screening evaluations are required: (1) TUBERCULOSIS screening (IGRA or TST) — TNF-alpha is critical for granuloma formation and containment of M. tuberculosis; TNF inhibition can reactivate latent TB, causing disseminated or miliary TB. If LTBI is detected, treatment must be initiated before starting the biologic. (2) HEPATITIS B serology (HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc) — TNF inhibitors and other biologics can cause HBV reactivation with fulminant hepatitis. Additional considerations: hepatitis C screening, baseline CBC/LFTs, and live vaccine administration (complete before starting immunosuppression).
explanation.c: Incorrect. While HIV testing may be appropriate in some clinical contexts, TB and hepatitis B are the MANDATORY pre-biologic screening tests specifically required by guidelines and drug labeling. HIV testing is not universally required before TNF inhibitor initiation.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Biologic immunosuppressive agents significantly increase infection risk — including reactivation of latent infections (TB, HBV) that can be fatal. Pre-treatment screening is MANDATORY and is required by FDA labeling, ACR guidelines, and standard of care. Failing to screen for TB and HBV before starting a TNF inhibitor is a recognized medical error with potentially life-threatening consequences.

musculoskeletal.question: A 68-year-old postmenopausal woman undergoes DEXA scanning as part of routine screening. Her femoral neck T-score is -2.8. She has no history of fragility fractures. Which of the following is the correct diagnostic classification based on this T-score?

answers.text: Normal
Osteopenia
Osteoporosis
Osteomalacia

explanation.correct: Correct. Osteoporosis is defined by the WHO as a T-score of -2.5 or lower at the femoral neck, total hip, or lumbar spine on DEXA scanning. This patient's T-score of -2.8 meets diagnostic criteria for osteoporosis. Pharmacologic treatment is indicated for: (1) T-score ≤-2.5; (2) prior hip or vertebral fracture (regardless of T-score); or (3) T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 with FRAX 10-year hip fracture risk ≥3% or major osteoporotic fracture risk ≥20%. First-line pharmacotherapy: bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, or zoledronic acid).
explanation.a: Incorrect. Normal bone density on DEXA is defined as a T-score of -1.0 or HIGHER (≥-1.0). A T-score of -2.8 is well below this threshold and indicates significantly reduced bone mineral density.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Osteopenia (low bone mass) is defined as a T-score between -1.0 and -2.5. A T-score of -2.8 falls below the -2.5 threshold, classifying this as osteoporosis, not osteopenia. For patients with osteopenia, the FRAX calculator (Fracture Risk Assessment Tool) is used to determine 10-year fracture probability and guide treatment decisions.
explanation.c: Correct. Osteoporosis is defined by the WHO as a T-score of -2.5 or lower at the femoral neck, total hip, or lumbar spine on DEXA scanning. This patient's T-score of -2.8 meets diagnostic criteria for osteoporosis. Pharmacologic treatment is indicated for: (1) T-score ≤-2.5; (2) prior hip or vertebral fracture (regardless of T-score); or (3) T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 with FRAX 10-year hip fracture risk ≥3% or major osteoporotic fracture risk ≥20%. First-line pharmacotherapy: bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, or zoledronic acid).
explanation.d: Incorrect. Osteomalacia is a disorder of bone MINERALIZATION (softening of bone) caused by vitamin D deficiency or phosphate depletion, distinct from osteoporosis (which is decreased bone MASS/density). Osteomalacia is diagnosed by elevated ALP, low vitamin D, and sometimes with iliac crest bone biopsy showing unmineralized osteoid. DEXA T-scores are used for osteoporosis classification, not osteomalacia diagnosis.

musculoskeletal.question: A 65-year-old woman is started on oral alendronate 70 mg weekly for osteoporosis. The ACNP reviews proper administration technique with the patient before discharge. Which of the following instructions regarding alendronate administration is most important to prevent esophageal complications?

answers.text: Take at bedtime with food
Take AM on empty stomach with full glass of water, remain upright 30-60 min
Take with calcium at the same time
Take with food to reduce GI upset

explanation.correct: Correct. Oral bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate) must be taken first thing in the morning on a completely empty stomach with a FULL glass of plain water (not coffee, juice, or mineral water — calcium and other minerals chelate the drug). The patient must remain fully UPRIGHT (sitting or standing) for at least 30 minutes after alendronate (60 minutes after ibandronate) to prevent esophageal reflux of the pill, which can cause esophagitis, esophageal ulceration, and stricture. No food, other beverages, or other medications should be taken during this waiting period.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Taking bisphosphonates at bedtime with food dramatically reduces drug absorption (bioavailability of oral alendronate is already only ~0.7%). Food in the stomach binds the drug and prevents absorption, and lying down after ingestion increases the risk of esophageal contact and ulceration. This represents the opposite of correct administration.
explanation.b: Correct. Oral bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate) must be taken first thing in the morning on a completely empty stomach with a FULL glass of plain water (not coffee, juice, or mineral water — calcium and other minerals chelate the drug). The patient must remain fully UPRIGHT (sitting or standing) for at least 30 minutes after alendronate (60 minutes after ibandronate) to prevent esophageal reflux of the pill, which can cause esophagitis, esophageal ulceration, and stricture. No food, other beverages, or other medications should be taken during this waiting period.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Calcium supplements must NOT be taken at the same time as bisphosphonates because calcium (and other divalent cations like iron, magnesium) chelates the bisphosphonate molecule, dramatically reducing absorption. Calcium supplements should be taken at a different time of day (e.g., lunchtime or evening), at least 30 minutes after the bisphosphonate. Total daily calcium intake goal: 1000-1200 mg from diet + supplements combined.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Taking bisphosphonates with food reduces absorption to nearly zero. Oral alendronate already has extremely low bioavailability (~0.7% under optimal conditions), and food reduces this further. The drug must be taken on a completely empty stomach to achieve even minimal absorption. For patients who cannot comply with these administration requirements or have esophageal disorders, IV zoledronic acid (given annually) or denosumab (SQ q6 months) are alternatives that bypass the GI tract entirely.

musculoskeletal.question: A 70-year-old woman on oral alendronate for 7 years presents with a 3-week history of aching pain in her right mid-thigh with no preceding trauma. Plain radiograph shows cortical thickening along the lateral femoral diaphysis with a faint transverse lucency. Which of the following complications should be suspected?

answers.text: Osteomalacia
Atypical femur fracture
Paget disease
AVN of femoral head

explanation.correct: Correct. Atypical femur fractures (AFFs) are a recognized complication of prolonged bisphosphonate use (>3-5 years), occurring in the subtrochanteric or diaphyseal femoral shaft — anatomically DISTINCT from typical osteoporotic hip fractures (which occur in the femoral neck/intertrochanteric region). AFFs result from over-suppression of normal bone remodeling, creating brittle cortical bone. Prodromal thigh or groin pain (weeks to months before complete fracture) is a warning sign requiring imaging (X-ray ± MRI for stress reaction). This complication is the rationale for considering a 'drug holiday' (temporary bisphosphonate discontinuation) after 3-5 years of therapy in lower-risk patients.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Osteomalacia results from vitamin D deficiency causing defective bone mineralization — it presents with diffuse bone pain, proximal muscle weakness, elevated ALP, and Looser zones (pseudofractures) on X-ray. Osteomalacia is not caused by bisphosphonate use.
explanation.b: Correct. Atypical femur fractures (AFFs) are a recognized complication of prolonged bisphosphonate use (>3-5 years), occurring in the subtrochanteric or diaphyseal femoral shaft — anatomically DISTINCT from typical osteoporotic hip fractures (which occur in the femoral neck/intertrochanteric region). AFFs result from over-suppression of normal bone remodeling, creating brittle cortical bone. Prodromal thigh or groin pain (weeks to months before complete fracture) is a warning sign requiring imaging (X-ray ± MRI for stress reaction). This complication is the rationale for considering a 'drug holiday' (temporary bisphosphonate discontinuation) after 3-5 years of therapy in lower-risk patients.
explanation.c: Incorrect. Paget disease of bone is an independent disorder of accelerated bone remodeling (osteoclast overactivity followed by disorganized osteoblast response), unrelated to bisphosphonate therapy. Paget disease is actually TREATED with bisphosphonates (particularly zoledronic acid) to suppress the excessive osteoclast activity.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Avascular necrosis (AVN/osteonecrosis) affects the femoral HEAD (not the shaft/diaphysis), most commonly associated with chronic corticosteroid use, alcohol abuse, sickle cell disease, and systemic lupus erythematosus. AVN presents with groin pain and is diagnosed by MRI showing femoral head signal changes. It is unrelated to bisphosphonate therapy.

musculoskeletal.question: An ACNP is discussing osteoporosis medications with a 72-year-old woman who has severe osteoporosis (T-score -3.4) with a prior vertebral compression fracture. The patient has failed bisphosphonate therapy. The provider wants to recommend an anabolic agent that stimulates new bone formation. Which of the following is classified as an anabolic (bone-building) osteoporosis medication?

answers.text: Alendronate
Denosumab
Teriparatide
Zoledronic acid

explanation.correct: Correct. Teriparatide (Forteo) is a recombinant parathyroid hormone (PTH 1-34) analog that stimulates OSTEOBLAST activity, promoting new bone formation (anabolic effect). This fundamentally differs from anti-resorptive agents (bisphosphonates, denosumab) which only slow bone loss. Teriparatide is reserved for severe osteoporosis (T-score ≤-3.5, multiple fractures, failed anti-resorptive therapy) and is limited to ≤2 years of use due to theoretical osteosarcoma risk (observed in rats, not confirmed in humans). After completing teriparatide, patients must transition to an anti-resorptive agent to maintain bone density gains. Contraindicated in: Paget disease, unexplained elevated ALP, bone metastases, prior skeletal radiation, open epiphyses.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Alendronate is an ANTI-RESORPTIVE agent — it inhibits osteoclast activity, slowing bone breakdown. It does not stimulate new bone formation. The distinction between anti-resorptive (slows bone loss) and anabolic (builds new bone) agents is an important pharmacologic concept for osteoporosis management.
explanation.b: Incorrect. Denosumab (Prolia) is a RANKL inhibitor — an anti-resorptive agent that blocks osteoclast differentiation and activation. While highly effective for bone density improvement, it does not stimulate osteoblasts to build new bone. Important caution: denosumab discontinuation causes rapid rebound bone loss and increased vertebral fracture risk — patients must transition to a bisphosphonate after stopping denosumab.
explanation.c: Correct. Teriparatide (Forteo) is a recombinant parathyroid hormone (PTH 1-34) analog that stimulates OSTEOBLAST activity, promoting new bone formation (anabolic effect). This fundamentally differs from anti-resorptive agents (bisphosphonates, denosumab) which only slow bone loss. Teriparatide is reserved for severe osteoporosis (T-score ≤-3.5, multiple fractures, failed anti-resorptive therapy) and is limited to ≤2 years of use due to theoretical osteosarcoma risk (observed in rats, not confirmed in humans). After completing teriparatide, patients must transition to an anti-resorptive agent to maintain bone density gains. Contraindicated in: Paget disease, unexplained elevated ALP, bone metastases, prior skeletal radiation, open epiphyses.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Zoledronic acid (Reclast) is an IV bisphosphonate — an anti-resorptive agent, not anabolic. It is given as a once-yearly IV infusion and is one of the most potent anti-resorptive options available. It bypasses the GI tract (avoiding esophageal side effects of oral bisphosphonates) and ensures adherence through annual dosing. It shares the same class risks as oral bisphosphonates: osteonecrosis of the jaw (rare) and atypical femur fractures with prolonged use.

musculoskeletal.question: A 64-year-old woman has never had a DEXA scan. She has no significant risk factors for osteoporosis. At what age do current guidelines recommend universal DEXA screening for all women, regardless of risk factors?

explanation.correct: Correct. The USPSTF and major medical societies recommend universal DEXA screening for ALL women beginning at age 65, regardless of risk factors. For MEN, universal screening is recommended at age 70 (or earlier with risk factors). For postmenopausal women aged 50-64, DEXA screening is recommended when clinical risk factors are present, guided by tools such as the FRAX calculator (treat if 10-year hip fracture risk ≥3% or major osteoporotic fracture risk ≥20%). DEXA scans measure bone mineral density at the femoral neck, total hip, and/or lumbar spine, reported as T-scores compared to young adult reference values.
explanation.a: Incorrect. Age 50 is too early for UNIVERSAL DEXA screening in women without risk factors. However, postmenopausal women UNDER 65 with specific risk factors (low body weight <127 lbs, smoking, family history of hip fracture, early menopause, chronic corticosteroid use, prior fragility fracture) should be screened earlier based on FRAX risk assessment.
explanation.b: Incorrect. While age 60 may prompt screening in women with risk factors, it is NOT the threshold for universal screening. Universal (all-comers, regardless of risk factors) DEXA screening begins at age 65 for women per USPSTF, ACOG, and Endocrine Society guidelines.
explanation.c: Correct. The USPSTF and major medical societies recommend universal DEXA screening for ALL women beginning at age 65, regardless of risk factors. For MEN, universal screening is recommended at age 70 (or earlier with risk factors). For postmenopausal women aged 50-64, DEXA screening is recommended when clinical risk factors are present, guided by tools such as the FRAX calculator (treat if 10-year hip fracture risk ≥3% or major osteoporotic fracture risk ≥20%). DEXA scans measure bone mineral density at the femoral neck, total hip, and/or lumbar spine, reported as T-scores compared to young adult reference values.
explanation.d: Incorrect. Waiting until age 75 for initial DEXA screening would miss 10 years of potential intervention (age 65-75), during which significant bone loss and preventable fractures could occur. Osteoporotic fractures (hip, vertebral, wrist) cause substantial morbidity, mortality, and loss of independence in elderly women. Hip fractures alone carry a 20-30% one-year mortality rate in older adults.
